question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the scientific, economic and policy evidence that supports the decision to continue with a calendar-based slurry spreading ban, rather than a weather-led or risk-led approach.
answer_text: My department has given careful consideration to the suggestion to regulate slurry spreading based solely on a weather- led or risk-led approach rather than fixed dates. DAERA commissioned the Agri Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) to undertake a comprehensive review of the underpinning science of the closed period in 2020 and again in 2024. The AFBI analysis concluded that “The current evidence base supports the closed period as the most effective strategy for minimising the risks associated with slurry spreading from 15 October to the 31 January. Removal of the closed period and reliance solely on the current Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) restrictions on weather and soil conditions, (i.e. restrict applications based on soil moisture and rainfall conditions), will not sufficiently mitigate the risk associated with slurry applications during this period.”Science shows that during this period grass and crop growth is limited by lower temperatures and short day length. So, while there may be dry spells in some winters and soils may be trafficable on occasions, there is minimal nutrient uptake. Therefore, any nutrients in slurry applied during this period remain in the soil and are highly vulnerable to run off to waterways during any subsequent wet weather until grass and crop growth picks up in the spring. Nutrients lost to waterways are nutrients wasted. This incurs both economic costs to farmers, who need to replace nutrients with chemical fertilisers or additional slurry, and environmental costs through water pollution.  There is no scientific, agronomic or economic evidence supporting a change to the closed period. This policy position is consistent with other comparable countries in Northwest Europe where similar or longer closed periods are implemented.The NAP Regulations require farms to have at least 22 weeks of slurry storage, ensuring sufficient capacity for the 16 week closed period and any unsuitable conditions for spreading before or after it. The overarching aim is to protect our watercourses at a time when significant improvements are urgently needed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of cattle that were recorded as reactors to the Tuberculin Skin Test for bovine TB, but subsequently determined to be false positives, in each of the last five calendar years, broken down by agricultural sector.
answer_text: The tuberculin skin test has a very high test specificity (i.e. the ability of a test to correctly identify an animal that is free from infection as negative) of 99.98%. This means that it is very rare that a non-infected animal will be wrongly classified as diseased (approximately one animal in 5000).The number of animals that display ‘visible TB lesions’ at the time of slaughter following a positive test is not considered a standard of disease confirmation, but rather an indicator of disease progression as the progression to ‘visible TB lesions’ may take months or years to develop, and given the specificity of the test most reactor animals with no visible lesions will actually be infected. Data is collated on the number of reactor animals where disease has been confirmed by the presence of visible lesions at slaughter and/or by laboratory confirmation when samples have been taken for histopathology and/or culture, but not all animals have samples submitted to the laboratory. These statistics typically lag; this is attributable to pending laboratory results. The data for 2021-2024 has been extracted as below.YearTotal no. of CITT reactorsNo. of CITT reactors with visible lesions at slaughter and/or positive laboratory testing 2021143596841202217370844920231815087912024205109632202518892Complete data not yet availableMy Department does not collate this data by agricultural sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of cattle that were recorded as reactors to blood tests for bovine TB, but subsequently determined to be false positives, in each of the last five calendar years, broken down by agricultural sector.
answer_text: Data for Great Britain estimates that the interferon gamma (IFN-g) blood test has a specificity (the ability of a test to identify correctly an animal that is free from infection as negative) of 96.55% which equates to an average of one false positive animal for every 30 uninfected cattle tested. Due to this specificity, which is significantly lower than that for the tuberculin skin test, the IFN-g test is currently only used in selected TB breakdown herds in Northern Ireland (NI).  Cattle slaughtered for disease control on the basis of IFN-g test results are not subject to additional confirmation testing in NI apart from subsequent findings at post-mortem examination at abattoirs. However, such post-mortem inspections are not considered a standard of disease confirmation following a positive test, but rather an indicator of disease progression as the progression to ‘visible TB lesions’ may take months or years to develop. The visible lesion rate for IFN-g animals is lower than for skin reactors.Indeed, relatively few IFN-g reactors will have visible lesions at slaughter. This may be because animals are detected at an early stage of infection (visible lesions have not developed), or because slaughterhouse inspection has a low sensitivity (small lesions are not found). However, in a small proportion of cases this may be because the animals are false positives. The number of animals removed on the basis of positive IFN-g results alone where visible TB-like lesions have been found at slaughter over the past 5 years is shown below. Where animals were reactors to both tuberculin skin and IFN-g tests, these are removed as skin reactors and are not included in these figures.  My Department does not collate this data by agricultural sector. Prior to 21st April 2021, the removal of IFN-g only positive animals was not mandatory, although most positive animals were voluntarily surrendered. YearIFN-g positive animals removedVisible TB-like lesions at slaughter2021156321920222185245202314381782024143225320251379393
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the NIEA is able to provide consent for new-build properties proposing the use of septic tanks in areas where the mains wastewater system is currently at capacity.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) assesses all applications seeking consent to discharge in accordance with the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, the Water Framework Directive objectives, and wider environmental protection duties.		It is important to emphasise, NIEA does not grant consent for the use of septic tanks or private sewage systems as an automatic alternative where the public wastewater infrastructure is at capacity.	The proliferation of these systems is not considered environmentally sustainable. Departmental guidance is clear that connection to the public foul infrastructure, provided by NI Water, is the preferred and most sustainable means of wastewater disposal. Applicants are expected, in the first instance, to engage with NI Water to establish whether a connection is feasible.		In the case of residential developments involving multiple properties and shared wastewater infrastructure, adoption of the infrastructure by Northern Ireland Water is a key consideration. Where no suitable adoptable solution exists, interim or private wastewater treatment systems cannot be supported, to ensure the long-term protection of homeowners, the environment, and the integrity of wastewater infrastructure.		Further information is available in NIEA’s published guidance on applying for consent to discharge, available at:	https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/regulating-water-discharges
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of Freedom of Information requests handled by their Department were responded to outside the statutory 20 working day limit, including extensions under section 10(3) of the Freedom of Information Act, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why the Knowledge Network system, described by his Department as logging, tracking and maintaining an auditable record of ministerial correspondence and related workflows, was not searched or identified in response to Freedom of Information request DOH/2025-0303 and the subsequent Internal Review; and (ii) the information relating to the drafting, clearance and delay of Assembly Written Questions is recorded on that system.
answer_text: Knowledge Network provides a tracking system for ministerial correspondence received within the Department and is used widely within all Private Offices throughout the NICS. However, as it is not the acknowledged electronic repository for Departmental records, it appears this was unfortunately overlooked when responding to your Freedom of Information request dated 1 December 2025 and again in response to your request for Internal Review dated 16 January 2026. As you will already be aware, I have requested that officials rectify this oversight as urgently as possible and provide you with the information you requested originally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the phased review of taxi legislation, in particular the outcome of the consultation on the class c category concerning funeral and wedding cars.
answer_text: A review of Class C taxi licensing is presently being finalised. Officials will provide me with any recommendations arising from that review. If any changes are proposed to Class C Taxi Licensing, then these will go out to public consultation this year. As part of the review process officials and I met with representatives of the funeral and wedding car industry to get a clear understanding of the issues they face.Officials will provide an update in due course, most likely next month.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of Freedom of Information requests submitted to their Department have required enforcement action by the Information Commissioner before a substantive response was issued, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024 the ICO has taken enforcement action on two occasions prior to a substantive response being issued by the Department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 39943/22-27, to detail the number of Freedom of Information requests assigned specifically to the First Minister’s Principal Private Secretary in each of the last two years.
answer_text: We refer the member to the response to AQW 39943/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Loughs Agency staffing is reduced to the approved complement or less.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to liaise with the Agency, our co-sponsoring Department in Ireland, and NSMC Joint Secretariat to consider the interpretation and application of the Loughs Agency’s staffing figures against its approved complement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 36966/22-27, to detail (i) the approvals required for the Loughs Agency to create 11 new and additional posts; (ii) whether the Loughs Agency have approval for the 11 new and additional posts created in the last five years; (iii) how the Loughs Agency has evolving statutory responsibilities when its functions are clearly set out in the IBO; (iv) , given the Loughs Agency stated it did not have the skills supposedly required, whether he will take steps to bring all science and corporate service delivery within hi
answer_text: Loughs Agency’s Chief Executive Officer is responsible for managing and controlling recruitment in line with its NSMC approved staffing complement. Any changes to the Agency’s complement require approval from Sponsor Departments, Finance Departments and the NSMC.Loughs Agency has advised that the 11 posts referred to were all created within the Agency’s existing approved staff complement. Officials are continuing to engage with Agency on this matter.While the Agency’s statutory responsibilities have not changed, the operational environment in which its core functions are carried out has evolved over the years. There are no plans to bring the Agency’s science and corporate service delivery functions into my Department. This is subject to ongoing consideration by my officials.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the £3.18 million of capital monies allocated to NI Water will be spent.
answer_text: NI Water was awarded an additional £3.18m general Capital allocation for use in 2025/26 and have allocated this funding to the following projects:£0.78m to Killinchy, Inisharoan pumping station; £1m to Rathfriland, Sleepy Valley Wastewater Pumping Station; £0.6m to Derryhale, Dobbin’s Wastewater Pumping Station; and £0.8m to Magherafelt Drainage Area Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any of the 32 deaths registered since 31 March 2020 under ICD-10 code P96.4 involved babies who were born alive.
answer_text: It is important this matter is treated with sensitivity and compassion.Tragically, the vast majority of these deaths were medically complex cases.Deaths can only be registered for those born alive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the number of people that have gone blind in both eyes whilst on an NHS waiting list for cataract surgery in the last five years.
answer_text: Cataract surgery although temporarily sight-limiting, is reversible, and is rarely a time-critical procedure.On referral for potential cataract surgery each patient is triaged, and allocated an outpatient appointment according to clinical priority. The probability of cataract cases deteriorating to severe or total sight loss is low and, should the condition deteriorate, referrers can upgrade the referral information, allowing the Trust to re-prioritise Management Information figures collated for SPPG show no cases of bilateral sight impairment or severe sight impairment relating to cataract over the past five years. Age-related macular degeneration is the dominant cause (ca. 50%), with glaucoma (ca. 12%) and diabetic eye disease (ca. 10%) the next major causes. These figures, although not official statistics, represent accurate and reproducible management information on the causes of sight loss in Northern Ireland. Although it is acknowledged that cataract can have a debilitating effect on quality of life, and it remains unacceptable that anyone should wait longer than is clinically-necessary for optimal care and treatment, any vision loss due to cataract is reversible. Macular degeneration, diabetic eye disease, and glaucoma represent greater risks to sight loss, with some interventions being time-critical.For this reason, my Elective Care Framework is identifying and targeting ophthalmology time-critical interventions, including building capacity to manage and treat these conditions, within the available funding envelope.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to introduce a statutory duty for prevention on public agencies that support people at risk of homelessness.
answer_text: There is already considerable scope within the existing statutory framework to provide additional support and prevention measures, as evidenced by the amount of work already taking place.Placing additional statutory duties on public bodies outside my area of responsibility may require legislation and Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the amount it provided for the production and publication of the Committee on Standards and Privileges Report on a complaint against Mr Timothy Gaston MLA (NIA103/22-27), including (i) staff time costs within the Commissioner for Standards’ Office; (ii) Committee staff support costs; (iii) legal advice costs, both internal and external; and (iv) any other administrative or publication costs incurred from receipt of the complaint on 11 February 2025 to publication on 2 February 2026.
answer_text: Pursuant to the Assembly Members (Independent Financial Review and Standards) Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (the 2011 Act), the Commissioner for Standards is not a member of staff of the Assembly, and is an independent corporation sole. The 2011 Act requires that the Assembly Commission shall provide the Commissioner for Standards with staff, services and accommodation, as the Commissioner may reasonably require for the purpose of discharging the functions of the office. The Assembly Commission is statutorily required to respect the independence of the Commissioner for Standards, including that office’s role to determine and secure the services it considers necessary itself.The Assembly Commission does not hold any records in respect of staff or any other costs incurred by the office of the Commissioner for Standards in relation to the particular complaint you cite. The support provided to the Committee on Standards and Privileges in relation to this complaint, including for staff time and the provision of any internal legal advice, have been provided from within existing budgets and have not been charged separately. Such costs are not apportioned across individual complaint cases and there are not, therefore, any records of these specific costs in relation to this complaint. The Assembly Commission did not provide resources for the Committee for external legal advice in relation to its consideration of this complaint.  No other administrative or publication costs were incurred at any time by the Assembly Commission in relation to the Committee’s consideration of this complaint.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any practical support or guidance his Department is providing to Northern Ireland vessel owners who have paid for Isle of Man fishing licences but may now be unable to operate legally due to the Isle of Man’s visa restrictions on overseas crew.
answer_text: Licences for access to fish within the territorial waters of the Isle of Man are normally purchased in April each year. My officials have advised fishing industry representatives to contact the Department of Environment, Food and Agriculture (DEFA) in relation to licence renewals and immigration rules. I am seeking urgent meetings with the Isle of Man Ministers for Environment, Food and Agriculture and The Treasury to seek to resolve this issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the rationale for; and (ii) the cost of, the installation of a partition screen in the Communications Office in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: As part of the rolling programme of office refurbishment in Parliament Buildings, works are underway in the Communications Office. These include the replacement of the existing partglazed partition with one that is fully glazed, designed to maximise natural light within this basement office and to meet current health and safety standards through the use of modern safety glass.The estimated cost, based on supplier quotations, is £8,000 (excluding VAT).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will commission a working group with the hospitality industry to review the methodology used to calculate rates for the sector, to ensure that future rates are fair and equitable.
answer_text: Since stopping Reval 2026 I am considering the most appropriate form of engagement to enable sectors to bring forward their information and evidence. I met with hospitality representatives on the day I made my announcement and I was clear that I am in listening mode.  I advised I would take the time to examine any evidence or proposals submitted before deciding on next steps. Any future approach will be informed by that material and will aim to ensure that the rating system remains fair, transparent and equitable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any engagement he or his Department has had with the Isle of Man Government regarding the immigration and visa requirements now being applied to crews on Northern Ireland fishing vessels operating in Manx waters; and (ii) whether he has sought an urgent resolution or transitional arrangement.
answer_text: I have written to the Ministers for the Environment, Food and Agriculture and the Treasury for the Isle of Man to meet as a matter of urgency to discuss this concerning development and to explore potential solutions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether he is aware of the Isle of Man Government’s recent indication that Northern Ireland fishing vessels with non-British or Irish crew may no longer be permitted to fish commercially within Manx territorial waters; and (ii) any assessment he has made of the potential impact on the Northern Ireland fishing industry.
answer_text: I am aware of the recent changes to Isle of Man immigration policy that bar non-UK and non-Irish crew from working within its territorial seas. The evaluation of the consequences for the Northern Ireland fishing fleet is ongoing. At present, there are approximately 58 vessels that possess a licence to fish in Isle of Man waters. Over the past four years, catches by these licensed vessels within Isle of Man territorial limits have been valued at around £9 million.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether it is her responsibility for ensuring that data relied upon by her Department for policy and safeguarding decisions is clear, transparent and fit for purpose, notwithstanding that operational data collection is undertaken by policing bodies.
answer_text: I recognise the significant value that statistics and data have in both supporting evidenced-based policy development and keeping the public informed.  The Department utilises a wide range of statistics from across the criminal justice system as part of its broader evidence base in delivering its objectives.A large proportion of the statistics and research which is utilised by the Department of Justice is based on information which had originated in management information systems operated by justice partners.  It is the responsibility of each organisation to ensure the quality of the information produced by it is fit for purpose and, to this end, much of the statistical information published by the Police Service of Northern Ireland, the Public Prosecution Service and other Criminal Justice Organisations is produced by staff in professional statistical grades, seconded to them by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA).  These staff work to a Code of Practice produced by the UK Statistics Authority. The Code of Practice for Statistics sets the standards that those producing official statistics should apply to deliver statistics that serve the public good, in line with section 10 of the Statistics and Registration Service Act 2007 and is based on the core principles of Trustworthiness, Quality and Value.  The Department has the assurance of the standards these staff work to as part of NISRA and the core principles underpinning the UK Code of Practice for Statistics in the production of official statistics across the criminal justice system.Definitions for information shared across and between criminal organisations are developed jointly and shared where relevant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of whether sex-disaggregated data is necessary for effective safeguarding policy, particularly in relation to sexual offences, domestic abuse and violence against women and girls; and (ii) to detail any steps her Department has taken to ensure that the data it relies upon enables meaningful sex-based analysis.
answer_text: In relation to part (i) of the question, the Department utilises a wide range of statistics from across the criminal justice system as part of its broader evidence base in delivering its objectives, including in relation to sexual offences, domestic abuse and violence against women and girls.  In relation to the issue raised in part (ii), a large proportion of the statistics and research which is published by the Department of Justice, the Police Service of Northern Ireland the Public Prosecution Service and other Criminal Justice Organisations is produced by staff in professional statistical grades seconded to them by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA).  These staff work to a Code of Practice produced by the UK Statistics Authority.The Code of Practice for Statistics sets the standards that those producing official statistics should apply to deliver statistics that serve the public good, in line with section 10 of the Statistics and Registration Service Act 2007 and is based on the core principles of Trustworthiness, Quality and Value. The Department has the assurance of the standards these staff work to as part of NISRA and the core principles underpinning the UK Code of Practice for Statistics in the production of official statistics across the criminal justice system.The Review of data, statistics and research on sex and gender was commissioned in February 2024 by the UK’s Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, and was led by Professor Alice Sullivan. It aimed to (a) identify obstacles to accurate data collection and research on sex and gender identity in public bodies and in the research systems, and (b) set out good practice guidance for how to collect data on sex and gender identityAs a large proportion of the statistics and research which is published by the Department of Justice is produced by NISRA.  NISRA is considering this review and its recommendations in discussion with officials in Departments and with partners across the UK government statistical system, to determine the most appropriate way to implement the recommendations from the review, where they are needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of cases where patients on routine cataract waiting lists deteriorate into severe or total sight loss before being assessed; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to prevent such outcomes.
answer_text: Cataract surgery is one of the highest volume surgeries carried out across the UK, including in Northern Ireland. It is almost always daycase, not usually requiring general anaesthesia, and the intervention enjoys excellent Quality of Life outcomes. Although sight-limiting, it is reversible, and not a time-critical procedure.i) On referral for potential cataract surgery each patient is triaged with reference to the clinical and social information outlined in the referral, and allocated a combined diagnostic and assessment outpatient appointment according to clinical priority. Those triaged as routine are allocated on the basis of clinical need and are not deemed to be at sight-threatening risk. The probability of routine cases deteriorating to severe or total sight loss is assessed as low but, should the condition deteriorate whilst on a routine waiting list, patients and referrers are encouraged to upgrade the referral information, allowing Trust ophthalmology teams to re-prioritise the case.ii) You will be aware that waiting lists and times are a Departmental and Ministerial priority, including those for ophthalmology. Extensive work has been ongoing, within the context of my Elective Care Framework (ECF), to manage ophthalmology waiting lists and reduce the waiting times. In recognition of the sustained demand-capacity gap which impacts on treatments, including those for cataract, my officials are currently working with providers around business cases to build capacity within clinical teams with additional recruitment. I acknowledge that capacity-building is a key strategic pillar to address waiting lists sustainably, and prioritisation of ECF new and re-purposed funding, including investments to support in-reach Independent Sector activity, is ongoing. This additional capacity will have a positive impact on waiting times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the ongoing engagement he has planned with the hospitality industry following the pause of Reval 2026.
answer_text: I have asked the hospitality sector to gather the necessary evidence and any proposals it wishes to bring forward following the pause of Reval 2026. Once that material is submitted, officials will assess it and I will determine the appropriate next steps for further engagement with the industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a timeline on when the Proposed Ammonia Strategy will be brought to the Executive.
answer_text: The Proposed Ammonia Strategy will be brought to the Executive once the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) 2026 – 2029 has been agreed, as the NAP review includes the Strategy’s two mandatory measures.  A NAP Stakeholder Task and Finish Group has been reviewing these since October 2025 - covering the phased move to Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment by 2030 and the restriction on granular urea without urease inhibitors.An eight -week public consultation on updated NAP proposals will follow once the Group concludes its work.While an exact timeline cannot yet be confirmed, the aim is to progress the Proposed Ammonia Strategy to the Executive as quickly as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the reasons why pay increases for Health and Social Care Trust chief executives were considered necessary; and (ii) any assessment made of Health and Social Care productivity and service performance prior to approving the revised pay framework.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether any element of the revised Health and Social Care Trust (HSC) Senior Executive Pay Framework involved backdated or retrospective pay, including (i) the period covered by any back pay; (ii) the total cost; (iii) the number of HSC Trust chief executives and other senior executives that benefited; (iv) the date on which the arrangements were approved; and (v) whether the Minister personally approved any retrospective pay increases.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will commission a working group with the hospitality industry to review the methodology used to calculate rates for the sector, to ensure that future rates are fair and equitable.

To ask the Minister of Health whether the Systems Oversight Measures or Support and Intervention Framework include any direct financial consequences, including the reduction, withholding or clawback of pay, for Health and Social Care Trust chief executives in cases of sustained poor performance.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any new productivity, performance or delivery-related conditions were incorporated into the contracts or pay arrangements of Health and Social Care Trust chief executives as part of the revised pay framework.
To ask the Minister of Health to publish the productivity statistics relied upon by his Department to assess Health and Social care Trust performance for each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: My Department uses a range of indicators to assess productivity, including new and review attendances, ward attendances, missed appointments (DNAs), patient cancellations (CNAs), hospital cancellations, and reasons for cancellation. Information is also provided on outpatient activity at integrated clinical assessment and treatment services (ICATS) and activity undertaken in the independent sector. Please see the link below for official statistics published on the Department of Health’s webpage:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/doh-statistics-and-researchThe Department’s published statistics provide a consistent, transparent and objective basis for monitoring performance across the health and social care system over time. This data is validated by Trusts prior to publication on a quarterly basis. These measures support robust oversight of how services are being delivered, highlight areas where improvement may be needed and help ensure that public resources are being used as efficiently and effectively as possible.In addition to the published statistics, my Department also uses other benchmarking data on an ad hoc basis to assess performance against peers, including information from the British Association of Day Surgery (BADS) and Caspe Healthcare Knowledge Systems (CHKS).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 29800/22-27, for an updated position in relation to the capacity for intensification of foul sewage discharge in Cullybackey.
answer_text: The management of the public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. I have sought further information from NI Water to inform the response to your question.NI Water has advised that Cullybackey is part of the Ballymena Drainage area. This area of the catchment is currently closed to future development. This means that within this area it is no longer possible for a developer funded solution to achieve a connection to the sewer network. Therefore, the wastewater impact assessment process is no longer able to provide a solution that a developer can implement.NI Water advised it is currently engaging with a trader in the area to work towards an increase in network storage capacity at the Cullybackey wastewater pumping station. However, it is likely that NI Water will not be able to consider upgrades in Cullybackey until the next Price Control period (PC28, 2028/29 – 2032/33). Delivery of any upgrades would be subject to the availability of funding. 	NI Water will continue to support developments in closed catchments where a site has extant planning with no conditions relating to capacity constraints, or it is a redevelopment and there is no intensification of foul sewage discharge post development compared to the sewage discharge from the site within the last 5 years. NI Water recommends that developers continue to apply through the NI Water pre-development enquiry process and it will advise of the next steps. NI Water will continue to consider each planning application on a case-by-case basis depending on the location within the catchment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the future investment planned for Ballymena Wastewater Treatment Works to permit additional connections to the foul sewer network, including the areas of Ballymena and surrounding villages that will benefit from this investment.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has confirmed that there has been no investment in relation to increasing the capacity of Ballymena Wastewater Treatment Works in the last five years as there are no capacity issues at the works which has stopped development. NI Water further advises that future investment requirements are yet to be determined as part of the PC28 business plan, however investment in terms of the capacity of Ballymena Wastewater Treatment Works to unlock development is not an issue currently.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether priority will be given to investing in areas where mitigation measures, such as storm-water offsetting, is not permitted to achieving a connection.
answer_text: The prioritisation of capital investment is an operational matter for NI Water. NI Water prioritises its investments by focusing on the most urgent risks to public health, environmental compliance, and economic growth. The investment needs are then defined as part of the regulatory Price Control determination.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the foul sewer capacity on the Ballymena Road, Cullybackey, being classified as a closed catchment to all new developments that will see an intensification of foul sewage discharge, to detail any investment that has been assigned to allow future connections in this area.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has informed me that it does not have any investment scheduled to allow future connections on the Ballymena Road, Cullybackey.However, NI Water has also declared that, during PC21(2021 to 2027), it has been investing in the development of a water quality model of the wider Ballymena catchment to better understand the impact of NI Water assets (storm overflows and wastewater treatment works) on water quality. NI Water is currently engaging with NIEA and DAERA on the outcome of this modelling and associated opportunities for more targeted investment in the Ballymena area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the investment provided to increase the capacity of the Ballymena Wastewater Treatment Works in each of the last five years; and (ii) the number of additional connections this investment permitted, broken down by area.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has confirmed that there has been no investment in relation to increasing the capacity of Ballymena Wastewater Treatment Works in the last five years as there are no capacity issues at the works which has stopped development. NI Water further advises that future investment requirements are yet to be determined as part of the PC28 business plan, however investment in terms of the capacity of Ballymena Wastewater Treatment Works to unlock development is not an issue currently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of children aged 10 to 13 that have been charged with criminal offences in each of the last ten years, broken down by offence category.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the information requested in the question.  The recording of information on persons charged with criminal offences is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the number of children charged with criminal offences to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has assessed the impact of inconsistent sex and gender recording practices, as identified in the Independent review of data, statistics and research on sex and gender carried out by Professor Alice Sullivan, on the integrity and comparability of crime statistics relied upon for justice policy in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer to AQW 40279/22-27.The recording and publication of information in relation to police-recorded crime data is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any further query relating to practices used in the recording of crime data to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 37736/22-27, whether he will give an assurance that any decision to renew the relationship with Stonewall will be brought to the Executive for approval.
answer_text: Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 places a statutory duty on all public authorities, in carrying out their functions, to promote equality of opportunity and good relations, and to make equality central to the whole range of public policy decision-making. Engagement with equality groups, for example from the disability, race and minority ethnic sectors as well as LGBTQ+ groups, is an important aspect of delivering our equality duties.	Stonewall is one of several organisations that the Civil Service engages with to support its wide-ranging work on equality, diversity and inclusion.Approval of expenditure for membership of organisations aligned to section 75 groups to support a diverse and inclusive Civil Service, including Stonewall, is not considered to be a matter requiring Executive approval pursuant to section 20(4) of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether her Department relies on police-recorded crime data in the development of justice policy, safeguarding frameworks and the allocation of resources; and (ii) to detail any assurance her Department has in relation to the consistency and reliability of that data where differing practices are used in the recording of biological sex, legal sex and gender identity.
answer_text: In relation to part (i) of the question, I recognise the significant value that statistics and data have in both supporting evidence-based policy development and keeping the public informed.  The Department utilises a wide range of statistics from across the criminal justice system as part of its broader evidence base in delivering its objectives.  In relation to part (ii), a review of data, statistics and research on sex and gender was commissioned in February 2024 by the UK’s Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, and was led by Professor Alice Sullivan. It aimed to (a) identify obstacles to accurate data collection and research on sex and gender identity in public bodies and in the research systems, and (b) set out good practice guidance for how to collect data on sex and gender identity. The Sullivan review was published on 19 March 2025.  Following the publication of the review, the Government Statistical Service (GSS) Harmonisation team are undertaking priority work to develop best practice harmonised standards for sex and gender identity data. They intend to publish research findings for the development of updated sex and gender identity harmonised standards for the online mode in Autumn 2026. Here, the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) within the Department of Finance is considering this review and its recommendations in discussion with officials in Departments and with partners across the UK government statistical system, to determine the most appropriate way to implement the recommendations from the review, where they are needed.Recorded Crime statistics in Northern Ireland hold an Accredited Official Statistics designation, awarded by the Office for Statistics Regulation. It signifies that official statistics have been independently reviewed and comply with the highest standards of trustworthiness, quality, and value as outlined in the Code of Practice for Statistics.Recorded crime statistics published by the Police Service of Northern Ireland are produced by staff in professional statistical grades seconded to them by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA).  These staff work to a Code of Practice produced by the UK Statistics Authority. The Code of Practice for Statistics sets the standards that those producing official statistics should apply to deliver statistics that serve the public good, in line with section 10 of the Statistics and Registration Service Act 2007 and is based on the core principles of Trustworthiness, Quality and Value.The Department has the assurance of the standards these staff work to as part of NISRA and the core principles underpinning the UK Code of Practice for Statistics in the production of official statistics across the criminal justice system.However, the recording and reporting of information in relation to police-recorded crime data is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am commit

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any alternative statutory powers are available to deal with a child aged 10 to 13 who commits attempted murder, arson with intent to endanger life, armed robbery, firearms offences or serious sexual assault, if such offences are no longer prosecutable under the proposed amendment.
answer_text: Children under the Minimum Age of Criminal Responsibility (MACR) cannot be prosecuted; however, if there are concerns regarding a child’s behaviour, there is a range of non-criminal statutory powers available to address those behaviours.  The most important of these is the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, which allows for investigations into a child’s circumstances to be carried out, and which places a duty on Trusts to reduce the need to bring criminal proceedings against children and to encourage children not to commit offences.  The Children's Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 also places a duty on a wide range of children’s authorities to cooperate to improve the well-being of children in Northern Ireland, including their mental health, their ability to live in safety and with stability; and to help them to make a positive contribution to society.  Other powers dealing with mental health, safeguarding, child criminal coercion and a variety of other issues can also be found in legislation.   In the types of cases referenced, offending or risk-taking behaviour would be addressed through the provision of a welfare-based, rather than punitive, response using existing statutory provisions as would be employed were such behaviours exhibited by those younger than the current MACR. Often young children who display such concerning behaviour have experienced past adversity and trauma, and present with many unmet needs.  A number will have been groomed, coerced, or exploited as victims themselves.  Taking these factors into account, it is essential that our response is proportionate to individual circumstances, age appropriate, and delivered through a coordinated, multiagency approach that addresses the root causes of a child’s behaviour.  By doing so, we can ensure the best outcomes for children, families, communities and victims, whilst also preventing recurrence and reoffendingCurrently, where there is a concern over a child’s offending behaviour, a referral can be made to the Children’s Diversion Forum, which is a multi-agency panel available on a region-wide basis in Northern Ireland. These panels, or other such partnership arrangements, will be considered as potential models to work with children under any new age of criminal responsibility, should the Assembly support the amendment to the Justice Bill to increase the Minimum Age of Criminal Responsibility.  Whilst any new arrangements will need to be defined and agreed, they will be aimed at working with the child to help them understand the impact and consequences of their behaviour; to take responsibility for their actions; and to support them to make changes and avoid future offending.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the governance arrangements that exist to ensure that an auditable record of political clearance when comments, amendments or instructions from officials, the Minister or his Special Adviser on draft answers to Assembly Written Questions are not retained.
answer_text: The Department of Health meets its retention and disposal requirement through the application of its Good Management, Good Records (GMGR) framework, which sets out the standards expected for the creation, maintenance, retention and disposal of records across the Department and by those working within or under contract to Health and Social Care. GMGR reflects professional best practice and the retention requirements in place at the time of its publication, and business areas are expected to follow these arrangements. All records created in response to Assembly Written Questions are managed within these parameters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department takes to ensure transparency and accountability in the Ministerial clearance process when it does not retain any drafts, internal submissions or clearance records relating to the preparation of answers to Assembly Written Questions.

To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to cases where draft answers and associated correspondence are not retained, to detail the assurance his Department can provide that answers to Assembly Written Questions have not been materially altered or delayed during clearance.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are taken to ensure his Department’s handling of records connected to Assembly Written Questions complies with Section 46 Code of Practice issued under the Freedom of Information Act 2000.
answer_text: Section 46 of the Freedom of Information Act 2000 provides a statutory Code of Practice that advises public authorities on the keeping, management and destruction of their records, ensuring that these processes support compliance with wider information rights legislation.The Department of Health meets this requirement through the application of its Good Management, Good Records (GMGR) framework, which serves as the Department’s Disposal Schedule and sets out the standards expected for the creation, maintenance, retention and disposal of records across the Department and by those working within or under contract to Health and Social Care. GMGR reflects professional best practice and the retention requirements in place at the time of its publication, and business areas are expected to follow these arrangements.Records connected to Assembly Written Questions are managed in line with these standards, including requirements for accurate creation, appropriate retention, and secure storage and disposal.A review of GMGR is currently under way. Until that process concludes and a revised schedule is published, existing measures remain in force.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his Department’s policy on the retention and destruction of emails and correspondence relating to the drafting and clearance of answers to Assembly Written Questions; and (ii) whether such material is routinely deleted.
answer_text: The Department of Health meets its retention and disposal requirement through the application of its Good Management, Good Records (GMGR) framework, which sets out the standards expected for the creation, maintenance, retention and disposal of records across the Department and by those working within or under contract to Health and Social Care. GMGR reflects professional best practice and the retention requirements in place at the time of its publication, and business areas are expected to follow these arrangements.All records created in response to Assembly Written Questions are managed within these parameters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide the GDP growth rates for (i) Northern Ireland; (ii) Great Britain; and (iii) the United Kingdom as a whole for (a) 2020; (b) 2021; (c) 2022; (d) 2023; (e) 2024; and (f) 2025.
answer_text: The latest available data in relation to GDP growth in the jurisdictions specified are available at the following link: Regional economic activity by gross domestic product, UK - Office for National Statistics 	It should be noted that ONS regional GDP data are, at the time of writing, only available up to 2023. 		An alternative source is the NISRA Composite Economic Index, which can be found at the following link: 		NI Composite Economic Index | Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the criteria that is applied when assigning a Freedom of Information request within their Department; and (ii) whether record authors may request that such cases be assigned to them.
answer_text: Requests for information are assigned by the Information Management Unit to the head of the business area most likely to hold recorded information in scope of the request.The policy allows for a request to be transferred to a more appropriate business area if necessary.In the case of the Private Offices, which operate jointly, the request will be assigned jointly.  Business areas allocate the request to an official to take the lead in drafting.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the departmental policy which authorises an official who authored a record of a ministerial meeting to have responsibility for handling a Freedom of Information request seeking the disclosure of that record.
answer_text: It is normal practice in departments for requests for information to be assigned to the business area most likely to hold recorded information in scope of the request.  This ensures the request is dealt with by staff with knowledge of the subject area and helps promote a culture of transparency.A final, pre-issue check is conducted by the departmental Information Management Unit before the response is issued.Should a requestor be dissatisfied, they may request an independent review.  This is conducted by a senior member of staff not involved in the handling of the request.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of Freedom of Information requests that have been assigned to the First Minister's Principal Private Secretary in each of the last two years.
answer_text: The Private Offices within TEO operate on a joint basis, and requests are therefore allocated by the Departmental Information Management Unit to the Private Offices jointly.The numbers allocated in each of the last two years are shown below.2024 – 12 FOIs allocated to Private Offices.2025 – 20 FOIs allocated to Private Offices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide the GDP growth rates for the goods producing sector in (i) Northern Ireland; (ii) Great Britain; and (iii) the United Kingdom as a whole for (a) 2020; (b) 2021; (c) 2022; (d) 2023; (e) 2024; and (f) 2025.
answer_text: Data in relation to parts (i) and (iii) are publicly available on the NISRA website and can be found at the following link: Index of Production | Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency Data in relation to part (ii) are publicly available on the ONS website and can be found at the following link: Regional gross value added (balanced) by industry: all ITL regions - Office for National Statistics	It should be noted that data for parts (i) and (iii) are available for the time period specified, but for part (ii) data are, at the time of writing, only available up to 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide the GDP growth rates for the services sector in (i) Northern Ireland; (ii) Great Britain; and (iii) the United Kingdom as a whole for (a) 2020; (b) 2021; (c) 2022; (d) 2023; (e) 2024; and (f) 2025.
answer_text: Data in relation to parts (i) and (iii) are publicly available on the NISRA website and can be found at the following link: Index of Services | Northern Ireland Statistics and Research AgencyData in relation to part (ii) are publicly available on the ONS website and can be found at the following link: Regional gross value added (balanced) by industry: all ITL regions - Office for National Statistics	It should be noted that data for parts (i) and (iii) are available for the time period specified, but for part (ii) data are, at the time of writing, only available up to 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy in relation to her Department’s press release on 15 January 2026, stating that economic output in Northern Ireland increased by 2.9 per cent, which is higher than in Great Britain, to detail (i) how economic output is defined in that context; and (ii) the exact publication, table and page number for the Great Britain figure used to facilitate that comparison.
answer_text: i) The definition of NICEI can be found here: https://www.nisra.gov.uk/system/files/statistics/2026-01/NI-Composite-Economic-Index-Q3-2025-statistical-bulletin.pdf.ii) The source of the figures contained in my press release were included in the 'Notes to editors' that accompanied the press release.  https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/news/minister-welcomes-latest-economic-output-figures-north

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 37627/22-27 and AQW 31577/22-27, and in light of the Loughs Agency spending £48,730 on the three external reports, to detail (i) the purpose of each of the three external reports; (ii) the date each report was commissioned; (iii) the cost of each report; (iv) whether the project manager was interviewed as part of any of the reports; and (v) any assessment that has been carried out by the Department or the Loughs Agency as to whether earlier internal engagement with the project manager would have impacted the nee
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that: - the TREES Project Forestry Review Report was commissioned to review the progress of the pilot ‘TREES’ project from a forestry perspective;the Provision of Audit and Assessment of TREES Project (Governance and Oversight Review) was commissioned to review the governance framework of the TREES Project; andthe Investigation Report was commissioned in relation to employee matters. The Agency has further advised that: - the TREES Project Forestry Review Report was commissioned in June 2024;the Provision of Audit and Assessment of TREES Project (Governance and Oversight Review) was commissioned in June 2024; and the Investigation Report was commissioned in August 2024. The Agency has confirmed that: - the TREES Project Forestry Review Report cost £16,200;the Provision of Audit and Assessment of TREES Project (Governance and Oversight Review) cost £23,940; andthe Investigation Report cost £8,590. 	The Agency has advised that this relates to employment matters. My Department has not carried out any assessment of this as it is an operational matter for the Loughs Agency to consider.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 37627/22-27 and AQW 31577/22-27, and in light of the Loughs Agency spending £48,730 on the three external reports, to detail (i) the date on which (a) the concerns raised by the Woodland Trust were first received by the Loughs Agency; (b) the relevant member of staff was suspended; and (c) the formal investigation commenced and concluded; and (ii) whether the relevant member of staff was provided an opportunity to respond to the concerns raised by the Woodland Trust prior to suspension or dismissal.
answer_text: (i)(a)	Loughs Agency has advised that Agency management became aware of a range of issues with the project in January 2024. Specific issues raised by the Woodland Trust were formally received by the Agency on 7 February 2024.(i) (b), (i) (c) and (ii) These questions relate to employment matters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) who authorised the publication of the court listings naming PSNI officers who are taking civil cases arising from the 2023 data breach; (ii) any guidance or risk assessment that was applied in deciding not to anonymise or redact the names involved; and (iii) for her assessment of how this publication adheres to heightened protections in a case that is directly linked to a serious data breach.
answer_text: (i)  Court details, other than those relating to family proceedings, are routinely listed publicly online unless an application is made to the court, by legal representatives of parties to the proceedings, for anonymity.  (ii)  No decisions were taken by administrators on whether or not to anonymise or redact the names.  It is the responsibility of the legal representative or parties to proceedings to make an application seeking anonymity.  Following an application, any decision to grant anonymity can only be made by a judge.  (iii)  Having been alerted by one of the parties to the cases, as a precautionary measure my Department took immediate action and made the decision to have Public Court Lists Online taken down from approximately 8.30pm on the evening of 3 February 2026 until 6am on 4 February 2026, when refreshed lists were available following the application of an interim Judicial direction. Any individual wishing to seek anonymity should discuss the matter with their legal representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any concerns have been raised in relation to the existence of geographical blackspots in the North Antrim constituency where patients assessed as requiring a domiciliary care package are unable to secure one due solely to their address.
answer_text: When approached in respect of this query, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust has expressed regret that whilst providing a Trust-wide Home Care Service, there are areas within its geography which are more challenging to deliver services.  These challenges are particularly pronounced for individuals living in very rural areas who are frequently having to wait longer for a package of care than the Trust would wish.The Trust has established an ambitious 2-year Strategic Reform of Home Care with the aims and objectives to stabilise Home Care Services, optimise service delivery and level up service provision across the Trust’s geography.  This reform will address the geographical blackspots.Whilst further improvements are required, the Reform programme is making significant improvements and unmet need for home care across the Northern Trust is currently at its lowest level for six years.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of farmers that were contacted on the morning of the lifting of the slurry ban by representatives of the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme to inform them that soil sampling had been carried out incorrectly by an official and that a new sample would be required before slurry spreading could commence.
answer_text: My Department is managing the delivery of soil sampling and analysis in the fourth and final zone of the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme which covers Co Antrim and a mop up of fields in the other 3 zones. As a priority, the soil sampling contractor engaged with livestock farms, where possible, before the end of the closed period for slurry spreading on 31 January 2026. No farms were contacted, on the morning of the end of the closed period for slurry spreading, regarding incorrect soil sampling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of medicine graduates from Queen’s University Belfast in each of the last ten years; (ii) the proportion of those graduates that have remained working in Northern Ireland as doctors; and (iii) a breakdown of the specialties in which they are currently working.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the proportion of patients in the North Antrim constituency with a cancer diagnosis that are currently waiting to commence cancer treatment, broken down by (a) cancer type; (b) average waiting time; and (c) the range of waiting times; and (ii) any action being taken to reduce these waiting times.
answer_text: The Northern Health & Social Care Trust (NHSCT) has provided the most recent data for patients living in the North Antrim constituency who have received a cancer diagnosis and are currently waiting to commence cancer treatment.  This data is given in Table 1.Table 1 – Patients in North Antrim constituency1 with a cancer diagnosis, awaiting treatment within NHSCT.Cancer Type	Numbers waiting	Average waiting time in days	Range of waiting time in daysBreast 	5	17 	6 - 24Lower GI 	8	10 	3 - 22Haematological	<5	7 	7Notes:1North Antrim determined using postcodes BT42, BT43, BT44, BT53, BT54, BT55.Patients within the North Antrim constituency represent 24% of all patients currently awaiting cancer treatment within the Northern Health & Social Care Trust. The Northern Health & Social Care Trust has implemented a number of actions to reduce delays.  These include weekly monitoring of theatre utilisation to ensure capacity is maximised.  Cancer performance is continuously monitored, with monthly performance meetings held for each specialty to review performance, identify any areas of delay, and take actions to address delays.  In addition, a monthly Director led Cancer Performance meeting provides senior oversight to review performance and drive ongoing improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the progress made against each of the 58 actions contained in A Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022–2032 Funding Plan since its publication.
answer_text: Since 2024/25, £10.6m in recurrent funding has been invested in delivering the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022–2032. However, this remains significantly below the levels set out in the Cancer Strategy Funding Plan, which projected £55.7m recurrent funding by Year 4 of the Strategy, rising to £145m by Year 10. This funding gap reflects the broader financial pressures facing Health and Social Care.Despite these financial constraints, good progress has been made across the Strategy’s 60 actions, including but not limited to the following:•	Two Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDC) opened in Whiteabbey and South Tyrone hospitals in December 2022. •	Investment in RDCs has created additional CT and MRI imaging capacity. During 2025/26 this is expected to provide an additional c9,000 CT scans and c5000 MRI scans targeting those patients who have been waiting longest following red flag and urgent referrals.•	A demand and capacity model for Systemic Anti Cancer Therapy (SACT) has been completed.•	Optimal pathways reviews for the top three haematology pathways have been completed and will underpin wider service transformation.•	A review of Adolescent and Young Adult Cancer Services has been completed and minimum standards for the service have been developed. A new regionally networked service model is in the process of being established.•	A review of Advanced Communication Skills training needs identified 703 staff working within cancer and palliative care teams that required training. By March 2026, ~600 professionals will have Advanced Communication Skills training.•	A review of Breast Cancer services is underway, and a regional waiting list was introduced in May 2025.•	A Cancer Research Strategic Framework for Northern Ireland, published on 20 November 2025. The pace of implementation of the Strategy will continue to be shaped by the resources available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of hospitality businesses that have been revalued between Reval 2023 and January 2026, broken down by (i) local council area; and (ii) month.
answer_text: The tables below show the count of properties that have been added or altered in the current list by i) Local Council Area and ii) by month. The figures represent those licenced (pubs) and hotel premises subject to action taken between the requested dates to review the Net Annual Value of premises by the District Valuer in the current 2023 valuation list. Such cases can be triggered by the ratepayer, notification of material changes to property from Council building controls, or the District Valuer raising a case to review the valuation.The statistics do not represent the valuation of pubs and hotels properties carried out as part of the Reval 2026 exercise.Table 1: Case closure 1/4/23 – 30/01/26 Licensed Premises - PubsYear Case ClosedMonth Case ClosedNumber of Licensed Premises Properties in 2023 list where a new/alterations case was closed since 1 Apr  1 shows all cases broken down by year and month that were completed in the current Revaluation list from effective date of 1 April 2023 to 30 January 2026. question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any action being taken to secure the rounded stone bosses (i) on the wall beside the West Door of Parliament Buildings; and (ii) at other sites around Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is committed to the ongoing preservation and maintenance of Parliament Buildings and keeps the condition of the building under regular review:The rounded stone bosses on the wall beside the West Door are scheduled for remedial works in March 2026.Where repairs to architectural stone fixings elsewhere around Parliament Buildings are identified following inspection, such works will be carried out as required and prioritised according to condition and risk, to ensure the continued safety and preservation of the building fabric.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 37540/22-27, to detail (i) whether a redundancy was requested at any time for the first Loughs Agency vessel that sank; (ii) all dates a redundancy was fitted to the two Loughs Agency vessels that sank; and (iii) any consideration he has given to the Loughs Agency hiring survey boat services from (a) local harbour authorities; (b) commercial boat operators; and (c) his Department, instead of owning such vessels.
answer_text: The Loughs Agency has advised that it is not aware of any request for a redundancy for the first Loughs Agency vessel which sank in 2015.The Agency has further advised no redundancy devices were fitted to either vessel which sank.(a) – (c) I consider this to be an operational matter for the Loughs Agency. The Agency has advised that it makes use of industry vessels as a more cost-effective option, and that it has also utilised Departmental vessels where suitable and available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of staff employed by each branch of the North South Language Body, broken down by community background.

Both agencies have submitted community background monitoring information to the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland. However, this information is not publicly available, as each organisation has fewer than 26 employees based in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 37540/22-27, for his assessment of (i) whether a redundancy or device that alerts crew remotely if a vessel is taking on water should have been fitted to the two Loughs Agency boats that sank ten years apart and while unattended and at berth; and (ii) whether any management decisions by the Loughs Agency led to the sinking of the two survey vessels ten years apart, especially as these incidences happened in the same circumstances and at the same location.
answer_text: I consider that these are both operational matters for the Loughs Agency. The Agency has advised that independent Marine Surveyors were appointed to investigate the circumstances of both sinkings and that their reports did not identify any information suggesting that management decisions by Loughs Agency led to either incident.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he will publish the annual results of the Irish Sea Cod Fishing Industry Partnership Survey since its inception; and (ii) to detail (a) the number of cod recorded; (b) the number of survey tows undertaken; (c) the locations of those tows; and (d) the methodology used to ensure year-on-year comparability, in each year since inception.
answer_text: The Fisheries Science Partnership survey is a fishery independent survey using commercial fishing vessels that provides data on older fish populations to support the scientific assessment of the fish stock. The results of each year’s survey are distributed to those involved in the survey, including the commercial vessel but are not routinely published. There are no plans to routinely publish this survey.The table below reports the information you have requested for the last six years. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) to detail whether the Irish Language Commissioner’s powers, as of 26 January 2026, have been commenced; (ii), in order to publish best practice standards, whether these must be approved by the First Minister and deputy First Minister acting jointly; and (iii) whether the discretionary power to offer non-binding advice and guidance in Section 78L (3) of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 is expressly limited to public authorities as defined in Section 78Q of the Act.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Office have recently commenced, on the 27th January 2025 further provisions of the Identity and Language (NI) Act 2022As per section 78M(2) of the legislation, the ‘First Minister and deputy First Minister acting jointly may approve best practice standards submitted under subsection (1) with or without modifications’.  As per 78Q(1) of the legislation ‘In this Part “public authority” means an authority listed in Schedule 3 of the Public Services Ombudsman Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (c.4 (N.I))’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32204/22-27, to detail (i) any additional funding that has been allocated specifically to reduce dermatology waiting times in each Health and Social Care Trust in (a) 2024/25; and (b) 2025/26; and (ii) how that funding has been used.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32204/22-27, to detail (i) any targets that have been set for reducing dermatology waiting times in each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) by what timeframe that patients waiting over 104 weeks are expected to be treated.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32204/22-27, (i) for his assessment of dermatology patients waiting in excess of 300 weeks for an initial assessment; and (ii) to detail any immediate actions he is taking to address the longest waits.
answer_text: Dermatology waiting times are too long and unfortunately the demand for dermatology in all Trusts continues to exceed capacity.  Regrettably patients are experiencing excessive waits for appointments and treatment which is not the service that Trusts would wish to provide.All new referrals, appointments and outpatient waiting lists are managed by clinical priority. Each patient should be assigned a priority based on the urgency of their treatment. Trusts will manage patients under three categories:TwoWeek Wait (red flag/suspected cancer)UrgentRoutinePatients with the same priority are then booked strictly in chronological order, in line with the Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP).In terms of routine waits all Trusts have patients waiting over 300 weeks for an initial assessment.  These patients have been triaged as low clinical priority and the waiting times will vary by Trust depending on referral demand.  Several waiting list initiatives (WLI) are underway to manage red flag demand and improve waiting times. Approximately £4.7m has been earmarked for dermatology waiting list initiatives during Q1-Q3 of 2025/26. From April 2025 to September 2025 this resulted in a spend totalling £2.3m.  This spend has delivered almost 6,100 dermatology new/review outpatient assessments and day case procedures.The Regional Dermatology Forum continues to meet to consider medium- and long-term solutions, including addressing workforce challenges, exploring alternative pathways to mitigate clinical workforce shortfalls, and identifying opportunities to increase capacity within each Trust.Primary and Secondary Care services are also working collaboratively to reduce waiting times and improve access.  Ensuring that patients are seen in the right place by the right professional. This includes:Within Primary Care Elective Services:• Investment to deliver 4,410 face-to-face and remote consultations annually for patients presenting with routine dermatology conditions such as adult and childhood eczema, psoriasis, acne, non-specific rashes, and suspected actinic keratosis.• A pilot launched in March 2025 enables GPs with enhanced skills to surgically treat patients diagnosed with low-risk basal cell carcinomas in primary care.• The Regional Dermatology Photo Triage Pathway supports image capture and e-referral for suspected skin cancer and urgent basal cell carcinoma cases. As of October 2025, 90% of GP practices are contracted to provide this pathway, enabling effective decision-making at the point of triage in secondary care.Within Secondary Care Services:• Development of specialist nursing roles to assess and treat patients;• Use of biologic medications to manage chronic dermatological                         conditions;• Introduction of virtual and telephone consultations to provide advice           and guidance and reduce waiting times;• Implementation of Patient-Initiated Follow-Up (PIFU) services and            enhanced triage clinics to refo
