question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32204/22-27, to detail (i) the number of dermatology patients that have been placed on (a) virtual consultation pathways; (b) telephone consultation pathways; and (c) Patient Initiated Follow-Up pathways, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) any evaluation that has been undertaken of the impact of these measures on waiting times and patient outcomes.
answer_text: Each Trust has been asked to provide a response to this information which can be seen below: SEHSCT Responsethe number of dermatology patients that have been placed on (a) Virtual Consultation Pathways - currently 0 dermatology patients on a 	virtual consultation only pathway	(b) Telephone Consultation Pathway - currently 0 dermatology patients who are on a virtual consultation only pathway	(c) Patient Initiated Follow-Up – currently 0 patients awaiting Patient Initiated Follow-Up review, but the Trust are moving forward with this in 2026	any evaluation that has been undertaken of the impact of these measures on waiting times and patient outcomes - Once ‘live’ with PIFU the Trust will evaluate it. 	Some patients receive telephone consultation on a 6-month basis alternated with face to face on a 6 monthly as part of their biologics pathway, when they are already at a hospital appointment.WHSCT Response:(a) The Western Trust can confirm that there are currently 0 Dermatology patients on a virtual pathway.(b) The Western Trust can confirm that there are currently 0 Dermatology patients on a telephone consultation pathway.(c) The Western Trust can confirm that there are currently 0 Dermatology patients on a patient initiated follow- up pathwayThe Western Trust advises that as there are currently 0 patients waiting on any of these Pathways therefore there are no measures in place. BHSCT Response:(a) and (b) Virtual/ Telephone consultation pathways – Total of 960 patients on telephone review pathway.(c) Patient Initiated Follow-Up pathways - Total of 878 patients added to Patient Initiated Follow up review list. Within BHSCT the Dermatology service has been proactive in the training and development of their nursing team and have expanded their specialist nursing workforce. This includes an advanced nurse practitioner and highly trained nurses who are proficient in surgical procedures and biopsies within dermatology. This has enabled the service to maximise nurse led activity, which has helped transform the service the Trust offer from a consultant delivered dermatology service to a consultant led service. The service is committed to improving waiting times and have introduced a number of service improvement initiatives such as virtual activity aimed at reducing waiting times which are supported by the Trust’s nursing, medical and AHP staff – examples are; the Photo Hub pilot, advice and guidance to GPs and direct to surgery pilots (Business case submitted). The service continues to receive complimentary patient feedback regarding both face to face and virtual consults.5176 Dermatology patients remain within the Trust service on a review waiting list for further follow up (following on from a first initial appointment with a Dermatology Consultant).SHSCT Response: (a) Virtual Consultation Pathways - SHSCT currently has 116 patients awaiting virtual telephone consultation. 	(b) telephone consultation pathways - SHS

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline his Department's plans for County Hall, Ballymena.
answer_text: My officials met with you on 12 December 2025 and outlined the position with regard to County Hall, Ballymena.The Business Case for the consolidation of Civil Service accommodation in the Ballymena area has recently been approved, with a fully refurbished Academy House, Broughshane Street, Ballymena being the preferred option. When fully refurbished, Academy House will provide fit for purpose, modern accommodation to hybrid working standards and will accommodate Civil Service staff currently working in both Academy House and County Hall, Ballymena. Non civil service bodies located in County Hall will progress their own plans for their future accommodation. This project is expected to begin late in 2026 with completion in 2029 and will require staff to decant from Academy House to County Hall while the refurbishment is undertaken. Civil Service staff who will be impacted have been informed and Trade Union Side has been advised.County Hall Ballymena will be disposed of upon completion of the project as it will then become surplus to the Civil Service needs in that area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32204/22-27, to detail any assessment he has made of the impact of workforce vacancies on dermatology waiting times in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Dermatologists play a crucial role in diagnosing and treating skin conditions across every HSC Trust in Northern Ireland. They manage a wide range of dermatological issues, from common conditions such as acne, eczema, and hair and nail disorders, to more complex illnesses including skin cancer.As with many healthcare specialties, recent years have brought significant challenges in attracting suitably skilled clinicians to the HSC. This has resulted in recruitment delays, long-term vacancies, and extended waiting times for assessment and surgery. Northern Ireland is not alone in facing these pressures; balancing capacity and workforce shortages in Dermatology is a widespread issue across the UK.The British Association of Dermatologists (BAD) published a Workforce Census in September 2024 highlighting a significant shortfall in dermatology consultants across the UK. The report notes: “There are at least 96.8 WTE vacant posts in dermatology, and an approximate shortfall of 180 WTE by BAD calculations. The long-term workforce strategy needs to recognise the growing deficit in dermatology WTEs due to changes to workforce practice with a majority of trainees working part time, more consultants taking early retirement, and a cohort of doctors aged 60–65 who are about to retire.”Similarly, the Getting it Right First Time (GIRFT) programme has set out recommendations to improve recruitment and retention, and to harness new technologies to ensure patients with conditions such as skin cancer, eczema, psoriasis, and acne receive faster and more equitable access to care.The shortage of dermatology consultants has contributed to increasing waiting times for specialist care across HSC Trusts. It has also placed growing pressure on consultants currently in post, who often undertake additional sessions to mitigate gaps when locum cover is unavailable. To address waiting list pressures, several Waiting List Initiatives (WLIs) are in place to manage regional red flag demand and improve access. Approximately £4.7m has been allocated for dermatology WLIs across Q1–Q3 of 2025/26. Between April and September 2025, £2.3m of this funding delivered almost 6,100 new and review outpatient consultations and day-case procedures across the HSC Trusts.The Regional Dermatology Forum continues to explore medium and long term solutions, including workforce planning, alternative pathways to reduce reliance on consultant manpower, and opportunities to expand capacity within each Trust.Primary and Secondary Care services are also working collaboratively to improve waiting times, enhance access, and address workforce challenges ensuring patients are seen in the right place by the right professional.In Primary Care Elective Services:•	Investment to deliver 4,410 face-to-face and remote consultations annually for patients presenting with routine dermatology conditions such as adult and childhood eczema, psoriasis, acne, non-specific rashes, and suspected actinic keratosis.•	A pilot laun

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether members of the public frequent the site where the Loughs Agency boat sank, including those that have boats moored at the location and those using the river for other activities.
answer_text: Further to my response to AQW 37461/22-27, my officials have received further clarification from the Loughs Agency who have advised that while the area is not open to the general public, it can be accessed by members of the public who have controlled access to the site, such as those with boats moored at the location. Access to the site is through a coded security gate which is managed and controlled by the Port Authority.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether any formal coordination policy or protocol exists between departments within the Western Health and Social Care Trust for the transport of tests and samples from Enniskillen, including the former Erne Hospital and the South West Acute Hospital, to Altnagelvin Hospital; and (ii) any assessment made of the financial impact of multiple uncoordinated journeys being commissioned on the same routes each day.
answer_text: (i)  The Western Trust currently have two scheduled in house transport runs Monday to Friday and one scheduled in house transport run on Saturday, Sunday and Bank Holidays from SWAH to Altnagelvin.(ii) The Western Trust has processes in place for ongoing monitoring of Laboratory expenses for the use of taxis for urgent samples.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he has received legal advice on the compatibility of the proposed UK ban on certain tobacco and vaping products with the EU Tobacco Products Directive as it applies to Northern Ireland under the Windsor Framework; and (ii) for his assessment of the risk that such measures could expose Northern Ireland to infringement proceedings or require regulatory divergence from Great Britain.
answer_text: The UK-wide Tobacco and Vapes Bill is a Westminster Bill and it is therefore for the UK Government to ensure its compliance with the Windsor Framework. The UK Government has advised that its position remains that the Bill will apply in Northern Ireland and that its provisions are consistent with its domestic and international obligations under the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether there was a risk of injury, or worse, given that a person, or persons, have fixed a rope from the sinking Loughs Agency boat across the pontoon in an effort to stop it from being completely submerged.
answer_text: As this is an operational matter for the Loughs Agency, I have asked it to consider the risk associated with this incident. The Agency has advised that the additional ropes and anchorage point installed were assessed on the evening of the incident by Loughs Agency staff and barrier tape was added to increase awareness for anyone in the vicinity. Agency staff also liaised with Port Authority personnel who, as owners of the marina, have overall responsibility for assessing and mitigating the levels of risk at the site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32204/22-27, to detail the (i) current waiting times for; and (ii) the number of patients currently awaiting (a) a first outpatient dermatology assessment; and (b) a follow-up dermatology appointment, in each Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by waiting time bands of under 26 weeks, 26–52 weeks, 52–104 weeks, 104–208 weeks and over 208 weeks.
answer_text: (a) The numbers of patients waiting and the waiting times for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment under the Dermatology or Paediatric Dermatology specialties at quarter ending September 2025, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, are set out in Table 1.Table 1. Numbers of patients and waiting times for a first consultant-led outpatient Dermatology or Paediatric Dermatology appointmentat quarter end September 2025, by HSC Trust and time band.HSC TrustNumbers of patients waiting 1, 20 to 26 (weeks)>26 to 52 (weeks)>52 to 104 (weeks)>104 to 208 (weeks)>208 (weeks)Total waitingBelfast 33,1241,5022,5513,9091,89712,983Northern3,5801,2772,3512,3122,33411,854South Eastern3,0381,3652,3971,9291,81410,543Southern2,8001,6052,7121,8852,25611,258Western1,024298574433812,410Source: Department of Health Hospital Waits Information Branch, Outpatient Waiting Times Dataset.Notes:1 Data sourced from the encompass system. The encompass specialty classification differs from, and is not directly comparable to, legacy (pre-encompass) data sources.2 The figures above refer to the number of patients waiting for a first consultantled outpatient appointment under the Dermatology specialty, as at the reporting date. They do not indicate completed waiting times or expected future waiting times.3 Figures for all patients waiting under the encompass Dermatology and Paediatric Dermatology specialties have been combined in the above table. Belfast HSC Trust is the only Trust which has Paediatric Dermatology appointments in addition to Dermatology appointments.(b) To detail the (i) current waiting times for; and (ii) the number of patients currently awaiting a follow-up dermatology appointment, in each Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by waiting time bands of under 26 weeks, 26–52 weeks, 52–104 weeks, 104–208 weeks and over 208 weeks.The appropriate review time period for a patient under dermatology care can vary considerably and should be tailored to the individual. It depends not only on the stability and severity of the dermatological condition itself but also on the presence of any comorbidities or wider medical issues that may influence disease progression or treatment response. Patients with wellcontrolled, chronic conditions may require only routine, scheduled followup over a long period of time, whereas those with unstable, complex, or rapidly changing conditions may necessitate more frequent monitoring. Additionally, factors such as treatment type, risk of complications, and the need for ongoing investigations or medication adjustments also play an important role in determining the optimal review timeframe. Taking this into account Trust were asked to provide their current follow up waiting times by waiting time band. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps his Department is taking to reduce the incidence of bovine TB in Northern Ireland, including (i) the role of wildlife intervention; (ii) testing regimes; and (iii) support for affected farmers.
answer_text: I am acutely aware that the increasing level of bTB in Northern Ireland is challenging and unacceptable. On coming into post in early 2024, I asked the Chief Veterinary Officer to undertake a review of bTB. His report was published in November 2024 and I immediately accepted his recommendation to establish a new stakeholder body, the TB Parnership Steering Group (TBPSG).  The TBPSG’s Bovine Tuberculosis in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication sets out clear targets for the eradication of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in Northern Ireland (NI). Further information can be found here: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/tb-partnership-steering-group-bovine-tb-northern-ireland-blueprint-eradicationThe aim of the TBPSG, working with DAERA, is to reduce bTB herd incidence by 2% by 2030, placing NI on a pathway to halve current bTB levels by 2040 and achieve eradication by 2050. The Blueprint is based on three pillars: people; cattle; and wildlife. By making a collective effort in those three areas, we can move forward and reduce TB in Northern Ireland. New departmental structures are now in place to oversee the implementation of the Blueprint with initial work focused on increasing the use of interferon gamma testing; the provision of on farm biosecurity advice; improved TB testing; preparing a new consultation on potential wildlife intervention options; and an innovative new regionalisation initiative in partnership with the Republic.We must proceed collectively, both government and stakeholders, in a way that is evidence based and enables us to deliver for the farming community around those three pillars. That means delivering on wildlife, but also on people and cattle. I have made this a key priority for the Department as well as the DAERA support for farmers by way of compensation for animals removed and increased supplementary testing to allow the removal of diseased animals earlier to reduce disease spread.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of whether UK badger control legislation is more restrictive than required under the Bern Convention; and (ii) whether he will challenge the UK Government to align its policy with what international law explicitly allows.
answer_text: The Bern Convention sets out a framework for signatory countries, which is considered in formulating relevant national legislation. There is no specific badger control legislation in the UK; the Protection of Badgers Act 1992 applies to Great Britain and primarily aims to protect badgers from persecution and harm, with certain exceptions. In Northern Ireland, badgers are protected under the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985 as amended, again with certain exceptions. Both instruments fulfil the requirements of the Bern Convention.My officials continue to engage with DEFRA on wildlife related matters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of whether any decision by the Commonwealth Games Northern Ireland to move away from the use of the Ulster Banner would be compatible with their articles of association.
answer_text: Given that Commonwealth Games Northern Ireland is independent of Government, I cannot comment on matters relating to its Articles of Association.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total cost of journeys funded by (i) his Department; and (ii) each Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last three financial years, broken down by (a) patient transport; and (b) the transport of tests, samples, or other clinical materials.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to review home-to-school transport eligibility criteria to allow account to be taken of subject availability at post-primary level.
answer_text: Eligibility for transport assistance is based solely on two criteria: distance and suitable school. Postprimary pupils qualify if there is no suitable school within three miles of their home, or if they have applied for and been refused a place at all such schools. A suitable school is defined as a grant aided school within one of six categories: controlled, integrated, Irish-medium, maintained, denominational grammar and non-denominational grammar.  The policy clearly states that the categories of schools do not extend to take account of other factors such as the range or nature of curriculum provided. It is for each school to determine its subject offerings at A-Level, subjects offered can change each year and pupils may alter their choices during the year. Given the level of flexibility, extending transport eligibility to reflect subject provision would be extremely difficult for the EA to administer, it would increase the number of pupils entitled to assistance and have significant financial implications.Northern Ireland’s transport policy is already more generous than elsewhere in the UK, supporting parental choice across different categories of school rather than limiting free transport to the nearest school. The cost of delivering school transport is high and has risen from £90 million in 2019/20 to almost £140 million in 2024/25. The transport policy is underpinned by legislation, and any change to the eligibility criteria would likely require legislative amendment and most careful assessment of the impact on families and affordability. Given the very significant budgetary pressures currently facing my department, it is likely that any future review of the policy would need to focus on reducing demand for transport services rather than expanding eligibility criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is considering any reduction, capping or reform of bovine TB compensation payments to affected farmers.
answer_text: I have no immediate plans to change the current compensation provision for cattle slaughtered for the control of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB).  The Blueprint for Eradication has, however, committed the Department and the TB Partnership Steering Group to develop proposals to assess options to address the rising costs of the programme, including consideration of potential changes to compensation arrangements and I expect that this work will commence in the coming months. 			My Department continues to monitor closely the overall costs of the Northern Ireland bTB programme and its impact on the public purse, keeping expenditure under regular review to ensure it remains proportionate, sustainable, and represents value for money for taxpayers.  		Should any changes be proposed to existing compensation legislation, a public consultation would be required to inform any decision.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any representations his Department has made to the UK Government in relation to badger control policy in Northern Ireland, including the extent to which current Westminster policy restricts measures permitted under the Bern Convention.
answer_text: My officials have regular discussions with Defra counterparts on general wildlife policy including badger intervention.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions his Department has taken in relation to Casement Park in the last 12 months.
answer_text: As Minister for Sport, I welcome investment in sporting infrastructure in Northern Ireland.  However, it is vital that any public funding is allocated on a fair and equitable basis, across all sports.Over the last 12 months the Department has continued to work with the GAA and other partners to explore viable options for delivery, address funding, and ensure compliance with value-for-money and governance principles.Budget for the remainder of 2025/26 financial year is circa £150,000. The budget for 2026/27 has not yet been finalised.Any final budget allocation for the project will be for the Executive to determine following the conclusion of the Finance Minister’s Budget consultation. Ulster Council GAA are restarting their site clearance at Casement Park in January 2026 under their own contract and expense and all works must comply with statutory obligations. Procurement is underway in relation to specialist time-bound predevelopment works at the Casement Park site. This is standalone and does not form part of the stadium construction contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is currently (i) preparing; and (ii) seeking, tenders or contracts in relation to the (a) development; and (b) pre-development of Casement Park.

These movement restrictions may be imposed following a confirmed case of bovine TB, suspicion of bovine TB disease, or for compliancerelated reasons (e.g. overdue herd tests).(ii) See table below for comparable figures:Year endHerds under bovine TB movement restrictions (NI)20253,47120243,868 20233,608 20223,907 20213,501

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the report in the News Letter on 14 January 2026 in relation to the assessment of gender identity services provision in Northern Ireland carried out by Baroness Hilary Cass, to detail (i) what is meant by describing the visit by Baroness Cass and her team as the beginning of a collaborative relationship; and (ii) for his assessment as to whether such collaboration risks compromising the independence of clinical review.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of cattle that were deemed to have failed statutory bovine TB tests in Northern Ireland during 2025; and (ii) the number of herds this involved.
answer_text: The total number of cattle that failed statutory bovine TB tests in Northern Ireland during 2025 was 20,545. This includes 19,166 animals that tested positive to the Comparative Intradermal Tuberculin Test (CITT) i.e. the skin test, and 1,379 additional animals that were not skin test positive but tested positive on the IFN-g (blood) test. These figures remain provisional, as some tests conducted in the latter part of 2025 may require re-interpretation based on TB post-mortem or laboratory results. The number of herds involved in 2025 is 3,081.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to (i) laying the final Cass report before the Assembly upon publication; and (ii) detailing the cost of the report.
answer_text: My Department published the Cass report on 10 February 2026: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/cass-assessment-report-gender-identity-services.As outlined to the member in my response to AQW 38254/22-27 and AQW 38255/322-27, I will now give careful consideration to its content, including its findings, recommendations, and any implications for the service, before determining the way forward.The total cost of the assessment and the subsequent report, including travel, accommodation, and meals was £2,455.05.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to publish the annual number of bovine TB reactors and herd breakdowns in Northern Ireland for each of the last five years; and (ii) to detail any assessment his Department has made of the trend over that period.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) disease statistics are published by my department each month.  These monthly statistics include figures relating to the number of cattle that have reacted positively to the tuberculin skin test for bTB and the number of herd breakdowns that have been recorded in the preceding 12 months.  These statistics date back to 2007 and can be accessed at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/tuberculosis-statistics-northern-ireland-2007-feb-25. 		For ease of reference, the table below sets out the figures for each of the last five calendar years, with the exception of 2025 which relate to the ten months to the end of October 2025 (the most recently confirmed and published figures).  			2021	2022	2023	2024	Oct 2025	Number of bTB reactors	14,359	17,370	18,150	20,510	16,004 	Unique herd breakdowns	2,599	2,977	3,107	3,270	2,720 	Clearly this increase in the level of bTB in Northern Ireland since 2021 is challenging and unacceptable.  On coming into post in early 2024, I asked the Chief Veterinary Officer to undertake a review of bTB.  His report was published in November 2024 and I immediately accepted his recommendation to establish a new stakeholder body- the TB Parnership Steering Group (TBPSG)- which met for the first time in January 2025.  In April 2025, I was pleased to endorse the TBPSG’s Bovine Tuberculosis in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication.  The Blueprint sets out actions needed across the three key priorities of People, Cattle and Wildlife to set Northern Ireland on a pathway to reduce and, ultimately, eradicate bTB. New departmental structures are also now in place with the bTB Eradication Transformation Programme set up to oversee the implementation of the Blueprint.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the recommendations from Baroness Cass relating to Northern Ireland are advisory or binding in nature; and (ii) whether he retains discretion to reject or modify any recommendation following the final report.
answer_text: Baroness Hilary Cass submitted a draft report of her assessment of the gender identity service for children and young people in Northern Ireland to my officials in December 2025 and the final report is expected by the end of January 2026.   I will carefully consider the content of the final report once received, including its findings and recommendations, and any implications for the service, before any decisions are taken on next steps; this will include a decision on the publication of the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the draft report submitted by Baroness Hilary Cass has been shared in full with his Department; and (ii) , in light of media coverage of its content, whether he will publish the draft report, in whole or in part, prior to the final report being issued in the interests of transparency.
answer_text: Baroness Hilary Cass submitted a draft report of her assessment of the gender identity service for children and young people in Northern Ireland to my officials in December 2025 and the final report is expected by the end of January 2026.   I will carefully consider the content of the final report once received, including its findings and recommendations, and any implications for the service, before any decisions are taken on next steps; this will include a decision on the publication of the report.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail all groups which include transwomen that received funding under the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls, broken down by the amount awarded in each case.
answer_text: There is no data on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any clinical practices currently used in Northern Ireland have been identified by Baroness Cass as requiring cessation or restriction.
answer_text: To date my officials have only received a draft report from Baroness Cass.  The final report is expected by the first week of February 2026.  Once the report is received and fully considered, my Department will be in a position to advise whether any clinical practices currently used in Northern Ireland have been identified by Baroness Cass as requiring cessation or restriction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the report in the News Letter on 14 January 2026 in relation to the assessment of gender identity services provision in Northern Ireland carried out by Baroness Hilary Cass, to detail what is meant in practice by aligning Northern Ireland services with those in England, and whether this includes alignment with England’s move away from routine medicalisation of children and adolescents.
answer_text: To date my officials have only received a draft report from Baroness Cass.  The final report is expected by the first week of February 2026.  Once the report is received and fully considered, my Department will be in a position to detail what is meant in practice by aligning Northern Ireland services with those in England if that is indeed the appropriate action to take.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the draft Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan for residential buildings primarily promotes electrification and a switch to natural gas, to detail any assessment her Department has undertaken of the potential for introducing a renewable liquid heating fuel obligation, similar to the Renewable Transport Fuel Obligation, allowing a 20 per cent blend of Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil in domestic heating oil, in order to achieve immediate decarbonisation without upfront costs for households.
answer_text: My Department is progressing work to ensure a holistic approach is taken to heat policy, including through publishing a Call for Evidence regarding the potential role liquid biofuels, such as Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil, could play in moving households and businesses away from fossil fuels for heating. Analysis and review of the responses to the Call for Evidence is in the final stages, with a report presenting the findings due to be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the number of call outs to plumbers to Parliament Buildings in each of the five financial years prior to the restoration of the Executive in February 2024; and (ii) the total expenditure on these plumbing services.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission recognises the importance of maintaining the fabric and essential services of Parliament Buildings, including responding appropriately to plumbing issues when they arise. The way in which the Assembly Commission records information on maintenance activities has changed over time.For the years 2020 to 2022, call-outs were not recorded by individual trade. Instead, repairs and maintenance activity were logged in a more general manner. As a result, information specifically relating to plumbing call-outs for these financial years is not available.The number of call-outs to plumbers to Parliament Buildings in each of the financial years from 2019-20 to the period prior to the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, for which information is available, is as follows:2019-20 – 45 call outs2020-21 – information not held2021-22 – information not held2022-23 – 31 call outs 2023-24 – 27 call outs The total expenditure incurred on plumbing services in the years in this period for which specific records are held was £39,391.The Assembly Commission continues to take a proportionate approach to maintenance activity, seeking to ensure that essential services are maintained while making effective use of available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any consideration has been given to introducing a mandatory seven-day custodial sentence for anyone who breaches a non-molestation order.
answer_text: My Department was responsible for the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (NI) Order 1998, which sets out the statutory criteria relating to non-molestation orders. Article 25 of the 1998 Order provides that the contravention of a non-molestation order is a criminal offence. The facility to impose a custodial sentence is, therefore, already available to the courts. Sentencing procedures relating to breaches of non-molestation orders are a matter for the independent judiciary. That sentence was originally set at a maximum of 3 months but following a review by my Department in the years following the Order, was increased to 6 months.As advised by the Justice Minister in response to AQW 24847/22-27, judges will take into consideration all of the details of the case presented to them in making sentencing decisions, and in doing so, take account of both aggravating and mitigating factors, with the former including factors indicating higher culpability, or where there is a more than usual degree of harm. To mandate a 7 day prison sentence for all breaches of non-molestation orders would require primary legislation and would involve a careful analysis of the various issues at play in such cases to ensure compliance with the European Convention of Human Rights. At this time, no such legislative proposal has been developed by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of pregnancies in Northern Ireland in which a diagnosis of Down syndrome was made antenatally; (ii) the number of those pregnancies which proceeded to live birth; and (iii) the number which ended in termination of pregnancy, in each year since 2019.
answer_text: Northern Ireland does not have a commissioned antenatal screening programme for Down syndrome equivalent to England.  Consequently, there is variation across Trusts in terms of what tests are offered and to whom, while some people will also pay to have private antenatal tests for congenital anomalies.  As Trust data does not distinguish between antenatal and postnatal diagnoses of Down syndrome, it is not possible to detail the number of pregnancies where this has been diagnosed antenatally, nor on the outcomes of such pregnancies.Regarding termination of pregnancy, Trust maternity systems collect data on terminations for congenital anomalies; however, this dataset does not disaggregate figures to identify cases specifically associated with Down syndrome.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will assess the potential implementation of a renewable liquid heating fuel obligation, including (i) her Department’s estimates for the resulting carbon savings in tonnes per year; and (ii) potential contribution to the 2030 climate targets outlined in the draft Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department published a Call for Evidence regarding the potential role of liquid biofuels may play in the energy transition to clean and renewable heating, which included discussion and questions relating to renewable liquid fuel blends and the potential carbon savings from using Biofuels. The Government Response to this is in the final stages of analysis and evaluation and will be published in the coming weeks.
