question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any audit that has been carried out on the adequacy of the penalties imposed for breaches of non-molestation orders.
answer_text: My Department has responsibility for the substantive law in this area. The Family Homes and Domestic Violence (NI) Order 1998 sets out the statutory criteria relating to non-molestation orders. Article 25 of the 1998 Order provides that a breach of a non-molestation order is a criminal offence which may attract a maximum penalty of six months’ imprisonment and/or a level-5 fine of £5,000. There is statistical information available on the volume and outcomes of breaches. Sentencing in individual cases is a matter for the independent judiciary, with the circumstances of each case taken into account in application of the sentencing guidelines, which require consideration of aggravating factors where appropriate. I note that the Justice Minister has already provided information relating to prosecutions and convictions relating to breaches of such Orders in response to her answer to question 26686/22-27. In addition, my Department has noted further statistical data relating to the main disposals applied by the courts over the period 2020 – 2024 following breaches which I outline below:Main disposal20202021202220232024Imprisonment3757506644Suspended Custodial5084745551Community sentence3145284024Monetary Penalty4657524139Discharge/Other1740483127Total181283252233185Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to main disposal imposed following conviction for at least one count of Breach of a Non-molestation Order offence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Loughs Agency vessel that recently sank, to detail (i) the works that were carried out since the Loughs Agency took delivery of the vessel, including maintenance, modifications, fitting of equipment to the hull, fitting equipment elsewhere; (ii) what each of the works consisted of; (iii) whether any of the work impacted the stability or watertight integrity of the vessel; (iv) whether the works were completed by a competent and qualified person or persons; and (v) who oversaw the works on behalf of the Loughs
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that: -The vessel was received by the Agency in 2019, following which arrangements were made to have the vessel coded for Category C waters. A qualified marine surveyor inspected the vessel and provided a list of required works. These were completed by a competent marine electronics contractor engaged by the Agency. Following completion of works, the vessel underwent an out-of-water inspection in October 2019 and a subsequent inspection on 04 October 2021.In addition, the vessel has been lifted annually at Lisahally Port for routine maintenance.The works consisted of modifications and equipment installations as specified by the marine surveyor including:approved radar reflector;30W aft deck flood lights (2 units);single channel petrol vapour alarm;bilge float switches (2 units);12V Bilge alarms (2 units);automatic bilge pump;DSC VHF class D with position;12V horn and dash mount button;Victron smart charger (shore supply);external socket and plug for hauler;shore supply inlet socket and plug;shore supply breaker/box/socket with USB13;custom stainless bracket for radar;NMEA 200 accessories for radar network; andinstallation of owner-supplied Radar, GPS antenna, Heading sensor.All tasks were performed by a qualified marine electronics contractor. During annual routine maintenance lifts, the vessel hull was painted and inspected, engines were serviced by a qualified marine engineering company, and all onboard safety equipment was updated.The vessel was inspected by a qualified marine surveyor after the works were completed, and no issues were reported regarding stability or watertight integrity.All works were carried out by a competent and qualified marine contractor engaged by the Agency.Loughs Agency personnel were present during the works but did not sign off on them, as they were not qualified to do so. The contractor responsible for the works was a competent and qualified marine electronics contractor.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of directors and non-executive directors in each Health and Social Care Trust, including the total combined salaries.
answer_text: The number of executive and non-executive directors in each Health and Social Care Trust and their combined total salaries are shown in the table below (2024/25). Non-Executive Directors do not receive a salary but are paid an allowance in line with the applicable rates set out at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/hscf-23-2024.pdfHSC TrustNumber of Executive DirectorsNumber of Non-Executive DirectorsTotal Combined SalariesBelfast HSC Trust168*£2,345,140Southern HSC Trust9***8*£962,794South Eastern HSC Trust58*£905,598Northern HSC Trust7**8*£1,108,070Western HSC Trust118*£1,480,010Northern Ireland Ambulance Service65£666,749Source: HSC Trusts (salary figures exclude Employers Costs)*Trust have advised that this Non-Executive Director headcount figure includes the Chair. **Northern Trust have advised this Executive Director headcount figure includes the Medical Director, who is not paid on a Senior Executive Pay Scale (Medical & Dental Terms and Conditions) and includes two other non-voting Operational Directors on Trust Board who are paid on a Senior Executive Pay Scale. ***Southern Trust have advised this Executive Director headcount figure represents the total in post throughout the year and that there were some leavers and joiners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the location of grit boxes will be added to the NI Direct portal in a manner similar to street lights.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,850 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents and proprietors can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria based system, which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly. Salt boxes can also be removed by the department if requested to do so, or if a box isn’t being used.Due to salt boxes not being “fixed” items of street furniture and because they are installed and removed on request, my Department has no current plans to include these on the portal at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of vehicles owned by her Department that are currently unused and in storage; (ii) the cost associated with their (a) storage; (b) maintenance; and (c) depreciation, in each of the last 5 years; (iii) the intended operational purpose of these vehicles at the time of purchase and whether that purpose remains valid; (iv) the training and certification required for staff to operate these vehicles; (v) the number of her departmental staff that currently hold valid certification to operate these vehicles; and (vi) whether the Departmen
answer_text: Within the Road Asset Management Directorate, the Fleet Management Unit manages vehicles for DfI Roads, and DfI Rivers, and DVA. Within DfI Roads and Rivers, there are 22 vehicles currently unused and in storage: 6 newly delivered operational vehicles and 16 fourwheeldrive vehicles, that are being redeployed within the Department and now awaiting updated logbooks (V5) from DVA. There are no storage or maintenance costs for these vehicles. Depreciation on the 6 newly delivered vehicles has only recently begun and is not yet quantified; depreciation data for the 16 redeployed vehicles is not yet available. Where appropriate, staff undertake licencebased training, and specialist training relating to Driver Certificate of Professional Competence (CPC). All staff are trained for the vehicles and equipment they use, with training needs reviewed annually in line with the Department’s policy on the delivery of road maintenance. Workforce and training considerations were completed prior to vehicle purchase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the arrangements that exist between her Department and the 11 local council areas for the provision of footway snow clearance and self help grit piles for community use.
answer_text: The Department provides gritting salt under existing agreements with a number of councils, for the removal of snow and ice from town centre footways and pedestrian areas. These agreements are activated during prolonged periods of severe weather.Priority is given to the provision of salt for the treatment of the gritted road network, after which the Department makes salt available to local Councils for the treatment of town centre footways and pedestrian areas. This agreement is for salt supplies in addition to stocks held by Councils for the treatment of council facilities.Salt bins and grit piles are also provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. These will be found at locations which meet certain criteria such as gradient, topography, proximity to salted network, etc.These grit piles and salt boxes are filled in advance of Winter and are regularly refilled by my Winter Service staff. Members of the public can also request refills via NI Direct.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of specialist lorries that have been purchased by her Department in the last five years; (ii) the date of purchase of each vehicle; (iii) the individual and total cost involved; and (iv) how many of these vehicles (a) have entered operational service; and (b) have not been used since purchase.
answer_text: I can advise that in the last five years the Department has purchased 152 specialist lorries at a total cost of £19.4m.  The release of the individual cost of each vehicle would be considered commercially sensitive.150 vehicles have entered operational service, with the remaining 2 currently being prepared for issue.  These two specialist vehicles have remained unused since their purchase.A breakdown of the vehicle type and the year of their purchase has been included in the table below:Vehicle Class21-2222-2323-2424-2525-26Total13 Tonne tipper/ compressor404018 Tonne cone / sign lorry6618 Tonne interchangeable body/chassis 4x4101017 Tonne interchangeable body/chassis1628176126 Tonne interchangeable body /chassis72917 Tonne gully emptier / jetter10101026 Tonne insulated tipper4416 Tonne sweeper / gully emptier22Totals: 07234442152

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the lines of communication that exist for the provision of updates and guidance to local authorities and elected members during periods of inclement weather.
answer_text: The Department provides contacts for emergency purposes, through telephone numbers which are monitored 24 hours a day, as well as the NI Direct service, where complaints are logged. The high priority reports on the NI Direct service are also monitored 24 hours a day. The Department's Trafficwatch website is operational 24 hours a day, with regular updates from the Traffic Information and Control Centre where appropriate.During periods of inclement weather, the Department's primary source of communication is the Trafficwatch website. Additional information and guidance is made available through press releases and social media platforms.Depending on the forecast weather conditions, the Department may also participate in multi-agency co-ordination groups with partners including local councils, emergency services, health trusts, key utilities and certain government Departments. Information and guidance from this group is also made available through press releases and social media platforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will commission a review of the criteria around the provision of grit boxes in adopted areas not covered by the salting network.
answer_text: Salt bins are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents and proprietors can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria based system, which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.The current criteria for the provision of salt bins is considered appropriate, and therefore there are no plans to review it at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she review the functionality of the NI Direct portal and implement interactive and user-friendly update alerts as cases are assessed.
answer_text: Members of the public reporting a road or lighting fault receive an automated acknowledgement email if a valid address is provided and are provided with an enquiry reference number and link to track the progress or outcome of any action initiated online.My officials are aware of concerns raised regarding the online track-a-fault self-service facility, such as further automated follow-up email alerts when an enquiry is updated.  While there are currently no plans to change the tracking system at this time, my officials will keep any potential updates to the system under review.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of Stonewall’s reported financial crisis, whether they will end any engagement between their Department, its arm’s-length bodies and Stonewall.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the sub contractors held on a retainer by her Department for the purposes of keeping the network clear in North Antrim during snowfall; and (ii) the amount each sub contractor is paid per hour.
answer_text: My Department does not operate on a constituency basis, rather our section boundaries mirror those of local councils.  North Antrim is covered by the Causeway Coast & Glens (East) and Mid & East Antrim offices and my Department has 20 external snow clearance contractors available within these areas for the 2025/26 winter period.The table below details the hourly rates attributable to these works which are applied consistently across the North.PlantRate applied to normal working hours (£)Rate applied outside normal working hours (£)Tractors/ Telescopic material handlers / Hydraulic half circle slew excavators with 1.0 cubic metre bucket or over33.4539.89Loading Shovel with 2.0 cubic metre bucket or over45.0356.61Tracked excavator, 360 degree, 13-tonne to 21-tonne with bucket capacity not less than 1.0 cubic metre45.0356.61

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why her Department's forward planning did not provide for the blanket refilling of grit boxes prior to the onset of winter.
answer_text: Salt bins are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met andmy Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,850 salt bins and 52,300 grit piles.All grit boxes and grit piles were replenished prior to the start of the winter season, however during conditions such as the recent spell of very cold weather we experienced, salt bins and grit piles can be consumed within a few hours.Given the limited resources available to the Department, it can then take some time to again replenish all boxes and piles, at a time when priority must be given to treating the main road network. However, salt bins and grit piles are always replenished as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why roads to rural schools are not included on the gritting network or for snow clearance.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by my Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes and can be viewed on the Department’s website below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. Not only does this directly benefit the majority of drivers, but once gritting is completed, it requires traffic to work the grit into the snow and ice in order to activate it and make it most effective.It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed. Route treatment is primarily based on traffic volume not road classification, with approximately only 9% of the network classed as “A” road. Once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is assured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. This Secondary Salting Network currently gives priority to 80 rural schools across the North. The criteria for the provision of grit/salt piles and salt bins to these rural schools is also provided in the policy. While I would like to be able to expand our gritting service to many other routes, it is simply not feasible to do so at present due to the severe budget constraints faced by my Department.In addition to this, all schools requesting a salt box will be provided with one to help mitigate the effects of snow and ice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why sufficient salt and grit was not available on Monday 5 January 2026 to support the Memorandum of Understanding with Mid and East Antrim Borough Council.
answer_text: The Department provides gritting salt under existing agreements with a number of councils, for the removal of snow and ice from town centre footways and pedestrian areas. These agreements are activated during prolonged periods of severe weather.Priority is given to the provision of salt for the treatment of the gritted road network, after which the Department makes salt available to local Councils for the treatment of town centre footways and pedestrian areas. There are currently no issues impacting this supply of salt.This agreement is for salt supplies in addition to stocks held by Councils for the treatment of council facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the reported financial crisis at Stonewall, including a significant operating deficit and depleted cash reserves, whether he will review the Northern Ireland Civil Service’s continued membership of Stonewall’s Diversity Champions Programme and participation in Stonewall’s Workplace Equality Index.
answer_text: The Civil Service is an equal opportunities employer and is committed to building an inclusive Civil Service that reflects the society it serves.It engages with a wide range of internal and external organisations from a wide range of sectors.Stonewall is a charity recognised for its work with, and training for, public and private sector employers in helping to create LGBTQ+ inclusive and welcoming working environments.The Civil Service’s membership of the Stonewall Proud Employers Programme (formerly the “Diversity Champions Programme”) and participation in its accreditation process will be considered when membership is next due for renewal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans, including associated timeframes, he has to require the systematic recording and publication of information relating to terminations of pregnancy in Northern Ireland, including (i) the statutory ground relied upon in each case; (ii) an equivalent classification to the grounds set out in section 1(1)(a) to (g) of the Abortion Act 1967, in order to bring Northern Ireland abortion statistics in line with those published for England and Wales; (iii) key demographic and clinical data associated with each termination; and (iv) whether a baby is born
answer_text: I refer to my previous responses to AQW 21771/22-27, AQW 21772/22-27 and AQW 21773/22-27.  The recording and publication of information relating to terminations of pregnancy in Northern Ireland is in line with the classifications set out in the relevant legislation, the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No. 2) Regulations 2020.  There are no plans to change this at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will suspend any further expenditure of public funds on Stonewall-related programmes, pending a formal review of the organisation’s financial position, governance and advice to public bodies.
answer_text: The Civil Service is an equal opportunities employer and is committed to building an inclusive Civil Service that reflects the society it serves.Stonewall is a charity recognised for its work with, and training for, public and private sector employers in helping to create LGBTQ+ inclusive and welcoming working environments.My Department has not forecast any further expenditure on Stonewall-related programmes for the remainder of this financial year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the reported financial crisis at Stonewall, including a significant operating deficit and depleted cash reserves, whether they will review their Department's continued relationship with Stonewall.
answer_text: The Executive Office has no ongoing financial relationship with Stonewall.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how long his Department anticipates that cod-related restrictions on the local fishing fleet will remain in place.
answer_text: I anticipate that there will be a long-term requirement to continue to implement some form of cod-related fishing restriction. These measures are aimed at rebuilding, protecting, or maintaining the stock. Such measures, to protect cod and other species, are integral to the sustainable management of our fisheries to provide future fishing opportunities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact of long-term fishing restrictions on the size and viability of the Northern Ireland fishing fleet.
answer_text: My officials maintain close and regular engagement with the fishing industry to monitor operational pressures, gather evidence and understand the ongoing viability of the Northern Ireland fleet. In advance of annual fisheries negotiations, officials also work directly with industry representatives to develop economic assessments of the potential impact of changes to quota allocations, including scenarios involving reduced quotas. This collaborative approach ensures that policy decisions and negotiation positions are informed by robust data and industry insight.	Fishing measures are based on the best available scientific advice and are designed to support the sustainable use of marine resources, protect vulnerable species, and secure continued access to key commercial stocks for the future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24665/22-27 and the revised answer, to detail (i) why the answer was revised to include additional material, including the statement that the Loughs Agency has approval to employ temporary staff as required; (ii) whether the Loughs Agency can employ temporary staff to any of its posts; (iii) all posts within the Loughs Agency that can be filled by temporary staff; (iv) the legislative basis for the provision to employ temporary staff; (v) whether there is an official document that provides the basis for employin
answer_text: The answer provided to AQW 24665/22-27 was updated to reflect my officials’ understanding of the Agency’s complement figure at that time. This was that the Agency had approval to employ temporary staff in certain seasonal roles in addition to its complement figure of 53. Since then, further work has been undertaken by officials to seek to clarify this position. This work is ongoing. (ii)(iii)	Work to determine which posts the Agency can fill on a temporary basis above the complement figure of 53 is ongoing. Additionally, it is accepted that the Agency can use temporary staff to fill other positions within its complement figure of 53 to meet operational business needs. (iv)(v) 	The Loughs Agency complement was approved in 2006 under Interim Procedures that provided for Ministers in the UK and Irish Governments to provide approval in the absence of the North South Ministerial Council. (vi) 	No limit is currently set for the overall employment of temporary staff.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures his Department is taking to support the recovery and long-term sustainability of the local fishing industry in the Irish Sea.
answer_text: My department undertakes a broad range of measures from supporting scientific stocks assessment to the managing, monitoring and control of fishing activity. My department is leading in the development and publication of four Fishery Management Plans that cover all of the key commercial fisheries within the Irish Sea. These plans will set out the policies and actions to recover and manage fish stocks to support sustainable fisheries and contribute to economically viable fleets.In addition to the management actions my department also currently provides financial assistance through the Marine Environment and Fisheries Fund.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the cost, including VAT, of the investigation report relating to the Loughs Agency and Woodland Trust TREES programme; and (ii) the date on which (a) the Loughs Agency became aware of issues that led them to undertake an investigation; (b) the consultant working on this investigation was appointed; and (iii) the report was finalised.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that: -the Investigation Report cost £8,590;(a) the Agency became aware of issues in January 2024;(b) the investigation commenced in August 2024; andthe report was finalised in January 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail on what scientific evidence current cod-related fishing restrictions in the Irish Sea continue to be based.
answer_text: The International Council for the Exploration of the Seas (ICES) provides scientific assessment and advice for the stock of cod in the Irish Sea. Their assessment is based on several evidence sources, including catch data, annual scientific trawl surveys, the fishing industry partnership survey, sampling of commercial catches and tagging studies. The key evidence sources are referenced in the ICES annual science advice for Irish Sea Cod.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the current status of the rates bill relating to the office of the former MP for North Antrim at 9-11 Church Street, Ballymena.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of two Loughs Agency vessels sinking while unattended on a berth, to detail (i) any redundancies that were fitted to the vessel to alert crew to alarm activation while unmanned; (ii) whether any redundancies alerted Loughs Agency management prior to (a) the recent sinking; (b) the previous instance of another Loughs Agency vessel sinking.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that:No redundancies were fitted to the vessels to alert crew to alarm activation.No redundancies alerted Loughs Agency management prior to(a) the recent sinking; or(b) the previous instance of another Loughs Agency vessel sinking.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment his Department has made of the effectiveness of the Cod Recovery Programme in the Irish Sea since its introduction.
answer_text: The Cod Recovery Programme was a series of EU initiatives, effectively replaced in 2019 following revision of the EU Common Fishery Policy and introduction of specific technical measures for the Irish Sea. This programme was assessed at EU level.		My department continues to operate cod conservation measures that support avoidance and minimisation of cod catches. Annual stock assessments confirm the effectiveness of these measures, showing that fishing-induced mortality remains at a low level.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what the most recent scientific data held by his Department indicates in relation to trends in species composition of trawl catches in the Irish Sea.
answer_text: The Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute undertake a programme of catch monitoring, covering both landings and discards. There is significant annual and seasonal variation in the composition of catches due to factors including, changes in fishing behaviour due to weather and fuel costs, uptake of fishing opportunities, seasonal behavioural changes of marine organisms and stock development. Analysis of catch composition, while informing annual assessments, is not routinely reported to DAERA but reviewed on request or if there is a significant concern identified by AFBI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any explanation his Department's scientists have provided for reports from the fishing industry that the lesser-spotted catshark, a non-commercial species, now constitutes a significant proportion of trawl catches in the Irish Sea.
answer_text: The Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute undertake a programme of catch monitoring, covering both landings and discards. They have not identified any significant concern over the prevalence of this species. My department will continue to monitor this issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25687/22-27 in relation to category 2 ambulance calls not being responded to in the last hour of a shift due to action short of strike, to outline the progress made to ensure that crews, at the very least, are being made aware that a call has been logged.
answer_text: When we published the Department’s Overarching Winter Plan alongside the Trusts’ local plans in October, I stressed that the system and the public need to work together and all play our part. This will help to make sure that HSC services are ready and available for those who need them most. Improving effectiveness includes measures such as getting vaccinated, taking steps to stay well and co-operating with the hospital discharge processes.The 2025/26 Winter Plan builds on last year’s foundations, focusing on expanding urgent care alternatives such as Phone First, minor injury units, and urgent care centres. These initiatives are embedded within each Trust’s Plans. However, while these plans can help mitigate winter pressures, they cannot prevent them. In terms of mitigation, in advance of Christmas, all Trusts focused on increased discharges to maximise bed capacity, putting hospital sites in an improved position compared to the same period in 2024.While Emergency Department (ED) attendances and ambulance arrivals were on some days higher than forecast, we have seen improvement in comparison with 2024.  There have been reductions in both the number of patients in ED awaiting admission and the number of patients waiting over 12 hours once admitted.While Flu rates peaked earlier in 2025, I am encouraged that related admissions had begun to decrease over the festive period in all age groups. We must however remain vigilant and continue to take all the steps we can to reduce the transmission of infectious diseases. As Minister of Health, I remain committed to doing everything I can and us all working together to ensure we achieve a stable reformed and sustainable health service for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total cost to the Health and Social Care system of providing abortion services in Northern Ireland in 2024/25, broken down by (a) gestational stage; and (b) procedure type; and (ii) how these costs compare with tariffs charged by independent providers such as British Pregnancy Advisory Service for equivalent procedures.
answer_text: The Department is unable to provide the cost breakdown requested by (a) gestational stage; and (b) procedure type as the information is not recorded in this way by the Health and Social Care Trusts. However, the following information is provided based on the funding allocated to each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts.From 2022/23, following the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland’s instruction to formally commission abortion services, there has been specific recurrent funding investment for the delivery of enhanced contraception, sexual reproductive health and abortion services in Northern Ireland.  The total investment per annum from 2024/25 is £4.3m.Of this £4.3m investment, approximately £2.6m related specifically to funding for the five Health and Social Care Trusts to provide abortion services in Northern Ireland.  This figure includes staffing costs, outpatient appointments, consent, procedures and follow up. This figure does not include funding for example, counselling services and Sexual Reproductive Health services, which were part of the original commissioning of abortion services as noted above, and does not include funding provided for staff training costs.In relation to part (ii), the British Pregnancy Advisory Service (BPAS) is an independent charity which provides the main referral point into Health and Social Care (HSC) abortion services in Northern Ireland.  It does not provide abortion services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the groups and individuals Baroness Cass met during her work on reviewing gender identity services.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32562/22-27, whether anyone appointed to the (i) voluntary and community sector; and (ii) Gender Identity Liaison Panel, were (a) from organisations critical of modern gender theory; (b) detransitioners; (c) members of the Rainbow Project; (d) members of Cara-Friend; (e) members of HERe NI; (f) members of TransgenderNI; (g) members of Belfast Trans Resource Centre; (h) members of QueerSpace Belfast; (I) members of Non-Binary+ Northern Ireland; (j) members of Trans Partners NI; (k) members of Transderry; and (l) members of Stonewall.
answer_text: Keen to ensure the voices of those with lived experience of transgender issues could continue to inform and influence the review, the Patient Client Council (PCC) were tasked with recruiting voluntary and community sector representatives to join the Gender Identity Review Group. From the applications received and with the advice and support of the Patient and Client Council (PCC), two individuals representing the voluntary and community sector were appointed to join the Review Group.  Those members were selected based on their relevant knowledge and expertise rather than their due to their membership of a specific organisation/group.  Membership of the Gender Identity Liaison Panel was by application to the PCC.  Panel members  for this group were also selected based on their relevant knowledge and expertise. In total, 21 panel members were selected via a formal process which was led by the PCC.While some members of both the Gender Review Group and Gender Liaison Panel may have also been members of other groups – as this was not the basis of their appointment the specific information you have requested has not been captured.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the sinking of a second Loughs Agency survey vessel while tied to the Foyle Port pontoon, to detail (i) the code of practice survey the vessel was covered by; (ii) when was it last inspected by a surveyor; and (iii) the certifying authority that issued the code of practice certificate.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that the vessel did not fall under the requirements of the Maritime and Coastguard Agency (MCA) Small Commercial Vessel Code of Practice or similar codes, as these apply to vessels operating at sea or beyond categorised waters. This vessel operated exclusively within Category C waters (Lough Foyle), which are sheltered inland waters, and therefore was not subject to certification under the MCA Code of Practice for Small Commercial Vessels. Its operations were confined to non-seagoing activities within the Lough, meaning compliance with full sea-going code provisions was not required.The vessel was last inspected by a qualified Surveyor in May 2019. In addition to this formal inspection, the vessel underwent annual servicing which included lifting the vessel clear of the water for a full hull assessment, application of antifouling coatings and routine maintenance of all critical systems.Not applicable.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 35686/22-27, to detail (i) any assessment that has been carried on in relation to this incident, which is the second large Loughs Agency survey vessel to have sank while at berth, including whether it could have resulted in serious injury and or fatalities to Foyle Port staff, Loughs Agency staff or members of the public; (ii) what the routine inspections consist of; (iii) where and how inspections were recorded; (iv) the vessel daily checklist; (v) the procedures in place for the inspections; and (vi) the train
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that: a specialist Marine Surveyor investigated the incident, and it is acknowledged that it was a serious incident. However, the risk of serious injury and/or fatalities to Foyle Port staff, Loughs Agency staff or members of the public was low because the vessel was moored within a secure area where access is controlled. No members of the public can access this area which significantly reduces the likelihood of third-party interference or accidental exposure. In addition, Loughs Agency staff are not based at the pontoon and only access the area to utilise the vessel for operational purposes. The risk to Port Authority staff was also minimal as they do not regularly attend the pontoon;routine inspections consist of a comprehensive visual and operational check of the vessel and its mooring arrangements. This includes:inspecting all mooring lines and securing points on the pontoon for integrity and tension;conducting an external hull inspection for signs of damage, corrosion or water ingress;boarding the vessel to check bilge compartments for water accumulation and verify bilge pump functionality;starting engines to confirm operational readiness and ensure water circulation systems were functioning correctly; andperforming an overall condition assessment of the vessel, including deck fittings, safety equipment and structural components;a formal record of inspection activities was documented in the Official Vessel Log which includes inspection dates, findings and any corrective actions taken. A Personal Log was also maintained by staff where observations and checks were noted;the boat safety checklist includes checks on all safety equipment on board, as well as engine, fuel and weather checks;routine walk-on inspections were carried out in accordance with standard operating practices commonly adopted across the marine industry; and all crew had Certification of Proficiency Personal Survival Techniques on Board Ship. The Skipper is also certified in:                     Elementary First Aid on Board Ship;Fire Prevention and Fire Fighting;Personal Safety and Social Responsibilities;Efficient Deck Hand;GMDSS Restricted Operators Certificate (ROC);ISA advanced Power Boat;Category B commercial endorsement on ISA Advanced Powerboat Certificate; andMCA Basic Diesel Engine Maintenance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will request that the UK Government extends the Statutory Gambling Levy to apply in Northern Ireland, in order to ensure that those suffering gambling-related harm are not less protected than others in Great Britain.
answer_text: In September 2024 I, along with the Minister for Health and Minister of Education, wrote to the Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, Lisa Nandy MP requesting engagement on the NI dimension of the GB Statutory Gambling Levy. In response the UK Government committed to further engagement  and my officials are continuing to discuss this matter with their Department of Culture, Media and Sport counterparts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has undertaken any assessment of the economic and social impact of delayed diagnosis and inadequate treatment for polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis and adenomyosis, particularly regarding employment, education and mental health.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken a specific assessment of the economic and social impacts of these conditions, however I am very cognisant of the relevant published research and other evidence that has been considered by the Parliamentary Women and Equalities Committee. The evidence highlights the avoidable costs and financial burden on women and their families linked to delayed diagnosis of many women’s health conditions, as well as on the healthcare system and on the economy as a whole.  It also highlights profound social impacts of delayed diagnosis including mental health problems, impact on education and career progress, and on fertility. These impacts are reflected in the emerging findings of the ongoing public listening exercise on women’s health which my Department is supporting and which is expected to report in the coming months. These findings and other relevant research will help inform the Women’s Health Action Plan, which will reflect the ongoing work to improve diagnostic and treatment pathways for conditions such as these and providing women and girls with the care they need to live healthy and fulfilling lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to reduce the backlog in gynaecology outpatient appointments and diagnostic procedures, including laparoscopy.
answer_text: There is currently considerable work underway across the health and care system to try to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology specific conditions such as endometriosis in each Trust across Northern Ireland.The Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioner representation. This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvement, and the implementation and development of gynaecology service pathways at primary, intermediate, secondary and tertiary care levels. as well as overseeing the implementation of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report. Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this. If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional. In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service. Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology. This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  A total of 6,072 facetoface and review appointments have been commissioned for the Primary Care Elective Gynaecology Service in 2025/26.As of December 2025, 2,105 procedures—including LARCs, coil fittings, and pessary replacements—have been delivered within primary care.In November 2025, 490 patients were transferred from the South Eastern Trust gynaecology waiting list to the Primary Care Service. Further work is underway through the Gynaecology Collaborative Forum to assess and progress the transfer of additional eligible gynaecology patients from all Trusts who meet the criteria for management within primary care. This will help address the ongoing pressures on secondary care gynae waiting lists. Additional funding has also been approved non-recurrently for waiting list initiatives (both independent sector and in-house) within secondary care to help reduce waiting times for patients requiring red flag and other time critical assessments and treatments across a range of specialties, including gynaecology.  The waits have started to show improvements, and actual numbers will be presented in the coming months.Trusts have established a Gynaecology Provider Collaborative - February 2025. As part of this process, two Collaborative sub-groups have been established, namely:   -A Clinical Standardisation Group – with Clinical and Directo

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the resources available to Health and Social Care Trusts to adequately provide multidisciplinary care, including access to pain specialists, fertility support and dietetic services, for patients with polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis and adenomyosis.
answer_text: There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.  To support this, the Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, which includes representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioners.  This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvements and the development of gynaecology service pathways across primary, intermediate, secondary, and tertiary care levels which are clinically led.  It is also responsible for implementing the recommendations of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this.  If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional.  In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service.  Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology.  This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  As of 30 September 2025, the Enhanced Primary Care Service had delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations at a cost in-year of £1.07m.  When fully operational, it is expected to manage approximately 6,072 patient contacts annually (including both face-to-face and remote appointments).Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust.  Between April and December 2025, additional non-recurrent funding has been approved of £3.9m to provide waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments.  As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review and cleanse current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists.  Additional outpatients are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries that cannot be provided in primary care.   Trusts have established a Gynaecology Provider Collaborative - February 2025.  As part of this process, two Collaborative sub-groups have been established, namely:  A Clinical Standardisation Group – with Clinical and Director/Assis

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support available to women experiencing chronic pelvic pain, fertility difficulties or heavy menstrual bleeding while they remain on waiting lists for diagnosis or treatment.
answer_text: Women experiencing chronic pelvic pain in Northern Ireland have access to various support services and resources.  The Pelvic Pain Support Network is a key organisation that provides information, advocacy and support for those with pelvic pain, by offering a patient-led approach.  The network is also a member of several international organisations including the International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP) and the International Alliance of Patient Organisations (IAPO) which contribute to the education and support of pelvic pain patients.For immediate support and information, the Pelvic Pain Support Network online community is available.  Members can ask questions, learn from others’ experiences and contribute their own wisdom.  Additionally, the network provides resources and clinics where patients can receive care from various healthcare professionals.Doctors will discuss a treatment and management plan with all women referred.Fertility Difficulties	Women experiencing fertility difficulties in Northern Ireland can access a range of support services while they remain on waiting lists for diagnosis or treatment.  These services include:Counselling support: many fertility clinics offer counselling support to help manage the emotional impact of fertility challenges;Support Groups: online and local support groups provide a community for sharing experiences and coping strategies;Helplines:  helplines offer information, advice and support for those affected by fertility issues;Cultural and Reproductive Health Resources: Resources tailored to the cultural and reproductive health needs of women in Northern Ireland; and,Mental Health Resources: Information and support for mental health issues related to fertility challenges.These resources are designed to help women navigate the emotional and practical challenges of fertility difficulties ensuring they receive the support they need during this difficult time.  While access to mental health support during fertility struggles can still be limited, especially early on, there are valuable options available, many of which people find incredibly helpful.Heavy Menstrual Bleeding (HMB)Women experiencing heavy menstrual bleeding in Northern Ireland have access to various support options, including digital solutions and healthcare services.  Sista Care: An application designed to assist women with HMB by providing personalised guidance and connecting them with local health clinics for further support.	Healthcare Consultations: Women can consult healthcare professionals to discuss their symptoms and explore treatment options which may include medications, hormonal therapies or surgical interventions.Support Groups: Women can join support groups to share experiences and receive emotional and practical support from others facing similar challenges.Counselling and Therapy: Seeking support from qualified counsellors or therapy providers can help manage the emotional impact of heavy peri

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he was provided with a draft answer from officials to AQW 33950/22-27; and when he will answer this question.
To ask the Assembly Commission whether any complaint has been received in relation to restricting or impeding the role of the Chairperson of the Assembly Commission Audit and Risk Committee.
answer_text: No complaint has been received from the current Chairperson of the Assembly Commission Audit and Risk Committee (ACARC) or the previous Chairperson in relation to restricting or impeding their role.   A confidential complaint from a former Chairperson of the Assembly Commission Audit and Risk Committee on a specific issue of governance was, however, received by the then Speaker of the Assembly on 27 June 2023. On that same day, ACARC invited the Assembly Commission to commission a Governance Fact Finding Review from the Internal Auditors, Ernst and Young (EY), in relation to the same issue raised in the complaint.  The Assembly Commission agreed to do so.After detailed consideration of the conclusions of the Governance Fact Finding Review, the Assembly Commission concluded that there were no grounds to proceed further with the complaint and agreed a number of actions.  This included that an already scheduled Corporate Governance Review would be brought forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is reviewing the diagnostic pathways for adenomyosis, given that many women in Northern Ireland do not receive a definitive diagnosis until after undergoing a hysterectomy.
answer_text: Adenomyosis can cause painful periods, heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding with clotting and abdominal/pelvic pain.About 1 in 3 people with adenomyosis don’t have signs or symptoms.If adenomyosis is suspected, based on the woman’s symptoms, then a pelvic examination, ultrasound scan or imaging scan(MRI) may be performed.  Then a woman may be treated with hormones, as the hormone oestrogen promotes endometrial tissue growth.  Adenomyosis symptoms often go away after menopause. In the meantime, these treatments can ease pain, and help with heavy bleeding and other symptoms.The SPPG is working with the Gynae Collaborative to develop and enhance all pathways for gynae conditions, which include adenomyosis.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they were provided with a draft answer from officials to (i) AQW 30062/22-27; (ii) AQW 28725/22-27; (iii) AQW 28120/22-27; (iv) AQW 21695/22-2;7 and (v) AQW 17459/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current diagnostic pathways for (i) polycystic ovary syndrome; (ii) endometriosis; and (iii) adenomyosis, across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: (i) Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)	PCOS is a common condition that affects how a woman’s ovaries function. The exact cause is unknown, but it often runs in families and is associated with abnormal hormone levels, including high insulin levels.There is no cure for PCOS; however, symptoms can be managed. Individuals who suspect they have the condition should seek advice from their GP, who will provide guidance on diagnosis and treatment options.(ii) Endometriosis	Endometriosis services are currently delivered as part of general gynaecology services in Northern Ireland.The diagnostic pathway includes symptom assessment, investigations to determine severity and progression, and planning next steps. Management options range from ongoing monitoring to hormonal therapies and/or surgical intervention, with consideration of fertility throughout.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is working with the Gynaecology Collaborative to invest in all five Trusts, enhancing capacity for additional laparoscopy sessions and clinical appointments. Work is also underway to develop two regional Endometriosis Centres—in the West and Belfast—which will provide complex surgical care for women who require it.(iii) Adenomyosis	Adenomyosis occurs when the lining of the womb (uterus) grows into the muscle wall of the womb. It is most commonly diagnosed in women aged 35 and above, up to menopause.If a woman presents with symptoms, her GP will discuss these, examine the abdomen for swelling or bloating, and may carry out an internal examination of the vagina and cervix. If further tests are needed to rule out conditions such as endometriosis, an ultrasound or MRI scan may be arranged, requiring referral to secondary care

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 34793/22-27 and AQW 34796/22-27, to place a copy of the memorandum of understanding in the Assembly library.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to place a copy of the memorandum of understanding in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether a former Chairperson of the Assembly Commission Audit and Risk Committee formally raised any concerns in 2023 about impediments in delivering the role.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the previous answer in AQW 37260/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the whistleblowing procedures currently in place; and (ii) whether any Northern Ireland Assembly Commission staff have made a complaint under the whistleblowing procedures, since 2016.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is committed to the highest ethical standards in public life and in all of its practices. To achieve these ends, it encourages workers to report/disclose potential misfeasance or wrongdoing via a Whistleblowing Policy, first published in 2012 and contained in the Staff Handbook. The Policy applies to all Assembly Commission employees, agency workers, inward secondees and those in training with the Assembly Commission.The Whistleblowing Policy clearly sets out how staff can make an internal disclosure and how the Assembly Commission will manage the matter. It also recognises that there may be circumstances in which a staff member may prefer to make a disclosure to an external person or body such as the Health and Safety Executive of Northern Ireland and explains how this should be done. The Policy also provides links to other internal and external documents that may be of relevance. Since 2016, no Assembly Commission employee has made a complaint to the Assembly Commission under the Whistleblowing Policy.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission if it paid any travel costs for the Clerk/Chief Executive to travel with the Speaker to the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association annual conference in Barbados.
answer_text: While the Assembly Commission has no role in determining the activities of the Assembly Branch of the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association (CPA), it does provide the resources to support the participation of the Assembly in the CPA and other inter-parliamentary bodies.As has been previously reported, the Assembly Commission paid travel costs for the Clerk/ Chief Executive to attend the 68th meeting of the CPA in Barbados.  The Clerk/ Chief Executive attended both the CPA conference, which the Speaker also attended, and the 59th meeting of the Society of the Clerks at the Table (SoCATT) which is traditionally held alongside the CPA conference.  SoCATT is the forum for the exchange of parliamentary practice amongst officers of different parliaments.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it has commissioned an independent review in relation to workplace culture of Northern Ireland Assembly Commission staff.
answer_text: In November 2023, an anonymous letter was received by the Chairperson of the Assembly Commission Audit and Risk Committee (ACARC) at that time, containing a number of allegations relating to matters within the Assembly Commission’s responsibilities. The then Chairperson of ACARC forwarded the anonymous letter to the then Speaker, who conferred with the Members of the Assembly Commission.  The Assembly Commission agreed that an external and independent review would be conducted to gather information and assess the allegations made.  In June 2024, the Assembly Commission considered the report of this independent review.  The Assembly Commission concluded that there was no basis to take the issues raised to any further formal process and agreed a number of actions.Throughout this matter, the Assembly Commission has been conscious of its duty of care to all staff and its obligations to ensure fair process.  Consequently, in line with best practice on HR and personnel matters, the review was dealt with through a confidential process and the Assembly Commission will not comment on the detail of HR matters.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it has undertaken any investigation in relation to any allegations from Northern Ireland Assembly Commission staff of fear, bullying or micromanagement incidents since 2016.
answer_text: Since 2016, the Assembly Commission has undertaken eight investigations from its staff in relation to allegations that could fall into the descriptors of ‘fear’, or ‘bullying’ or ‘micromanagement’ incidents since 2016.  The allegations were made by employees across a range of Assembly Commission business areas, in respect of staff at various grades.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether confidentiality directives were issued to Members of the Assembly Commission or Northern Ireland Assembly Commission staff in relation to audit and oversight matters.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is a corporate body, not a committee, with a broad range of responsibilities including some sensitive matters such as HR and personnel issues, security, commercial contracts and legal advice.  Therefore, the Assembly Commission meets in private with a principle of confidentiality with minutes of meetings taking appropriate account of information which can be published. AQW 37160/22-27 outlined that an external and independent review had been conducted. In line with best practice on HR and personnel matters, those participating in the independent review, and those considering the report of it, were asked to respect confidentiality. This was necessary to protect the integrity of the process to ensure a robust and fair review and to ensure that the Assembly Commission respected its duty of care towards all of its employees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the publication of the Northern Ireland Abortion Statistics 2024/25, which showed that over 2,800 pregnancies were terminated in 2024/25 and more than 8,000 since decriminalisation, to detail any assessment he has made of the statistics and their impact on the protection of unborn life and support for women in crisis pregnancies.
answer_text: I have noted the recent publication showing that the number of abortions increased by 3.7% compared with 2023/24 as services continue to be embedded.  Although it is not yet possible to fully assess long-term demand since these services were commissioned in December 2022, I can confirm that the level of abortions remains within the assumption made at that time, based upon the average demand in England and Wales for 2017 – 2019. Informing Choices NI provides non-directive pregnancy choices counselling, support and information for women facing an unplanned or crisis pregnancy, including signposting to appropriate services in line with their decisions. A range of other supports are available within the health and social system and the community and voluntary sector.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it has received any formal complaints, since 2016, from Northern Ireland Assembly Commission staff in relation to (i) workplace culture, bullying or harassment; and (ii) the conduct of senior management.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission currently has 344 Full Time Equivalent permanent employees.  In the approximately 10 years since 2016, the Assembly Commission has received 16 formal complaints from its staff in relation to allegations that could fall into the descriptors of ‘(i) workplace culture, bullying or harassment; and (ii) the conduct of senior management’.  The complaints were made by employees from a range of Assembly Commission business areas and across various grades.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any processes that set out how the Clerk/Chief Executive is accountable for staff welfare and compliance with Assembly policies.
answer_text: Like all employees, of the Assembly Commission, the Clerk/ Chief Executive, is governed by the provisions of the Assembly Commission Staff Handbook and is required to comply with organisational policies. The Clerk/ Chief Executive and Senior Management Team are responsible and accountable to the Assembly Commission.  Responsibility for the management of staff has been delegated by the Assembly Commission to the Clerk/Chief Executive. The Clerk/ Chief Executive is accountable for staff welfare by ensuring a safe, inclusive, and supportive working environment across the Assembly Commission. In October 2025, the Clerk/Chief Executive and Senior Management Team approved a new Wellbeing Framework 2026 - 2029 for the Assembly Commission. The Framework sets out five key areas that most influence wellbeing and a number of commitments that will be delivered through a combination of leadership accountability, colleague involvement, and ongoing investment in resources and support.  Ongoing welfare support is delivered through a Health and Wellbeing Group comprising of staff from across the Assembly Commission and also a network of Mental Health First Aiders and Harassment Contact Officers who are trained to offer confidential advice, guidance and support to staff members who require support dealing with issues relating to mental health and general work-related concerns.The Clerk/ Chief Executive and Senior Management Team has strengthened the Organisational Development and Learning Team who regularly promote welfare activities to assist staff with managing their health and wellbeing including courses on mental health awareness for staff and their line managers. Several virtual learning events relating to health and wellbeing topics have also been created as part of interparliamentary networks with Scotland, Wales and Ireland.In terms of external support, the Assembly Commission has an Employee Assistance Programme provided by Lena (formerly known as Inspire Workplace Services).  Staff can avail of a number of services, including the provision of 24/7 counselling, online training and access to a range of electronic resources through the Inspire Support Hub. Assembly Commission staff can also avail of confidential support and advice provided by the NICS Welfare Support Service.  The Assembly Commission also utilises the services of Occupational Health providers to obtain medical advice during periods of sickness absence and in cases where requests or requirements for workplace adjustments need to be considered.  Compliance with organisational policies is supported through clear internal communication strategies and via resources such as the Staff Handbook, complemented by structured training and guidance. There are clear mechanisms and processes for staff to seek clarification to support compliance or to raise concerns in relation to the conduct of any member of staff.  Final decisions on matters of discipline or grievance relating to the Clerk/ Chief Executi
