question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 30627/22-77, to detail the governance and oversight arrangements in place for the refurbishment of the Blue Flax restaurant.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission first considered and approved a Business Case for the retendering of the Support Services Contract, including capital investment for the refurbishment of the Blue Flax restaurant, at its meeting in February 2022.Following an unsuccessful tender for the Support Services Contract, a further business case, again including capital investment for the refurbishment of the Blue Flax restaurant, was considered and approved by the Assembly Commission in October 2022.A subsequent tender resulted in the current Support Services contractor being appointed. The contract stipulated that the refurbishment of the Blue Flax would be funded by the Assembly Commission but designed and delivered by the service provider, and that the service provider would be required to appoint a subcontractor(s) in line with public procurement regulations to deliver value for money and ensure that the principles of public procurement were upheld. The service provider was also required to invoice the Assembly Commission for the costs paid to their sub-contractor(s) and include evidence of the actual costs paid.As the Blue Flax refurbishment project was within the delegation of the Clerk/ Chief Executive and Senior Management Team (SMT), SMT had oversight of the governance and oversight arrangements regarding delivery of the project.The Blue Flax refurbishment works were initially tendered by the Support Services contractor in 2024, as required under the Support Services Contract, but that tender was abandoned on the grounds of cost. A second tender process was launched earlier this year. to commission a “valued engineered design” to optimise the Assembly Commission’s return on investment.  As part of that tender process, contractors were asked to provide a range of finishes and design options in relation to the budget allocation available. Following the tender evaluation, the Senior Management Team was presented with costs and options in April 2025. An Addendum business case for this amount was then presented to, and approved by SMT, in May 2025 for the capital refurbishment element. Design and project management costs relating to the refurbishment were bid for under the GAE budget.The Speaker met with SMT on 9 December 2025 and the Assembly Commission met on 19 December 2025 to consider management of the project.  SMT will be conducting a review of lessons learnt and reporting back to the Assembly Commission

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 30627/22-77, whether any post-project review or value-for-money assessment (i) has been conducted; and (ii) is planned to be conducted.
answer_text: A post-project evaluation, including lessons learned, will be conducted following completion of the and reported back to the Assembly Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31192/22-27, to detail (i) how the Loughs Agency recruited and paid additional staff, given the scrutiny of officials at quarterly assurance meetings and budgets being agreed by Ministers and the North South Ministerial Council; and (ii) any measures he will take to (a) ensure that the Loughs Agency board has been made aware of unapproved overstaffing to ensure due diligence; and (b) reconstitute the advisory forum to bring relevant expertise and local knowledge to ensure management delivers on its functions.
answer_text: It has not been determined if Loughs Agency has recruited and paid additional staff above its approved staffing complement. My officials continue to liaise with officials in the Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment (DCEE), North South Ministerial Council and the Loughs Agency to establish an agreed position.Loughs Agency continues to undertake a range of stakeholder engagement activities, with the aim of understanding its customers’ needs and helping to inform its planning process. However, my Department will liaise with the Agency to consider the need for the Foyle Area and Carlingford Area Advisory Forum going forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the purpose; and (ii) the need for all new and additional posts, including (a) head of science; (b) strategic project implementation manager; and (c) executive support officer, created at the Loughs Agency in the last five years.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised (i) the purpose of the three specific posts referenced is as follows: -Head of ScienceTo provide strategic leadership and oversight of scientific research and monitoring programmes, ensuring evidence-based decision-making and compliance with statutory obligations.Strategic Project and Innovation ManagerTo provide leadership in the development of strategic and innovative projects and programmes across all Agency functions. Executive Support OfficerTo provide support at a senior level, strengthening governance and administrative capacity, to senior management, the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) and the Board in meeting regulatory and operational requirements.The Agency has advised in relation to (ii) the need for these posts, that these positions were identified to address evolving statutory responsibilities, enhance operational efficiency and deliver on commitments under the North South Ministerial Council framework. Each role responds to increased demand for scientific expertise, project delivery capability and governance support in line with the Agency’s mandate. The Agency has also advised of other new and additional posts created in the last five years and (i) the purpose; and (ii) the need for these, as follows: -Health and Safety ManagerTo lead and maintain an effective Health and Safety Management System, ensuring compliance and continuous improvement.To ensure the Agency is compliant with Health and Safety Regulations in both Northern Ireland and Ireland.  The Agency previously relied on an external managed service for delivery of this crucial function.Climate Action and Sustainability OfficerTo lead on and contribute to the implementation and monitoring of the Agency’s Climate Action Plan.To deliver the Climate Action Plan and ensure that the Agency is compliant with legislation.Staff Officer AccountantTo provide essential support in the production of financial and management accounts and ensure accurate financial reporting, compliance with accounting standards and timely delivery of management information to support decision-making.To sustain robust financial governance and ensure that statutory and internal reporting obligations are met without disruption.Fisheries Sustainability ManagerTo lead initiatives to promote sustainable fisheries management within the Agency’s remit, implementing strategies that balance ecological protection with economic viability, and ensuring compliance with environmental and fisheries legislation.To deliver targeted sustainability objectives and address the need for specialist expertise to manage fisheries resources responsibly and mitigate environmental impacts.Business AnalystTo support internal and external audit activities, reviewing systems and controls and implementing improvements to meet best-practice standards.To strengthen governance processes and ensure the Agency’s compliance with all statutory and internal governance requirements.IT and Facilities ManagerTo ov
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 30627/22-77, to detail (i) the projected completion date for outstanding works; and (ii) whether any further delays are anticipated.
answer_text: It was hoped that the project could be completed in early December 2025 however availability of contractors means that the completion date is now scheduled for week commencing 26 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 30627/22-77, to detail the small number of final elements still to be addressed in the refurbishment of the Blue Flax restaurant.
answer_text: In the intervening period since the response to AQW 30627/22-27 was provided, the majority of the final elements outstanding have now been completed. Currently, the remaining elements to be addressed to complete the refurbishment are:Fit curved cladding at coffee station and till units;Fit a tray slide at the coffee station;Alter compostable bin chute to allow all containers to be disposed of;Fit missing panel at microwave station; Install dimmer switch for wall lights;Install missing picture frame light;Install missing power socket; Install blanking plates to both distribution boards;Provide remaining soft furniture for low seating area; andFinalise safety files, warranties and certificates.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 30627/22-77, to provide details of the procurement process for the refurbishment of the Blue Flax restaurant, including (i) the number of tenders received; (ii) the evaluation criteria applied; and (iii) the contractors and sub-contractors appointed.
answer_text: As per the Support Services contract specification, the procurement process was undertaken by the Support Services provider. The Support Services provider was therefore required to appoint a subcontractor(s) in line with public procurement regulations to deliver value for money and ensure that the principles of public procurement were upheld.Four bids were received in relation to the second tender competition.The evaluation criteria applied were as follows:Most economically advantageous tender		50%Contractors program of works			25%Previous experience in similar works		10%Financial standings					5%Health & safety management			5%Quality of submission				5%Johnston Shopfitters were awarded the contract and delivered the majority of the refurbishment works with the assistance of a number of subcontractors such as Halpenny (electrical), S&I (painting and decorating); Floorform (flooring), Galgorm Group (whitegoods), Plantscapes (internal planting).

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 30627/22-77, to provide a copy of the original (i) cost estimate; and (ii) approved budget, for the refurbishment of the Blue Flax restaurant, including any subsequent revisions.
answer_text: The cost for refurbishment of the was initially estimated at £350k in February 2022 and a budget was set at that level.The Blue Flax refurbishment works were initially tendered by the Support Services contractor in 2024, as required under the Support Services Contract, but that tender was abandoned on the grounds of cost. A second tender process was launched earlier this year. to commission a “valued engineered design” to optimise the Assembly Commission’s return on investment.  As part of that tender process, contractors were asked to provide a range of finishes and design options in relation to the budget allocation available. Following the tender evaluation, the Senior Management Team was presented with costs and options in April 2025, with the preferred option totalling £441k (plus £22k of contingency). An Addendum business case for this amount was then presented to, and approved by SMT, in May 2025 for the capital refurbishment element. A substantial proportion of the increase was due to the increase in building costs over the 3-year period since February 2022 and the remainder was due to the finishes selected. These capital costs were reflective of the lowest-cost tender bid for the preferred design option.To date, design and project management costs relating to the refurbishment are £39,770 and £27,949 respectively. These costs were bid for under the GAE budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will bring forward legislation, similar to that contained in Article 21(5) of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, giving parents the right to withdraw their children from Relationships and Sexuality Education.
answer_text: The teaching of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) is mandatory for all pupils of compulsory school age. All grant-aided schools are required to have an RSE policy that is based on consultation with parents and pupils. It is the responsibility of the Board of Governors of each school to ensure that a comprehensive programme is delivered which meets the needs of its pupils and aligns with its RSE policy.The Relationships and Sexuality Education (Northern Ireland) (Amendment) Regulations 2023 amend the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 and the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 in respect of Learning for Life and Work (LLW) to ensure that pupils at Key Stages 3 and 4 have the opportunity to “Receive age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights, covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion” (Referred to here as “Article 5(1A) education”).The 2023 Regulations also require my Department to make provision about the circumstances in which, at the request of a parent, a pupil may be excused from Article 5(1A) education or specified elements of that education.Under the Curriculum (Circumstances in which a Pupil may be Excused from Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights Education) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2023, a pupil may be excused from either all of Article 5(1A) education or specified elements of that education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the current arrangements for handling mail within HMP Maghaberry comply with prisoners’ rights to family correspondence under domestic law and relevant international standards.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQW 36674 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the Loughs Agency having been found to have employed 28 per cent more staff than their approved staff complement, to detail (i) whether the associated spend on unapproved staff will be treated as irregular spend in the (a) 2024-25 annual accounts; and (b) 2025-26 annual accounts; and (ii) whether the accounts will be reopened and qualified for previous years where unapproved staff were employed.
answer_text: It is not considered that the Loughs Agency has employed 28 percent more staff than its approved complement. As previously advised, my officials continue to liaise with officials in the Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment, North South Ministerial Council and the Loughs Agency on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any communication that has been issued to prisoners and their families in relation to changes to mail-handling procedures at HMP Maghaberry.
answer_text: All committals to Maghaberry undertake an induction programme, which sets out, amongst other things, how they can maintain contact with their families, including using mail.  The presentation states that all incoming and outgoing mail may be censored.  Regarding the censorship of mail, a notice was sent to all prisoners on 30 May 2025 advising that all incoming domestic mail to prisoners in a specific residential area would be subject to photocopying. The steps taken (photocopying all originals and providing a photocopy) removed the risk of conveying unauthorised substances into the prison while maintaining the existing process of providing mail to prisoners. The notice highlighted that this process would be kept under regular review and the areas affected may be subject change, to enable NIPS to manage the risk presented to any other residential area.  As a background to this notice, the Northern Ireland Prison Service evidenced a risk regarding the conveyance of unauthorised substances into the prison via mail. As an operational prison Maghaberry regularly sends out notices to both prisoners and prison staff. These operational notices are not shared with families. If a prisoner or a family member (on the back of discussions with the prisoner) have any concerns they can seek further guidance or clarification from a member of staff.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 35262/22-27, and given the current condition of the tomb, to detail any plans to clean it.
answer_text: As the tomb is Grade A listed, the Assembly Commission is currently working closely with the Historic Environment Division to determine how best to preserve it and identify appropriate methods for its cleaning, with the aim of commencing cleaning in the New Year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will rule out introducing a multi-faith or pluralistic model of religious education in controlled schools, in light of the legal requirement for religious education to be based upon the Holy Scriptures.
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 and another has implications for our schools. I will consider the judgment in full before determining any essential remedies that both respect the law and safeguard the role of religion in education. I have requested further legal advice to understand the full implications of the judgment and the next steps.The case is not about whether Christianity should be the main or primary faith that pupils learn about in schools in Northern Ireland.  Historically and today, Christianity is the main religion in Northern Ireland. Furthermore, the Court has explained that “it is within the Department’s margin of appreciation in planning and setting the curriculum for the greater part of religious education to focus on knowledge of Christianity”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment he has made of the compatibility of a neutral or non-confessional approach to religion in schools with Article 21 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 and another has implications for our schools. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the recent sinking of the Loughs Agency vessel on tidal water, being a marine incident, has been reported to the Marine Accident Investigation Branch.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that the incident was reported to the Marine Accident Investigation Branch by telephone on the same day and that a follow-up written report was provided on 4 December.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the current policy in operation at HMP Maghaberry in relation to the acceptance, screening and distribution of incoming mail to prisoners.
answer_text: The handling of mail for prisoners is governed under the Prison and Young Offenders Centres Rules (Northern Ireland) 1995, which in turn stem from the Prison Act 1953 (Northern Ireland).In HMP Maghaberry, dedicated letter censors are responsible for the collection, recording and inspecting of all incoming mail for prisoners.  Incoming mail is received by Maghaberry Reception and is collected daily by the letter censors. These letter censors ensure that:  Mail is processed in a timely manner;  All mail entering the prison is accurately recorded;  All Privileged mail is processed correctly;   Other barring processes including non-molestation orders, termination of contact lists, and intercepted mail are complied with;   The contents of each letter do not contain any prohibited or unauthorised articles; and  All money, postal orders, stamps, cheques etc are removed, recorded and forwarded to the Finance Office for processing.All incoming mail is censored in the ratio of 1 letter in 10 unless otherwise specified.  Interception of mail is permitted under Prison Rule 68A. Any interception, and censoring of mail must be deemed to be necessary, proportionate and reasonable. This ensures compliance with Prisoners’ rights under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. Once censored and recorded, mail is sealed in a delivery bag and delivered to all residential areas of the prison.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of whether Article 21 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 remains fully in force and was not altered or struck down by the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant).
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I provided to his question for Urgent Oral Answer on 24th November.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will (i) give an assurance that the assessment of the gender identity services provision in Northern Ireland, conducted by Baroness Cass during her recent visit, will be published in full and without delay once it is completed and submitted to his Department; and (ii) set out the expected timeline for publication.
answer_text: Baroness Cass’s report of her assessment of the gender identity service for children and young people in Northern Ireland, including any recommendations, is expected to reach my Department by 18 December 2025. Its contents will then be carefully considered, including any implications for the service.  A decision on the publication of the report will be determined once the report has been given due consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that religious education in controlled schools continues to be delivered based upon the Holy Scriptures according to some authoritative version or versions thereof, as required by Article 21(2) of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I provided to his question for Urgent Oral Answer on 24th November.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any procedures in place to ensure that pedigree breeding stock, including rams, bulls and heifers that have been bred and fed specifically for specialist December sales in Great Britain, can continue to travel to Great Britain in light of the current Bluetongue situation; and (ii) any steps his Department is taking to prevent significant financial losses for pedigree breeders.
answer_text: The authorities in Great Britain set the conditions under which they are prepared to accept live animals moves from Northern Ireland, according to the perceived risk. Moves of animals from outside the Temporary Control Zone to Great Britain can take place. Discussions are ongoing, but DAERA hopes that animals from inside the Temporary Control Zone will soon be eligible for export.My officials are investing significant time and effort in order to reopen live animal trade with Great Britain in full knowledge of the importance of this trade route to Northern Ireland pedigree animal producers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department will ensure that the broadly Christian character of collective worship, as required by section 386(2) of the Education Act 1996, is maintained following the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant).
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I provided to his question for Urgent Oral Answer on 24th November.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department provided any input to the information provided on the China Chamber of Commerce in the UK’s website which describes co-operation between her Department, Invest NI and the Chinese Government.
answer_text: My Department did not provide any input to the information published on the China Chamber of Commerce in the UK’s website regarding co-operation between the Department, Invest NI and the Chinese Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the National Association of Schoolmasters Union of Women Teachers encouraging teachers to participate in the Irish Congress of Trade Unions' Stand for Palestine during the school day on 28 November 2025, to detail any guidance his Department had in place to ensure political impartiality in schools.
answer_text: A trade union calling for and encouraging teachers to participate in a, ‘taking a stand for Palestine lunchtime vigil’, or any action of this nature, risks creating division within school communities and may lead to an environment that detracts from the primary focus which is meeting the educational and wellbeing needs of children. Schools should remain places of learning, neutrality, and inclusion, where pupils feel safe and supported regardless of differing opinions on external political issues.Teachers in schools should not be encouraged by any trade union to promote a divisive and contentious agenda in schools.However, the Department of Education is not the employer of teachers in schools as they are employed by the Board of Governors of the school and the relevant employing authority.Guidance has been jointly agreed and issued to all schools through the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) in the form of a TNC Joint Declaration of Protection, TNC 2023/2. This Declaration is a joint Management Side and Teachers’ Side agreement which recognises the moral and legal duties placed on employers and trade unions as a result of fair employment legislation, anti-discrimination law and the Section 75 Statutory Equality Duty. This agreement aims to provide a working environment that fosters the dignity of its employees, is inclusive and where individual differences are valued and respected.Department officials will remind trade unions of the joint commitments outlined in the Joint Declaration of Protection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to place all (i) correspondence; (ii) minutes; and (iii) briefing papers related to the drafting, negotiation or signing of the strategic co-operation framework agreement, in the Assembly library.
answer_text: The strategic co-operation framework agreement referred to is a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) between Invest NI and the Chinese Chamber of Commerce in the UK (CCCUK). 		A copy of the MoU has already been placed into the Assembly library. It is not necessary, nor would it be appropriate, to place all of the other requested documents in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any public expenditure by (i) her Department; and (ii) Invest NI in hosting or supporting the China-Northern Ireland Economic, Trade and Investment Cooperation Forum in Belfast.
answer_text: My Department did not incur any public expenditure in relation to the China–Northern Ireland Economic, Trade and Investment Cooperation Forum in Belfast. Invest NI partnered with the Chinese Consulate in Belfast and the China Chamber of Commerce in Britain to support the event and contributed £7,000 towards its delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the due diligence process undertaken on the 120 Chinese companies attending the China-Northern Ireland Economic, Trade and Investment Cooperation Forum; and (ii) to publish any assessments that were made.
answer_text: Chinese companies were invited to the Forum by the China Chamber of Commerce. The provisional company list was shared with the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) for advice. The FCDO advised that the companies have a presence in Britain and Ireland already and no concerns were raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any security risk assessment conducted before (i) engaging with companies such as Huawei, ZTE and BYD at the China-Northern Ireland Economic, Trade and Investment Cooperation Forum; and (ii) concluding the strategic co-operation framework agreement.
answer_text: Chinese companies were invited to the Forum by the China Chamber of Commerce. The provisional company list was shared with the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) for advice. The FCDO advised that the companies have a presence in Britain and Ireland already and no concerns were raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department consulted the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office prior to engaging with Chinese Government officials on trade-related matters.
answer_text: My department engages regularly with the British Government on various trade-related matters.In relation to China, Invest NI maintains offices embedded within the British Embassy in Beijing and consulates in Shanghai and Guangzhou, and collaborates with the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office, at post, to promote the interests of businesses across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she notified the Northern Ireland Office, or any UK Government department, in advance of Invest NI entering into a strategic agreement with representatives of the Chinese Government.
answer_text: No. There is no legal or procedural obligation to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the statutory authority under which her Department, or Invest NI, entered into a strategic co-operation framework agreement with representatives of the Chinese Government, given that international trade is a reserved matter.
answer_text: Invest NI signed a high-level, legally non-binding Strategic Cooperation Framework Memorandum of Understanding with the China Chamber of Commerce in Britain. This is entirely within its remit acting in the furtherance of the trade interests of the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will instruct the Education Authority to gather information on the methods of slaughter used by suppliers of meat to schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) wrote to all school principals on 2 December 2025, providing clarity on the poultry and meat used its provision of school meals. This letter is available on its website (https://www.eani.org.uk/news/ea-sets-out-school-dinner-supply-details) and is therefore able to be accessed by the general public, including parents. The EA letter explains the market supply realities that mean that maintaining chicken supplies to school kitchens would not be possible if orders were restricted to only non-halal certified chicken. This would inevitably have a negative impact on the choice of dishes available to pupils. The EA is therefore prioritising continuity of supplies and menu variety. In light of this, the EA is not able to offer menu choices that only contain non-halal certified chicken. Other public sector caterers and hospitality businesses face the same supply limitations.All poultry products offered and supplied to the EA must comply with all current relevant legislation applicable to Northern Ireland. The EA has engaged with its suppliers in relation to the methods of slaughter used. All poultry must be slaughtered and processed in United Kingdom or European Union approved premises, ensuring compliance with strict food safety and animal welfare standards. My Department does not provide any additional guidance on this issue.Poultry provided by EA suppliers adheres to the aforementioned requirements, ensuring compliance with food hygiene, traceability and quality standards from production through to delivery.

The EA has engaged with its suppliers in relation to the methods of slaughter used. All poultry must be slaughtered and processed in United Kingdom or European Union approved premises, ensuring compliance with strict food safety and animal welfare standards. My Department does not provide any additional guidance on this issue.Poultry provided by EA suppliers adheres to the aforementioned requirements, ensuring compliance with food hygiene, traceability and quality standards from production through to delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will instruct the Education Authority to notify all parents of school age children that school meals may contain meat slaughtered according to Islamic dietary requirements.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) wrote to all school principals on 2 December 2025, providing clarity on the poultry and meat used its provision of school meals. This letter is available on its website (https://www.eani.org.uk/news/ea-sets-out-school-dinner-supply-details) and is therefore able to be accessed by the general public, including parents. The EA letter explains the market supply realities that mean that maintaining chicken supplies to school kitchens would not be possible if orders were restricted to only non-halal certified chicken. This would inevitably have a negative impact on the choice of dishes available to pupils. The EA is therefore prioritising continuity of supplies and menu variety. In light of this, the EA is not able to offer menu choices that only contain non-halal certified chicken. Other public sector caterers and hospitality businesses face the same supply limitations.All poultry products offered and supplied to the EA must comply with all current relevant legislation applicable to Northern Ireland. The EA has engaged with its suppliers in relation to the methods of slaughter used. All poultry must be slaughtered and processed in United Kingdom or European Union approved premises, ensuring compliance with strict food safety and animal welfare standards. My Department does not provide any additional guidance on this issue.Poultry provided by EA suppliers adheres to the aforementioned requirements, ensuring compliance with food hygiene, traceability and quality standards from production through to delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35382/22-27, to detail her Department’s long-term plan for securing a permanent resolution to flooding on Maralaco Road, including whether redesignation of the watercourse has been considered.
answer_text: My Department will continue to engage with riparian landowners to encourage their voluntary cooperation to carry out maintenance necessary of the watercourse. It is often possible for drainage problems to be resolved more effectively by mutual agreement and co-operation between neighbours once the cause has been identified, and my Department encourages and assists such informal resolution wherever appropriate. However, in the absence of a resolution, Schedule 5 of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 enables my Department to serve formal notice on riparian landowners requiring them to carry out maintenance necessary to ensure the free flow of water in undesignated watercourses. Where such notices are ignored, my Department may refer the matter to the Public Prosecution Service for legal proceedings.The Drainage Council determines which watercourses and sea defences should be maintained by my Department at public expense.  A request to designate a watercourse may be put forward by a member of the public or Government Department.  In deciding to designate a watercourse the Drainage Council must be satisfied that any drainage works needed are outside the capability of landowners and that works at public expense on the watercourse would provide value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the next round of funding under the Access and Inclusion Programme is due to be released to local councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the next round of funding under the Access and Inclusion Programme is due to be released to local councils.
answer_text: Access and Inclusion funding has improved accessibility at arts, cultural, and active recreation venues across both urban and rural areas for people with disabilities. My officials, in partnership with colleagues in DAERA, are currently considering options regarding future delivery.I have also asked DfC officials to examine opportunities to enhance DfC-owned assets for access and inclusion purposes. These assets include libraries and museum buildings, the Armagh Observatory and Planetarium, the Public Record Office NI, and a range of State Care Monuments across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) why the Investigation Report commissioned in relation to the TREES Project was not included in the answer to AQW 31577/22-27 on the basis that it related to employee matters, given that (a) the Forestry Survey and the Governance and Oversight Review also contain material relating to employee matters, with the governance report noting that a culture of finger pointing is in evidence at a cultural level with the organisation; (b) the Investigation Report states, at page 4, that it was appropriate to wait for the Rapi
answer_text: AQW 31577/22-27 asked for the details of reports commissioned to look at the TREES Project. Loughs Agency has advised that the Investigation Report was not included in the answer to AQW 31577/22-27 as it was primarily focused on employee related matters, not on technical or governance aspects of the TREES Project itself.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35382/22-27, to detail (i) whether her Department accepts any responsibility for addressing flooding on Maralaco Road caused by road drainage where riparian landowners do not cooperate; and (ii) the powers she intends to exercise to ensure necessary works are completed.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate first engaged with riparian landowners regarding the maintenance of the undesignated watercourse network at Marlacoo Road in March 2025.  During initial engagements, one landowner completed channel works within their property, while other landowners verbally agreed to undertake maintenance. However, my Department could not pursue enforcement action at that time due to environmental constraints linked to the bird nesting season. Officials continue to engage with landowners through site visits and correspondence to encourage voluntary cooperation, but agreement from all parties has not yet been achieved.Schedule 5 of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 enables my Department to serve formal notice on riparian landowners requiring them to carry out maintenance necessary to ensure the free flow of water in undesignated watercourses. Where such notices are ignored, the Department may refer the matter to the Public Prosecution Service for legal proceedings.These powers are used only as a last resort after efforts to secure voluntary cooperation have failed. It is often possible for drainage problems to be resolved more effectively by mutual agreement and co-operation between neighbours once the cause has been identified, and my Department encourages and assists such informal resolution wherever appropriate.Landowners normally oblige through engagement and diplomacy to carry out the required maintenance works on undesignated watercourses and therefore my Department has not had to use the enforcement powers under Schedule 5 of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 in the last five years.  The road drainage system at Marlacoo Road will be reviewed when the necessary work on adjacent lands is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35382/22-27, to detail the number of times, in each of the last five years, her Department has used its enforcement powers under Schedule 5 of the Drainage (Northern Ireland) Order 1973 in cases involving failures by riparian landowners to maintain undesignated watercourses.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate first engaged with riparian landowners regarding the maintenance of the undesignated watercourse network at Marlacoo Road in March 2025.  During initial engagements, one landowner completed channel works within their property, while other landowners verbally agreed to undertake maintenance. However, my Department could not pursue enforcement action at that time due to environmental constraints linked to the bird nesting season. Officials continue to engage with landowners through site visits and correspondence to encourage voluntary cooperation, but agreement from all parties has not yet been achieved.Schedule 5 of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 enables my Department to serve formal notice on riparian landowners requiring them to carry out maintenance necessary to ensure the free flow of water in undesignated watercourses. Where such notices are ignored, the Department may refer the matter to the Public Prosecution Service for legal proceedings.These powers are used only as a last resort after efforts to secure voluntary cooperation have failed. It is often possible for drainage problems to be resolved more effectively by mutual agreement and co-operation between neighbours once the cause has been identified, and my Department encourages and assists such informal resolution wherever appropriate.Landowners normally oblige through engagement and diplomacy to carry out the required maintenance works on undesignated watercourses and therefore my Department has not had to use the enforcement powers under Schedule 5 of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 in the last five years.  The road drainage system at Marlacoo Road will be reviewed when the necessary work on adjacent lands is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action he is taking to ensure that Northern Ireland’s herring industry, including vessels, processors and dependent onshore businesses, is not negatively impacted devastated by advice emerging from ICES.
answer_text: The international negotiations on establishing fishing opportunities are underway and Defra is the lead negotiator for the UK. I have already written to Dame Eagle, the Minister of State for Defra, on the importance of seeking an outcome that enables the long-term growth and recovery of stocks while maintaining the ongoing economic viability of the fleets highlighting my concerns with the implications of the reduction in herring advice.My officials are engaged in these negotiations where I will continue to seek a fair and equitable outcome for this important fishery.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35382/22-27, to provide a timeline of all engagements her Department has had with riparian landowners regarding the maintenance of the undesignated watercourse at Maralaco Road, including (i) dates of initial contact; (ii) follow-up correspondence; (iii) site visits; and (iv) any warnings issued.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate first engaged with riparian landowners regarding the maintenance of the undesignated watercourse network at Marlacoo Road in March 2025.  During initial engagements, one landowner completed channel works within their property, while other landowners verbally agreed to undertake maintenance. However, my Department could not pursue enforcement action at that time due to environmental constraints linked to the bird nesting season. Officials continue to engage with landowners through site visits and correspondence to encourage voluntary cooperation, but agreement from all parties has not yet been achieved.Schedule 5 of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 enables my Department to serve formal notice on riparian landowners requiring them to carry out maintenance necessary to ensure the free flow of water in undesignated watercourses. Where such notices are ignored, the Department may refer the matter to the Public Prosecution Service for legal proceedings.These powers are used only as a last resort after efforts to secure voluntary cooperation have failed. It is often possible for drainage problems to be resolved more effectively by mutual agreement and co-operation between neighbours once the cause has been identified, and my Department encourages and assists such informal resolution wherever appropriate.Landowners normally oblige through engagement and diplomacy to carry out the required maintenance works on undesignated watercourses and therefore my Department has not had to use the enforcement powers under Schedule 5 of the Drainage (NI) Order 1973 in the last five years.  The road drainage system at Marlacoo Road will be reviewed when the necessary work on adjacent lands is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the Republic of Ireland’s closure of its 6–12 mile zone to Northern Ireland vessels and indications of further restrictions within 0–6 miles for vessels over 18 metres, and considering that Republic of Ireland fleets catch a substantial share of their quotas in UK waters, whether he will review the continuation of licences for Republic of Ireland vessels in UK waters in the absence of a proportionate reciprocal benefit.
answer_text: The Department is aware that reciprocal access for Northern Ireland vessels to the Republic of Ireland’s 6–12 nautical mile zone was not provided for in the UK–EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement, nor in the subsequent UK–EU reset agreement. Consequently, Northern Ireland vessels cannot access the Irish 6–12 mile zone, and Irish vessels likewise have no access to Northern Ireland’s 6–12 mile zone.My Department continues to engage with officials in the Republic of Ireland to seek a resolution to this issue and to press for arrangements that deliver fair and proportionate outcomes for our fishing industry.In relation to the Republic of Ireland’s proposed restrictions on trawling within its 0–6 mile zone for vessels over 18 metres, I would add that existing legislation applies in many areas within Northern Ireland’s own 0–6 mile inshore area which prohibits trawling. These restrictions which apply to all vessels regardless of length and equally to Irish vessels operating in our waters and are designed to protect local fisheries and ensure sustainability.The Department remains committed to constructive engagement with the Irish authorities and the UK Government to address these matters and to safeguard the interests of Northern Ireland’s fishing fleet.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the regulatory implications of the Windsor Framework, whether the UK Government retains full freedom to allocate and defend quota shares for Northern Ireland fishermen, or whether its discretion is constrained in practice by Protocol-related obligations.
answer_text: The UK Government retains full freedom to allocate and defend quota shares for Northern Ireland fishermen, subject only to international agreements. The Windsor Framework and the Northern Ireland Protocol deal with trade in goods and regulatory alignment for the EU Single Market; they do not cover fisheries quota allocation, which remains governed by UK domestic law and international agreements like the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department has taken to address concerns previously raised by Northern Ireland fishermen that they would be disadvantaged under the UK quota allocation system compared with fleets in other parts of the UK.
answer_text: Quota is allocated to each devolved administration in line with the UK’s quota management rules. The share of each quota apportioned to Northern Ireland is based on the number of Fixed Allocation Units for each quota associated with the licences managed by DAERA. It is then for each administration to allocate quota to their respective fleets based again on the Fixed Quota Allocation Units. Additional quota, derived from increased shares negotiated when the UK exited the EU, is allocated by the Secretary of State to DAERA for subsequent allocation, in line with the requirements of the Fisheries Act 2020. Northern Ireland operates its own allocation system and the current system has been in place since 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the socio-economic impact assessment provided by industry, detailing the hardship likely to arise from the proposed quota reductions, is being factored into his Department’s discussions with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
answer_text: My officials have worked closely with industry in developing the socio-economic impact assessment, which has been integral in informing our position in UK coordination and engagement with DEFRA ahead of international negotiations with other coastal states. My department has also enabled industry to present its case directly to DEFRA. Throughout, I have emphasised that quota decisions must balance sustainability objectives with the viability of Northern Ireland’s fishing communities, in accordance with the Fisheries Act 2020.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of his Department’s emissions-reduction targets and the financial challenges faced by fishermen in replacing older diesel engines, whether he will consider introducing a decommissioning scheme for older vessels as a means of reducing fleet capacity, lowering emissions and improving the long-term viability of the remaining Northern Ireland fleet.
answer_text: I have no plans to bring forward a decommissioning scheme for the Northern Ireland fishing fleet at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any mitigation strategies his Department will put in place to support Northern Ireland vessels facing rising fuel costs and limited access to alternative fishing zones, in order to prevent forced tie-ups, permanent fleet loss or vessels departing the Northern Ireland register.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressures facing Northern Ireland’s fishing fleet, including rising fuel costs and restricted access to alternative fishing zones. To mitigate these challenges, the Marine Environment and Fisheries Fund (MEFF) 2025/26 provides targeted financial support to strengthen fleet resilience	and ensure the sector can adapt to economic and environmental challenges.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will publish a recovery and sustainability plan for 2026 - 2030 aimed at rebuilding quota opportunities for the (i) Irish Sea mixed-fishery fleet; and (ii) Northern pelagic fleet, including clear targets and timelines.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs is committed to ensuring the long-term sustainability of our fisheries. As the lead authority for the Irish Sea demersal and Irish Sea pelagic Fisheries Management Plans (FMP), we are currently drafting these plans, which will be published by the end of 2026.Under section 2(6) of the Fisheries Act 2020, an FMP is defined as a document that sets out policies designed to restore one or more stocks of sea fish to, or maintain them at, sustainable levels, or contribute to their restoration or maintenance at sustainable levels. These plans are evidence-based action frameworks that support the delivery of sustainable fisheries for current and future generations.The FMPs will include measures aimed at rebuilding opportunities for both the Irish Sea mixed-fishery fleet and the Northern pelagic fleet. They will set out clear objectives and actions to achieve sustainability, informed by the best available science and stakeholder engagement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given Norway’s unilateral quota increases in recent years, to detail any steps he will take to ensure that proposed reductions to Irish Sea quotas prioritise limiting Norwegian access to EU and UK waters for mackerel and blue whiting.
answer_text: Quotas for the Irish Sea are set in the UK / EU annual negotiation. These discussions involve only those coastal nations with an interest in Irish Sea shared stocks. Norway is not part of this negotiation. Access and fishing opportunities for stocks of interest to Norway are set in separate negotiations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any immediate financial support schemes under consideration, including emergency tie-up support, resilience funding for processors or temporary diversification grants, to prevent long-term economic damage to Northern Ireland’s coastal communities.
answer_text: My Department recognises the significant economic pressures currently facing Northern Ireland’s coastal communities. The Marine Environment and Fisheries Fund 2025/26 provides financial support for projects that promote resilience and sustainability across these communities. This fund is designed to help mitigate immediate economic pressures while supporting longer-term diversification and adaptation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any actions being taken to ensure that scientific assessments for Nephrops and mackerel stocks incorporate region-specific data from the Irish Sea and northern waters, so that Northern Ireland vessels are not disadvantaged by broader international modelling; and (ii) his assessment as to whether ICES assessments accurately reflect local stock dynamics.
answer_text: (i) Scientific assessments for fisheries management of internationally shared stocks are undertaken by the International Council for the Exploration of the Sea (ICES), utilising both national and international data and expertise. Each stock assessment is based on survey and sampling data for that stock, and modelling undertaken by experts. The advisory process is subject to ongoing peer review and scrutiny to ensure the best scientific advice is provided. Each stock is subject to benchmarking, which uses the most up-to-date evidence and approach available. This approach would not be expected to disadvantage the Northern Ireland fleet.(ii) ICES assessments reflect the current data and information available for each stock. Unexpected or outlying outcomes are carefully scrutinised in the assessment process, and this may result in revision of advice or indeed revision of the assessment process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the UK Government's previous warnings about security risks associated with Chinese state-linked companies such as Huawei and ZTE, to outline any assessment she undertook before facilitating the China-Northern Ireland Economic, Trade and Investment Cooperation Forum involving representatives from those companies.
answer_text: The aim of the China–Northern Ireland Economic, Trade and Investment Cooperation Forum, was to promote the north as an attractive location for trade and investment. The Forum provided an important platform to strengthen economic ties and showcase our region’s capabilities to Chinese partners.At the Forum, a Strategic Cooperation Framework Memorandum of Understanding between Invest NI and the China Chamber of Commerce in the UK (CCCUK) was signed. This high-level, non-binding framework is designed to promote collaboration and explore opportunities for local businesses, reinforcing our commitment to building long-term partnerships and unlocking new avenues for growth.The north already has strong trade links with China, especially in advanced manufacturing, life and health sciences and agri-food, and I am keen to see these grow. We are also building on connections with local universities and working to encourage more visitors from China to experience our world-class tourism offer. I believe it is possible to maintain a strong and productive economic relationship with international partners, including China, while ensuring that we continue to advocate for values underpinned by an ethical approach to investment.It is worth noting the British Government resumed trade talks with China in September.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the combined economic impact on Northern Ireland’s fishing industry of the advised 70 per cent reduction in the mackerel Total Allowable Catch for 2026 and the 35 per cent reduction in Nephrops advised for the Irish Sea, particularly regarding (a) vessel viability; (b) crew employment; and (c) supply-chain stability; and (ii) whether he has met with fishermen to discuss these proposals.
answer_text: The overall economic impact of the potential quota reduction has been assessed and used to inform the Department’s negotiating position in engagement with Defra and negotiations with EU. The approach within these negotiations is to seek to balance the economic viability of the fleets with the long-term sustainability of the stocks. Officials have met with fishing industry representatives to assist them in building a robust socio-economic case and have supported them in presenting this case to the UK Government, while continuing to engage with stakeholders and UK counterparts to explore mitigation options ahead of final quota decisions. The fishing opportunities for mackerel and Irish Sea Nephrops are set following negotiations with other coastal states. These negotiations are ongoing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why the conversion of slurry into biomethane and digestate for use as fertiliser is described as innovative in the promotional material for the Catchment Action for Local Management programme, given that such systems have been in operation for many years in Northern Ireland and elsewhere.
answer_text: The Catchment Action for Local Management programme (CALM) is funded by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB) and is not administered by my Department. The East Border Region, a local authority-led cross-border group acts as the lead partner for CALM and is responsible for overall coordination and delivery of the project.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the simultaneous quota reductions across multiple key species, to detail any measures his Department will take to support small-to-medium Irish Sea vessels and Northern Ireland’s pelagic fleet.
answer_text: Fishing opportunities for the stocks of importance to the Northern Irish fleet are currently under negotiation. The approach to these negotiations is to seek to balance the economic viability of the fleets against the longer-term growth and recovery of the stocks. Any economic impact on the Northern Ireland fleet will not be fully apparent until the negotiations conclude. My officials are engaged in these negotiations in the setting of fishing opportunities on these shared stocks and I am monitoring progress closely. 		The current Marine Environment and Fisheries Fund provides opportunities for vessel owners and processors to seek support towards investments that will enhance their economic resilience to annual fluctuations in fishing opportunities. When the outcome of the current negotiations on quotas is known, my department will engage with stakeholders to determine what, if any, support can be provided to support industry in these challenging times.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with the UK Government in relation to the proposed 70 per cent reduction in the mackerel Total Allowable Catch; and (ii) any representations made to secure a fair and sustainable outcome for Northern Ireland’s pelagic sector.
answer_text: The overall economic impact of the potential quota reduction has been assessed and used to inform the Department’s negotiating position in engagement with Defra and negotiations with EU. The approach within these negotiations is to seek to balance the economic viability of the fleets with the long-term sustainability of the stocks. Officials have met with fishing industry representatives to assist them in building a robust socio-economic case and have supported them in presenting this case to the UK Government, while continuing to engage with stakeholders and UK counterparts to explore mitigation options ahead of final quota decisions. The fishing opportunities for mackerel and Irish Sea Nephrops are set following negotiations with other coastal states. These negotiations are ongoing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to place the full text of the Strategic Cooperation Framework Agreement between Invest NI and the China Chamber of Commerce in the UK in the Assembly library.
answer_text: Invest NI signed a high-level, legally non-binding Strategic Cooperation Framework Memorandum of Understanding with the China Chamber of Commerce in Britain. This is entirely within its remit acting in the furtherance of the trade interests of the north.A copy of the document is available in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 34793/22-27 and AQW 34796/22-27, to publish the memorandum of understanding.
answer_text: I have published the memorandum of understanding on my Department’s website at the following link:https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/publications/memorandum-understanding-between-center-language-education-and-cooperation-and-department-economy-cooperation-chinese-language-education

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission why the simultaneous translation of MLAs who choose to make their contribution in Irish in the Assembly Chamber is not featured on the internal TV system.
answer_text: When a contribution in the Assembly Chamber is made in Irish, the simultaneous interpretation into English is broadcast on channel 1 of the internal TV system and livestream 1 on NIAssembly.tv. While a simultaneous interpretation is being delivered, the volume of the spoken Irish is lowered to ensure that the English interpretation is clearly audible.Audio “as spoken” from the Chamber, without simultaneous interpretation, is broadcast on channel 2 on the internal TV system and livestream 2 on NIAssembly.tv.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the number of times that the services of a plumber has been required in Parliament Buildings since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, including (i) the reason for each call out; and (ii) the total cost to the public purse.
answer_text: Since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, plumbing services have been requested 92 times.  The majority of these relate to normal maintenance issues e.g. broken taps.It is not possible to provide a breakdown of each of these incidents and costs within the deadline given for the Priority Written Question.  However, I will arrange for more detail and an estimated overall cost to be provided when available and be placed within the library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 29695/22-27, to detail (i) why the Loughs Agency position differs from that of the North Atlantic Salmon Conservation Organization (NASCO), in stating that it does not consider stocking from hatcheries as a remediation measure, given Atlantic salmon are classified as endangered and NASCO has stated that support for stocking may be beneficial where the wild Atlantic salmon population has been extirpated or is at immediate risk of extirpation, with scientific evidence showing success when stocking is used alongsid
answer_text: Loughs Agency has reiterated that its position on the use of hatcheries for salmon conservation is exactly aligned with NASCO’s stating “where integrity (i.e. evolutionary and ecological naturalness) of the wild stock is a management priority, stocking should not be considered as a remediation measure”. The Agency’s evidence-base shows that none of the stocks in the Foyle and Carlingford catchments are at immediate risk of extirpation (in that spawning activity and the presence of juveniles still exist) and therefore the integrity of the wild stock is the management priority. Yes, my Department was consulted before the demolition of the hatchery. NASCO reviewed its guidelines for salmon stocking recently and the scientific evidence reviewed shows that the risks associated with the stocking of Atlantic salmon from hatcheries generally outweigh any benefits except in some specific circumstances such as stocks that are at or close to extirpation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 31192/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 31192/22-27 was answered on 30 September 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of staff in his Department that currently work from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years.
answer_text: The revised NICS Hybrid Working Policy came into effect on 1 November 2025. Staff who wish to participate in hybrid working must attend a recognised workplace for a minimum of 40% of their contracted hours each week. Those who can avail of hybrid working combine office and home working in alignment with business need and must request their work pattern by completing a Workstyle Agreement that is agreed by their line manager.  Some job roles require staff to attend their designated workplace more frequently or every day.  Staff attendance is assured by their line managers.Data metrics for office attendance are not currently available as the renewed policy has just gone live. Colleagues in NICSHR will be developing Management Information to show the number of staff with Workstyle Agreements in place.My department does not hold historical office attendance records.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to deliver an uplift in pay for care workers and restore morale in the workforce.
answer_text: I remain fully committed to resolving pay issues across the social care sector and to funding the Real Living Wage (RLW) at the earliest affordable opportunity.Unfortunately, I do not currently have the capacity within my budget to deliver this commitment in the current financial year. It will, however, be a priority for me in planning expenditure for 2026–27. When this uplift becomes possible, I intend to ensure that funding is allocated transparently, with clear timing and ringfencing for the pay uplift.I recognise this will be disappointing for many but my officials will continue to engage with independent sector organisations, which are integral to the delivery of care across Health and Social Care services and the wider programme of social care reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the sinking of a second Loughs Agency boat at Foyle Marina, to detail (i) whether the Loughs Agency will receive public funding for a replacement vessel; (ii) the dates that routine checks were undertaken to prevent incidents such as this happening; and (iii) whether any member of (a) staff; and (b) senior management have been suspended, pending investigation, in relation to this matter.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that it is awaiting the outcome of the insurer’s report. Any decision to repair or replace the vessel will be considered by the Agency based on operational need.From 13 January 2025 to Wednesday 10 November 2025, the Agency carried out 53 routine inspections on its vessels. These inspections were conducted on the following dates: 13th, 20th, 29th January 2025;5th, 10th, 11th, 18th, 25th February 2025;10th, 13th, 18th, 27th, 31st March 2025;10th, 14th, 15th, 17th, 18th, 30th April 2025; 7th, 8th, 9th, 14th, 15th, 19th, 22nd May 2025;5th, 9th, 11th, 19th June 2025;2nd, 7th, 10th, 15th, 22nd, 24th July 2025; 6th, 13th, 14th, 20th, 26th August 2025;5th, 9th, 16th, 22nd, 25th September 2025; 2nd, 6th, 23rd, 27th October 2025; and5th, 7th, 10th November 2025.The Agency has advised that no staff or senior management have been suspended, pending investigation, in relation to this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the statutory basis on which the PSNI commissioned the Operation Denton report; (ii) how this aligns with Section 32 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1998; and (iii) whether the full findings of the review will be published.
answer_text: PSNI’s cooperation with Operation Denton is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of staff in his Department's arm's-length bodies that currently work from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of staff in her Department that currently work from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years.
answer_text: DfE does not hold this information centrally. Staff were advised by the Permanent Secretary in June 2022 that it was safe to return to the office (post covid pandemic) and that they could avail of the NICS Hybrid Working Policy at that time.  This was managed at local level (both branch and division) depending on business need.A revised Hybrid Working Policy was implemented on 31 October 2025. This continues to be managed locally depending on business need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of staff in her Department that currently work from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years.
answer_text: Staff in my Department, whose roles allow, can apply to work from home under the NICS Hybrid Working Policy.Many staff across my Department deliver front line services which cannot, by their nature, be delivered from home.While line managers closely monitor the location of staff who can avail of hybrid working, the Department does not hold historic information on the number of staff who are working from home on a full or part-time basis centrally. This information could not be obtained without disproportionate costs.However, following the implementation of the revised NICS Hybrid Working Policy from 31 October 2025, standardised monitoring and reporting has been introduced and reports will be provided to departments biannually.  This will allow the Department of Finance to provide more detailed information in the future, which will include the number of staff availing of the NICS Hybrid Working Policy.  As the revised policy took effect on 31 October, this information will be provided for the first time at the end of the 2025-26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of staff in his Department that currently work from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years.
answer_text: Hybrid working in the Civil Service is a locally agreed, noncontractual arrangement that allows a mix of workplace, home, and remote working, subject to business needs. Staff wishing to work in this way complete a Work Style Agreement (WSA), which sets out the agreed split and is approved by their line manager.Following implementation of a revised Hybrid Working Policy on 31 October 2025, standardised monitoring and reporting has been introduced. Data for earlier years is not available.The Department of Finance provides departments with a biannual suite of workforce information, which will include the number of agreed WSAs. As the revised policy took effect on 31 October, this information will be reported at the end of the 2025/26 financial year.

answer_text: Staff within my Department’s arm’s-length bodies have the opportunity to avail of their respective employer’s agile/hybrid working arrangements which is determined by the needs of the role which they undertake.Between each organisation, the definition of agile/hybrid working varies slightly however each arm’s-length body’s policy requires staff to regularly attend offices or schools (if on school-facing duties).My ALBs do not collect the information requested, with the exception of the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI),The number of GTCNI staff currently working from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years is included in the table below:DateNumber of GTCNI staff working from home all weekNumber of GTCNI staff working from home part of the week01/11/202501401/11/202401501/11/202301401/11/202201801/11/20218001/11/202013001/11/201900

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of staff in his Department that currently work from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years.
answer_text: Hybrid working in the Northern Ireland Civil Service is a locally agreed, noncontractual arrangement that allows a mix of workplace, home, and remote working in certain roles, subject to business needs. Staff in posts that are suitable for hybrid working who wish to avail of the arrangement must complete a Work Style Agreement (WSA), which sets out the agreed split which must be approved by their line manager.Following implementation of a revised Hybrid Working Policy across the NICS on 31 October 2025, standardised monitoring and reporting has been introduced. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of abortions performed in each Health and Social Care Trust since the change in Northern Ireland’s abortion laws, broken down by reason.
answer_text: The Department publishes an annual report on abortions carried out in Northern Ireland which is broken down by Health and Social Care Trust and statutory grounds for abortion.  The most recent publication on Northern Ireland abortion data was released on 30 January 2025 and can be accessed at the following link:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-abortion-statistics-202324.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of staff in his Department that currently work from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years.
answer_text: All NICS DoH staff operate in line with the NICS Hybrid Working Policy which requires staff to attend their designated workplace for at least 40% of their contractual working hours each week. Senior Civil Servants are encouraged to exceed this minimum requirement.  DoH staff in the Strategic Planning and Performance Group operate as per the Business Services Organisation Hybrid Working Scheme, which requires staff to be in their designated office three out of five days. These working policies are managed at a local level, and my department does not hold this information centrally.

answer_text: The Northern Ireland annual abortion statistics for 2024/2025 will be published on 2 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) who is legally responsible for the repeated flooding on Malacoo Road, Portadown; and (ii) any action her Department and its agencies has taken to address this matter.
answer_text: My Department has a responsibility to manage surface water which falls onto a road.  The owner or occupier of land adjoining a road is responsible for ensuring that surface water from their land does not flow onto or over the road. Similarly, landowners have a responsibility to ensure that drainage from a road is not impeded by a lack of maintenance of ‘drains’ within their property.The flooding at Marlacoo Road is the result of a lack of maintenance on an undesignated watercourse which is the responsibility of riparian landowners. The Department has investigated and engaged with several landowners to request maintenance of the undesignated watercourse to provide an outlet for the road drainage which is impacted. Some landowners have completed maintenance, whilst others have shown willingness to proceed, however, difficulties remain in securing agreement from all riparian landowners.Should agreement not be reached with all landowners, the Department may commence enforcement proceedings under schedule 5 of the Drainage (Northern Ireland) Order 1973. Schedule 5 of this Order applies to undesignated watercourses only and outlines the requirement for riparian “occupiers” to carry out such work as is necessary to maintain the efficiency of a watercourse for the due discharge of any water flowing into it.
