question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail when the tomb of Lord and Lady Craigavon was most recently cleaned and the grounds around it maintained.
answer_text: I can confirm that the tomb of Lord and Lady Craigavon underwent specialist conservation cleaning in spring 2019. The surrounding grounds were cleaned and maintained on Friday 7 November 2025 and Monday 10 November 2025, in preparation for the Remembrance events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of staff in her Department that currently work from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years.
answer_text: Hybrid working in the Northern Ireland Civil Service is a locally agreed, noncontractual arrangement that allows a mix of workplace, home, and remote working, subject to business needs.  Staff wishing to work in this way complete a Work Style Agreement (WSA) which sets out the agreed split and is approved by their line manager.Following implementation of a revised Hybrid Working Policy on 31 October 2025, standardised monitoring and reporting has been introduced. Data for earlier years is not available.The Department of Finance provides departments with a biannual suite of workforce information, which will include the number of agreed WSAs. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of staff in their Department that currently work from home (a) all week; and (b) part of the week; and (ii) how this compares to the figures on 1 November in each of the last six years.
answer_text: The most recent staffing figures for month ending October 2025 reflect a TEO staffing compliment of 468 staff, including 28 agency workers. Of this, 461 members of staff operate hybrid working arrangements, working from home part of the week, while 6 members of staff are office based on a full-time basis. While no substantive TEO staff work from home all week every week, one agency member of staff works from home on a full-time basis (contracted for three days per week). This is a short, fixed-term arrangement to deliver a piece of work requiring specialist skills. We are unable to provide comparative figures on 1 November for each of the last six years, as this information is not held.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of drug-related deaths in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: ANSWEROfficial statistics on death registration numbers are produced by the NISRA, using registration data collected by the General Register Office (GRO). The number of drug-related deaths by local government district are published annually and can be found in Table 9a in the Drug Related Deaths in NI Report. Please note that geographical location is based on the usual address of the deceased, or in the absence of this it is based on location of death. It is possible that address and place of death fall into different districts.Drug Related Deaths are defined using the definitions, detailed on the NISRA website Drug-Related Deaths | Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of death certificates that have been issued for babies which were born alive after an abortion procedure.
answer_text: The introduction of The Abortion (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2020, which came into force on 31st March 2020, allows for medical abortions for pregnancies up to 24 weeks (and over 24 weeks in limited circumstances), and surgical abortions up to 21 weeks and six days. Performing a search of death registrations for ‘termination of pregnancy, affecting fetus and newborn’ (ICD-10 code P96.4), for the period since the introduction of the Regulations, shows there have been 32 deaths registered here up to the end of 2024 where this cause was mentioned anywhere on the death certificate i.e. where this was either a contributory or underlying cause of death.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current status of the rates bill relating to the office of the former MP for North Antrim.
answer_text: I understand your question relates to the property at 9-11 Church Street, Ballymena. I can confirm that an outstanding balance of rates remains payable and is currently being pursued through ongoing rate collection processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when her Department first became aware that the disabled bay on High Street, Ballymoney, was operational without legislative backing; and (ii) the immediate action that was taken upon that realisation.
answer_text: The Public Realm works undertaken in Ballymoney, including the installation of disabled and other parking bays, were led by the Causeway Coast and Glens Council and funded by the Department for Communities.  This work removed the previously marked kerblines, making the existing restrictions obsolete. Since then, my department has been working to bring forward the associated legislative changes to align the parking restrictions to the new layout.  Unfortunately, due to the time-consuming nature of legislation in this area, and resources being diverted to other high priority work such as traffic management for the recent 2025 Open Championship, work has not proceeded as quickly as anticipated.  However, my department is working to complete the required legislation as soon as possible.I can assure the member that normal practice on schemes led by my department ensures that the necessary legislation is enacted prior to any markings being put in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) disabled parking bays; and (ii) standard parking bays, that have been installed and marked but cannot currently be enforced due to the absence of the requisite secondary legislation.
answer_text: The following standard and disabled parking bays have been installed and marked but are not currently enforceable due to the absence of the requisite secondary legislation: 	Regent Street, Newtownards – 4 Disabled baysHigh Street, Ballymoney – 1 Disabled bay and Limited waiting area which is not individually marked and not quantifiable. As part of an environmental improvement scheme in Banbridge, several parking areas have been amended, these are not yet enforceable.  When notified, the Parking Enforcement Unit will commence a period of advisory enforcement, during which Traffic Attendants will monitor the locations and engage with motorists to encourage voluntary compliance. This approach helps raise awareness and supports responsible parking behaviour ahead of full enforcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a timeline for (i) when the necessary legislation will be made to regularise the disabled and other parking bays on High Street, Ballymoney; and (ii) when all similar locations across Northern Ireland will be brought into legislative compliance.
answer_text: The Public Realm works undertaken in Ballymoney, including the installation of disabled and other parking bays, were led by the Causeway Coast and Glens Council and funded by the Department for Communities.  This work removed the previously marked kerblines, making the existing restrictions obsolete. Since then, my department has been working to bring forward the associated legislative changes to align the parking restrictions to the new layout. Unfortunately, due to the time-consuming nature of legislation in this area, and resources being diverted to other high priority work such as traffic management for the recent 2025 Open Championship, work has not proceeded as quickly as anticipated.  It is anticipated that this work will be completed by Summer 2026.I can assure the member that normal practice on schemes led by my department ensures that the necessary legislation is enacted prior to any markings being put in place, permitting immediate enforcement of the location. However, where disparities such as those in High Street, Ballymoney arise, my officials endeavour to progress the necessary changes as quickly as possible to regularise enforcement action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to publish a list of locations where parking bays have been installed and marked but remain unenforceable pending legislative completion.
answer_text: The following standard and disabled parking bays have been installed and marked but are not currently enforceable due to the absence of the requisite secondary legislation: 	Regent Street, Newtownards – 4 Disabled baysHigh Street, Ballymoney – 1 Disabled bay and Limited waiting area which is not individually marked and not quantifiable. As part of an environmental improvement scheme in Banbridge, several parking areas have been amended, these are not yet enforceable.  When notified, the Parking Enforcement Unit will commence a period of advisory enforcement, during which Traffic Attendants will monitor the locations and engage with motorists to encourage voluntary compliance. This approach helps raise awareness and supports responsible parking behaviour ahead of full enforcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any interim measures being taken to ensure disabled motorists are not disadvantaged in locations where disabled bays are installed but cannot be enforced.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to enhancing accessibility and supporting disabled motorists in travelling safely and with dignity.Blue Badge holders have the right to park in various restricted areas, including on-street pay-and-display bays and limited waiting zones, as outlined in the Blue Badge scheme guidance. These provisions help ensure that disabled motorists can access essential services and destinations. If a disabled bay cannot be enforced due to the absence of legislation, then this right is not infringed whatsoever. Further details on the rights and responsibilities of Blue badge holders can be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/rights-and-responsibilities-blue-badge-holders

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of occasions (i) police officers have been asked to remove their sidearms by Sinn Féin representatives; and (ii) a request of this nature has been complied with, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Policies and procedures relating to firearms is an operational matter for the PSNI which is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the oversight role of Policing Board, it would not be appropriate for me to comment.You may, therefore, wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable.In the meantime, I would draw your attention to the Chief Constable’s public statement on this matter on 6 November, in which he stated that relevant District Commanders had reported no instances of officers being asked to remove their personal protection weapons, and that “discussing any matter relating to the protection of officers, including the carriage of their personal protective weapons, in a public arena is unhelpful.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the total savings arising from the decision to cease providing hot meals to people serving on a jury.
answer_text: Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, jurors may have been provided with a sandwich lunch or hot meal for longer trials. During the pandemic, the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS), in consultation with the Public Health Agency, implemented a range of measures designed to protect all court users and mitigate against the risk of disease. One of the measures implemented at that time was to cease providing jurors with a meal and to replace this with a meal allowance. Similar arrangements were put in place and also remain in England & Wales.Cost figures for hot meals are not separately available. Given the unpredictable nature of court business, previous arrangements often resulted in significant amounts of food having to be discarded. The current arrangements, by which jurors are provided with tea and coffee making facilities and claim a meal allowance when appropriate, reduces the amount of food waste.Each year NICTS supports approximately 9,000 jurors and in the last three years, a total of three complaints have been received via the NICTS complaints process about the provision of food for jurors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the evidence used in deciding that Invest NI should adopt a unique policy position toward Israel.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) who authorised the installation and marking of disabled and other parking bays on High Street, Ballymoney, prior to legislative provision being made; and (ii) any procedures in place to prevent such action occurring in advance of legal authority.
answer_text: The Public Realm works undertaken in Ballymoney, including the installation of disabled and other parking bays, were led by the Causeway Coast and Glens Council and funded by the Department for Communities.  My department has been working to bring forward the associated legislative changes to align the parking restrictions to the new layout.  Unfortunately, due to the time-consuming nature of legislation in this area, and resources being diverted to other high priority work such as traffic management for the recent 2025 Open Championship, work has not proceeded as quickly as anticipated.  However, my department is working to complete the required legislation as soon as possible.I can assure the member that normal practice on schemes led by my department ensures that the necessary legislation is enacted prior to any markings being put in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why disabled and other parking bays were installed and marked on High Street, Ballymoney, before completion of the necessary secondary legislation to enable enforcement.
answer_text: The Public Realm works undertaken in Ballymoney, including the installation of disabled and other parking bays, were led by the Causeway Coast and Glens Council and funded by the Department for Communities.  My department has been working to bring forward the associated legislative changes to align the parking restrictions to the new layout.  Unfortunately, due to the time-consuming nature of legislation in this area, and resources being diverted to other high priority work such as traffic management for the recent 2025 Open Championship, work has not proceeded as quickly as anticipated.  However, my department is working to complete the required legislation as soon as possible.I can assure the member that normal practice on schemes led by my department ensures that the necessary legislation is enacted prior to any markings being put in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to reintroduce hot meals for people serving on a jury.
answer_text: The provision of jury meals is an administrative matter managed at operational level by the NI Courts and Tribunals Service. Given the unpredictable nature of court business, previous arrangements often resulted in significant amounts of food having to be discarded. The current arrangements, by which jurors are provided with tea and coffee making facilities and claim a meal allowance when appropriate, reduces the amount of food waste.Each year NICTS supports approximately 9,000 jurors and there is little evidence that the current arrangements, which are explained in advance, are not working for jurors or that there are significant issues. This is evidenced by the fact that, in the last 3 years, a total of 3 complaints have been received via the NICTS complaints process about the provision of food for jurors.Jury facilities will be considered as part of the implementation of the NICTS Estate Strategy. NICTS recently launched a survey on the quality of court buildings and facilities. This includes jury facilities and will help inform future plans.In the meantime, NICTS remains committed to the substantive work of delivering justice via high quality court and tribunal services. Senior officials being forced to spend their time answering administrative queries about food provision detracts from this mission and does nothing to support the needs of victims and witnesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any agreement, memorandum of understanding or other formal arrangement was entered into by her Department, or any of its arm’s-length bodies, with the Vice Minister of Education of the People’s Republic of China, Mr Wu Yan, or any representatives of the People’s Republic of China, during his visit to Northern Ireland in 2025.
answer_text: I met with Professor Wu Yan, Vice Minister for the Ministry of Education in China, in May this year.  Three officials from my department were in attendance at the meeting. During the meeting I signed a memorandum of understanding with the Chinese Center for Language Education and Cooperation with the purpose of facilitating support for the Confucius Institute at Ulster University.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration given to placing a mandatory housing order on all poultry.
answer_text: Mandatory housing measures for all kept birds and poultry and a ban on certain bird gatherings came into force in Northern Ireland at 00:01 on Thursday 6 November 2025.These measures are an additional requirement of the Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) that was introduced on 1 November 2025.Further details can be found on the DAERA website at the link below.https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/avian-influenza-prevention-zone-aipz
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any expenditure on external recruitment consultancy fees or advisory body fees in the recruitment process of the Irish Language Commissioner.
answer_text: TEO’s external recruitment is provided through the NICS contract with HR Connect, which hard charges for work on public appointments.  The charge in this case was approximately £5,100.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to publish the (i) job description; (ii) person specification; and (iii) assessment scoring matrix used in the recruitment of the Irish Language Commissioner.
answer_text: The job description and person specification were published in the Candidate Information Booklet (CIB) for the appointment of the Irish Language Commissioner, a copy of which has been placed into the Assembly Library.The CIB also details that candidates undertaking the Irish Language Proficiency Assessment would be expected to demonstrate C2 level, as per the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the locations of the three public events referenced in his ministerial statement on the outcome of the review of environmental governance in Northern Ireland on 4 November 2025.
answer_text: The three public events took place at the following dates and locations:	6 February 2025 – Queen’s University, Belfast	18 February 2025 – Loughry College, Cookstown	27 February 2025 – Online via Microsoft Teams		Further details on stakeholder engagements undertaken as part of the review are included in the final report which is available on the DAERA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of applicants for the post of Irish Language Commissioner; (ii) the number of people shortlisted; and (iii) the basis on which candidates were (a) excluded; and (b) advanced at each stage.
answer_text: Eleven applications were received for the post of Irish Language Commissioner.A sift was then carried out to assess the applications against the published essential criteria as set out in the candidate information booklet. Four applicants met the required standard and were invited to the language proficiency assessment.  One of the four applicants subsequently withdrew from the process prior to attending the assessment.The remaining three applicants attended the language proficiency assessment, which was designed to evaluate their proficiency in Irish at the level required for the post.  Of the three applicants who attended the language proficiency assessment, two achieved the required standard and were invited to interview.  The information sought in part 3 (a) and (b) of the question is contained within the Candidate Information Booklet for the appointment of the Irish Language Commissioner, a copy of which has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the (i) names; and (ii) roles of the panel members involved in the shortlisting and interview process for the Irish Language Commissioner.
answer_text: The interview panel consisted of a member of the stakeholder community, two senior NICS officials and an Independent Assessor allocated by the Commissioner for Public Appointments (CPANI):Richard Irwin, G5, Good Relations and Inclusion Directorate, TEO; Iain Greenway, G5, Department for Communities, Engaged Communities Group (ECG); Seán Ó Coinn, CEO of Foras Na Gaeilge (recused);Professor Malachy O'Neill, Professor of Irish at Ulster University (replaced Seán Ó Coinn); andBronagh Hinds, Independent Assessor for CPANI.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 34080/22-27, when this issue first came to the attention of the Assembly Commission.
answer_text: Assembly Commission staff were first made aware of the recent incidents of flooding in the toilets on 14 October 2025.  Since that date, staff are closely monitoring the situation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the funding allocated to victims groups in each of the last ten years, broken down by group.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the recruitment process used for the appointment of the Irish Language Commissioner, including (i) the criteria applied; (ii) who approved the criteria; and (iii) how merit was assessed.
answer_text: Information regarding the selection process for the Irish Language Commissioner was included in the competition Candidate Information Booklet (CIB). The selection process included screening and a sift of applications, an Irish Language proficiency assessment, a presentation and interview. Suitable appointees then provided presentations to us. The CIB was approved by us. The information sought in this question, at parts (i) and (ii), is contained in the CIB for the appointment of the Irish Language Commissioner, a copy of which has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of his oral ministerial statement on Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMO), and given the differential and inconsistent approaches adopted by local councils in relation to HMO licences and investigation and enforcement, whether he will exercise his powers under section 117 of the Local Government (Northern Ireland) Act 2014 to commence the process of issuing guidance to local councils in respect of the approach which ought to be adopted in respect of the granting, investigation and enforcement of HMOs across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has already produced guidance for councils on implementing the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016. Councils may, of course, exercise their discretion to develop their own policies for HMOs, including setting limits on the percentage of HMOs within specific localities. This will result in a different approach in different areas.  While these policies fall outside the remit of my Department, I took the opportunity in my letter to council Chief Executives, regarding council responsibilities for HMOs, to remind them of this important tool for managing this type of housing within their areas.

answer_text: Loughs Agency has confirmed that all relevant reports commissioned to look at the TREES project were included in the previous answer to AQW 31577/22-27. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of occasions where a patient has had to lie on the floor in a hospital setting in each of the last 12 months, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: HSC Trusts have confirmed this is not data they collect on an information system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average ambulance down time at each ambulance station in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: Table 1 shows the average and longest time that an ambulance waited at each Type 1 emergency department (ED) in the last 12 months.Table 1 HospitalAverage ambulance waiting time / down timeLongest ambulance waiting time / down timeAltnagelvin01:09:0114:55:04Antrim01:25:3125:15:05Causeway01:41:1818:59:39Craigavon01:42:2818:48:56Daisyhill00:59:1011:41:00Mater00:51:3216:09:28RBHSC00:24:2903:57:20Royal Victoria01:19:5514:48:38South West Acute 00:51:4409:34:00Ulster02:01:2523:36:16Reducing ambulance handover delays at EDs is a key priority due to the impact on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s (NIAS) ability to respond to calls in the community. The importance of timely ambulance responses was highlighted in the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine.There is a need to eradicate the very long waits outside EDs and the data in table one reflects the longest wait on one day only in the year. The average handover time is a more reliable indicator of performance.Earlier this year, my Departmental officials, alongside clinicians from Trusts and NIAS, visited a hospital in London to learn from their approach to promptly releasing ambulances from EDs. Following this visit, Trusts and NIAS were asked to adopt a similar model.At a regional workshop in August 2025, the Departmental Permanent Secretary, supported by the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, endorsed a plan to eradicate the very long delays for ambulance handover. Performance trajectories were agreed for Phase 1, with incremental improvement from September through to December 2025. By 1 December, ambulance handover delays should not exceed two hours. Ambulance handover guidance, based on a similar approach to that adopted in London has been developed by my Department, working with NIAS and provider Trusts. The target for October was that no ambulance handover should exceed three hours. There have been some notable improvements across Trusts, especially at the Royal Victoria, Mater, Altnagelvin, South Western Acute and Daisy Hill hospitals, where ambulance handover delays exceeding three hours were less than 5% of total arrivals. Whilst performance levels at the Antrim, Craigavon and Ulster hospitals have not yet reached the required standards, there are signs of improvement at these sites.Ambulance handover delays are monitored daily, including average and longest delays at each hospital. This data is shared widely across the health and social care system, including with Trust chief executives as part of the HSC Support and Intervention Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the longest ambulance down time at each ambulance station in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: Table 1 shows the average and longest time that an ambulance waited at each Type 1 emergency department (ED) in the last 12 months.Table 1 HospitalAverage ambulance waiting time / down timeLongest ambulance waiting time / down timeAltnagelvin01:09:0114:55:04Antrim01:25:3125:15:05Causeway01:41:1818:59:39Craigavon01:42:2818:48:56Daisyhill00:59:1011:41:00Mater00:51:3216:09:28RBHSC00:24:2903:57:20Royal Victoria01:19:5514:48:38South West Acute 00:51:4409:34:00Ulster02:01:2523:36:16Reducing ambulance handover delays at EDs is a key priority due to the impact on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s (NIAS) ability to respond to calls in the community. The importance of timely ambulance responses was highlighted in the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine.There is a need to eradicate the very long waits outside EDs and the data in table one reflects the longest wait on one day only in the year. The average handover time is a more reliable indicator of performance.Earlier this year, my Departmental officials, alongside clinicians from Trusts and NIAS, visited a hospital in London to learn from their approach to promptly releasing ambulances from EDs. Following this visit, Trusts and NIAS were asked to adopt a similar model.At a regional workshop in August 2025, the Departmental Permanent Secretary, supported by the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, endorsed a plan to eradicate the very long delays for ambulance handover. Performance trajectories were agreed for Phase 1, with incremental improvement from September through to December 2025. By 1 December, ambulance handover delays should not exceed two hours. Ambulance handover guidance, based on a similar approach to that adopted in London has been developed by my Department, working with NIAS and provider Trusts. The target for October was that no ambulance handover should exceed three hours. There have been some notable improvements across Trusts, especially at the Royal Victoria, Mater, Altnagelvin, South Western Acute and Daisy Hill hospitals, where ambulance handover delays exceeding three hours were less than 5% of total arrivals. Whilst performance levels at the Antrim, Craigavon and Ulster hospitals have not yet reached the required standards, there are signs of improvement at these sites.Ambulance handover delays are monitored daily, including average and longest delays at each hospital. This data is shared widely across the health and social care system, including with Trust chief executives as part of the HSC Support and Intervention Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the taxi bill for (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The taxi bill for (i) my Department and (ii) each of its arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years, is set out in the tables below.Department of Health £k2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/250.70.31.52.12.5Arm’s Length Bodies £m2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/2569111110The use of taxis by Health and Social Care Trusts is planned and monitored and taxis are only used when it is not economically viable to use Trust transport for vulnerable patients and clients, particularly children in care and adults with a physical disability or learning disability.  There was a notable increase in spend on public taxis in 2022/23 and 2023/24 by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) due largely to reduced capacity of the Voluntary Car Service (VCS), with a significant loss of Voluntary Car Service (VCS) capacity throughout the COVID Pandemic as not all Volunteer Drivers who were stood down at the outset, returned to the service.  NIAS ran a very successful VCS recruitment campaign to improve the position which is currently being reinvigorated to enhance partnership working with the voluntary sector.	The continued HSC system wide pressures and the associated deteriorating position with Ambulance Handover Delays at Emergency Departments has also resulted in a significant loss of operational capacity to respond to emergency calls. This increased the reliance on taxis for non-emergency work in 2022/23 and 2023/24. NIAS is continuing with plans to reduce the use of this modality of conveyance and is engaged with DoH colleagues regarding the development of eligibility criteria for non-emergency transport.Reducing the use of Taxis and Independent Ambulance Service providers remains the strategic direction of NIAS who have been engaging with colleagues in DoH in relation to the review of the Transport Strategy including the eligibility criteria. Taxi use by NIBTS is for the transportation of blood products, blood- donors and staff.NIFRS does not have an account with any Taxi firms.  The use of taxis is not normal practice in NIFRS.As my department continues to drive efficiencies and savings across our ALBs the utilisation of taxis will be closely monitored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32447/22-27, whether he will now gather the information.
answer_text: I do not intend to gather the information. This is an operational responsibility for school management.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of whether the absence of an agreed definition of a woman has any impact on groups awarded funding through the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Local Change Fund.
answer_text: We remain committed to doing everything we can to create a society where women and girls are free from all forms of gender-based violence, abuse and harm. The Local Change Fund supports a wide range of groups including sports organisations, schools, businesses, youth groups and the arts sector. This activity is being delivered across all 11 Councils. Crucially, the activities delivered through the local change fund are creating awareness of this issue. We are also working in partnership with local councils to help ensure grassroot groups across cities, towns, villages and rural areas are equipped to prevent and respond to violence against women and girls.This aligns with the Strategic Framework, which places prevention at its core.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average waiting time for patients with rheumatoid arthritis to have their regular six monthly appointment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount of money that has been saved on electricity by the Causeway Hospital since solar panels were installed; and (ii) the cost of installing the solar panels.

To ask the Minister for the Economy, given that the Oil Firing Technical Association and the UK and Ireland Fuel Distributors Association have demonstrated the use of Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil in 150 domestic and non-domestic properties without modifications to existing heating systems, to detail any assessment his Department has made of Northern Ireland’s infrastructure, trained workforce and regulatory framework to support the widespread adoption of renewable heating fuel blends.
answer_text: Earlier this year, following a period of stakeholder engagement, my Department carried out call for evidence to assess the role liquid biofuels, including HVO, could play in moving households and businesses away from fossil fuels for heating. This work is progressing through the final stages of analysis and evaluation prior to publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions that have taken place in relation to upgrading the infrastructure for the new build for Cullybackey College.
answer_text: Departmental officials have been involved in discussions with officials from the Education Authority on proposals for a new build Cullybackey College for a number of years.  These discussions have been related to access and transportation infrastructure. An application such as this one would be submitted to the planning authority at Mid and East Antrim Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build for Cullybackey College.
answer_text: The project is progressing through the design stages. The current programme estimates the construction of the new build school will commence in May 2027, however this will be dependent on a number of factors including all relevant statutory approvals being granted, site acquisitions being completed and funding being available for construction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of research indicating that households using heating oil incur lower running costs than those using natural gas or heat pumps, to detail any measures his Department will take to ensure that decarbonisation policies do not disproportionately increase costs for low and middle-income households.
answer_text: My Department will ensure that decarbonisation policies do not disproportionately increase costs for low and middle-income households by taking account of the Just Transition principle, as required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  The current Affordable Warmth Scheme and the replacement Warm Healthy Homes Scheme, which is currently being developed, take account of the Just Transition principle by supporting low-income households to install energy efficiency measures in their homes, thereby helping to lower energy bills.The policy for domestic heating falls under the remit of the Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will assess the potential implementation of a renewable liquid heating fuel obligation, including (i) his Department’s estimates for the resulting carbon savings in tonnes per year; and (ii) potential contribution to the 2030 climate targets outlined in the draft Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan.
answer_text: As Minister for the Department for Communities, I have overall responsibility for the Residential Buildings sector chapter within the draft Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan.  However, while the chapter contains policies and proposals to reduce emissions within the Residential Buildings sector, many of these fall to other Departments to deliver.  The question of assessing the potential for introducing a Renewable Liquid Heating Fuel Obligation, including estimates for resulting carbon savings and potential contribution to the 2030 climate targets outlined in the draft Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan, would be for the Minister for the Economy to answer, as this falls within her remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31905/22-27, how, without involving the Northern Ireland Audit Office, he will ensure a transparent and impartial investigation of (i) how the Loughs Agency was able to recruit unapproved staff members without approval; (ii) how those salaries and pension contributions were paid; and (iii) whether there were any breaches of the IBO in respect of staffing numbers and payment of pension contributions.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 31905/22-27, my officials are considering the interpretation and application of the Loughs Agency’s staffing figures against its approved complement. At the current time we do not consider that an investigation is required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32561/22-27, why a conflict of interest was not considered, given that many of the groups are campaigners for transgenderism.
answer_text: As previously stated, the Patient and Client Council (PCC) supported the development of a balanced membership within the Gender Identity Service Review Group. This included ensuring that the voices of individuals with lived experience of transgender issues could continue to inform the review process.While some voluntary and community sector groups involved in the review may also engage in advocacy, their inclusion was based on their role in supporting service users and their families, and their ability to provide insight into the lived experience of accessing gender identity services.To ensure a balanced and objective approach, the review group also included a wide range of other perspectives, including clinical views from primary and secondary care, CAMHS personnel, professionals from medical and nursing fields, and officials from the Department of Health, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), and the Public Health Agency (PHA).Service Users contributions were considered valuable in helping the review group understand the challenges faced by service users and in shaping a more responsive and inclusive service.

answer_text: The following table details the number of occasions toilet facilities have been flooded in the last 12 months.Date Location 14 January 2025First Floor – Ladies toilets beside Room 144.19 May 2025Second Floor – Male Toilets beside Room 282.14 October 2025 Second Floor - Male Toilets beside Room 282. Two separate incidents recorded on this date.16 October 2025Second Floor – Male Toilets beside Room 282.20 October 2025Third Floor – Male Toilets beside Room 368.The Assembly Commission is extremely concerned at the number of these incidents, has informed all building users, increased surveillance across all floors and is keeping the matter under active review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the draft Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan for residential buildings primarily promotes electrification and a switch to natural gas, to detail any assessment his Department has undertaken of the potential for introducing a Renewable Liquid Heating Fuel Obligation, similar to the Renewable Transport Fuel Obligation, allowing a 20 per cent blend of Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil in domestic heating oil, in order to achieve immediate decarbonisation without upfront costs for households.
answer_text: As Minister for the Department for Communities, I have overall responsibility for the Residential Buildings sector chapter within the draft Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan.  However, the chapter contains a range of policies and proposals to reduce emissions within the Residential Buildings sector, many of which fall to other Departments to deliver.  The question of assessing the potential for introducing a Renewable Liquid Heating Fuel Obligation, would be for the Minister for the Economy to answer, as this falls within her remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32309/22-27, how he can provide an assessment of adherence to the policy given the stance outlined in his response to AQW 31661/22-27.
answer_text: This is an operational responsibility for school management, not my department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to publish the guidance issued to local councils on the allocation of funding under the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: Councils were required, as a condition of funding, to adhere to core components of EVAWG Local Grants Schemes. These core components were designed to ensure that the funding was aligned to the Framework, particularly the Prevention Outcomes which are: Outcome 1 - Changed Attitudes, Behaviours & Culture Everyone in society understands what violence against women and girls is, including its root causes, and plays an active role in preventing it.Outcome 2 - Healthy, Respectful RelationshipsEveryone in society is equipped and empowered to enjoy healthy, respectful relationships.Outcome 3 - Women and girls are safe and feel safe everywhereOrganisations and institutions across government and society embed the prevention of violence against women and girls in all that they do so that women and girls are safe and feel safe everywhere.Each Council published individual guidance on their specific Local Change Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, whether similar scrutiny will be applied to other countries.
answer_text: As set out in my written statement, I expect companies seeking grant support to confirm that they are not manufacturing arms or components for any country committing genocide.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any guidance from their Department in relation to the usage of funding provided to local councils under the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, to ensure all monies are used for the benefit of adult human females.
answer_text: The Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework is intended to address the full spectrum of gender-based violence against women and girls.  The funding provided to local Councils is aligned to the Framework. Councils were required, as a condition of funding, to adhere to core components of EVAWG Local Grants Schemes. These core components were designed to ensure that the funding was aligned to the Framework, particularly the Prevention Outcomes which are: Outcome 1 - Changed Attitudes, Behaviours & Culture Everyone in society understands what violence against women and girls is, including its root causes, and plays an active role in preventing it.Outcome 2 - Healthy, Respectful RelationshipsEveryone in society is equipped and empowered to enjoy healthy, respectful relationships.Outcome 3 - Women and girls are safe and feel safe everywhereOrganisations and institutions across government and society embed the prevention of violence against women and girls in all that they do so that women and girls are safe and feel safe everywhere.Each Council published individual guidance on their specific Local Change Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) average; and (ii) longest, wait time for women seeking treatment for gynaecological conditions, broken down by (a) Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) condition.
answer_text: The most recent official statistics for the total number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led Gynaecology outpatient appointment and the median and 95th percentile waiting times at quarter end June 2025, by Health and Social Care Trust, are given in Table 1 which follows.Table 1. The figures do not indicate completed waiting times or expected future waiting times.2 To avoid distortion due to long waits, the Department reports on the median (the ‘middle’ waiting time when all patients are ordered by length of time waiting) instead of mean, as an average of waiting times.3 The 95th percentile waiting time is the waiting time that 95% of patient waiting times are less than. One in twenty patients i.e. 5%, have been waiting longer than this time. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, to detail any assessment made of (i) the potential negative impact on Invest NI-supported companies that have legitimate business relationships with Israeli firms; and (ii) whether these companies could face unfair restrictions or reputational damage.
answer_text: The additional measures announced as part of my written statement on 16 October aim to ensure that grant support is not given to companies that manufacture arms or their components for countries committing genocide. Strong ethical safeguards help protect the reputations of local firms and therefore serve the interest of companies operating legitimately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, (i) why her Department has decided not to engage in British Government trade talks with Israel; and (ii) whether an assessment has been made as to whether this unilateral position risks harming Northern Ireland businesses and economic relations with a key international partner.
answer_text: We all have a moral duty to do what we can to prevent genocide and a responsibility to uphold international law. Ensuring that local companies are not assisting genocide protects our international reputation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether the new Ethical Investment Framework risks creating a bias against Israel; and (ii) how her Department will ensure that companies are not unfairly penalised for engaging with Israeli partners.
answer_text: (i) The Ethical Investment Framework is in development. It will be country neutral, and principles based, grounded in the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights. It will apply consistently across my Department and its core delivery partners in due course.(ii) Companies will continue to be assessed on a case-by-case basis, against clear criteria and through Invest NI due diligence processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, whether her Department sought input from the Israeli Government before preparing the additional measures outlined.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the Minister for the Economy sought the approval of the Executive for the contents of her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments.
answer_text: The Minister for the Economy has not referred the contents of her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October to the Executive for consideration.
