question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of occasions that a baby which was intended to be aborted was born alive since the change in Northern Ireland's abortion laws, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As outlined in my response to AQW 23073/22-27, this information is not collected by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the resources allocated to his Department’s work on the high-rise residential buildings fire safety policy, including any additional funding sought to accelerate progress.
answer_text: My departments Residential Building Safety Division (RBSD) is resourced as follows, 1 x Grade 5, 2 x Grade 7, 2 x Deputy Principals , 2 x Staff Officers, 1 x Executive Officer Grade 1 (Temporary Promotion) and 1 x Executive Officer Grade 2.As advised in my response to AQW 33653/22-27 my Department has no plans to expedite the development and introduction of a high-rise fire safety policy in the remainder of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate. Therefore, no additional funding has been sought to accelerate progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the number of staff from his Department, and its arm's-length bodies, that work in County Hall, Ballymena.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any steps he will take to reduce the reliance on temporary or term-time only contracts for classroom assistants; and (ii) whether he will set a target date to ensure that all classroom assistants have secure contracts.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is the employing authority for the majority of classroom assistants in schools across Northern Ireland. The EA acknowledges that the current one-to-one classroom assistant support model has resulted in a high number of classroom assistants employed using temporary contracts. To address this issue, the EA is currently taking forward a strategic review of the classroom assistant employment model, with a view to reducing the reliance on short-term temporary contracts and having a more responsive and flexible approach that provides the best possible solution for both pupils and staff.Given that the strategic review is a longer-term measure, the EA is proactively working in the short-term to support schools in reducing their reliance on temporary staff.  A new protocol has been introduced in the 2025/26 academic year which applies to any classroom assistant engaged on a temporary basis who has not had continuous service from the previous academic year. This requires the school to commence a formal recruitment process if there is an ongoing need for a post beyond a six-month period. To streamline the recruitment process, the EA has recently piloted a programme of regional recruitment for Special Educational Needs classroom assistants and this will now be rolled out across all of Northern Ireland. Successful candidates appointed through the formal recruitment process will be offered a choice of contract types, including all year round (52 week) contracts. In addition, the EA is planning to introduce a streamlined process to move long-term temporary classroom assistants onto permanent contracts of employment. This work will be taken forward over the coming months and it is anticipated that it will be completed by the end of March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to the amendment passed on 8 September 2025, (i) how he plans to ensure that classroom assistants receive (a) job security; (b) fair and consistent pay; (c) accredited training; and (d) defined career progression pathways; (ii) to provide clarity on the number of children currently without access to an appropriate placement for the 2025-26 academic year; and (iii) when he will publish timescales for the implementation and delivery of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.
answer_text: (i) The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the recent implementation of the EA’s Pay and Grading Review delivered pay increases for classroom assistants of between 5.3% and 14.4% and ensured all staff reach the top of their pay scale within three years. In line with the SEN Reform Agenda, the EA is progressing a strategic review of the classroom assistant employment model that will build on the pay and grading work carried out to date. As part of the TransformED Strategy, the Department has committed to supporting all education staff, including classroom assistants, in accessing and applying education research. In addition, the Department is actively working with local Higher Education Institutions to develop a part-time pathway into teaching. (ii) The EA has advised that as of 21 October 2025, they are not aware of any children with statements of SEN who remain unplaced for the 2025–26 academic year.  (iii) An indicative timescale of five years for implementation is included within the SEN Reform Delivery Plan published in February 2025.  This is predicated on additional investment in the region of £570 million.   I am not in a position to confirm timescales for full delivery in the absence of certainty about the funding position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all occasions since January 2020 on which her Department has instructed the removal of unauthorised signs, banners or billboards from departmental land, stating (i) the location; (ii) the reason for removal; and (iii) the outcome in each case.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain records of the number of signs, markings or advertisements removed and has not sought to recover costs in recent years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any steps his Department is taking to expedite the development and introduction of the high-rise residential buildings fire safety policy in the remainder of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: My officials are working at pace to expedite the development and introduction of a residential building safety policy for Northern Ireland.  Following a period of extensive research and a number of co-design workshops, policy proposals will shortly be presented to the Executive for its consideration prior to the commencement of a public consultation, which is due to commence early in the New Year.This is a complex, cross-cutting area and it is important that adequate time is given to ensure the development and delivery of a Residential Building Safety Bill that places the safety of people at its heart.  The current timescale anticipates the introduction of the Bill to the Assembly early in the next mandate, and given the substantive amount of work involved I cannot see this being shortened to allow its introduction during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the reasons for the delay in progressing the high-rise residential buildings fire safety legislation in Northern Ireland, particularly when compared to the implementation of similar legislation in Great Britain in the wake of the Grenfell tragedy.
answer_text: In December 2021, the Executive agreed to the Department of Finance (DoF) establishing an Expert Panel to review the key issues highlighted by Dame Judith Hackitt’s Independent Review of Building Regulations and Fire Safety and to look at the legal and policy responsibilities of NI Departments and Local Authorities for building and fire safety in Northern Ireland.The Expert Panel’s report, ‘Improving Safety in High Rise Residential Buildings in Northern Ireland’, was published in April 2023.In line with the recommendations of the Expert Panel’s report, my Department established the Residential Building Safety Division in January 2024 to take forward the implementation of the Expert Panel’s recommendations.My Officials are currently developing a high-rise residential building safety policy that priorities the safety needs of Northern Ireland citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the terms of reference for the forensic investigation being carried out by PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP into NI Water.
answer_text: The Terms of Reference, under which PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP carried out its Forensic Investigation, were published on 14 April 2025 and are publicly available at the following link:-https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/terms-reference-tor-forensic-accountant-investigation

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is considering any interim measures to enhance fire safety in high-rise residential buildings, pending the enactment of forthcoming legislation.
answer_text: My Department introduced the Cladding Safety Scheme for all residents of high-rise residential buildings over 11ms in heigh as an urgent interim measure in August 2023.   My Department has no further plans to introduce interim measures regarding fire safety in high-rise residential buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the unauthorised billboard on land owned by her Department at Camlough Road, Newry, will be removed.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 31058/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a copy, or summary, of all correspondence issued by her Department to Sinn Féin since 2023 in relation to the unauthorised billboard at the Camlough Road, Newry.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she (i) has taken; and (ii) will take, steps to avoid any potential conflict of interest in relation to enforcement action concerning the unauthorised billboard at the Egyptian Arch, given that it previously displayed the Sinn Féin party logo.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department takes to ensure that its enforcement of unauthorised attachments or structures on department-owned land is applied consistently and without regard to political affiliation or message.
answer_text: The erection of advertising signs within the boundaries of the public road is an offence under Article 87 of The Roads (NI) Order 1993. This Article gives my Department discretionary power to either seek to have the advertisement removed, or to remove it themselves if considered necessary. Given my Department’s ongoing resource limitations, priority is given to the removal of advertising where there is a clear road safety risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any enforcement action her Department has taken in relation to the unauthorised billboard at the Egyptian Arch on the Camlough Road, Newry, which remains in place over two years after the Department instructed its removal.
answer_text: When assessing the risks associated with billboards, my Department evaluates various factors related to road safety, including potential obstructions to vehicles and pedestrians, impairment of sightlines, and the likelihood of an errant vehicle colliding with the structure. In cases where unauthorised signage presents a clear safety hazard, its removal will be prioritised. However, when no immediate danger is identified, officials will collaborate with stakeholders to request it’s removal.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the purpose of the NICS EU study visit in January 2025 was to build capacity for implementing the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: The purpose of the NICS EU study visit in January 2025 was to give Northern Ireland Civil Service Officials an overview of how the EU works.  It also allowed staff to meet with the Brussels Office, the EU institutions and key EU officials.The NICS EU study visit was an action on the NICS Capability and Capacity Action Plan, which aims to support NICS-wide knowledge and skills to facilitate and enhance engagement with the EU and the UK Mission to the EU.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a copy of the full survey report from the NICS EU study visit in January 2025.
answer_text: The survey report is consistent with NICS guidelines and the principles for managing public resources as set out in Managing Public Money NI, which encourages a proportionate approach to evaluation and appraisal. The key findings of the report are:88% of respondents said they worked in a role that required knowledge of EU institutions, EU policy or EU legislation.100% of respondents said they had achieved the specific goals or outcomes they had set for the visit.100% of respondents reported an increase in their level of knowledge of EU institutions and policies post visit compared to their level before the visit. On average, respondents reported a 3-point increase, from 4 out of 10 to 7 out of 10, in their level of knowledge post visit. 100% of respondents reported an increase in how confident they felt to navigate EU institutions and policies post visit compared to before the visit. On average, respondents reported a 4-point increase in how confident they felt post visit, from 3 out of 10 to 7 out of 10.100% of respondents rated their satisfaction with the visit at 4 or above out of 5, with 56% being highly satisfied with a rating of 5 out of 5.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the estimated cost to the public purse of the NICS EU study visit in January 2025, including (i) travel; (ii) accommodation; (iii) subsistence; and (iv) staffing.
answer_text: The costs for (i) travel £1,795.31; (ii) accommodation £8,505.00; (iii) subsistence £6998.29 and (iv) there were no additional staff costs associated with the visit.  These costs were for 23 Civil Servants from across NICS.All expenditure was carried out consistently with NICS guidelines and the principles for managing public resources as set out in Managing Public Money NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the current status of the high-rise residential buildings fire safety policy being developed by his Department, including the anticipated timeline for consultation and enactment.
answer_text: My officials are currently drafting a high-rise residential building safety policy. Subject to Executive approval it is intended to go out for public consultation in the new year. A Bill is planned to be brought forward early in the next Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the measurable outcomes that resulted from the NICS EU study visit in January 2025; and (ii) whether an assessment has been made as to the value for money of these outcomes.
answer_text: The NICS EU study visit is an action on the NICS Capability and Capacity Action Plan.  It is a foundation course for NICS officials to develop knowledge and understanding of how the EU works. This supports upstream monitoring and engagement and facilitates access to networks and understanding of funding streams. The measurable outcomes of the NICS study visits in 2025 were that participants:		Established a closer working relationship between participants and staff in the Brussels Office. 		Liaised with staff from other Devolved Government to learn about they engage with the EU.		Developed an understanding of the role of the UK and Irish Permanent Representations as well as the European Council and gaining a high-level insight into the EU legislative process.		Met with MEP assistants to better understand the political context and the key role of the European Parliament.		Developed an understanding of the role of the European Commission.		Met with key contacts in own particular work area.		Developed an understanding of the interaction between the EU and Countries that are not EU Member States.Following the study visit a post project evaluation was carried out to assess the project for value for money consistent with NICS guidelines and the principles for managing public resources as set out in Managing Public Money NI, which encourages a proportionate approach to evaluation and appraisal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total number of people employed in the health service, in each of the last five years, broken down by staff groupings, including (i) medical and dental staff; (ii) nursing and midwifery staff; (iii) allied health professionals; (iv) social services staff; (v) ambulance staff; (vi) administrative and clerical staff; (vii) managerial staff; and (viii) all other staff categories.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Loughs Agency, whether his Department has investigated any potential breaches of section 3.2 of the North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, with reference to Annex 2, Part 7, with particular regard to whether approval of temporary staff appointments by the North South Ministerial Council were retrospective or in advance of the appointments.
answer_text: My officials continue to liaise with officials in the Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment, North South Ministerial Council and the Loughs Agency. In this context no investigation has been undertaken.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 30996/22-27, to provide a timeline for when the data will be available.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 30996/22-27, I am unable to provide a timeline for the information request as further consideration needs to be given to this matter to determine what, if indeed any, costs have been incurred.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide details on what the £20 million investment in 2025-26 is being spent on, as referenced by the deputy First Minister during her briefing at the Committee for the Executive Office on 10 September 2025.
answer_text: My Department’s allocation for Lough Neagh in the 2025-26 financial year comprises £6.3 million in Resource DEL and £11.1 million in Capital DEL, totalling £17.4 million.This funding supports the implementation of the Lough Neagh Action Plan, which includes 37 science-led actions aimed at improving water quality and ecosystem health. Of these, 14 actions have been completed, 22 are currently progressing, and one remains outstanding, pending the completion of a related action.Key projects supported by this investment include Sustainable Utilisation of Livestock Slurry; Sustainable Catchment Programme; and Digital Transformation initiatives to support delivery of the Action Plan.While this investment has enabled significant progress, the challenges facing Lough Neagh are complex and long-standing. There are no quick fixes. Substantial recovery will require sustained investment and continued Executive support over many years, potentially decades.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 30996/22-27, to detail the reasons why the total costs incurred by the Loughs Agency staff since its inception cannot be provided.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 30996/22-27, my officials are continuing to consider matters around the interpretation of the Agency’s staff complement figure and its application.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of press officers in her Department; and (ii) the cost of the same, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: (i)The Department for Infrastructure employs 4.608 Full Time Equivalent press officers as follows:1 x Principal Information Officer3 x Senior Information Officer 1 x Senior Information Officer (part-time) As well as being the point of contact for the media, press officers provide a wide range of communication services to the Minister and Department, including for DfI Roads, DfI Rivers, DfI Planning and the Driver Vehicle Agency.  (ii)  The cost of the same since the restoration of devolution in February 2024 is £597,059.32.   This figure includes employers National Insurance and pension contributions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to the appointment to the Northern Ireland Judicial Appointments Commission on 3 September 2025, whether the individual (i) met the required standard in; and (ii) passed, all security clearance and vetting processes prior to or during the appointment, including any renewal of a previous clearance.
answer_text: There were no appointments made to the Northern Ireland Judicial Appointments Commission on 3 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of press officers in his Department; and (ii) the cost of the same, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: There are currently 6 Press Officers in the Department of Health. The breakdown of those roles is as follows: 1 Principal Information Officer. 3 Senior Information Officers. 2 Information Officers.  The breakdown of costs since restoration: Excluding NI & Pension - £467,805.06Including NI & Pension - £674,747.53

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost of works carried out by contractors, relating to NI Water's wastewater stations, compared to in-house teams carrying out the works, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my department that the cost breakdown per year, for the last 5 years is as follows:2020/21 2021/22 2022/23 2023/24 2024/25 In-house staff costs 6,499,8466,621,0246,777,094 8,015,6188,009,577£m 6.56.66.8 8.08.0Contractor costs 6,697,4729,174,69110,004,198 11,036,10612,939,451£m 6.79.210.0 11.012.9For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why NI Water prioritises sub-contractors over in-house maintenance teams to carry out the majority of its works.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water determines whether works should be delivered by contractors or in-house maintenance teams through strategic considerations, including operational efficiency, regulatory obligations, risk mitigation, and workforce planning. Before any service is tendered, a business case is developed that evaluates delivery options against these criteria. The decision to outsource is not automatic; each service model is assessed individually to determine the most effective and value-for-money solution for that specific requirement.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of their statutory obligation to represent the interests of victims, whether an assessment has been made of the impact that recent comments on the BBC Borderland podcast, that IRA murders were justified, has had on victims.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why there is no context provided to the questions contained within the Northern Ireland's draft Climate Action Plan Children and Young People’s Survey.
answer_text: Education for sustainable development is an important element of the Northern Ireland curriculum. The draft Climate Action Plan Children and Young People’s survey, shared with school principals, provided an opportunity to involve children and young people in discussions about climate change today, that will impact their future. When sending out details of the draft Climate Action Plan Children and Young People’s Survey, a cover letter sent to school principals set out the broader context of the draft Climate Action Plan consultation. Schools were invited to consider responding to the survey as a class or school. In addition to explaining the context and key terms in simple language throughout the survey, a link to the ‘easy read’ version of the draft Plan was also shared to assist teachers who wished to complete the survey with their class.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail who (i) designed; and (ii) commissioned the Northern Ireland's draft Climate Action Plan Children and Young People’s Survey.
answer_text: Our children and young people will be impacted by climate change today and in the future. They have an important role to play in telling us what is important to them and how we can work to better protect our local environment against climate change. I am keen that their views are taken into consideration as we make decisions about climate action.The draft Climate Action Plan Children and Young People’s survey was developed by DAERA and issued to school principals via letter, inviting them to consider completing the survey with classes or on behalf of the school. In doing so, the department engaged with the Department of Education officials to seek guidance on how the survey should be administered.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commit to pay for classroom assistants during periods of half term.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is the employing authority for the majority of classroom assistants working in schools in Northern Ireland. The EA offers different contract types to classroom assistants when they take up a post. Whether a classroom assistant is paid during half term breaks depends on the terms of their individual contract. The main contract types are ‘term time’ and ‘52 week’ contracts.  Classroom assistants employed on term time contracts are contracted to work only during term time and are therefore not paid during holiday periods, including half term. Those on a 52 week contract are contracted to work all year round and are therefore paid during periods of half term. In line with the Department’s SEN Delivery Plan, the EA is currently progressing a strategic review of the classroom assistant employment model. This will include a range of work-streams that will focus on contracts, the development of job descriptions and a range of other employment-related matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consultation he undertook with the Minister of Education prior to circulating the Northern Ireland's draft Climate Action Plan Children and Young People’s Survey.
answer_text: The Minister of Education, along with all Executive Ministers, was notified of my intention to issue a children and young people’s survey to schools as part of the draft Climate Action Plan consultation and that this would be done in collaboration with the Department of Education. The aim of the survey, to involve children and young people in the process, was outlined. As the next generation, it is vital that young people have their say.Prior to circulating the draft Climate Action Plan Children and Young People’s survey, my officials engaged with Department of Education officials to discuss the survey both prior to and after its development. The survey was shared with the Department of Education in advance of issuing to school principals and no issues were raised.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what the data collected in the Northern Ireland's draft Climate Action Plan Children and Young People’s Survey will be used for.
answer_text: We have a moral and legal obligation to do everything we can to ensure that we secure a sustainable and prosperous society for our future generations. The draft Climate Action Plan Children and Young People’s survey, shared with schools in good faith, provided an opportunity to involve children and young people in discourse about proposed actions to tackle the impacts of climate change and create a cleaner, greener environment that maximises the economic and social opportunities that come from transitioning to net zero. This survey was intended as a method to engage with children and hear their views as part of the draft Climate Action Plan consultation. However, having reviewed responses, it is evident that some have not been completed by children and young people. Feedback from this survey will be deleted and disregarded. I find it extremely disappointing that some seek to deny our young people the opportunity to be involved in discussions about actions that will affect their own future. It is important that children and young people have the opportunity to voice their opinions on this important issue and as such, officials have engaged with children and young people throughout the draft CAP consultation, including 2 in person stakeholder events in Derry~Londonderry and Belfast, organised in conjunction with Youth Action NI, an event for CAFRE students and young people have attended the public sessions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of press officers in his Department; and (ii) the cost of the same, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: ANSWERThere are four press officers within the Department of Finance.  The cost of this since restoration of the Executive in February 2024 until September 2025 is £396,595. This figure includes National Insurance and Pension Contributions. These staff provide communications support and advice to the Minister, the Department, Land & Property Services and NISRA. Through a range of media and social media activities, they inform the public and media about public services, statistics, departmental initiatives, policy changes and the work of the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of press officers in her Department; and (ii) the cost of the same, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: There are five Press Officers in the Department for the Economy. This equates to a full-time equivalent (FTE) of 4.85. Since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024 the cost of same has been £363,305.68. These costs exclude Pension and NIC contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of press officers in his Department; and (ii) the cost of the same, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the specific powers that individual local councils hold in relation to the governance and management of Houses of Multiple Occupation.
answer_text: The legislative provisions in relation to Houses in Multiple Occupation are set out in the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016. Parts 2, 3, 4, and 5 contain detail of offences, councils’ powers to obtain information and enter premises, and councils’ powers to issue fixed penalty notices, fines and proceed to prosecution if necessary.Other provisions relating to the governance and management of private tenancies which also apply to Houses in Multiple Occupation, are contained in the Private Tenancies (Northern Ireland) Order 2006. These provisions include requirements relating to property fitness, tenancy deposits, and information that must be given to tenants on the terms of their tenancy. Councils may have other relevant legislative powers such as those contained in the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 in relation to litter and noise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Loughs Agency staff review of the approved staff complement figure will take account of (i) the long-term suspension of commercial salmon fishing; (ii) the aquaculture function never having been enacted; and (iii) marine tourism being part of local authority tourism portfolios.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that the staffing complement review will consider internal and external factors to ensure that it remains fit for purpose and allow it to continue to deliver its legislative responsibilities and government policy on behalf of both Sponsor Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether consideration has been given to utilising the independent sector to help address the backlog in dermatology outpatient appointments.
answer_text: A package of measures is being implemented to tackle red flag and long-wait dermatology patients. These include additional waiting list clinics in hospitals, use of the independent sector, expansion of elective services in Primary Care, and the new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme.Waiting List initiative clinics in hospitals and the independent sector are expected to deliver approximately 10,500 outpatient and day case procedures between April and December 2025, at an estimated cost of £5 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 31343/22-27, whether the Loughs Agency was making any pension contributions to staff that were appointed without pre-approval.

In this context a response cannot therefore be provided to this question at the current time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department has reviewed GP referral pathways for dermatology; and (ii) to detail any steps being taken to ensure those with suspected cancer are prioritised.
answer_text: The Primary Care Elective Service was established in 2018 to improve access for patients and help reduce the pressure on secondary care services. To date over 80,000 patients have been assessed and managed across the five pathways delivered in primary care (dermatology, minor surgery, musculoskeletal, non-scalpel vasectomy and gynaecology). As part of the recent investment in Primary Care Elective Services, a dermatology business case was approved which included the provision of 4,410 face to face and remote consultations each year for patients who present with routine dermatology conditions including adult eczema, childhood eczema, psoriasis, acne, non-specific rashes or suspected actinic keratosis. By reducing pressure on routine services this has helped free up capacity within secondary care for suspect cancer referrals. In March 2025, my Department launched a pilot which enabled the surgical treatment of patients, with a diagnosed low risk basal cell carcinoma (BCC) diagnosis, in primary care by GPs with enhanced skills. A total of 75 patients were successfully transferred from secondary to primary care for treatment during the pilot period and work is underway to agree a regional primary care led service. In addition to the Primary Care Elective Service, my Department commissioned a regional dermatology photo triage pathway which was implemented in 2021. Each GP practice in Northern Ireland now has access to an iPod and dermatoscope which enable images to be taken and attached to the referral for patients who present with a suspected skin cancer or urgent BCC lesion. Uptake to the photo triage pathway has been successful with 90% of GP practices contracting to this pathway. This pathway enables informed decision making at the point of triage to ensure patients with a suspected skin cancer or urgent BCC are directed to an outpatient appointment or to a diagnostic/surgical appointment as soon as possible within secondary care. Significant investment has also been committed from my Department to address the current waiting lists across Northern Ireland with a specific focus on targeting long waits, utilising independent sector and in-house capacity to reduce outpatient waits across a range of specialties.£61m has been allocated for waiting list initiative (WLI) activity in 2025/26 (Q1-Q3) for patient requiring red flag and other time critical assessments and treatments. As at the end of August 2025, almost £1.7m of WLI funding has been spent on dermatology services, delivering over 4,500 assessments and treatments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any specific actions his Department is taking to reduce dermatology waiting times; and (ii) any targets that have been set for improvement over the next (a) 12 months; and (b) 24 months.
answer_text: (i) In dermatology, several waiting list initiatives are underway to manage red flag demand and improve waiting times. These include additional activity within hospitals and the independent sector, expected to deliver approximately 10,500 outpatient and daycase procedures between April – December 2025, at an estimated cost of £5m. (ii & iii) The Regional Dermatology Forum continues to meet to consider medium- and long-term solutions, including addressing workforce challenges, exploring alternative pathways to mitigate clinical workforce shortfalls, and identifying opportunities to increase capacity within each Trust.Primary and Secondary Care services are also working collaboratively to reduce waiting times and improve access. This includes:In Primary Care Elective Services:Investment to deliver 4,410 face-to-face and remote consultations annually for patients presenting with routine dermatology conditions such as adult and childhood eczema, psoriasis, acne, non-specific rashes, and suspected actinic keratosis.A pilot launched in March 2025 enabling GPs with enhanced skills to surgically treat patients diagnosed with low-risk basal cell carcinoma in primary care.The Regional Dermatology Photo Triage Pathway, which supports image capture and e-referral for suspected skin cancer and urgent basal cell carcinoma cases. As of October 2025, 90% of GP practices are contracted to provide this pathway, enabling effective decision-making at the point of triage in secondary care.In Secondary Care Services:Development of specialist nursing roles to assess and treat patients;Use of biologic medications to manage chronic dermatological conditions;Introduction of virtual and telephone consultations to provide advice and guidance and reduce waiting times;Implementation of Patient-Initiated Follow-Up (PIFU) services and enhanced triage clinics to reform outpatient care.Performance is managed at the provider level, ensuring that activity aligns with investment and that waiting lists are addressed within available resources. However, it is acknowledged that reducing waiting times is a long term challenge. Sustained investment over up to five years will be required to bring hospital waiting times to acceptable levels. I hope this information is helpful in addressing your query.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the Executive Department responsible for ensuring that holders of public office with access to sensitive or classified material, including the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland, have undergone the appropriate level of Developed Vetting or equivalent national security clearance.
answer_text: The Department with sponsorship and oversight responsibility ensures that individuals appointed to roles within their remit undergo the appropriate level of vetting.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the protocols and procedures that apply to holders of public office in Northern Ireland that require access to Top Secret or compartmentalised intelligence, including the Department that oversees adherence to these protocols.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any mechanisms in place to ensure that Executive Departments apply UK Security Vetting standards, including Developed Vetting, for senior public office holders with access to sensitive or national security information; (ii) whether there is an independent verification of compliance.
answer_text: The policy governing this matter is not under the direction of The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether the current Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland has undergone Developed Vetting or an equivalent national security clearance; (ii) whether that clearance remains active; and (iii) the procedures that are followed to ensure that the clearance is maintained in line with United Kingdom Security Vetting standards.
answer_text: The responsibility for processing the vetting requirements for the position of Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland rests with The Executive Office (TEO). However, I am aware that successful Developed Vetting is a condition of appointment for the post of Police Ombudsman.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment made of the impact of current dermatology waiting times on patient outcomes, including late diagnosis of skin cancer.
answer_text: The health impact regarding the current dermatology waiting times on patient outcomes, including late diagnosis of skin cancer, will be specific to each patient’s condition and diagnosis.A patient may be referred as a red flag with suspected cancer symptoms, but this may not be the case when they are seen in a dermatology clinic.However, such long waits are not what I want for any patient with suspected cancer symptoms or other skin conditions. Unfortunately, the demand for dermatology in all Trusts continues to exceed capacity.  Regrettably patients are experiencing excessive waits for appointments and treatment which is not the service Trusts would wish to provide.  The Trusts are, however, working very hard to reduce the waiting lists and waiting times for their patients.Approximately £4.7m has been earmarked for additional dermatology waiting list initiative (WLI) work during Q1-Q3 of 2025/26. For the 6-month period from April 2025 to September 2025, spend on dermatology WLI work totalled £2.3m.  This spend delivered almost 6,100 dermatology new/review outpatient assessments and day case procedures.The Regional Dermatology Forum is also meeting to consider addressing demand in the medium to long term, challenges in workforce and alternative pathways to address shortfalls in clinical manpower, and potential ways to increase the capacity available in each Trust. Primary and Secondary Care are working together to seek alternative ways to provide outpatient consultations to help reduce waiting times, such as photo triage services for the diagnosis of moles, virtual and telephone appointments that provide advice and guidance.  As well as implementing ‘Patient initiated follow up’ (PIFU) and enhanced triage clinics, to further reform the outpatient service. However, reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix and it will not be resolved in a year or two.  Continued investment will need to be sustained for up to five years to bring hospital waiting times down to acceptable levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30512/22-27, AQW 30513/22-27 and AQW 30514/22-27, to detail (i) the membership of the (a) Gender Identity Review Group; and (b) Gender Identity Liaison Panel; and (ii) the experience that each member brought to the (a) Gender Identity Review Group; and (b) Gender Identity Liaison Panel, that led to their appointment.
answer_text: (a) Membership of the Gender Identity Review Group includes:Health & Social Care Board/now SPPGAssistant Director, Commissioning Lead Senior Manager, Service ContractsProgramme Manager, Mental Health & Learning DisabilityDepartment of HealthPolicy Lead for Gender Identity and Transgender Issues Patient and Client CouncilCommunication and Events ManagerBelfast Health & Social Care TrustMental Health Co-DirectorConsultant, Endocrinology Service Manager, CAMHs Gender Identity PsychiatristLead Mental Health Service ManagerChair of Mental Health DivisionCo-Director Planning & PerformanceConsultant in Addictions PsychiatryGeneral Practitioner representative  Representative from the Royal College of General PractitionersVoluntary and Community sectorKeen to ensure the voices of those with lived experience of transgender issues could continue to inform and influence the review, the Review Group tasked the Patient Client Council (PCC) to recruit voluntary and community sector representatives to join the Review Group. From the applications received and with the advice and support of the Patient and Client Council (PCC), two individuals representing the voluntary and community sector were appointed to join the Review Group.In addition, Joint Chairs of the Liaison Panel which was later established  were appointed by the PCC to be members of the Review Group to help strengthen the links between the Review Group and Liaison Panel.(b) Membership of the Gender Identity Liaison Panel:Names of individuals will not be released as this constitutes identifiable/personal information.  (ii) the experience that each member brought that led to their appointment.(a) Gender Identity Review Group;This group was made up of officials from the Health and Social Care Board, Departmental Officials, Clinical Leads from the Regional Gender Identity Service and the Mental Health Directorate in the Belfast Health and Social Care  Trust, and professions from across acute and community services as well as primary care. Each member of the group was deemed as a Subject Matter Expert in their designated service area.  As already referenced earlier,  the PCC undertook a recruitment exercise  to select voluntary and community sector representatives to join the Review Group. Subsequently, two individuals were appointed to join the Review Group. Joint Chairs of the Liaison Panel, which was later established,  were appointed by the PCC to be members of the Review Group. (b) Gender Identity Liaison Panel, The PCC  established a Gender Identity Liaison Panel to help inform and influence the direction of travel of the Review, hearing directly from people with lived experience of transgender issues.Membership of the Panel was by application to the PCC.  Panel members were selected based on their relevant knowledge and expertise. In total, 21 panel members were selected via a formal process which was led by the PCC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps which have been taken to (i) notify; and (ii) protect, the individuals whose data was disclosed in the recent data breach that occurred in his Department, in relation to Personal Independence Payment applications.
answer_text: My Department follows a risk-based approach when considering contacting data subjects where their information has been disclosed in error. This is in line with the Information Commissioner’s Office guidance. The data subjects were not contacted in this instance as no sensitive information was disclosed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any contact he has had with the Education Authority, seeking the withdrawal of their guidance on transgender pupils since the Supreme Court judgement in the case of For Women Scotland versus The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: I wrote to the Education Authority (EA) on 5 September 2025 requesting that the ‘Guidance for Schools, EOTAS Centres and Youth Service on Supporting Transgender Young People’ be withdrawn immediately. I also asked the EA to determine any next steps required to ensure alignment with my department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30512/22-27, AQW 30513/22-27 and AQW 30514/22-27, whether any member of the (a) Gender Identity Review Group; and (b) Gender Identity Liaison Panel, has declared an interest in relation to a current or past membership of a LGBT+ group.
answer_text: The Gender Identity Review Group were keen to ensure the voices of those with lived experience of transgender issues could continue to inform and influence the review, they tasked the Patient Client Council (PCC) with recruiting voluntary and community sector representatives to join the Group. From the applications received and with the advice and support of the Patient and Client Council (PCC), two individuals representing the voluntary and community sector were appointed to join the Review Group.In addition, Joint Chairs of the Liaison Panel which was later established  were appointed by the PCC to be members of the Review Group to help strengthen the links between the Review Group and Liaison Panel.The PCC established a Gender Identity Liaison Panel to help inform and influence the direction of travel of the Review, hearing directly from people with lived experience of transgender issues.I am advised by my officials that members of both groups who  were either current or past members of a LGBT+ group were very open and transparent  regarding this aspect at meetings of those groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any assessment made of the potential impact of the Public Health Agency's guidance on illegal drugs, on young people in schools; and (ii) how this guidance is being communicated to ensure it does not undermine established drug prevention and health education programs.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency’s (PHA) guidance would be a matter for that agency and the Department of Health.Schools in Northern Ireland have a statutory duty to deliver drugs education to pupils in relation to both legal and illegal substances. Alcohol and drugs education is integrated into primary and post primary curriculum.Should they wish to, schools have the flexibility to work directly with the PHA and other organisations to provide learning opportunities in support of delivery of the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to his response to AQW 31661/22-2,5 to detail whether any schools in Northern Ireland currently provide unisex toilets or changing rooms, and if so, how these facilities comply with Regulation 10 (2) of the School Premises (Standards) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1973, which requires separate sanitary fittings for boys and girls above the age of six.
answer_text: Neither the Department of Education nor the Education Authority hold information on schools in Northern Ireland which currently provide unisex toilets or changing rooms.

However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The main chemicals used in wastewater treatment include Polyelectrolytes, Ferric compounds, sodium carbonate and odour control chemicals.The table below outlines the volume of the chemicals used in each of the last five years.Chemical NameTotal Volume Used2020-212021-222022-232023-242024-25Polyelectrolytes (kgs)177,070219,355206,335198,330229,805Ferric Sulphate/ Ferric Aluminium Sulphate (Tonnes)3,4143,7663,7453,6343,932Sodium Carbonate (kgs)5,2007,31111,32436,60535,950Odour Control Chemicals (litres)104,30498,79894,671101,40982,405For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the quantity of untreated sewage discharged into the water system, in each of the last five years, broken down by wastewater treatment station.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water interprets the term sewage discharges to mean wastewater discharges. Wastewater discharges are generally considered under 2 categories: (a) discharges that occur on a continuous basis from a wastewater treatment works (WwTW) known as final effluent discharges. This is wastewater which has been treated to a standard that is considered safe to return to the environment on continuous basis; and (b) discharges that occur on an intermittent basis from storm overflows when dilute wastewater is discharged direct to the environment during periods of heavy rainfall. NI Water has 1,020 WwTWs which are subject to discharge consents issued by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA).  NIEA outlines the standards that must be achieved for a discharge into the water environment. NI Water collects data on the daily volume of treated discharges at some larger WwTWs, however, not all WwTWs have instrumentation to measure volumes discharged to the environment. The data collected is used to inform industry standard models, which  calculate the predicted annual spill volumes of treated final effluent. Measured data or predictions for individual years is not available.The table below sets out the wastewater treatment works and associated predicted annual spill volumes of treated final effluent, where this is available. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the safeguards in place to ensure that sub-contractors, carrying out NI Water works, are doing so competitively.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information and can advise as follows:Contractors contracting directly with NI Water are appointed through a competitive tendering process in accordance with the Utilities Contract Regulations 2016, Procurement Act 2023 and Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy, with a focus on achieving best value through a combination of cost and quality assessment. Part of suppliers’ bid submissions includes pricing schedule of rates to be applied throughout the life of the contract.Rates paid to sub-contractors by the lead contractor are agreed between both parties.It is in the lead contractor’s best interest to ensure that sub-contractors are delivering value in line with their tendered rates.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to recruit additional dermatology consultants and staff to reduce waiting lists.
answer_text: The British Association of Dermatologists (BAD) Workforce Strategy in 2024, is one of a number of documents highlighting the crisis in Dermatology Medical Workforce across the UK. This report indicates that “there were at least 96.8 WTE vacant posts in dermatology across the UK, and an approximate shortfall of 180 WTE by BAD calculations”. Compounding the growing deficit in dermatology WTEs, is changes to workforce practice with the majority of trainees working part time, more consultants taking early retirement, and a cohort of doctors aged 60-65 who are about to retire. Trusts across Northern Ireland have found the recruitment of dermatologists challenging. However, Trusts continue to pursued recruitment in a number of ways including International Medical Recruitment (IMR), advertising on HSC Jobs, social media platforms, BMJ journal (paper and online) and NHS Jobs. Like other parts of the UK, while NI is trying to recruit additional medical consultants, alternatives to consultant management is also actively being pursued to address waiting time including: In  Primary Care Elective Services: Investment to provide 4,410 face to face and remote consultations each year for patients who present with routine dermatology conditions including adult eczema, childhood eczema, psoriasis, acne, non-specific rashes or suspected actinic keratosis. By reducing pressure on routine services this will help free up capacity within secondary care for suspect cancer referrals. In March 2025, my Department launched a pilot which enabled the surgical treatment of patients, with a diagnosed low risk basal cell carcinoma diagnosis, in primary care by GPs with enhanced skills. A total of 75 patients were successfully transferred from secondary to primary care for treatment during the pilot period and work is underway to agree a regional primary care led service. The Regional Dermatology Photo Triage pathway which provides an image capture and e-Referral pathway for patients who present with a suspected skin cancer and urgent basal cell carcinoma. As of October 2025, 90% of GP Practices are contracted to provide this pathway which enables effective decision making at the point of triage in secondary care. In secondary CareThe use of specialist nurses to assess and treat patients in secondary care; andThe use of biologics medications to help with the treatment and management of chronic long term dermatology conditions.Significant investment has been committed from my Department to address the current waiting lists across NI with a specific focus on targeting long waits, utilising independent sector and inhouse capacity to reduce outpatient waits across a range of specialties including Dermatology

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients with suspected cancer, currently waiting for dermatology outpatient appointments; and (ii) the average waiting time for these cases.
answer_text: The Department does not publish information on the conditions or clinical priority of patients who are currently waiting for dermatology outpatient appointments.  The number of patients waiting, and the median and 95th percentile waiting times, for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment under the dermatology specialty is, however, published on a quarterly basis in Figures 2 and 4 of the ‘Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 30 June 2025 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/outpatient-waiting-timesIn addition, waiting times for patients starting treatment for skin cancer are published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Cancer Waiting Times’ statistics bulletin. The most recent figures have a reporting date of 30 June 2025 and are available on the Department’s website at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesAverage waiting time information by Health and Social Care Trust by Specialty and by level of priority is available at:https://online.hscni.net/my-waiting-times-ni/ A complete regional overview across the above-mentioned datasets is currently limited due to the phased rollout of encompass – a new electronic patient record system. This constraint particularly affects the Southern and Western Trusts, which have only recently transitioned to the new system. As a result, there is a necessary time lag while data are validated to ensure consistency and accuracy.

answer_text: The average waiting times by Trust, and supplied by each Trust are detailed below:Southern TrustAverage urgent wait = 43 weeks Northern TrustAverage urgent wait = 71 weeks, excluding paediatricsSouth Eastern TrustAverage urgent wait = 56 weeksBelfast TrustAverage urgent wait = 4 Year Western TrustAverage urgent wait = 20 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the variation in dermatology waiting times between the Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) the reasons for these differences.
answer_text: Unfortunately, the demand for dermatology in all Trusts continues to exceed capacity. Regrettably patients are experiencing excessive waits for appointments and treatment which is not the service the Trusts would wish to provide. Across the region most patients wait less than 26 weeks for a suspect cancer dermatology assessment. While, in terms of routine waits, all Trusts have patients waiting over 300 weeks for an initial assessment. These patients have been triaged as low clinical priority and the waiting times will vary by Trust depending on referral demand.Variations in waiting times across Trusts are linked to patient demand, service capacity, medical and nursing workforce pressures and vacancies in workforce, and available patient pathways. Trusts are continuing to seek alternative ways to provide outpatient consultations to help reduce waiting times, through initiatives such as virtual or telephone appointments that provide advice and guidance. The Trusts are also seeking to implement a ‘Patient initiated follow up’ (PIFU) service and enhanced triage clinics, to further reform the outpatient service. Dermatology Services will benefit from the package of measures being taken implemented to address long waiting patients including the expansion of elective services in primary care, investment in waiting list mega clinics and by providing additional waiting list clinics.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any reviews or audits being undertaken across all Departmental benefits systems to identify potential risks of personal data breaches.
answer_text: When incidents involving a compromise, or potential compromise, of personal data are reported within my Department, they are investigated in line with the Department’s Security Incident Reporting Policy, remedial action is taken and lessons learned inform future processes.The Data Protection Officer (DPO) within my Department conducts regular compliance reviews in business areas to monitor how personal data is collected, stored, and shared. The team is currently in the process of carrying out a review of PIP’s handling of correspondence.When a check is completed the DPO will make recommendations to help improve compliance and reduce the risks associated with processing personal data. Each business area also has a nominated Information Asset Owner (IAO) whose role includes reminding staff of the importance of handling personal data appropriately, as well as identifying and mitigating any risks around how personal data is processed.Where business areas make any changes to processes involving personal information, they are required to conduct detailed Data Protection Impact Assessments to ensure risks to personal information are identified and mitigated before implementation. Additionally, my Department’s Internal Audit Plan for 2025/26 includes targeted assurance and consulting reviews across individual benefit areas. These reviews are designed to assess the effectiveness of key controls, including those related to the protection of personal data. Each audit considers the specific data handling practices within the area under review, ensuring that appropriate safeguards, such as role-based access controls, are in place to maintain confidentiality, integrity, and availability in compliance with data protection requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will bring forward guidance, similar to NHS England’s Uniforms and Workwear: Guidance on Uniform and Workwear Policies for NHS Employers, to prohibit Health and Social Care staff from wearing official uniforms while engaged in political demonstrations or protests.
answer_text: My Department issued the Regional Dress Code Policy to the HSC in 2008 which seeks to ensure that both clinical and non-clinical staff across the HSC wear appropriate clothing to promote health and safety and adhere to best practice in infection prevention and control measures.  This document is broadly in line with the NHS Employers’ Guidance and does not specifically address the wearing of HSC uniforms by staff undertaking political demonstrations or protests. However, the general expectation is that staff should not do anything that could undermine their professionalism or impact on patient confidence across the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) whether the Department has been able to identify the individual or team responsible for the recent disclosure of Personal Independence Payment correspondence containing sensitive personal data; and (ii) any disciplinary or corrective actions which are being taken.
answer_text: My Department has conducted an initial review and identified the human error which resulted in the recent disclosure of Personal Independence Payment (PIP) personal information. Areas for improvement have been identified and implemented to avoid reoccurrence, including completion of a Compliance Audit of the PIP Centre’s document handling processes.  The incident has been highlighted to all Personal Independence Payment staff who have been reminded of the importance of maintaining the security of personal data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to bring forward legislation to transfer responsibility for Houses in Multiple Occupation from Belfast City Council to each individual district council, in order to improve (i) local accountability; (ii) enforcement; and (iii) responsiveness to residents’ concerns.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Houses in Multiple Occupation Unit (NIHMO) based in Belfast City Council administer the scheme on behalf of all councils, including processing all licence requests, validating the requests, checks and inspections of HMO properties, issuing enforcement notices for unlicensed HMOs or breaching licensing conditions. However, each local council is responsible for their own HMO licensing and policy, considering applications and granting HMO licences within their own area. A council may only grant a licence if it is content licensing conditions are satisfied.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what immediate action the Department is taking in response to the recent disclosure of Personal Independence Payment correspondence which included the personal data of other claimants; and (ii) what steps are being taken to prevent such breaches in future.
answer_text: I fully acknowledge that even one incident of personal data compromise is unacceptable and can potentially have a serious impact upon the data subject.  My Department has taken swift and commensurate action, including immediately retrieving and securing the personal data, instigating a data protection incident report and making an early referral to the Information Commissioner’s Office. In conjunction with the Department’s Data Protection Officer, a Compliance Audit of document handling within the Personal Independence Payment Centre has been commissioned.  The learning and improvements identified from the review of this incident and from the Compliance Audit will be shared across the Department’s benefit delivery business areas to help prevent such breaches from occurring in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what steps have been taken to notify and protect individuals affected by past data breaches; and (ii) what lessons have been learned to prevent recurrence.
answer_text: When incidents involving a compromise of personal data are reported within my Department, they are investigated in line with the Department’s Security Incident Reporting Policy, which considers if individuals affected by the data breach require notification and what remedial action is taken. Any lessons learned help inform future processes to prevent recurrence.My Department’s Data Protection Officer (DPO) and Security Officer, along with the relevant Information Asset Owner(s), carry out an assessment and consideration is given to the likelihood and severity of the risk to the rights and freedoms of any individual impacted by the breach. Where it is determined there is a high risk, the Department will inform the data subject, advise them of the potential impact of the breach and give them contact details for the Department’s Data Protection Officer. Consideration is also given to whether notifying an individual would cause unnecessary or disproportionate distress. This approach is in line with guidance from the Information Commissioner’s Office. My Department is committed to learning lessons from every data breach and continually review and update systems, processes, and training to ensure personal data is safeguarded to the highest possible standard to prevent recurrence.

answer_text: The table below provides a breakdown of the number of previous data breaches my Department has reported to the Information Commissioner’s Office for the previous five years.YearReported to ICO 2020/202132021/202212022/202302023/202412024/20251No sanctions were received from the Information Commissioner’s Office for any of the reported data breaches. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the changes made to his Department’s (i) systems; (ii) processes; and (iii) staff training, in response to any past data breaches of benefit claimant information.
answer_text: When incidents involving a compromise of personal data are reported within my Department, they are investigated in line with the Department’s Security Incident Reporting Policy, remedial action is taken and lessons learned inform future processes.My Department has implemented a range of improvements across its systems, processes, and staff training in response to previous incidents.With regard to systems, my Department has robust access controls in place for Departmental information and there are technical safeguards in place to protect claimant information.Processes are regularly reviewed and updated to improve data handling and data security within my Department. Information Transfer Guidance, Security Incident reporting procedures, data protection impact assessments and data sharing agreements are in place to ensure a consistent and secure approach to the handling of claimant information.  Senior Information Asset Owners provide assurance quarterly that the business areas for which they are responsible process data in line with legislation and Departmental policy.All staff are required to complete mandatory training on data protection and information security.  Targeted guidance and training have been issued to the Department’s Information Asset Owners (IAOs) who are also required to undertake NICS IAO training upon appointment to ensure they are aware of their responsibilities.  Data Protection issues are regularly discussed at forums and team meetings, and Data Protection reminders are issued to staff through the intranet.My department is committed to learning lessons from every incident and continually reviews and updates its systems, processes, and training to ensure claimant information is safeguarded to the highest possible standard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost to GPs of the Gender Identity Service, in light of the Freedom of Information material obtained by the Christian Institute.
answer_text: The Gender Identity service is a secondary care service.  GPs cannot refer directly into the KOI Gender Identity Service.  Patients are referred to the CAHMS service.  GPs do not incur costs in making such referrals.  If a patient is 17.5 years old or older, resident in Northern Ireland and registered with a GP practice in Northern Ireland they can be referred to the Brackenburn clinic.  The referral into the Brackenburn clinic can be made by a GP or other relevant healthcare professional.  GPs do not incur costs in making referrals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether the map on page 15 of the Invest Northern Ireland Performance Report 2020-21 to 2024-25 Employment by Council Area, refers to employment in Invest NI supported businesses; and (ii) why assistance offered in Mid and East Antrim is much lower than in Mid Ulster, Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon; and Newry, Mourne and Down, where employment is substantially higher.
answer_text: The map on page 15 refers to data collected through Invest NI’s Client Performance Indicators (CPI) survey, which includes most businesses financially supported by Invest NI. A small number of businesses that we support are not included in the CPI survey sample, mainly those trading in the north for less than 18 months. The survey collects financial and employment information through indicators such as employment, sales, external sales and exports to enable measurement of the performance and growth of the portfolio. The assistance offered in Mid & East Antrim (MEA) has been lower compared to the three other council areas because these council areas have, inter alia, higher numbers of internationally-trading businesses. Industrial structure and sectoral composition are also explanatory factors. For example, Mid Ulster accounts for 14% of the North’s exports and is home to 11% of our exporters. This indicates that there are 3x the number of businesses in the Mid Ulster area that are export-active than in the Mid & East Antrim and the area is, therefore, likely to attract greater levels of support towards these businesses. It is also important to highlight that local government districts are not self-contained labour markets.  Investment in any one area, has the potential to impact surrounding areas and further afield.  Variances between ‘place of work’ and ‘place of residence’ for the MEA area, would suggest that many MEA residents travel outside of the direct area to work. To improve regional balance, Invest NI is implementing a new regional approach that is increasing its capacity and capability across all areas of the North to seek out, identify and engage with more companies than ever before. This involves attracting businesses that are new to Invest NI, enhancing the support, advice and guidance it provides to companies and implementing a new purposeful partnership approach across its regional stakeholders and partners to align and maximise the impact that we collective will have on all parts of the North. To support this, Invest NI have increased the number of staff in its regional offices, including its Ballymena office, providing additional capacity to generate new business for it and, over the long term, drive better regional outputs and outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of applications for assistance made to Invest NI by businesses, broken down by each local council area, during the period 2020-21 to 2024-25; and (ii) the success rate of such applications.
answer_text: Between 2020–21 and 2024–25, Invest NI received close to 11,000 applications for support, with an overall success rate of 80%. The table below outlines the number of formal applications submitted by District Council Area during this period.Invest NI Activity – Number of Formal Applications Submitted by DCA and Outcome (2020-21 to 2024-25)	DCANumber of Applications% SuccessfulAntrim and Newtownabbey54975%Ards and North Down53677%Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon1,20680%Belfast2,54181%Causeway Coast and Glens70781%Derry City and Strabane65380%Fermanagh and Omagh63476%Lisburn and Castlereagh86683%Mid and East Antrim54582%Mid Ulster1,44482%Newry, Mourne and Down1,16479%Not Yet Determined / NI Wide / Unknown5252%Total10,89780%			You should note that a significant number of enquiries do not reach formal application stage.  Indeed, it is advantageous for both the business and Invest NI that a quick, informal indication is provided on whether an application is likely to be successful to ensure that the business is not unnecessarily engaging in a process that will require the commitment of time and resources. In such instances, where the business is perhaps not ready for Invest NI support, Invest NI will work with them to identify alternative funding or support, or will help them progress their plans to a point where a tangible, supportable investment emerges.  		In addition, Invest NI also provides a range of non-financial support, advice and guidance directly to businesses that do not require businesses to apply.  In effect, therefore, the above statistics represent only an element of Invest NI’s engagement with businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail all of the animals kept within the prison system, including the reasons why they are kept.
answer_text: The use of animals by the Northern Ireland Prison Service is an operational matter for the Service.  The Service operates a Dog Section which supports the safety and security of prisons which is made up of 49 service dogs for the purpose of General Patrol and Security / Passive Drug Search / Area Seach and Explosives.  It also utilises a range of animals to support the rehabilitation of prisoners including ability to gain accredited qualifications, develop new vocational skills and qualifications and provide emotional support to individuals.  Animals currently supporting rehabilitation within Northern Ireland prison establishments include:AnimalQuantitySheep29Rooster1Duck2Pony1Dog4Chickens20Goats4Bees7 (Hives)Fish9Birds58The successful use of animals within the NI Prison Service spans many years. All animals are registered with a vet, are maintained to a high standard of care and integrated into a prison’s daily schedule, working with operational staff, Prisoner Safety and Support Teams, AD:EPT and the Mental Health Teams.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 29695/22-27, to outline his Department’s current policy on the use of hatcheries for salmon conservation, including any reassessment of the 2015 decision to remove hatchery equipment from the Loughs Agency as a remediation measure.
answer_text: DAERA does not have a specific policy on the use of salmon hatcheries. The Department follows the North Atlantic Salmon Conservation Organisation’s (NASCO) guidelines on salmon stocking. Any applications to DAERA to remove wild salmon broodstock for use in a hatchery would be assessed against these guidelines.The Department has not carried out a reassessment of the 2015 decision by Loughs Agency to remove hatchery equipment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
