question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will issue guidance to schools on the necessity of single-sex toilets and changing areas based on biological sex.
answer_text: My department wrote to all schools on 8 September to communicate a policy statement on the use of certain facilities in schools.  The statement set out that in terms of the use of changing rooms, boys (biological males) should not use changing rooms when such changing rooms are being used by girls, and vice-versa. Boys and girls should use separate toilet facilities where these are provided. This is in line with Regulation 10 (2) of the School Premises (Standards) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1973 states that “For all pupils above the age of 6 separate sanitary fittings shall be provided for boys and girls. Support and advice remain available to schools on this matter from Education Authority support services in accordance with the department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what guidance currently exists on school sports being based on biological sex.
answer_text: The policy statement issued (8 September) states that ‘except where the Governing Bodies of a sport may have made provision for competitive participation between boys and girls, boys should not participate in competitive sport against girls, and girls should not participate in competitive sport against boys.’

As such we are unable to provide the information you have sought at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an independent review will be commissioned into the IT outage at the Southern Health and Social Care Trust on 17 September 2025, with findings published and lessons applied across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: As per my written statement of 19 September, the Southern Health and Social Care Trust had an operational IT outage on 17 September which was caused by a standard software update to IT infrastructure that failed and led to a loss of the computer network via two data centres, which prevented access to key systems across the Trust. No computerised systems, including encompass, caused this incident, but unfortunately were impacted, as were other electronic patient systems.Initial unverified data indicates approximately 1,600 patients were impacted by postponements.  It should be noted that there were no postponements beyond the 17 and 18 September and adult community clinics were not impacted during the major incident timeframe.  The Southern Trust has provided updates through social media and their website and are actively working through the process of communicating with all patients who were impacted. In addition, a patient helpline was established but was stood down on 19 September due to little uptake. Press enquiries and updates have been responded to by the Press Office. The Southern Trust has started the rescheduling of appointments and procedures for those impacted patients in line with clinical prioritisation.  My Department will support this process by making waiting list funding available if required. The Trust will complete this process as quickly as possible to try to minimise any further delays; however, as clinics are booked six weeks in advance, it may take some time to reschedule all affected patients.  The issue was isolated to the Southern Trust but impacted across their sites. There is no suggestion that this was a cyber related matter. As with any Major Incident, the HSC has a process to understand the cause, understand the impact and identify any learning or rectifications to prevent a recurrence.  In this space, I can confirm a root cause analysis of the reasons for the outage is underway and a working group has been set up. The system provider is also considering the technical assessment.  This is being supported by independent members of two Trusts, the Business Services Organisation and the Department’s Digital Health and Care team. The Trust is also setting up an Incident Review Group to which an independent chair will be appointed. The scope will be to look wider than the root cause of the technical issue and consider the steps taken by the Trust in advance of the outage and the steps taken subsequently.  This will include the Trust’s approach to decision making, and the effectiveness of business continuity arrangements. This group will be tasked with identifying learning from the event for the Trust as well as the rest of the region. My Department will be involved and will also ensure consideration is given to the wider system response to the incident. Considerations will be given as to the publication of any learning in due course.My Department will continue their oversight of the recovery and learning processes associ

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to support frontline staff in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust as they manage disruption and increased pressures caused by the IT outage on 17 September 2025.
answer_text: During the period of the Major Incident in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, clinicians and front-line staff from affected areas were redeployed to support other departments ensuring continuity of care.  In addition, to support this the Trust brought in additional staff to maintain patient safety, support staff wellbeing, and assist with clinical documentation.  Communication was maintained through control room meetings, global emails, and social media. A runner system enabled clear messaging between control rooms and wards. Control room meetings were increased to support two-way communication. A dedicated helpline was established to inform concerned patients and ease pressure on medical secretaries. Staff were issued scripts to ensure consistent messaging. Messages of appreciation have also been issued to staff by Trust management and time off in lieu will be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the titles of the reports commissioned to look at the TREES programme; (ii) the costs (including VAT) of each; (iii)  when each started; and (iv) when each were completed.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised that:The titles of the reports commissioned to look at the TREES programme are:TREES Project Forestry Review Report; and Provision of Audit and Assessment of TREES Project. The cost (including VAT) of each report was £16,200£23,940Both reports were started in June 2024.  The TREES Project Forestry Review Report was completed in November 2024, and the Provision of Audit and Assessment of TREES Project was completed in August 2024.       The Agency also carried out its own internal review at no cost.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what proportion of the total investment is represented by the total assistance provided by Invest Northern Ireland, during 2020-21 to 2024-25, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Over the period 2020-21 to 2024-25, Invest NI offered assistance to businesses totalling £434m, which represented 17% of the £2.5bn of investment committed by Invest NI supported projects.  The table below details this percentage contribution by District Council Area.Invest NI Percentage Contribution towards Supported Projects (2020-21 to 2024-25)DCATotal Assistance £mTotal Planned Investment £mInvest NI % ContributionAntrim and Newtownabbey18.0192.6219%Ards and North Down18.0785.3021%Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon42.40250.3817%Belfast151.691023.0815%Causeway Coast and Glens13.9995.1515%Derry City and Strabane35.17236.5115%Fermanagh and Omagh12.8559.4622%Lisburn and Castlereagh29.31131.8422%Mid and East Antrim15.2276.6720%Mid Ulster37.54172.4122%Newry, Mourne and Down28.15147.2419%Note: In addition to the support detailed above, Invest NI offered £23.48m to External Delivery Organisations or Universities, the beneficiaries of which will not be restricted to the council area in which they are located, and £8.43m to projects that cannot be allocated at District Council Area level, for example, remote working projects and those that have yet to determine a location.		For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will publish the contingency plans to protect emergency and routine care in the event of IT system failures in the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: As per my written statement of 19 September, the Southern Health and Social Care Trust had an operational IT outage on 17 September which was caused by a standard software update to IT infrastructure that failed and led to a loss of the computer network via two data centres, which prevented access to key systems across the Trust. No computerised systems, including encompass, caused this incident, but unfortunately were impacted, as were other electronic patient systems.Initial unverified data indicates approximately 1,600 patients were impacted by postponements.  It should be noted that there were no postponements beyond the 17 and 18 September and adult community clinics were not impacted during the major incident timeframe.  The Southern Trust has provided updates through social media and their website and are actively working through the process of communicating with all patients who were impacted. In addition, a patient helpline was established but was stood down on 19 September due to little uptake. Press enquiries and updates have been responded to by the Press Office. The Southern Trust has started the rescheduling of appointments and procedures for those impacted patients in line with clinical prioritisation.  My Department will support this process by making waiting list funding available if required. The Trust will complete this process as quickly as possible to try to minimise any further delays; however, as clinics are booked six weeks in advance, it may take some time to reschedule all affected patients.  The issue was isolated to the Southern Trust but impacted across their sites. There is no suggestion that this was a cyber related matter. As with any Major Incident, the HSC has a process to understand the cause, understand the impact and identify any learning or rectifications to prevent a recurrence.  In this space, I can confirm a root cause analysis of the reasons for the outage is underway and a working group has been set up. The system provider is also considering the technical assessment.  This is being supported by independent members of two Trusts, the Business Services Organisation and the Department’s Digital Health and Care team. The Trust is also setting up an Incident Review Group to which an independent chair will be appointed. The scope will be to look wider than the root cause of the technical issue and consider the steps taken by the Trust in advance of the outage and the steps taken subsequently.  This will include the Trust’s approach to decision making, and the effectiveness of business continuity arrangements. This group will be tasked with identifying learning from the event for the Trust as well as the rest of the region. My Department will be involved and will also ensure consideration is given to the wider system response to the incident. Considerations will be given as to the publication of any learning in due course.My Department will continue their oversight of the recovery and learning processes associ

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Loughs Agency, whether similar reviews of staff complements and funding practices are being conducted for other cross-border bodies under the North South Ministerial Council to ensure compliance with legislative requirements.
answer_text: As Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs I cannot answer for North South Bodies which are sponsored by other Northern Ireland Departments and their Ministers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the recent halt in recruitment processes at the Loughs Agency, including the non-issuance of contracts for advertised temporary posts, was directed by his Department due to concerns over exceeding the approved staff complement.
answer_text: My Department has not issued any directions to Loughs Agency relating to its recruitment processes but has been engaging with the Agency around its staffing complement and the need for Loughs Agency to bring forward a staff complement review to the NSMC for consideration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any arrangements the GAA has with Translink relating to (i) transport to matches; and (ii) sponsorship of events.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:As part of Translinks Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) Programme, Translink actively engages with the local communities they serve through a diverse range of sponsorships that promote both social and physical wellbeing. An example is their partnership with Ulster GAA, supporting health and wellbeing initiatives aimed at young people across Northern Ireland. These programmes encourage physical activity, improve mental health, and help reduce social isolation. While no direct financial support is provided to Ulster GAA, in-kind travel assistance is offered as part of the sponsorship agreement, in return for promoting key Translink messages. Translink also provide equivalent support to the Irish Football Association (IFA) through the Translink Schools’ Gold Cup Football Programme, reinforcing their commitment to youth development and active lifestyles.  In addition, Translink sponsors other sporting and wellbeing events such as the Belfast City Marathon and the Castlerock Walking Festival. These community initiatives help strengthen local relationships and provide valuable platforms to communicate important messages around safety, yLink youth card discounts, and the wider benefits of active travel—contributing to a healthier, more sustainable future for communities across Northern Ireland.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 29017/22-27, to provide an update on the ongoing review of the Loughs Agency’s staff complement, including any findings regarding the management of recent temporary appointments and their alignment with approved staffing levels.
answer_text: Loughs Agency is currently working on a review of staff complement and, subject to the approval of both Sponsor Departments and Finance Ministers, will present it at a future NSMC meeting for consideration. As the review is ongoing, I am unable to provide any further update at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Loughs Agency, whether his Department has investigated potential breaches of section 3.2 of the North South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, particularly regarding retrospective approvals of temporary staff appointments by the North South Ministerial Council.
answer_text: My Departmental officials are liaising with officials in the Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment, North South Ministerial Council and the Loughs Agency to clarify the position with regard to the Agency’s required staffing complement and establish whether any retrospective approvals will be required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs,to detail any oversight mechanisms in place to ensure that cross-border bodies, such as the Loughs Agency, maintain adequate control over staffing and prevent unauthorized expansions beyond approved complements.
answer_text: In relation to Loughs Agency my Department has a number of established arrangements in place for providing oversight. These include an agreed Financial Memorandum which sets out the governance and accountability arrangements between my Department and the Agency; joint quarterly assurance meetings; and Loughs Agency’s budgets being agreed as part of the business planning process (approved by Sponsor Department Ministers, Finance Ministers and North South Ministerial Council). My Department is currently engaged with Lough Agency’s in relation to the Agency’s staff complement, this is in conjunction with the Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment and North South Ministerial Council Joint Secretariat.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any arrangements Translink has in place for providing free or discounted transport in or to parades.
answer_text: Translink do not and have not operated any free trains or buses to/from Parades.  Translink does however use specific promotional buses to promote and showcase its services at a range of events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the decision by South Central Ambulance Service to use the Good Smartphone Activated Medics app to message volunteers within 500 metres of an incident, for his assessment of the potential to extend a similar scheme to Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the termination of temporary contracts and promotions at the Loughs Agency, including those for roles not designated as fishery officers, was implemented to address discrepancies in the staff complement.
answer_text: This is a matter for Loughs Agency as the employer. However, the Agency has confirmed that any recent staff changes have been made on the basis of operational and business requirements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any assessment made of the potential impact of the Public Health Agency’s guidance on illegal drug use on the enforcement of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971; and (ii) her assessment of whether the guidance risks sending mixed messages regarding the legality of controlled substances.
answer_text: A key aspect of the Public Health Agency (PHA) role is to protect the public and to provide advice and guidance on a range of issues, this includes the prevention and reduction of harms associated with drug use.  Addiction and drug misuse is unfortunately a reality of the society we live in. Whilst it is not for me to comment on the guidance or advice that the PHA issue, building awareness and informing the public of the risks, including how to mitigate against these, is vitally important.  I have been very clear that there is a risk associated with taking any drug, whether illicit or prescription, which has not been prescribed specifically for the individual. It only takes one episode to cause serious harm or put life at risk. The Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 sets out the law regarding the misuse of drugs and the various offences.  My role as Minister of Justice is understandably focussed on the criminal justice response: that includes enforcement of the law, and actions aimed at reducing the level of drug related harm and offending in Northern Ireland.   We must be mindful that there is no easy solution and the issues around drugs cut across the whole of the Executive – justice, health, communities and education. We can only succeed in improving outcomes if we see substance use in this broader context, working collectively together to target supplies, and educate and raise awareness to prevent individuals turning to drugs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of previous Freedom of Information responses indicating that any decision on the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) participating in events, such as Belfast Pride, requires consultation with both the First Minister and deputy First Minister, why there was no record of a veto or formal objection by the deputy First Minister regarding NICS participation in 2025.
answer_text: A record of the deputy First Minister’s comments is held in the Executive Office’s systems and has been disclosed by the Executive Office under the Freedom of Information Act.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the rationale as to why their Department holds a record of the deputy First Minister’s comment in relation to Belfast Pride, whilst the Department of Finance, which oversees the Northern Ireland Civil Service, holds no record of any objection or instruction.
answer_text: As is normal practice, the decision notice including the comment was copied to relevant officials in TEO.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the processes that exist for ensuring that any comments from the First Minister and deputy First Minister are formally communicated to departments; (ii) whether any guidance was issued to the Northern Ireland Civil Service regarding participation in Belfast Pride following the comment of the deputy First Minister on the issue; and (iii) whether any inconsistencies exist between departmental records, given that the Department of Finance has no record of the comment by the deputy First Minister.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the legality and potential public messaging impact of the Public Health Agency’s guidance stating if you choose to use drugs, dose low and go slow, given that possession and use of controlled substances remains illegal under the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971.
answer_text: The Executive’s Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’ has a vision that people in Northern Ireland are supported in the prevention and reduction of harm and stigma related to the use of alcohol and other drugs, have access to high quality treatment and support services, and will be empowered to maintain recovery.The strategy recognises the importance of reducing availability of, and accessibility to, illegal substance and we work collaboratively with the PSNI, the Organised Crime Taskforce Drug sub-group, and the Department of Justice in relation to several key actions in this respect.The Public Health Agency (PHA) are a key partner in the delivery of the strategy. The PHA takes a public health approach to the prevention, and reduction of harms associated with substance use. This approach enables the PHA to work with partners across Northern Ireland to promote key prevention messages to individuals, families, and communities so that the population are aware of the inherent dangers of substance use and can make informed choices to either delay personal engagement in substance use and or chose not to use substances at all.The PHA messaging often notes the health and legal risks of taking any illegal or illicit drug. However, the PHA also acknowledge the fact that illegal substances are available within Northern Ireland and a proportion of the population actively use these substances. Therefore, in line with public health approaches, the PHA use a range of methods to engage individuals that use substances to ensure they are aware of harm reduction methods. This reduces the risks of non-fatal overdose, deaths, sharing of equipment, the spread of blood born viruses and discarding drug paraphernalia in local communities, which actively protects the entire population. Alongside the harm reduction approaches individuals are actively supported to engage in treatment and recovery focused services.The PHA’s social media substance use messaging, alongside harm reduction advice, plays a critical supporting role as part of the overall prevention approach in Northern Ireland by enhancing public understanding of substance use and related risks, encouraging safe behaviours, and facilitating community-led prevention efforts. While the PHA does not have a role in enforcing the legislation concerning the possession of illegal drugs, they actively engage with the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) to ensure that both organisations support a joint policing and public health approach to substance use across Northern Ireland. Additionally, PHA also engage with the UK Border Force as appropriate in support of regional prevention activities.You may also wish to note that in the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Addiction Services in NI in 2020 notes that there is “clear evidence that harm reduction projects are a cost-effective way of tackling the harms related to alcohol and drug misuse. The Department should ensure the further develo

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given that buses cannot currently service the Northern Regional College on St Patrick’s Link Road, Ballymena, to outline any steps he has taken to ensure future development on the St Patrick’s regeneration site will incorporate public transport.
answer_text: Despite recent media reports, the Department for Infrastructure has confirmed that the St Patrick’s Link Road aligns to its guidance and bus services can use the road.However, the provision of bus services along the road is an operational decision for Translink and they have advised that there is not sufficient demand at present.  My officials are engaged with all stakeholders, including Translink, to monitor future public transport requirements.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the total expenditure by the Department on promoting the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme in Great Britain, as of the date of this question.
answer_text: Awareness raising of the Scheme in Great Britain has been undertaken by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB).The costs of awareness raising in Great Britain have therefore not been significant, as the focus has been on sharing material electronically to ensure the most efficient use of resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the actions taken by the Department to promote the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme in Great Britain, as of the date of this question.
answer_text: Awareness raising of the Scheme in Great Britain has been undertaken by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB).The VPB has developed a targeted awareness campaign and prepared information packs that will be rolled out to a range of stakeholders, political representatives and welfare groups throughout Great Britain and further afield in the coming weeks and months. This includes local Councils across England, Scotland and Wales, Victim Support, Victims’ and Veterans’ Commissioners, Church and UK Government networks, to reach applicants who may live across the world.  The VPB has also continued to engage with the wider Veterans’ community by attending the quarterly Veterans Commissioner’s Road Shows.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26122/22-27, (i) whether he will publish the professional guidance; (ii) to detail the number of occasions on which efforts were made to preserve the life of a child since the changes to our abortion laws, broken down by hospital; and (iii) to detail how many of these attempts were successful.
answer_text: My Department does not publish professional guidance; this information is available on the websites of the relevant professional bodies.As outlined in my response to AQW 26122/22-27, the Department does not require Trusts to collate this information.As above.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the total costs incurred since the Loughs Agency staff establishment went above approved limits including but not limited to (i) salary; (ii) employer National Insurance; (iii) employer pension contributions; (iv) equipment; (v) training; (vi) uniform; (vii) personal protective equipment; and (viii) other costs.
answer_text: My Departmental officials are working with officials in the Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment, North South Ministerial Council and Loughs Agency to seek clarity on the interpretation and application of the Agency’s staffing complement figure.  As such we are unable to provide the information you have sought at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the uptake of the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme in Great Britain.
answer_text: Since the Scheme opened in August 2021, 12,360 applications have been received with more than £119million paid to eligible applicants, offering meaningful recognition of the harm they have suffered.   With the Scheme now in its final year, and the application process due to close on 30 August 2026, we strongly encourage anyone who may be eligible, to submit an application.The Victims’ Payments Board has developed a targeted awareness campaign that will be rolled out in the coming weeks and months. The campaign aims to ensure that as many eligible individuals as possible are made aware of the opportunity to apply to the Scheme before it closes.To date, almost 900 applications have been received from outside of Northern Ireland, including 673 from Great Britain and 98 from the Republic of Ireland. Applications have also been received from South Africa, Canada, the USA, Australia and New Zealand, amongst others, and it is hoped that this campaign will further extend the reach to potential applicants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26979/22-27, for a breakdown of the 102 classified as other.
answer_text: The information provided in response to AQW 26979/22-27 was derived from the Hospital Inpatient System and identified using ICD-10 codes for the primary diagnosis field of the Hospital Inpatient System.  Primary diagnosis codes are the dominant reason for the patient’s hospital admission.  The ‘other’ category can be broken down further as follows: Primary Diagnosis CodeNumber of Women1,2O04.3 - Medical abortion, incomplete, with other and unspecified complications9O04.4 - Medical abortion, incomplete, without complication3 88O04.8 - Medical abortion, complete or unspecified, with other and unspecified complications5Total 102Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Activity Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH, NINotes:1Only admissions within the Acute Services and Maternity & Child Health Programmes of Care have been included. Admissions are approximated using deaths and discharges and counted in the period that they were discharged. Patients who were treated in the Emergency Department, but not admitted, are not included in these figures.2Data for 2023/24 includes data extracted from encompass (a new electronic patient record system) for South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) from 9 November 2023.  This subset of the data is classed as "Official Statistics in Development" whilst undergoing evaluation.  As a result, users should exercise some caution with this data.For discharges in encompass, where a patient transfers from one hospital to another within the same HSCT, this will count as a transfer, rather than separate admissions and discharges as it would have in the legacy system, and therefore the patient will be counted under the hospital of their final discharging episode during this stay.3‘O04.4 - Medical abortion, incomplete, without complication’ includes pregnancy with retained products of conception (RPOC) and therefore is included under the assumption that RPOC is considered a complication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of (i) why job creation in the Mid and East Antrim local council area has been comparatively low; and (ii) what steps Invest Northern Ireland is taking to address this imbalance.
answer_text: Over the past five years Invest NI has assisted 482 investments, including job creation projects, in the Mid & East Antrim area, which accounts for 5% of its total investments. This performance is comparable to the number of businesses in the area and is greater than both the percentage of exporters and exports. Invest NI is working closely with a range of stakeholders to promote entrepreneurship and business growth, with the aim of increasing the number of businesses, boosting business investment, supporting more businesses to look to markets outside of the North, accelerating investments in innovation and skills, and supporting businesses to reduce their carbon emissions.    To deliver against its business strategy, Invest NI is implementing a new regional approach that is increasing its capacity and capability across all areas of the North to seek out, identify and engage with more companies than ever before. This involves attracting businesses that are new to Invest NI, enhancing the support, advice and guidance it provides and implementing a new purposeful partnership approach across its regional stakeholders and partners to align and maximise its impact. To support this, it has increased the number of staff in its regional offices, including its Ballymena office, providing additional capacity to generate new business and realise its vision of a more globally competitive, regionally balanced, sustainable, and prosperous economy across all parts of the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what metrics Invest Northern Ireland uses to actively seek out businesses and identify them for support, as referenced on page 5 of its Performance Report 2020-21 to 2024-25.
answer_text: Invest NI has set a target to support 450 ‘New to Invest NI’ businesses to implement productive investment projects, over the course of its 3-year business strategy period.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of applicants from Great Britain to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme, as of the date of this question.
answer_text: As of week commencing 8 September 2025, almost £107m has been paid to eligible applicants since the Scheme opened for applications, offering meaningful recognition of the harm they have suffered. More than 11,800 applications have been submitted to the Scheme. Of that total number, 651 have been received from applicants based in Great Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will review the current composition of the Nutrients Action Plan stakeholder forum to ensure all key stakeholders are represented before the terms of reference are finalised.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of why there has been such a significant imbalance in the number of new jobs created through Invest Northern Ireland supported projects across local council areas, during the period from 2020-21 to 2024-25.
answer_text: Growth and investment trends can often be cyclical in nature, and at a more local level, influenced by the actions of a small number of larger employers.  This can result in fluctuations in investment variables, such as jobs, from year to year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why AgriSearch was not offered full membership of the Nutrients Action Plan stakeholder forum, given its recognised role in agricultural research and analysis.
answer_text: Following an introductory meeting of the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Stakeholder Task and Finish Group, I recognised that the Group may need to draw on additional expertise. For that reason, I have asked my Chief Scientific Advisor’s Office to establish a Science Sub-group which will include representation from AFBI, AgriSearch and other relevant organisations. This Sub-group will assist the work of the Task and Finish Group with ensuring that the final NAP proposals are scientifically robust and evidence-based.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why the Northern Ireland Food and Drink Association was excluded from membership of the Nutrients Action Plan stakeholder forum.
answer_text: Following an introductory meeting of the NAP Stakeholder Task and Finish Group, I reviewed its membership and I have extended this to include the Northern Ireland Food and Drink Association.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the criteria used to determine the membership of the new Nutrients Action Plan stakeholder forum.
answer_text: I have invited representatives from farming, agri-food and environmental groups to the new Nutrients Action Programme Stakeholder Task and Finish Group, to ensure representation across key sectors. My Department will keep membership under review and may invite additional participants as needed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has assessed the potential financial impact on the agri-food sector of proceeding with a Nutrients Action Plan developed without the direct involvement of organisations such as AgriSearch and the Northern Ireland Food and Drink Association.
answer_text: My Department published a Regulatory Impact Assessment (RIA) as part of the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) 2026 – 2029 consultation earlier this year. This provided an economic assessment of the proposed measures.  This RIA will be reviewed and updated when the NAP measures have been finalised. In addition, a draft Economic Impact Assessment on the proposed NAP measures will be developed, with input from relevant organisations, as appropriate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration given to including representation from the Department for the Economy on the Nutrients Action Plan stakeholder forum, given the potential economic consequences of new proposals.
answer_text: Following an introductory meeting of the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Stakeholder Task and Finish Group, I have given consideration to the membership of the Group. It is comprised of organisations that represent a range of interests across farming, agri-food and the environment as well as DAERA officials. A draft Economic Impact Assessment on the proposed NAP measures, will be developed and if any policy development falls under the remit of another Government Department, the relevant officials will be invited to contribute to the work of the Task and Finish Group.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of his comments in the News Letter on 8 September 2025, to provide further clarification on his answers to (i) AQW 17214/22-27; and (ii) AQW 17832/22-27.
answer_text: Further to the previous questions, and in light of the UK Supreme Court judgment (16 April 2025), I asked my officials to consider the same and revert to me with a policy statement for my consideration. I articulated the basis of my policy to the Northern Ireland Assembly (12 May) and following further consideration of both officials’ and legal advice received over the course of the summer, I concluded that the Education Authority guidance neither reflected departmental policy nor was it consistent with the law in Northern Ireland.Consequently, I wrote to the Education Authority (5 September) to ask that the abovementioned guidance was immediately withdrawn.I am satisfied that the policy statement I have issued (8 September) on this matter reflects what is in the best interest of all children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the terms of reference of the Cass assessment of gender identity services announced on 4 September 2025.
answer_text: I refer the member to his Priority Written Question AQW 30516/22-27 of 10 September 2025.I asked my officials to approach Baroness Hilary Cass to conduct an assessment of Gender Identity Services in Northern Ireland because of the volume of inaccurate public commentary on the service, not least by elected representatives.  Baroness Cass will be accompanied by a small group of expert clinicians who I believe are eminently qualified to provide advice to us.The assessment will also specifically consider the move to a Regional Lifespan Gender Service in Northern Ireland, and the greater emphasis on Multi-disciplinary Team working, which is a central tenet of the Cass Review. I believe an independent expert assessment of the service would go some way to address much of the regrettably somewhat misinformed commentary over recent months. The specific detail of the Terms of Reference for the assessment will be discussed and agreed with Baroness Cass at a meeting with my officials in October. The focus of the assessment will be on the service for children and young people initially, however, future consideration will be given to the adult elements of the service, if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why the Cass assessment was announced in September 2025 after £806,000 of public money was allocated to the gender identity service in July 2025.
answer_text: I refer the member to his Priority Written Question AQW 30516/22-27 of 10 September 2025.I asked my officials to approach Baroness Hilary Cass to conduct an assessment of Gender Identity Services in Northern Ireland because of the volume of inaccurate public commentary on the service, not least by elected representatives.  Baroness Cass will be accompanied by a small group of expert clinicians who I believe are eminently qualified to provide advice to us.The assessment will also specifically consider the move to a Regional Lifespan Gender Service in Northern Ireland, and the greater emphasis on Multi-disciplinary Team working, which is a central tenet of the Cass Review. I believe an independent expert assessment of the service would go some way to address much of the regrettably somewhat misinformed commentary over recent months. The specific detail of the Terms of Reference for the assessment will be discussed and agreed with Baroness Cass at a meeting with my officials in October. The focus of the assessment will be on the service for children and young people initially, however, future consideration will be given to the adult elements of the service, if required.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the cost of the refurbishment of the Blue Flax canteen in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: While the majority of work was completed for the Blue Flax restaurant to reopen on time for the resumption of Assembly business after the summer recess on Monday 8 September 2025, a number of final elements are to be addressed and put in place. Additionally, other planned elements were not ultimately required.  Therefore, the final cost will not be confirmed until the project is formally closed following sign-off with the contractors and designers in the coming weeks.I have asked Assembly Commission officials to write to you with the final figure once it is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline why the Rainbow Project and Focus: The Identity Trust were permitted to assist in designing a new gender identity clinic.
answer_text: In March 2019, the Department of Health asked the then Health and Social Care Board (HSCB) to conduct a review of the Gender Identity Service, to address concerns on the delivery of services at the Brackenburn Clinic. In September 2019, as part of this process, a Gender Identity Service Pathway Review Group was established and chaired by the HSCB, and liaised closely with NHS England colleagues. Two engagement events were held, where nominations for limited service user representatives on the Service Pathway Review Group were taken. A Gender Identity Liaison Panel, which sat under the main Pathway Review Group, was also established to enable those with lived experience to provide input to the review. There was significant engagement undertaken to help inform the review, including engagement and listening events, online surveys, online meetings, engagement across Great Britain and the Republic of Ireland, and meetings with the community and voluntary sector. While service users were one element of the co-design of the new service, the review was steered by expert clinicians.It is important to note individual groups such as the Rainbow Project and Focus: The Identity Trust were not provided with specific details of the service development, however members of the Gender Identity Review Group and the Gender Identity Liaison Panel, who actively contributed to the co-design and provided input into this service development, may have also been members of such groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the award of £806,000 for gender identity services in July 2025, whether the findings of the Cass Review will influence future funding and policy decisions.
answer_text: Funding of £806k allows a Regional Lifespan Gender Service, alongside an appropriately resourced endocrine service, to be established within Belfast Trust, bringing together the existing CAMHS Knowing Our Identity (KOI) Team and the Adult Gender Service into one model of care, which would encompass both a child and adult service, under a single management structure. The funding includes a dedicated Service Manager for the Lifespan model. The role will include developing governance and policy underpinnings for the newly established service, implementing learning from the Cass Report, co-ordinating the final approach to the research element of the Cass recommendations, outlining the resource required, and working nationally to develop procedures to facilitate data collection.Baroness Cass has agreed to undertake an assessment of our service against her Review Recommendations for gender identity services for children and young people. While she will primarily assess the service against the Cass Review Recommendations from her final report published in April 2024, Baroness Cass will also offer training and co-design support to the staff involved in delivering the service. I am content with the process undertaken to develop the revised service which involved significant engagement with expert clinicians, both here and in GB, those with lived experience, families and sector representatives; this included:In March 2019, the Department of Health asked the then Health and Social Care Board (HSCB) to conduct a review of the Gender Identity Service, to address concerns on the delivery of services at the Brackenburn Clinic.In September 2019, a Gender Identity Service Pathway Review Group was established and chaired by the HSCB and liaised closely with NHS England colleagues. A Gender Identity Liaison Panel, which sat under the main Pathway Review Group, was also established to enable those with lived experience to provide input to the review.  There was significant engagement including engagement and listening events, online surveys, online meetings, engagement across Great Britain and the Republic of Ireland, and meetings with the community and voluntary sector. The Pathway Review Group drafted fourteen objectives to underpin any new service model.  Engagement on the objectives with interested individuals and groups was held in 2020 and were endorsed by the group as the benchmark for any new service model.  It was agreed that the review should include services for adults as well as children and young people (Knowing Our Identity service).  While service users were one element of the co-design of the new service, the review was steered by expert clinicians.Following the review report, the Department requested that officials consider a more cost effective and affordable solution. Given ongoing challenging budget constraints this proposal for a Regional Lifespan Gender Service bringing together Children’s and Adult Gender Services would be an important first s

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of whether major funding commitments are made before expert evidence is sought; and (ii) how he plans to ensure that public confidence is maintained in the integrity of health policy decision-making.
answer_text: Funding of £806k allows a Regional Lifespan Gender Service, alongside an appropriately resourced endocrine service, to be established within Belfast Trust, bringing together the existing CAMHS Knowing Our Identity (KOI) Team and the Adult Gender Service into one model of care, which would encompass both a child and adult service, under a single management structure. While she will primarily assess the service against the Cass Review Recommendations from her final report published in April 2024, Baroness Cass will also offer training and co-design support to the staff involved in delivering the service. A Gender Identity Liaison Panel, which sat under the main Pathway Review Group, was also established to enable those with lived experience to provide input to the review. Given ongoing challenging budget constraints this proposal for a Regional Lifespan Gender Service bringing together Children’s and Adult Gender Services would be an important first s

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why Baroness Hilary Cass was invited to review gender identity services only after he had already made a decision to allocate £806,000 of additional funding towards the expansion of these services.
answer_text: I asked my officials to approach Baroness Hilary Cass to conduct an assessment of Gender Identity Services in Northern Ireland as she led the national Independent Review of Gender Identity Services for Children and Young People for NHS England.  Baroness Cass will be accompanied by a small group of expert clinicians who I believe are eminently qualified to provide advice to us.The assessment will also specifically consider the move to a Regional Lifespan Gender Service in Northern Ireland, and the greater emphasis on MDT working. My understanding is that that is the direction of travel advocated for by Dr Cass in her landmark national review but I look forward to her assessment.  I believe an independent expert assessment of the service would go some way to address much of the regrettably somewhat misinformed commentary over recent months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline why St Patrick’s Link Road was approved and constructed without ensuring it was suitable for bus services, given that bus access is a standard requirement for roads serving educational and community facilities.
answer_text: The planning applications for the link road were submitted by the Department for Communities and the department acted in its role as a statutory consultee through the process.The design of the St Patrick’s Link aligns with the guidance provided within the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges and within my Department’s Bus Stop Design Guide. The provision of any new public transport services in the area will be an operational decision for Translink in response to emerging demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline why the Rainbow Project was able to announce the £806,000 funding for the gender identity service before his Department.
answer_text: Funding of £806k allows a Regional Lifespan Gender Service, alongside an appropriately resourced endocrine service, to be established within Belfast Trust, bringing together the existing CAMHS Knowing Our Identity (KOI) Team and the Adult Gender Service into one model of care, which would encompass both a child and adult service, under a single management structure. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what steps will be taken to address the lack of lay-bys along St Patrick’s Link Road, which currently prevents bus services from operating safely to the new Northern Regional College campus.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure has advised that the absence of a lay-by on the road does not prevent the future provision of bus services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department plans to retrofit bus pick up and drop off facilities at (i) the new Northern Regional College Ballymena campus; and (ii) other key points along the link road.
answer_text: The design of the St Patrick’s Link aligns with the guidance provided within the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges (DMRB) and within the Department’s Bus Stop Design Guide. Bus pick-up and drop-off facilities can be located along the road but the provision of any new public transport services in the area will be an operational decision for Translink in response to emerging demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what (i) funding; or (ii) support will be provided to enable buses to operate safely along the St Patrick’s Link Road, Ballymena.
answer_text: St Patrick’s Link Road was funded by the Department for Communities as part of the wider St Patrick’s Regeneration Programme.My officials are routinely in contact with Translink to consider the suitability of existing bus facilities and any requirement for future public transport provision.The provision of new public transport services in the area would be an operational decision for Translink in response to emerging demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what discussions took place with Translink during the planning and construction of St Patrick’s Link Road, Ballymena, to ensure that the road would be accessible for bus services.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure has advised that the St. Patrick’s Link Road was designed and developed in accordance with the relevant DfI guidelines.  DfI is the statutory consultee on these issues and was consulted.  Translink is not a statutory consultee.In 2023, Translink confirmed to DfI that they had no plans to alter any of their existing bus routes and indicated that, as the site develops over the next few years, they would keep it under review. Translink said that, if there were a demand, they would consider how that could be serviced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown of the Programme Board oversight for St Patrick’s Link Road, including the role of college representatives and other stakeholders in ensuring the road meets public transport requirements.
answer_text: The Programme Board for the St Patrick’s regeneration project is comprised of stakeholders with an interest in delivering projects at the site. This includes representatives from Mid & East Antrim Borough Council, PSNI, Northern Regional College, NI Housing Executive and Radius Housing. During the design, development and construction phase of the St Patrick’s Link Road this included adviser representation from the Department for Infrastructure (DfI). Stakeholders are responsible for the planning applications of their own schemes, including undertaking transport assessments where applicable. My Department remains committed to working with all stakeholders to explore opportunities for public transport provision at the site. When the road was completed in 2023 Translink indicated they would keep the site under review and would consider future provision if there is demand. My officials will be meeting with programme board members, DfI and Translink shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of the effectiveness of the current legislation for Houses in Multiple Occupation.
answer_text: A review of the HMO Act was conducted in 2021. The findings highlighted mainly operational issues and teething problems with the administration of the Scheme, including issues with the IT system and the impact of the lockdown on services.I will be making an oral statement to the Assembly in due course on the matter of HMOs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the official business conducted by the deputy First Minister during her trip to London which involved attending Wimbledon.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister attended in her capacity as dFM.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail why, in light of the fact that the deputy First Minister was representing Northern Ireland when she accepted an invitation to Wimbledon, there was no mention of it on the Executive Office's social media accounts.
answer_text: All activity of Ministers is not reflected on social media.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the quantities of chemicals used in the treatment of water, sourced from Lough Neagh at each of the water treatment works, over the last five years, broken down by chemical (i) type; and (ii) year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 18866/22-27, to detail (i) the length of time it took to recover each of the benefit fraud sums listed; and (ii) any steps being taken to improve early detection and reduce the scale of individual overpayments.
answer_text: (i) Pursuant to AQW 18866/22-27, the time taken to recover each of the benefit fraud sums listed is provided in the table below.AQW 18866/22-27Financial YearSingle largest sum of benefit fraud recovered from one person*Time taken to repay (days)**2018/19£46,646.7882  2019/20£20,013.38177 2020/21£9,850.83327 2021/22£19,465.811 2022/23£29,198.144 2023/24£36,520.00341 2024/25(@ 17 December 2024)£51,636.26205 Data contained in the table is classed as administrative management information which is not subject to the same level of quality assurance as accredited statistics. *The total benefit fraud overpayment value for these individuals may be greater as the amount in the table reflects the highest amount repaid by an individual in that year**The time taken to repay the total benefit fraud overpayment for each of these individuals may be greater as the number of days quoted is the time taken to repay the amount quoted in that year (ii) My Department has a robust strategy to prevent and detect benefit fraud. IT system verification and checking regimes are in place to ensure benefit payments are accurate and cases are corrected at the earliest opportunity, thus preventing overpayments of benefit accumulating. Checks include the verification of identity and asking new or existing claimants to provide additional information to verify their declared circumstances. The onus is on the claimant to declare their true circumstances at the start and during the lifespan of the claim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 28372/22-27, to detail how each trip outlined in his previous answer delivered towards this end.
answer_text: The International Summit on the Teaching Profession (ISTP), held this year in Iceland between 24 and 26 March 2025, brings together education ministers, union leaders and other teacher leaders from high-performing and rapidly improving education systems to review how best to improve the quality of teachers, teaching and learning. I was part of a wider UK Delegation which included both government and Trade Union representatives from England, Wales and Scotland.  The ISTP in Iceland offered a unique opportunity for Ministers, educators, policymakers and union leaders to engage in high-level dialogue on the future of education. The summit, co-hosted by the Icelandic Government, Education International and the OECD, and focused on critical themes such as early childhood education, teacher well-being and child-centred learning. Like other participants, I benefited from exposure to discussion on global best practices, collaborative policy discussions and the chance to influence international education agendas across a range of plenary sessions.  The Northern Ireland delegation were able to contribute at these sessions.  The emphasis on equity, professional autonomy and mental health support at the ISTP ensured that attendees return with actionable insights to improve both teaching conditions and pupil outcomes. Northern Ireland agreed three country commitments jointly with Trade Union representatives, which we will report on in 2026. Moreover, the summit provides a powerful platform for networking and advocacy. ISTP 2025 aims to foster meaningful connections and partnerships that can drive systemic change. I was able to hold a range of bi-lateral discussions with representatives from other governments.  I also had the opportunity to visit an Icelandic school and learn more about their education system. The visit to Washington, D.C. in March 2024 coincided with the annual St. Patrick’s Day engagements and formed part of a broader Northern Ireland Executive delegation led by the First Minister and deputy First Minister. The purpose of the visit was to strengthen Northern Ireland’s international relationships, promote economic opportunities, and engage with key U.S. stakeholders across government, business, and education.The visit also allowed for bilateral discussions with U.S. education leaders and policymakers, including a roundtable on shared challenges such as early years and childcare. Panel discussions such as these provided a focus on the challenge of affordable childcare, leveraging support to help us address this challenge through engagement and access to expertise to assist in identifying and bringing the required interventions forward here in Northern Ireland.During my visit to Washington D.C., I also had the valuable opportunity to meet in person with Professor Victoria Graf from Loyola Marymount University in California, a globally recognised expert in the field of special education.  Our discussion provided insight into her latest research,

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to introducing a rent control cap to ensure rent remains affordable for families.
answer_text: As mandated by the Assembly, my Department commissioned and published a report on the impact of a rent freeze/cut in the private rented sector in NI. The report clearly outlined the potential negative impacts of these type of rent controls. It highlighted that a large proportion of landlords may exit the private rental market, and this would result in a greater shortage of available private rented properties in an already stretched market. Earlier this year, I commenced the power to restrict the frequency of rent increases to once in every 12 months with a 3-month written notice. This will assist low-income tenants by providing an assurance that they will not face multiple or unexpected rent increases over a short period of time while also allowing landlords to increase rent to meet their costs. NIHE rent increase requests are considered by my Department on an annual basis, with regard to the need to achieve a sustainable level of rental income for the landlord and affordable levels of rent for tenants. My Department has no powers in relation to rent setting or rent control for Registered Housing Associations, with the exception of a very small number of tenancies to which Article 8 of the Housing (NI) Order 1992 applies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to estimate the amount of funding required to bring Rasharkin's NI Water wastewater infrastructure up to a standard which creates capacity to allow for new housing and supports economic development in the village.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, my officials have sought the relevant information and NI Water has advised that: The Wastewater Treatment Works serving Rasharkin currently has available capacity to facilitate development, therefore, NI Water has no plans to undertake an upgrade of the Wastewater Treatment Works.However, there are issues within the wastewater network in Rasharkin.  Therefore, NI Water may be unable to accept additional connections within certain areas, as to do so would increase the risk of out of sewer flooding to homes, businesses, schools etc. and also increase environmental pollution.  Any planning consultations received within some areas of the catchment will be assessed on an individual basis depending on the location and the level of constraint that may exist.  NI Water is not in a position to provide costs associated with any potential upgrades required at this time.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has to cap the number of Houses in Multiple Occupation in residential areas.
answer_text: The responsibility for regulating HMOs including a licensing scheme rests with local councils through the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (NI) 2016, enacted by the Department for Communities. Councils are also responsible for operating effective development management in respect of planning applications for HMOs and in doing so, the provisions of their LDP, regional strategic planning policy, and all other material planning considerations must be taken into account. HMOs are generally considered to be a local planning matter best addressed though the local policies of councils under the plan-led system and in the context of councils separate HMO regulatory powers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has to ensure local councils develop a House in Multiple Occupation policy as part of their Local Development Plan.
answer_text: The content of Local Development Plans are a matter for Council’s to determine, in line with their onw local contexts, and taking account of regional strategic planning policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any direct or indirect (i) funding; (ii) support; and (iii) form of assistance, provided by his Department in connection with the PhD research project entitled Drug Consumption Amongst Members of LGBTQ Communities in Northern Ireland, being undertaken through Ulster University, including any partnership arrangements with the Rainbow Project or third-party funders.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead on the implementation of the cross-departmental strategy, Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, to prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use in Northern Ireland. The Strategy notes the importance of ensuring that we have timely access to evidence and research on substance use issues. Officials work collaboratively with the universities here to understand current areas of research and advise on evidence gaps. The project referenced addresses an important issue which lacks research in NI and, while no funding has been provided by the Department for the project in question, the Department are on an advisory group for this research and have provided a letter of support in relation to the funding application. Such local academic research activity plays an important role in informing our strategic delivery and in helping us target substance use services towards vulnerable and at-risk groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to estimate the amount of funding required to bring Broughshane's NI Water wastewater infrastructure up to a standard which creates capacity to allow for new housing and supports economic development in the village.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, my officials have sought the relevant information and NI Water has advised the following for each of the locations listed above. Kells & Connor, Cullybackey, Ahoghill and Broughshane are all served by Ballymena Wastewater Treatment Works which currently has available capacity to facilitate development. Therefore, NI Water has no plans to undertake an upgrade of the Wastewater Treatment Works.In Portglenone, the Wastewater Treatment Works serving Portglenone currently has available capacity to facilitate development, therefore NI Water has no plans to undertake an upgrade of the Wastewater Treatment Works.However, some areas of the wastewater network within these towns may be constrained due to the presence of storm overflow assets that are, in some cases, classified as Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharges (UIDs).  Therefore, NI Water is unable to accept additional connections within certain areas, as to do so would increase the risk of out of sewer flooding to homes, businesses, schools etc. and also increase environmental pollution.  Any planning consultations received within some areas of the catchment will be assessed on an individual basis depending on the location and the level of constraint that may exist.  As the extent and detail of any capital interventions are still being investigated, NI Water is not in a position to provide costs associated with any upgrades at this time.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, my officials have sought the relevant information and NI Water has advised the following for each of the locations listed above. Kells & Connor, Cullybackey, Ahoghill and Broughshane are all served by Ballymena Wastewater Treatment Works which currently has available capacity to facilitate development. Therefore, NI Water has no plans to undertake an upgrade of the Wastewater Treatment Works.In Portglenone, the Wastewater Treatment Works serving Portglenone currently has available capacity to facilitate development, therefore NI Water has no plans to undertake an upgrade of the Wastewater Treatment Works.However, some areas of the wastewater network within these towns may be constrained due to the presence of storm overflow assets that are, in some cases, classified as Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharges (UIDs).  Therefore, NI Water is unable to accept additional connections within certain areas, as to do so would increase the risk of out of sewer flooding to homes, businesses, schools etc. and also increase environmental pollution.  Any planning consultations received within some areas of the catchment will be assessed on an individual basis depending on the location and the level of constraint that may exist.  As the extent and detail of any capital interventions are still being investigated, NI Water is not in a position to provide costs associated with any upgrades at this time.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to estimate the amount of funding required to bring Cloughmills’ NI Water wastewater infrastructure up to a standard which creates capacity to allow for new housing and supports economic development in the village.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water however my officials have sought the relevant information. NI Water has advised the following for each of the locations listed above.Clough - The wastewater treatment works (WwTW) serving Clough is currently at capacity, therefore NI Water is unable to accept any further connections within this catchment as this would increase the risk of environmental pollution and non-compliance with the Water Order Consent for the works. The upgrade of Clough WwTW is not included within the PC21 Investment Programme (2021/22 – 2026/27). Therefore, NI Water does not have detailed costs for the upgrade of the works at this time.Cloughmills - The wastewater treatment works (WwTW) serving Cloughmills currently has available capacity to facilitate development, therefore, NI Water has no plans to undertake an upgrade of the WwTW. NI Water has also advised that any planning consultations received within this catchment would be assessed on an individual basis.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com .
