question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the working from home policy for office-based staff at the Loughs Agency.
answer_text: Loughs Agency’s Working From Home Policy allows employees to apply for one of three types of home working:Remote / Mobile Working – this allows staff to alternate between their contractual office base and other Loughs Agency offices or carry out work in the field and on occasion from home.Occasional Working from Home – on an ad hoc basis as required.Regular Working from Home – hybrid working with a mix of home and office working on a regular, agreed basis.The Home Working Policy cannot be applied to all roles within the Agency and all arrangements are subject to business requirements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people that have had to wait in (i) beds; (ii) wheelchairs; and (iii) trollies in corridors for more than 12 hours in each week of the last 12 months, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data on the number of patients who have had to wait in (i) beds; (ii) wheelchairs; and (iii) trollies in corridors for more than 12 hours in an Emergency Department (ED), each week.  However, we do monitor our waiting times based on 4-hour and 12-hour performance metrics in EDs; 95% of patients attending any Type 1, 2 or 3 ED should be either treated and discharged home, or admitted within four hours of arrival in the department, and no patient attending any ED should wait longer than twelve hours.  Table 1  Number of patients who waited over 12 hours in an ED from July 2024 to June 2025Site/MonthJulAugSepOctNovDecJanFebMarAprMayJunAltnagelvin1,0661,1051,1811,2461,1741,1921,1401,2931,1731,2861,3591,158Antrim1,5101,4371,5151,5951,9152,1201,7341,7401,7901,5831,3601,576Causeway581555588580567666591518580598645590Craigavon1,3551,3401,5931,8411,7751,7991,7291,5771,7231,6982,0581,818Daisy Hill569567671752657715713572587705945796Mater674618759816670711733616586534603486RBHSC459119396273540112829RVH2,1422,0522,1922,2761,9902,0021,8891,8151,8251,8471,9201,880South West535507487538564647627588543579709609Ulster1,8102,1122,1182,3641,9962,2871,8941,7172,0462,1221,8781,899Total10,29310,34511,17712,06811,43412,28611,11010,50510,92910,99911,54510,895Table 1 shows the number of patients who waited longer than 12 hours in an ED, in each hospital for the last 12 months.  This information is not available broken down by week.To improve waiting times for patients in EDs, a significant programme of work has been undertaken to provide alternative, appropriate services for patients such as Urgent Care Centres, in all our hospitals.  NIAS has also been working with Trusts to put in place pathways for patients to access a range of services as alternatives to going to an ED, including rapid access clinics and ambulatory services, where this is appropriate.  I am determined to find better ways to work as a system to tackle waiting times in EDs.  That is why I initiated a series of winter planning workshops to identify solutions to the system flow issues that we collectively face.  The workshop saw attendance from a diverse group of stakeholders within our system, including Royal Colleges, clinicians and other frontline services.  All our system leaders are engaged in this initiative. By September 2025, I anticipate having a plan for the year ahead that will aim to improve outcomes for the next winter period, including improvements to patient waiting times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) why the salmon hatchery at Loughs Agency headquarters was deemed to be not essential at a time when the Atlantic salmon is an endangered species; and (ii) the business need for demolishing the hatchery and building office space where the hatchery and the vehicle garage once stood.
answer_text: Scientific evidence shows that the risks associated with the stocking of Atlantic salmon from hatcheries outweigh any benefits. Therefore, Loughs Agency does not consider stocking from hatcheries as a remediation measure. The redundant hatchery equipment at Loughs Agency was removed in 2015. A business need for improved office accommodation was later identified as the previous space was damp, unsustainable and inefficient. In 2021/22 the site was developed to provide improved office space.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027 will impact Northern Ireland’s reliance on imported energy, food and manufactured goods.
answer_text: The measures in the draft Climate Action Plan do not require a reduction in output from the Northern Ireland agri-food sector and therefore do not change the position as regards imported food goods.  Energy and manufactured goods fall under the responsibility of the Minister for the Economy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any part of the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027 has been developed in response to EU directives or international obligations arising from the Northern Ireland Protocol or Windsor Framework.
answer_text: The draft Climate Action Plan contains references to various items of EU legislation with which Northern Ireland must continue to align under the terms of the Windsor Framework (formerly the Northern Ireland Protocol). The draft Plan does not, however, by itself implement, augment or amend any obligations arising under the Framework. Any such obligations will continue to be implemented and legislated for under existing processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any economic modelling was conducted to assess any potential negative impacts of the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027 on small businesses and farming families.
answer_text: Each policy and proposal within the draft Climate Action Plan (CAP) undertook a Financial, Social and Economic Impact assessment (FSEIA). The FSEIA covered a range of socio-economic indicators, such as impacts on income, employment, access to services and the wider economy. The overarching FSEIA at draft CAP level identified that policies and proposals across various sectors are expected to have measurable economic impacts, with many yielding substantial benefits for Northern Ireland. It also noted that agricultural policies and proposals aimed at increasing the productivity of dairy and beef cattle farmers may have a positive impact on income.These assessments have been published as part of the draft Climate Action Plan consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to set out the estimated cost to taxpayers and consumers of decarbonisation policies in the residential, transport and energy sectors between 2023 and 2027.
answer_text: Between 2023/24 and 2026/27, the estimated capital investment required from the Executive for these sectors as set out in the draft Climate Action Plan is as follows:Residential sector - £70mTransport - £341m Energy - £1m.In terms of cost to consumers, this information is not held by my department given that decarbonisation policies within the residential, transport and energy sectors are not part of my department’s remit. Within the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027 these sectors are led by:Residential – Department for CommunitiesTransport – Department for InfrastructureEnergy – Department for the Economy
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the quantification exercise used to project emissions reductions took into account the socioeconomic cost to rural households and agricultural producers.
answer_text: The quantification exercise published at Annex A to the draft Climate Action Plan (CAP) assessed the impact of policies and proposals on projected greenhouse gas emissions only.Social and economic factors and rural impacts were also assessed and published as part of the draft CAP consultation.A Financial, Social and Economic Impact Assessment was carried out at a policy and proposal level and collated into an overall assessment, which was published as Annex H (i) - Financial, Social and Economic Impact Assessment of the draft Climate Action Plan. At an overarching CAP level, the cumulative and interactive financial, social and economic effects of policies and proposals across all sectors were assessed and published as Annex H (ii) Overarching Financial, Social and Economic Impact Assessment of the draft Climate Action Plan. In relation to rural impacts, Rural Needs Impact Assessments were carried out at a policy and proposal level and collated into an overall assessment and published as Annex H (iv) Rural Needs Impact Assessment of the draft Climate Action Plan. At an overarching CAP level, the cumulative and interactive rural impacts of policies and proposals from different sectors were assessed and published as Annex H (v) - Overarching Rural Needs Impact Assessment of the draft Climate Action Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a breakdown of the £718 million capital investment referred to in the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027, including (i) the source of the funding; and (ii) the allocations by Department.
answer_text: The £718m capital investment represents the level of NI Executive investment required to deliver the policies and proposals within the draft Climate Action Plan. As such, this investment will be sourced from existing NI Executive allocations. Since the 2023/24 financial year, approximately £226m capital has already been invested through the implementation of CAP policies and proposals. Some of the expenditure will already be part of existing programmes and budgets. Not all of this investment is therefore new, or additional. All proposed spend will be dependent on normal budgetary decision-making, including business case development and evaluation. In line with the structure of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, the Climate Action Plan includes cost broken by sector rather than by Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has conducted any analysis of how the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027 will impact Northern Ireland’s competitiveness within the United Kingdom internal market.
answer_text: Each policy and proposal within the draft Climate Action Plan undertook an Economic Impact Assessment as part of the Financial, Social and Economic Impact assessment (FSEIA). These assessments were compiled into an overarching FSEIA, and the details have been published as part of the draft Climate Action Plan consultation. These assessments examined competitiveness, and the report concluded that “Competitiveness is expected to increase notably from policies and proposals in Energy Production and Supply, Waste Management, and Agriculture sectors. These sectors show the strongest potential for driving innovation and efficiency, supported by advancements in renewable energy technologies, sustainable agricultural practices, and waste-processing innovations. Together, these initiatives contribute to long-term economic growth by fostering resource optimisation, reducing costs, and enhancing the appeal of Northern Ireland businesses in global markets.”
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the external consultants and advisory bodies used in drafting the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027, including the total cost of their engagement.
answer_text: My department engaged the services of the external consultants to support work to assess impacts and who contributed to drafting the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027 through provision of reports which are included as part of the consultation documentation. The total cost of their engagement was £102,300. Organisation nameServices providedCost AECOMStrategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) and Habitats Regulations Assessment (HRA) for the draft Climate Action Plan £54,500Cambridge EconometricsAssessment of the cumulative and inter-active effects of financial, social and economic impacts of the draft Climate Action Plan£41,500Rural Community NetworkAssessment of the cumulative and inter-active effects of rural impacts of the draft Climate Action Plan£6,300
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of jobs he estimates will be lost in the agri-food sector as a result of the measures proposed in the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027.
answer_text: The measures in the draft Climate Action Plan do not require a reduction in output from the Northern Ireland agri-food sector. Therefore, no job losses are expected as a result.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of the green jobs, referred to in his Written Ministerial Statement on 19 June 2025, will be directly created in rural areas as a result of the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027.
answer_text: In a 2023 report, the Climate Change Committee identified that between 135,000 and 725,000 net new jobs could be created within the UK workforce by 2030 in low-carbon sectors, such as buildings retrofit, renewable energy generation and the manufacture of electric vehicles.The overarching Rural Needs Impact Assessment published alongside the draft Climate Action Plan highlights that the policies and proposals can be expected to positively impact on people in rural areas due to sustainable green economy job opportunities including through expansion of forestry, peatland restoration, renewable energy projects and other low carbon technologies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that Northern Ireland does not fall behind the rest of the United Kingdom in access to, and delivery of, new Alzheimer’s disease treatments.
answer_text: The Regional Dementia Project Board was established in May 2024 and one of the workstreams focuses on system readiness and service delivery. This workstream is reviewing the current systems within Dementia Services across the region to identify where improvements can be made and this will include diagnosis pathways. This workstream is also tasked with developing a proposal paper for a system readiness scoping study for any new disease modifying treatments (DMTs). The study will review existing resources and services with respect to their capability to identify suitable candidates for any new DMTs. It will also enable recommendations to be made as to the requirements within the current diagnosis pathways and infrastructure to ensure Northern Ireland is in a position to support new DMT delivery.My Department has regular engagement with the Alzheimer’s Society who are represented at the Regional Dementia Project Board and the system readiness and service delivery workstream. My Department also has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventionsThe projected 51 per cent increase in dementia prevalence in Northern Ireland by 2040 has been highlighted by the Alzheimer’s Society. The regional dementia care pathway is a strategic tool, developed by the Department of Health. It outlines a vision for high quality, person centred dementia care, emphasizing the importance of a timely diagnosis, a range of interventions across primary care, community care and secondary care to support people to live well with the condition and ensuring that specialist interventions are available when this is required. It is recognised that whilst some improvements to the current dementia pathway can be made through some service redesign within existing services, that other aspects of improvement will require a funding allocation. The current financial position within my department is extremely challenging in the context of many competing priorities. I recognise and support the need for timely and targeted interventions for those people diagnosed with a dementia. and I remain committed to providing additional funding for full implementation of the regional dementia pathway when in a position to do so, recognising that additional resources will be required to address the projected increase in prevalence of the condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the estimated annual cost to the Northern Ireland Executive of implementing the draft Climate Action Plan 2023–2027.
answer_text: It is estimated that between 2023/24 and 2026/27, the Northern Ireland Executive will invest £718m Capital to implement the policies and proposals within the Climate Action Plan. This equates to an average total capital investment of £93 per person, per year.The estimated annual capital investment incurred per year is as follows:£93.5m in 2023/24, £132.6m in 2024/25, £218.5m in 2025/26 and £273.7m in 2026/27.Approximately £226m capital has therefore already been invested and we estimate that £492m will be invested during 2025/26 and 2026/27. Some of the expenditure will already be part of existing programmes and budgets. Not all this investment is therefore new, or additional. All proposed spend will be dependent on normal budgetary decision-making, including business case development and evaluation. This investment will create economic opportunities and long-term savings. The scale of investment needed is significant, but I am clear that the cost of inaction – economically, socially and environmentally – would be significantly higher.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people in Northern Ireland currently living with Alzheimer’s disease; and (ii) an estimate of the number of people living with Alzheimer’s disease that remain undiagnosed.
answer_text: The total number of people with a diagnosis of dementia in Northern Ireland in 2024 was 13,900.The Department does not hold data on the precise number of people living with dementia in Northern Ireland who have not received a diagnosis. However, based on broader UK estimates and expert consensus it is generally accepted that approximately 30-40% of people living with dementia remain undiagnosed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of the recent National Institute for Health and Care Excellence decision not to recommend lecanemab or donanemab for use on the NHS.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions.On 19 June 2025, NICE published its final draft guidance which does not recommend the use of lecanemab (Leqembi®) or donenamab (Kisunla®) for treating mild cognitive impairment and mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease in adults. Final guidance is expected on 23 July 2025. An independent NICE committee concluded, based on all evidence submitted, that the benefits from both treatments are too small to justify the additional cost to the NHS and that the medicines do not represent good value for money for the health service.When the final guidance for these treatments is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfIn circumstances where NICE has not yet fully appraised, or does not recommend a particular drug, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland.Whilst there is a process to check the guidance for legal and policy applicability here, there is no Departmental reassessment of the clinical or cost evidence used by NICE in coming to its decisions and forming its advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to invest in dementia diagnosis pathways and supporting infrastructure to ensure readiness for the delivery of new treatments.
answer_text: The Regional Dementia Project Board was established in May 2024 and one of the workstreams focuses on system readiness and service delivery. This workstream is reviewing the current systems within Dementia Services across the region to identify where improvements can be made and this will include diagnosis pathways. This workstream is also tasked with developing a proposal paper for a system readiness scoping study for any new disease modifying treatments (DMTs). The study will review existing resources and services with respect to their capability to identify suitable candidates for any new DMTs. It will also enable recommendations to be made as to the requirements within the current diagnosis pathways and infrastructure to ensure Northern Ireland is in a position to support new DMT delivery.My Department has regular engagement with the Alzheimer’s Society who are represented at the Regional Dementia Project Board and the system readiness and service delivery workstream. My Department also has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventionsThe projected 51 per cent increase in dementia prevalence in Northern Ireland by 2040 has been highlighted by the Alzheimer’s Society. The regional dementia care pathway is a strategic tool, developed by the Department of Health. It outlines a vision for high quality, person centred dementia care, emphasizing the importance of a timely diagnosis, a range of interventions across primary care, community care and secondary care to support people to live well with the condition and ensuring that specialist interventions are available when this is required. It is recognised that whilst some improvements to the current dementia pathway can be made through some service redesign within existing services, that other aspects of improvement will require a funding allocation. The current financial position within my department is extremely challenging in the context of many competing priorities. I recognise and support the need for timely and targeted interventions for those people diagnosed with a dementia. and I remain committed to providing additional funding for full implementation of the regional dementia pathway when in a position to do so, recognising that additional resources will be required to address the projected increase in prevalence of the condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 28290/22-27, how many civil servants are being paid for activities in relation to Belfast Pride 2025, including any activities involved.
answer_text: The number of staff will be below five. In line with departmental practice the exact number has not been provided to prevent disclosure of junior staff.in Belfast Pride as part of its ongoing commitment to being an inclusive employer with under-represented groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 28662/22-27, whether the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) ranking in the Stonewall Equality Index played any part in the decision of the NICS to take part in Belfast Pride 2025.
answer_text: Civil Service participation in Belfast Pride 2025 is not connected to it’s ranking in the Stonewall Equality Index.The Civil Service participates in Belfast Pride as part of its ongoing commitment to being an inclusive employer and programme of outreach with under-represented groups.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given that Developed Vetting security clearance relates to matters of national security and the protection of Top Secret material, for her assessment of whether the UK Security Vetting protocols have been followed in relation to the current Police Ombudsman.
answer_text: The policy governing this matter is a UK government one. It is therefore not under the direction of The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the projected cost to the NHS of providing lecanemab and donanemab as treatments for Alzheimer’s disease.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.On 19 June 2025, NICE published its final draft guidance which does not recommend the use of lecanemab (Leqembi®) or donenamab (Kisunla®) for treating mild cognitive impairment and mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease in adults. Final guidance is expected on 23 July 2025. An independent NICE committee concluded, based on all evidence submitted, that the benefits from both treatments are too small to justify the additional cost to the NHS and that the medicines do not represent good value for money for the health service.When the final guidance for these treatments is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf In circumstances where NICE has not yet fully appraised, or does not recommend a particular drug, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland.Furthermore, the SPPG does not conduct detailed planning for the introduction of new medicines until NICE publish a positive Technology Appraisal which includes the associated access criteria. Given the current NICE assessment, my Department anticipates that, if they were to be commissioned, the associated drug and infrastructure costs would be significant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of her statement that there is currently no legal power to suspend the Police Ombudsman or remove the Police Ombudsman from office, to detail (i) whether this legal gap has been formally recognised by her Department; and (ii) any steps taken to address this situation.
answer_text: The Police Ombudsman is appointed by Royal Warrant, under Schedule 3, s.1(1) of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1998, on the recommendation of the First and deputy First Minister acting jointly.  The are powers in the legislation which enable the First and deputy First Minister to call upon the Ombudsman to retire in certain circumstances.   Consideration of potential legal gaps the legislation is a matter for TEO.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) whether the Police Ombudsman's Developed Vetting security clearance status has been reviewed; and (ii) whether the Police Ombudsman continues to hold Developed Vetting security clearance.
answer_text: The policy governing this matter is a UK government one. It is therefore not under the direction of The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of temporary staff employed by the Loughs Agency; and (ii) the nature of the work of these temporary staff.
answer_text: Loughs Agency currently employs 26 staff on a temporary basis.These roles cover a wide range of duties including fishery science, conservation, protection, project management, angling and fisheries improvements, and administration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when they intend to reopen the supply line of fish out of Movanagher Fish Farm.
answer_text: No decisions will be taken regarding stocking of the Public Angling Estate from Movanagher Fish Farm until a stocking protocol is in place to ensure compliance with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985 and minimise the risk of the spread of the zebra mussel.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any consideration has been given to create a Presidential trail by linking Arthur's Cottage, Cullybackey, the Andrew Jackson Centre, Carrickfergus, The Vance monument, Coagh and the Dervock riverside walk, which showcases their links to President McKinley's family to celebrate the semiquincentennial.
answer_text: As part of a programme of activities to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the American Declaration of Independence in 2026 the Ulster-Scots Agency will be revising and extending its existing Patriots, Pioneers and Presidents Trail, with the addition of new content specifically relevant to the American Revolution. This will build on an ongoing programme of work undertaken by the Ulster-Scots Agency and my Department in partnership with local councils to develop the interpretation at key sites including the Ulysses Simpson Grant Homestead at Dergenagh, Co. Tyrone, the Andrew Jackson Centre at Carrickfergus, the Arthur Cottage at Cullybackey and works in Dervock, highlighting the area's connection to William McKinley. The Patriots, Pioneers and Presidents Trail will be further enhanced by the development of a suite of digital resources funded through the Ulster-Scots Broadcast Fund via NI Screen, which will be taken forward by the Nerve Centre. The starting point of the trail will be the Discover Ulster-Scots Centre in Belfast, which houses an extensive exhibition on the impact of the Ulster-Scots and our Scotch-Irish diaspora on the United States, including unique artefacts relating to the Ulster-born founding fathers Charles Thomson and John Dunlap. The Ulster-Scots Agency will also be bringing forward a proposal for the development of physical site marking and public art around Ulster to make the trail visible, which will be subject to the availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the current Police Ombudsman (i) is in receipt of full salary during her current period of absence; and (ii) was in receipt of full salary during previous periods of absence from post.
answer_text: HR matters relating to the Ombudsman are managed in accordance with NICS guidance by the Office of the Police Ombudsman.  You may wish to contact OPONI for further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail who has the legal and administrative authority to (i) suspend; and (ii) dismiss the Police Ombudsman from office.
answer_text: The appointment of the Police Ombudsman is made by Royal Warrant on the recommendation of the First and deputy First Minister acting jointly in line with Schedule 3, s.1(1) of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1988.  There is no power in current legislation to suspend the Police Ombudsman. The First Minister and deputy First Minister acting jointly may call upon the Ombudsman to retire under Schedule 3, s.1(7) of the Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) who is responsible for performance oversight; and who acts as line manager (a) in effect; and (b) in law, for the Police Ombudsman.
answer_text: The Police Ombudsman is appointed as a Corporation Sole by Royal Warrant in line with Schedule 3, s.1(1) of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1998 on the recommendation of the First and deputy First Minister acting jointly.  The Police Ombudsman is employed by the Executive Office, not the Department of Justice.Neither I, as Minister, nor my Department, have any role in performance appraisal or line management for the Police Ombudsman, in effect or in law.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any legal changes she is considering to allow the Minister of Justice to remove a Police Ombudsman from office when he or she is (i) under police investigation; (ii) referred to the Public Prosecution Service; and (iii) convicted of an offence.
answer_text: Matters relating to the appointment or dismissal of the Police Ombudsman are for the consideration of the First and deputy First Minister who, acting jointly, recommend the appointment to His Majesty the King under Schedule 3, s.1(1) of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1998. The Ombudsman is employed by the Executive Office, not the Department of Justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) who authorised the current leave of absence for the current Police Ombudsman; and (ii) the statutory or administrative authority under which this decision was taken.
answer_text: Management of HR arrangements is a matter for the Office of the Police Ombudsman (OPONI). The CEO would, therefore, have responsibility for approving any leave of absence.You may wish to contact OPONI directly for further detail.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of refugees currently housed in hotels in Northern Ireland, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Our Department does not provide accommodation for refugees, and we are not aware of the numbers being accommodated in hotels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for the Loughs Agency assessment of whether the infilling of drains, ditches and ponds at selected TREES sites may undermine the intended function of these nature-based solutions; and (ii) whether the Loughs Agency will consider remedial measures, such as willow planting, leaky dams and the expansion of detention features, alongside completing originally planned works like fencing and tree planting.
answer_text: Loughs Agency assessment is that the infilling of drains, ditches and ponds at selected TREES site will enhance, not undermine, the intended function of these nature-based solutions.Loughs Agency is considering a range of mitigation measures including leaky dams and the planting of willow and other species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether the Loughs Agency has considered introducing mitigation measures within TREES sites, such as leaky dams or willow planting, rather than placing the burden on farmers; and (ii) for the Loughs Agency assessment of whether these nature-based systems require time to evolve and should not result in disproportionate inspections of farmers who have voluntarily engaged in the pilot schemes.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has considered a range of mitigation options within TREES sites including leaky dams and willow planting.Loughs Agency’s assessment is that these sites need time to evolve, and this will be supported throughout the 12-year agreement period.However, under the terms of the landowner agreements inspections are required to ensure no overrun is coming from ponds and to confirm that input volumes and material are appropriate. It is not the intention of the Agency that these inspections will be disproportionate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 27769/22-27, whether the Minister can confirm that the Loughs Agency is now fully cognisant of the need to conserve wetland habitat at the TREES sites and the species at risk therein.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has advised it is fully cognisant of the need to conserve wetland habitat at the TREES sites and the species at risk therein.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 28201/22-27, to breakdown the benefits of each trip towards building a globally competitive and sustainable economy.
answer_text: Participation in events, both at home and abroad, provide a unique opportunity for Ministers and senior officials to make this an even better place to live, work and visit. Earlier this year we led a delegation to North Carolina including partners from the NI Chamber of Commerce, Queen’s University Belfast, Invest NI and local businesses. These sort of trade missions have helped attract nearly 200 different companies from the US here in the last 20 years, creating over 28k jobs with a capital expenditure of £5.3 bn. In addition, the US market continues to be our second largest in terms of exports, worth £1.7bn in 2024 alone.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) when the damage to the pillar at the entrance to the upper car park at Parliament Buildings was first brought to its attention; and (ii) when repairs will be carried out.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission was made aware of the damage to the pillar on the evening of 12 May 2025 by a member of staff and was the result of relatively minor contact with a car.The repair was carried out on 20 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 27847/22-27, whether the Loughs Agency (i) will resume the positive engagement taken prior to February 2024 when Loughs Agency worked in partnership with farmers to develop these pilot farmyard run-off projects; and (ii) will complete the development of these nature-based solutions and take remedial action for their maintenance.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has consistently engaged with landowners during 2024 and 2025 through site visits, informal communications and an online briefing session. This engagement will continue throughout the 12-year agreement period.  The Agency and landowners are jointly responsible for the development and maintenance of the nature-based solutions at these sites for the 12-year agreement period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rationale for the decision of the Northern Ireland Civil Service to participate in Belfast Pride 2025, including (i) any consideration given to the political nature of the event; (ii) whether the Executive’s position on puberty blockers was considered during the decision-making process; and (iii) to what extent the potential impact on Stonewall Equality Index rankings was a factor in that decision.
answer_text: ANSWERAs one of the largest employers here and a public service provider, the Civil Service recognises and respects the diversity of people’s identities, experiences and backgrounds. As an equal opportunities employer, the Civil Service participates in Belfast Pride as part of its ongoing commitment to being an inclusive employer and programme of outreach with under-represented groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to plans to establish a fourth operating base for Loughs Agency, to detail the (i) tenancy; and (ii) title arrangements to the property involved.
answer_text: Loughs Agency already has a fourth operating base at Killygordon, County Donegal. No tenancy is in place for the site; Loughs Agency holds full title to this site which transferred to the Agency from the Foyle Fisheries Commission under The North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland ) Order 1999 and the British-Irish Agreement Act 1999.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 27847/22-27, to detail Loughs Agency advice as to what a farmer should do with these sites if Loughs Agency follow up on their proposal to send contractors to fill in ponds and redirect farm run-off.
answer_text: For sites where it is necessary to complete infilling works to prevent downstream environmental impacts and ensure compliance with UK Forestry Standards, long-term maintenance plans will be agreed between the landowners and Loughs Agency. These plans will include advice for landowners on how to achieve maximum long-term benefits from the project sites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any actions he has taken to progress regulation to govern Property Management Companies since receiving the detailed scoping report commissioned by his predecessor in April 2025.
answer_text: ANSWERI have examined the scoping report on the regulation of shared property that was submitted to me, which also relates to the issues you have raised in 3 further questions (AQWs 28602, 28603 and 28605). I am currently engaging with officials to determine what further progress can be made on these matters, taking into account the availability of resources and the competing legislative priorities that prevail during the remainder of this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to create a housing tribunal to resolve disputes affordably.
answer_text: I have no plans to create a housing tribunal at this time.  A dispute resolution service for private tenants and registered landlords is available through a mediation service, operated by Housing Rights. The contract for the service is managed by Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council, as part of their responsibilities for the Landlord Registration Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans to introduce a cap on unreasonable charges for leaseholders.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the answer given in response to AQW 28601/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans he has to address tenant protections to bring them into line with Great Britain and give leaseholders a statutory right to transparency on service charges.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the answer given in response to AQW 28601/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans to require sinking funds for apartments.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the answer given in response to AQW 28601/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide the business case in relation to each event (i) she attended; and (ii) officials from her Department attended, outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: I have not taken any overseas trips (outside of the UK and Ireland) on behalf of my Department since taking up office, however my officials have. Details of these have been given in AQW 28375 22-77.In response to your question, I can confirm that records are maintained of Ministerial and official attendance at events outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024. While my Department is committed to transparency, business cases frequently contain commercially sensitive information and internal policy discussions.  Disclosure of such documents could prejudice commercial interests and adversely affect my Department’s ability to effectively conduct public affairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide the business case in relation to each event (i) he attended; and (ii) officials from his Department attended, outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: My Department is unable to provide the business cases requested as they can contain commercially sensitive information, internal policy discussions, operational planning details, and personal information. Releasing such material may breach UKGDPR and have an impact on my Department’s ability to successfully carry out its functions, including future engagement and planning activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide the business case in relation to each event (i) she attended; and (ii) officials from her Department attended, outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: I have not travelled outside the UK and Ireland on official business since February 2024.  When officials are required to travel outside these islands to fulfil their duties, justification is required on a case-by-case basis, rather than a separate formal business case.  It is also a requirement to use the most efficient and economic form of travel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide the business case in relation to each event (i) he attended; and (ii) officials from his Department attended, outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: My Department applies the general principles as set out in the NICS Travel and Subsistence policy, including the need to use the most efficient and economic form of travel and is justified in terms of making an important contribution to business need.I can confirm that records have been maintained of Ministerial and official attendance at events outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024. Details of these have been given in AQW 25941/22-27.While I am committed to transparency, business cases frequently contain sensitive information and reflect internal policy discussions.  Disclosure of such documents could prejudice commercial interests and adversely affect my Department’s ability to effectively conduct public affairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide the business case in relation to each event (i) he attended; and (ii) officials from his Department attended, outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The need for international travel is considered on a case-by-case basis and only approved when there is a clearly demonstrated and justified business need. Approval must be obtained at the appropriate level before any travel commitments are made. After the necessary authorisations have been obtained, all travel is booked through the contracted travel operator.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social houses that his Department plans to build in the lifetime of the Programme for Government 2024-2027, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW28467/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide the business case in relation to each event (i) she attended; and (ii) officials from her Department attended, outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: All travel is booked through the contracted travel operator after quotations and the necessary authorisations have been obtained and this serves as the business case for each booking. However, the booking forms may contain personal data therefore it would not be appropriate to provide this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide the business case in relation to each event (i) he attended; and (ii) officials from his Department attended, outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: ANSWERNeither Minister has travelled outside these islands during this period. Where international travel is required for officials to fulfil their duties the application for travel requires a justification on a case by case basis, rather than a separate formal business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide the business case in relation to each event (i) he attended; and (ii) officials from his Department attended, outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Business cases are not routinely completed for travelling overseas.  In accordance with the NICS Travel and Subsistence Policy, Chapter 9 of the HR Handbook, all travel and accommodation arrangements on official business must be made by the most efficient and economic method and authorising officers must confirm that the journey on official business is necessary.  Business travel outside the United Kingdom and Republic of Ireland by NICS officials requires approval at Senior Civil Service level.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide the business case in relation to each event (i) they attended; and (ii) officials from their Department attended, outside the British Isles since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: All associated arrangements are consistent with NICS guidelines and the principles for managing public resources as set out in Managing Public Money NI, which encourages a proportionate approach to evaluation and appraisal

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to publish the (i) outcomes; and (ii) benefits to his Department from each overseas trip taken since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to publish the (i) outcomes; and (ii) benefits to her Department from each overseas trip taken since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: I have not taken any overseas trips  on behalf of my Department since taking up office. The previous Minister for Infrastructure also did not take any overseas trips during his time in office.A small number of my officials have taken overseas trips on behalf of my Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024 and these are summarised below.2-day seminar/conference on Flood Forecasting systems Delft, Netherlands.6-7 November 2024The conference brought together professionals, experts, and enthusiasts in the field of water management and decision support systems.   It took the form of presentations, pitches, breakout sessions and keynotes.  My officials built key connections with the global flood forecasting “community”, which will aid my Department in its considerations of options for the creation of a flood forecasting centre for the North.  The Fourth Global Ministerial Conference on Road Safety in Marrakech, Morocco18-20 February 2025The focus of this conference was to bring leaders and experts together to accelerate action towards the Sustainable Development Goals’ target of halving global road deaths by 2030.  A Report and policy options were prepared identifying approaches taken by other countries that are successfully reducing the number of people killed on their roads.The International Commission for Vehicle Testing (CITA) conference. Istanbul 6 May 2025Attendance at the conference presented a unique opportunity for my officials to network with wide range of government officials and testing authorities responsible for vehicle testing and road safety, road safety researchers, and vehicle test equipment manufacturers. The contacts made and information obtained during the conference are considered critical to my Department’s future development of its vehicle testing environment.Events Organised by CIECA, The International Commission for Driver Testing.My Department is part of CIECA, which currently consists of 72 members from 39 countries, covering almost all of Europe, and is also represented in Australia, New Zealand, Republic of Korea, the United Arab Emirates, and North America.   One of my officials currently holds the position of President of the Commission.  A small number of officials have attended events organised by CIECA during this time.The 56th General Assembly and Congress, Dubai 4-5 March 2024Workshop: Practical issues emerging from the new 4th Directive on Driver Licensing, Brussels 3 June 2024CICEA Permanent Bureau, Hamburg 19 September 2024Peer review at the Estonian Transport Administration, Tallinn 24 September 2024CICEA Workshop, Barcelona 20 November 2024The 57th General Assembly and Congress, Porto 26 May 2025Attendance at these events provided my officials with opportunities to share current research, learn from others and exchange best practice.  This forms part of a global approach to standardising training and testing with the aim of improving road safety and reduce the number of people kill

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to publish the (i) outcomes; and (ii) benefits to his Department from each overseas trip taken since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: ANSWER	Neither I nor my predecessor Minister Archibald travelled outside these islands during this period.It is important we do not adopt an insular approach, instead be outward looking and learn from best practice elsewhere, including ensuring awareness of emerging developments and external expertise, to improve the delivery of public service provision. Travel included meeting with European Commission in respect of delivery o the £1bn PEACE PLUS programme, mapping conferences, International Census Forums as well as exploring the legislative and operational implications of the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to publish the (i) outcomes; and (ii) benefits to his Department from each overseas trip taken since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Delivery of Departmental priorities, and the ambition of the Programme for Government to make Northern Ireland an even better place to live, work, study, visit and invest, requires a level of engagement overseas.  To that end, travel outside the British Isles by officials includes study visits, training courses, attendance at conferences and sharing best practice of experience. Forensic Science Northern Ireland is an accredited scientific organisation and a member of The European Network of Forensic Science Institutes (ENFSI).  Officials have attended conferences and specialist scientific and technical training delivered by the ENFSI to maintain specialist competences as well as horizon scanning for new methods and scientific developments.    An official participated in a cross-departmental visit that has helped inform policy thinking around the development of a Barnahus model for Northern Ireland, to provide coordinated wrap-around justice and therapeutic support and interventions for child victims of sexual offences.Attendance at an International Association of Chiefs of Police (IACP) conference in Boston provided access to good practice initiatives on a global scale in addition to presentations to a global audience on the evolution of policing in Northern Ireland.Officials participated in a study visit which provided them with exposure to international best practice in helping inform how services to children and victims of crime can be improved.   Learning from responses to shared challenges in other jurisdictions helps support evidence-informed change.An official was part of a UK delegation to give evidence to the United Nations Committee on the Eradication of Racial Discrimination to report on progress since the Committee’s last report and respond to questions from Committee Members.Attendance at a European conference enabled officials to increase their awareness and understanding of advances in the field of electronic monitoring as well as developing contacts in various jurisdictions to inform future policy development.  Officials have taken part in cross-departmental study visits to Brussels organised by the Executive Office. Those visits have helped provide a clearer understanding of how the EU operates and exploring the legislative and operational implications of the Windsor Framework. The Northern Ireland Prison Service is a member of the Europris membership association for prison professionals.  Officials have participated in Heads of Service meetings to discuss current challenges, share learning and develop plans for provision of expert support in respect of policy development and operational delivery in prisons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to publish the (i) outcomes; and (ii) benefits to his Department from each overseas trip taken since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Delivering on the Department’s priorities and realising the ambition of the Programme for Government to make Northern Ireland an even better place to live, work, study, visit, and invest, requires meaningful engagement beyond our borders.To strengthen our education system, it is essential that we adopt an outward-looking approach, learning from international best practice and staying attuned to emerging global developments and expertise. In March 2025, I launched TransformED NI, a comprehensive strategy for transforming teaching and learning in Northern Ireland. This strategy recognises the value of looking beyond our own system to harness global insights and raise expectations for what our schools can achieve.Many countries around the world are currently improving their education systems at a faster pace than Northern Ireland and are narrowing the attainment gap between pupils from disadvantaged backgrounds and their peers. We must learn from these examples. Engaging with representatives from other governments has already helped shape our policy thinking and reinforced the importance of evidence-informed change.To support this international outlook, I established an International Ministerial Advisory Panel, a group of exceptional individuals with global expertise in education. Some members come from countries that have already undergone significant education reform, while others represent systems at the beginning of their transformation journey. Their insights are helping to guide and inform our own path forward.For full transparency, my Department’s Annual Report and Accounts are publicly available and can be downloaded from the Department of Education website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to publish the (i) outcomes; and (ii) benefits to her Department from each overseas trip taken since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since February 2024 both I and my predecessor, Conor Murphy, have undertaken a number of overseas missions and trips to  progress trade, investment and tourism opportunities in key markets across the globe. These visits have helped to enhance investor confidence through direct ministerial engagement, reinforcing the North’s image as a stable and attractive destination for foreign direct investment. They have also strengthened bilateral trade relations, particularly with the United States, our second-largest export market. A key message throughout these engagements has been our dual market access to both the Britain and EU, which can be effectively leveraged to attract and retain international investment as well as encourage export opportunities.The outcomes of trade and investment missions often take time to fully materialise. Invest NI continues to work closely with participating companies, actively monitoring their progress and providing tailored support in the months following each mission. This ongoing engagement helps businesses strengthen their presence in key export markets and informs their strategic decisions. For example, as a direct result of a trade mission to Chicago and Toronto led by my predecessor in September 2024, I was able to announce last month that Belfast-based firms The Foundation and The Creative Media Company secured contracts worth over £200,000, while Seedling and Jo Hopkins Consulting signed new distribution agreements, expanding their reach in North America.Similarly, foreign direct investment (FDI) projects can require significant lead time to develop, and Invest NI plays a central role in progressing these opportunities, facilitating investor engagement, supporting due diligence, and helping to bring projects to fruition. The department remains committed to ensuring that these international engagements deliver long-term economic benefits and sustainable growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the projected cost to the public purse in relation to the Northern Ireland Civil Service participation in Belfast Pride, broken down by (i) registration fees; (ii) branded materials, including specific nature of branding; and (iii) staff time.
answer_text: The projected cost for my Department broken down by (i) registration fees; (ii) branded materials, including specific nature of branding; and (iii) staff time for the Civil Service’s participation in Belfast Pride 2025 is:Registration feesBranded materialsStaff time£250	£0	£486

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how he reconciles the duty of the Northern Ireland Civil Service to remain apolitical with the decision to take part in this year’s Belfast Pride, particularly in light of Belfast Pride's decision to ban political parties following the Executive’s decision in relation to puberty blockers.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the response provided for AQW 28291/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any consideration given to the views of civil servants, both Protestant and Roman Catholic, who hold conscientious or faith-based objections to Belfast Pride when the Northern Ireland Civil Service made the decision to participate in this year’s event.
answer_text: ANSWERAs one of the largest employers here and a public service provider, the Civil Service recognises and respects the diversity of people’s identities, experiences and backgrounds. As an equal opportunities employer, the Civil Service participates in Belfast Pride as part of its ongoing commitment to being an inclusive employer and programme of outreach with under-represented groups.In accordance with the Civil Service Code of Ethics, civil servants are required to carry out their role with dedication and a commitment to the Civil Service’s core values of: Integrity, Honesty, Objectivity and Impartiality. In living out the core value of ‘impartiality’, civil servants must carry out their responsibilities in a way that is fair, just and equitable and reflects the Civil Service’s commitment to equality, diversity and inclusion, including the obligations under Section 75.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) decision to participate corporately in Belfast Pride, whether NICS staff will be permitted, as NICS staff, to take part in other events such as pro-life marches.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the response provided for AQW 28291/22-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to publish the (i) outcomes; and (ii) benefits to their Department from each overseas trip taken since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Growing a globally competitive and sustainable economy is a key priority in our Programme for Government. Realising this ambition, whilst making this an even better place to live, work, study, visit and invest, requires a level of engagement overseas.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will engage with Executive colleagues in relation to the publication of an annual report to detail the costs and benefits to Northern Ireland arising from international travel by Ministers and officials.
answer_text: Growing a globally competitive and sustainable economy is a key priority in our Programme for Government.  Realising this ambition, whilst making this an even better place to live, work, study, visit and invest, requires a level of engagement overseas.Departments publish Annual Reports and Accounts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to publish the (i) outcomes; and (ii) benefits to his Department from each overseas trip taken since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Delivering my Department’s priorities and the ambition of the Programme for Government to make Northern Ireland an even better place to live, work, study, visit and invest, requires us to be outward looking and to engage and learn from best practice elsewhere.  Since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, overseas trips taken on behalf of my Department have been instrumental in achieving this.  These trips have: •	Facilitated Knowledge Exchange, allowing us to learn from international peers, understand global best practices, and share insights to shape our policy thinking inform our way ahead;•	Strengthened global partnerships across government, research, and industry, fostering international collaboration;•	Enabled us to develop our skills and build internal capabilities through formal and informal training; and •	Fostered innovation and collaboration with research institutes and universities. Visits to Brussels provided further opportunities to explore the legislative and operational implications of the Windsor Framework and discuss the important role DAERA continues to play in its implementation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to publish the (i) outcomes; and (ii) benefits to his Department from each overseas trip taken since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Highlighting Northern Ireland as a place to live, work, study, visit and invest requires a level of engagement overseas.  All overseas trips by my Department since February 2024 enhanced our cultural and heritage links, helped promote Northern Ireland on the international stage and unlocked new opportunities to grow our economy globally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether climate change-related travel is being offset or accounted for in his Department’s carbon commitments.
answer_text: Climate change related travel is not being offset or accounted for in DAERA’s carbon commitments. Carbon emissions associated with Civil Service air and rail travel are gathered at a NICS wide level and any related carbon reduction policy associated would most likely be NICS wide.  In relation to my Department, the DAERA Estate Transformation Division has undertaken work considering the development of a decarbonisation policy specifically for DAERA fleet vehicles.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 26525/22-27, (i) to detail why the cost of accommodation has not been finalised; and (ii) to provide the cost of accommodation when it is known.
answer_text: The Executive Office incurred no cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the policy when a tree on her Department's land obstructs natural light entering a property.
answer_text: My Department’s policy and procedures guide setting out the policies and standards for road maintenance is contained within RSPPG E019.The aim of my Department’s tree and hedge maintenance is to prevent obstructions to sight lines and traffic signs, prevent trees or hedges becoming a danger to road users, ensure safe passage for road users and to maintain the effectiveness of road lighting.The policy is based around the safety of road users and not the issue of natural light.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the last occasion they met with Unite the Union and GMB Union representatives from Spirit AeroSystems; and (ii) the dates on which these unions have requested meetings.
To ask the Minister of Education how he plans to meet recommendation 26 of the Strategic Defence Review to expand in-school and community-based Cadet Forces across the country by 30% by 2030.
answer_text: Cadet Forces provide significant benefits to young people and are a key element of school and community extra-curricular provision.   As this is an excepted matter, I await with interest the UK government's plans to progress this recommendation, including for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether a review has been carried out on the effectiveness and efficiency of the Youth Justice Agency since its establishment under the recommendations of the Criminal Justice Review (2000).
answer_text: The Youth Justice Agency (YJA) has been subject to a number of inspections by Criminal Justice Inspection NI (CJINI) and reviews by the Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) examining the effectiveness and efficiency of its services. CJINI’s last two reports on YJA are available on its website: an announced inspection of Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre in 2022 and an inspection of YJA’s Community Interventions in 2024.The NIAO produced a report on the youth justice system in 2017 called “Managing Children who Offend” and a follow up report in 2020.  In response to the NIAO’s recommendations, YJA has improved the measurement of the impact of its work and since 2021 has published an annual performance impact report.  These are available on the Department's website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) when a fish tracking array was installed by the Loughs Agency on the River Mourne; (ii) the total cost of the associated fish tracking project, including capital, revenue and salaries; (iii) the number of fish tracked using this system; (iv) whether the tracking array is still in place; and (v) the unit within the Loughs Agency that managed this project.
answer_text: A fish tracking array was installed on the River Mourne in June 2024; The capital cost of the project was £76,505. Other revenue and salary costs cannot be quantified, as the tasks required for this project form a part of staff duties, and time spent on this specific project was not/is not separately recorded;No fish have been tracked to date. Approval to implant the required tags into fish was received in October 2024 and tracking will commence in the next migratory season in July/August 2025;The tracking array is still in place; and The Loughs Agency Science Team manages this project.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment made of the continued need for a stand-alone Youth Justice Agency in 2025, in light of the changing landscape of youth offending and financial pressures on public services.
answer_text: The Youth Justice Agency's (YJA) work, in partnership with others, has had a significant positive impact, including on the number of children entering the formal criminal justice system.  It is vital that this positive impact is further built upon and YJA remains an important part of the justice system in Northern Ireland.When the structure of the five DoJ agencies has previously been considered by the Department, the available evidence indicated that the existing delivery models continued to be the most appropriate. The Department will, however, continue to consider all options and opportunities to maximise the use of its limited resources through, for example, the greater use of shared corporate and support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how the current cost of operating the Youth Justice Agency compares with youth justice models in other UK jurisdictions; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to alternative, integrated models.
answer_text: Youth Justice is provided in different ways across the UK by a range of different local and national organisations.  Therefore, making direct like-for-like comparisons with the Youth Justice Agency (YJA) in Northern Ireland, including in terms of operating costs, is very complex.YJA is a single, integrated organisation delivering youth justice services in a consistent way across Northern Ireland.  Similar functions are delivered across multiple organisations in the rest of the UK.  The Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services, commissioned by the Health Minister and published in June 2023, recommends the creation of a single children and families agency in Northern Ireland.  This agency, the Review notes, has the potential to include the functions of YJA.

answer_text: The opening annual budget allocated to the Youth Justice Agency (YJA) in each of the last five financial years is outlined in the table below.(i) YJA does not have budget for administrative costs. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's spend on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since February 2024, the Department spent a total of  £254 on social media which was for a subscription to X.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that the Loughs Agency employs 15 more staff than it has approval for, including 4 senior grade posts, (i) to outline why the governance procedures and his Department’s oversight has not detected this; and (ii) whether any action will be taken.
answer_text: As advised in amended AQW 24665/22-27, Loughs Agency has approval to employ 53 staff to cover permanent positions in its structure, and approval to employ temporary/seasonal staff as required in the Conservation and Protection Directorate.Loughs Agency is operating within its approved staff complement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department's spend on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Department of Justice uses social media to highlight departmental and Ministerial messages.  These posts are organic with no associated financial cost.Advertising campaigns promoted by my Department in relation to domestic abuse, sexual offences, non-fatal strangulation and the Victim and Witnesses Charters have also involved the use of social media and, in particular, Facebook, Instagram, Google, Spotify and YouTube.   It is not possible to provide a breakdown for the specific time period from February 2024 but the total expenditure within the core Department in the 2023/24 financial year was £43,668.31.  There has been no further expenditure since April 2024.Within the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime, expenditure on social media relating to the Ending Harm campaign promoted by that programme is as follows:2023/24£18,659.802024/25£18,559.772025/26 (to date)£500.00

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's spend on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: My Department has spent £1,103.53 on social media since the restoration of the Northern Ireland Executive in February 2024. The total includes £285.60 for X subscriptions in respect of the Department of Health and HSCNI accounts, and £817.93 spent on social media promotion of health information, principally in connection with winter pressures during the Christmas and New Year. This promotion aimed to inform the public about a wide range of services available to help alleviate pressures on our hospital services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's spend on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since February 2024 spend on social media has been £328 for the subscription of an official Department of Education (DE) X account. The subscription of £164 per annum has been held since March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's spend on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: My Department has spent £70,524 on social media during the period, February 2024 to April 2025. The majority of this is associated with advertising campaigns deployed to promote awareness of Universal Credit, Make the Call, Job Start, PRONI, the Child Maintenance Service and Landlord Registration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department's spend on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
