question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers on 16 April 2025, to outline any engagement he has had with sporting bodies in Northern Ireland in relation to participation in female sport.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his Department's spend on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance spent £6,472.75 on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024. £6,388.75 relates to Civil Service job advertisements, with the remaining £84 was spent on the DoF subscription to X Premium.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the number of asylum seekers in Northern Ireland currently in hotel accommodation, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Responsibility for the provision of accommodation for asylum seekers here is not devolved; this responsibility falls to Home Office and their contracted accommodation provider, Mears Housing. The most recently published Home Office statistics show that as of 31 March 2025 there were 288 asylum seekers housed in hotel accommodation here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her Department's spend on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: From 3 February 2024 to 31 May 2025, the Department for the Economy spent £66,921.28 on social media. This spend relates to social media advertising progressed as part of wider advertising campaigns, to ensure that as many people as possible are aware of skills opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Loughs Agency biodiversity project TREES,  to detail the steps proposed to restore the pilot project sites to good agricultural condition, having had drainage removed, been occupied by deep ponds and soil heavily disturbed.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has 12-year agreements with landowners for the development and maintenance of nature-based solutions at these sites.   The Agency is not aware of any landowners who have confirmed that they wish to return their site to agricultural use.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the departmental spend on social media since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since 3 February 2024, the Executive Office utilised social media as part of public information campaigns related to Historical Institutional Abuse and Truth Recovery, incurring costs of £22,934.17.This spend is broken down as follows:Public Information Campaign SpendHistorical Institutional Abuse£4,984.89Truth Recovery£17,949.28Total £22,934.17In addition, the Department spent £504 on subscriptions for social media.The total amount spent was £23,438.17.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Loughs Agency biodiversity project TREES, to detail why the Loughs Agency is telling farmers to ensure no dirty water is entering the pond system and to remove material from the ponds when these are supposed to be nature-based solutions to managing farm run-off.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has a statutory duty to protect and conserve fisheries and water quality. Where there is evidence of dirty water discharging intermittently from the pond systems into nearby watercourses, Loughs Agency, in consultation with NIEA Water Management Unit, has introduced mitigation measures at these affected sites to reduce the volume of inputs of dirty water (including farm effluent or other deleterious matter) into the system.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Loughs Agency biodiversity project TREES, to detail why the pilot project is being halted, with infrastructure removed and filled in, before this nature-based solution has had time to properly develop.
answer_text: The TREES pilot project has not been halted.  The construction phase to create the pilot sites completed in 2023 as planned, and Loughs Agency will monitor these sites for the 12-year period of the landowner agreements.An initial evaluation of the pilot recommended that measures should be considered for sites where discharges of dirty water into watercourses were observed and to ensure all sites are compliant with the UK Forestry Standard. In a number of selected sites measures were undertaken, including the infilling of some drains, ditches and ponds to prevent downstream environmental impacts. Loughs Agency is working collaboratively with landowners to achieve the changes necessary to ensure full compliance with the landowner agreements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Loughs Agency biodiversity project TREES, to detail the position of landowners who have a twenty year contract with Forest Service in the event that contractors move on site with large plant that will damage trees planted though a woodland grant.
answer_text: Loughs Agency has no plans to carry out maintenance or modification work to any TREES sites that would be inconsistent with the terms and conditions of the Forest Service Small Woodland Grant Scheme. Any work that does take place will be with the consent of the landowner.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of Masters of Science in Physician Associate studies that have been funded by his Department in each of the last five years; and (ii) how many of those upon graduation have obtained employment in the NHS.
answer_text: (i)  The information requested is included in the table below.Academic YearNumber of student places funded by DoH2020/21202021/22202022/23202023/24202024/2517(ii) There are approximately 80 PAs currently employed across health and social care settings in Northern Ireland in a range of settings, mainly in HSC Trusts. While my Department is unable to give a definitive answer to this question, the vast majority of these professionals will have graduated from the PA MSc programme at Ulster University.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how often the criteria for school transport eligibility is reviewed for (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools.
answer_text: The distance criteria in the Home to School Transport Policy are underpinned by legislation and are not subject to routine review. A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused by the then Minister due to the pandemic and further suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. The transport policy underpins other policies within my department such as school attendance and is a key support for many pupils with Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND).  Any amendments to the transport eligibility criteria would need to complement the work of the SEN Reform Agenda and it is in this context that I will consider the utility and timing of a new separate review of the transport policy. Given the very significant budgetary pressures facing my department I will also need to consider the affordability of any changes to the transport policy that would result in the widening of the criteria to access transport provision. The cost of delivering the current school transport service for over 90,000 pupils is significant at over £120 million in 2023/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the public retraction by a former PSNI officer of allegations of sectarianism within the Tactical Support Group, to outline the steps she will take to address the impact this has had on public confidence in the impartiality and integrity of policing in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Allegations of this nature made against the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) are operational matters for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I respect the Chief Constable’s operational independence and the role of the Policing Board, therefore it would not be entirely inappropriate for me to comment on, or become involved in any PSNI staff disciplinary or investigative process.There are mechanisms in place to deal with such matters to ensure they are robustly investigated, to maintain the integrity and public confidence in policing. I have no doubt that the Chief Constable takes any such allegations extremely seriously. In light of the public retraction of the allegations, I am confident that the Chief Constable is ensuring that both the former officer in question and those officers against whom the allegations were made are receiving appropriate support at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Loughs Agency biodiversity project TREES, to detail how he will address the concerns of participating farmers regarding the destruction of precious wetland habitat and the associated populations of duck, wading bird, newt, frog, fish, invertebrates and plants.
answer_text: My officials advise that they are not aware of any concerns raised by participating farmers regarding the destruction of wetland habitat or any associated populations of wildlife and plants in relation to the TREES Project.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the rationale for his Department recruiting six posts in the NI Protocol Premises Facilities Team based at Dargan Road, Warrenpoint, Larne and Foyle; and (ii) whether the internal transfer negates the need to advertise these posts.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the arrangements at Northern Ireland ports and airports, including staffing, are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.  As such, this does not sit within my ministerial responsibilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 26657/22-27, to detail (i) the procurement process for the selection of the communications agency; (ii) why the cost increased from £15,000 in 2021 to £57,600 in 2024; (iii) whether there was any attempt to recruit internally to cover this role; (iv) whether such a service is available through his Department or elsewhere within government; and (v) his assessment of the value for money for the communication agency.
answer_text: i.	The contract was procured through a competitive tendering process and awarded to the most economically advantageous tender. ii.	The 2021 costs relate to a pilot project with the same communication agency, the full contract with the Agency started in 2022.iii.	There was no attempt to recruit internally. Developing this capability internally would incur significant ongoing costs.  iv.	The Department does not offer outsourced communications services to external bodies and DAERA has not been made aware of other government departments that offer outsourced communications functions.v.	As a separate entity that operates at arm's length from the Department, Loughs Agency is responsible for assessing the value for money of this expenditure, in accordance with agreed framework documents and Managing Public Money Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will explore, with relevant central government authorities, permitting two trailers in a combination to use the A75 from Cairnryan port to the M6 at Carlisle, and then further along the UK motorway network.
answer_text: The British Department for Transport has responsibility for determining what types of vehicles can be operated legally on roads within Britain. They have confirmed, following a limited trial they carried out involving one vehicle,  they have no plans to permit vehicle combinations containing more than one trailer to be operated on any roads there.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will look into the granting of permissions to Northern Ireland hauliers to allow them to haul two trailers in a long vehicle combination along appropriate roads to and from ferry ports, with a view to streamlining logistics, reducing costs, improving competitiveness, easing the impact of a driver shortage and reducing emissions.
answer_text: The current regulations, [The Motor Vehicle (Construction and Use) Regulations (Northern Ireland)] 1999 permits a heavy motor vehicle to draw no more than one trailer up to a certain maximum length and I can confirm that I currently have no plans to amend this legislation.However, operators who wish to, may apply to the Department for a Vehicle Special Order (VSO) to operate Longer Semi-Trailers (LSTs). These trailers are longer than conventional trailers and their use can lead to benefits such as reduced journeys, reduced costs whilst also reducing emissions. Further information on the VSO application process can be requested by contacting vehicle.standards@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the estimated cost to the public purse for the transcription of any extracts of (i) The Nolan Show; and (ii) other radio and TV programmes by officials in her Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Officials in my Department do not transcribe extracts of any radio or TV programme. Transcription services are provided by a central shared service operated by the Executive Information Service, and managed by the Department of Finance, for the provision of media monitoring services, including transcriptions when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of occasions that officials in her Department have been tasked with transcribing extracts of (i) The Nolan Show; and (ii) other radio and TV programmes since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, broken down by (a) date; and (b) name of guest or contributor.
answer_text: Officials in my Department have not been tasked with transcribing extracts of any radio or TV programme. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the estimated cost to the public purse for the transcription of any extracts of (i) The Nolan Show; and (ii) other radio and TV programmes by officials in his Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.

The Department of Health does not pay individual charges for transcriptions.Transcribing programmes is an effective and well-established way of keeping Departmental officials informed on media coverage.

answer_text: Officials in my Department are not tasked with transcribing extracts of any radio or television programme. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the estimated cost to the public purse for the transcription of any extracts of (i) The Nolan Show; and (ii) other radio and TV programmes by officials in her Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Transcription services are not provided by officials in my Department. The Executive Information Service operates a central shared media monitoring service which is managed by the Department of Finance and utilised by all Departments. Transcripts are provided as part of a range of media monitoring services as required. Departments do not incur individual charges for transcriptions.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 26590/22-27, (i) given the impact of the policy on all MLAs and their staff, why there was no consultation with those parties that do not have representation on the Assembly Commission; and (ii) whether it will undertake to include MLAs not represented on the Assembly Commission in consultations impacting policy in Parliament Buildings moving forward.
answer_text: As set out in the Assembly Commission’s answer to AQW 26590/22-27, the Commission’s Transgender Policy involved changes to the Staff Handbook, which were in keeping with the settled legislative and legal position at the time and were therefore approved by the Senior Management Team.  Therefore, no parties were consulted at that time.   As per Chapter 3 of the Assembly Commission’s Equality Scheme (2022-2026), consultation on the draft Policy sought the views of those directly affected by it. This included representative groups of Section 75 categories, Assembly Commission staff and Trade Union Side. Further Assembly Commission staff and Trade Union Side internal consultation took place from 14 November to 12 December 2022.In relation to whether the Assembly Commission will undertake to include MLAs not represented on the Assembly Commission in consultations moving forward, those directly affected by a policy are reviewed prior to each consultation taking place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she is being kept fully informed of the case involving a former PSNI officer who alleged that he was subject to sectarian abuse by former colleagues in a newspaper interview on 8 March 2025.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 9 - AQO 2072/22-27			ANSWERI have regular meetings with the Chief Constable at which we discuss all the issues affecting the Police Service of Northern Ireland. I have no doubt that the Chief Constable takes any such allegations extremely seriously and will address them appropriately. As these are operational matters for the Chief Constable, I intend to respect his independence, and it would be inappropriate for me to comment further.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 26588/22-27, and in the interests of transparency, whether it will provide the name of the external organisation which does not wish their details to be published.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission undertook two consultations to inform the development of the Transgender Policy.At the time of the first consultation on the draft Policy in 2021, the Assembly Commission’s Privacy Notice stated that the lawful basis for processing personal data was consent. This meant that personal information, including the name of a consultee or organisation, was not publishable unless it was explicitly agreed to via a signed consent form. As a result, when a consultation response was received from Focus – The Identity Trust (‘Focus’) which was not accompanied by a signed consent form, it was treated as being anonymous. The Assembly Commission subsequently revised its lawful basis for processing personal data, which resulted in information on organisation names being publishable. As a result, when a consultation response was received from Focus during the second consultation this second submission was attributed.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission why a media monitoring service for the Northern Ireland Assembly Commission is considered necessary, given the work already carried out in this regard for the Northern Ireland Executive by the Department of Finance.
answer_text: The Department of Finance previously provided the Assembly Commission with media monitoring services for a charge but this agreement was ended in March 2024 on the basis of more appropriately tailored services being available from other service providers.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether the tender for the provision of media monitoring services for the Northern Ireland Assembly Commission will result in the replacement of an existing service.
answer_text: As set out in the Assembly Commission’s response to AQW 27572/22-27, the procurement of a new media monitoring service commenced on 8 May 2025,with an estimated contract cost of £9,000 per year.  It has been openly tendered and advertised across the Assembly Commission’s social media channels.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) when the decision to tender for the provision of media monitoring services for the Northern Ireland Assembly Commission was made; and (ii) whether this was supported by all members of the Assembly Commission.
answer_text: Media monitoring is widely used across the public and private sectors and the Assembly Commission has had a media monitoring service in place throughout the life of the Assembly.  The media monitoring service is used for the benefit of the Assembly, its Committees and its Members by ensuring that the Assembly officials who support them are up to date across the full breadth of events, issues, political reaction and commentary which impact on the Assembly and its work.  For many Assembly officials, retaining an awareness of current developments on different matters is an essential part of performing their jobs effectively. Following a review of needs from a media monitoring service, a procurement exercise was conducted in January 2024 and a replacement service commenced on 1 April 2024.This contract will end on 30 June 2025. The procurement of a further media monitoring service commenced on 8 May 2025. The operational decision to tender this contract is within the delegated authority of Assembly Commission management, which the elected Members of the Assembly Commission would not be involved in.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 26589/22-27, to detail who provided the legal advice.
answer_text: Legal advice was provided by the Assembly Commission’s Legal Services Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the Media Monitoring Unit, to detail (i) when the unit was formed; (ii) who requested the formation of the unit; (iii) the total spend on the unit in each year since its inception; and (iv) the number of staff employed in the unit in each year since its inception.
answer_text: The Media Monitoring Unit (MMU) was established in 1996 by the NIO and transferred to the Department of Justice in 2010 when justice was devolved. As part of the restructuring of Executive Departments in 2016, the Executive Office’s Executive Information Service assumed responsibility for the central shared MMU which is managed on their behalf by the Department of Finance.The MMU provides monitoring services of newspapers, television and radio to all nine Departments and one external customer – an Arm’s Length Body. They produce digests, transcripts, news bulletins and newsbreak items. The Unit also provides a newspaper cuttings service of daily newspapers which are uploaded to the Executive Information Service Clipping Portal.The annual costs for the past eight years associated with the staffing and running of the MMU are detailed in the table below:YearSalary and AdminIncome *Total Net Cost2017-18   376,741 - 159,540              217,201 2018-19   351706- 156,870              194,836 2019-20   381,774- 162,475              219,299 2020-21   423,825- 161,075              262,750 2021-22   454,812- 160,737              294,074 2022-23   462,470- 156,140              306,330 2023-24   486,295- 144,251              342,044 2024-25   447,770- 127,585              320,185 * MMU customers pay a contribution to the costs of administrating the service, in line with their agreed Service Level Agreement.The main costs of the Unit are the salary costs for the MMU team, which currently comprises eleven staff. It is not possible to provide a breakdown of costs for individual services, including the transcription service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Irish language is no longer covered by the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages on the grounds that the charter only protects languages that are different from the official language(s) of that State, and, under Section 7B of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022, it states that the purpose of this Part is to provide official recognition of the status of the Irish language in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Identity and Language (NI) Act 2022 gives official recognition of the status of the Irish language in Northern Ireland in the context of national and cultural identity. The Act does not make Irish an official language of Northern Ireland and it makes no comment on its status viz a viz the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women treated in hospital for complications arising from a medical abortion, either complete or incomplete, since the change in the abortion laws in 2020, broken down by (i) hospital; and (ii) complication.
answer_text: The tables below provide data on the number of hospital admissions due to complications arising from a medical abortion broken down by hospital and complication.  (i)	Number of women1 admitted2 to hospitals in Northern Ireland who have undergone a medical abortion that has resulted in a complication3, from April 2020-March 20244, broken down by Hospital:Hospital	Number of WomenAltnagelvin Area	16Antrim	18Causeway	10Craigavon Area	25Daisy Hill	17Royal Maternity	30Ulster	18Total	134Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Activity Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH, NI(ii)	Number of women1 admitted2 to hospitals in Northern Ireland who have undergone a medical abortion that has resulted in a complication3, from April 2020-March 20244, broken down by type of complication:Complication	Number of WomenInfection	12Bleeding	20Other5 	102Total	134Source: Hospital Inpatient System, Hospital Activity Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH, NINotes:1Figures denote individuals, so if a woman has been admitted more than once within the time period, she has been counted under the hospital of her first discharge.2Only admissions within the Acute Services and Maternity & Child Health Programmes of Care have been included.  Admissions are approximated using deaths and discharges and counted in the period that they were discharged.  Patients who were treated in the Emergency Department, but not admitted, are not included in these figures.3Individuals who have undergone a medical abortion that has resulted in a complication were identified using ICD-10 codes for the primary diagnosis field of the Hospital Inpatient System (O04.0 to O04.8).  Primary diagnosis codes are the dominant reason for the patient’s hospital admission. 4Data for 2023/24 includes data extracted from encompass (a new electronic patient record system) for South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) from 9 November 2023.  This subset of the data is classed as "Official Statistics in Development" whilst undergoing evaluation.  As a result, users should exercise some caution with this data.For discharges in encompass, where a patient transfers from one hospital to another within the same HSCT, this will count as a transfer, rather than separate admissions and discharges as it would have in the legacy system, and therefore the patient will be counted under the hospital of their final discharging episode during this stay.5Other category includes i) pregnancy with retained products of conception (RPOC) and therefore is included under the assumption that RPOC is considered a complication and ii) incomplete or complete abortion or unspecified with other and unspecified complications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why there has been a delay in the recruitment process for the Northern Ireland Civil Service Administrative Officer post, given that the process began in August 2024 and applicants were informed that outcome notifications would be issued by the end of April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment made of the long-term sustainability of addressing audiology waiting lists solely through hospital-based services.
answer_text: My Department is committed to the statutory provision of audiology services in the HSC.  It recognises, however, that there may be some opportunities to develop a mixed economy that involves the HSC and commercial providers in the community.  This type of model could contribute towards the reduction of waiting times in the HSC.  Extensive waiting times are due in part to a gap in the service where demand exceeds capacity and a backlog in referrals from the pandemic.  There are also noted difficulties in the recruitment and retention of staff. In this vein officials have had extensive consultation and engagement with commercial providers to understand their service models and those that are in place across the UK.   In the short term, should additional funding become available, it may be possible for non-HSC providers to help reduce HSC waiting times through the appropriate procurement process.  In the longer term, however, a mixed model of provision requires new additional recurrent money for both the service and its procurement/tendering exercise.   There is currently no additional funding for any service change and as you will be aware the HSC financial position is challenging.In considering this model of care, my Department is aware that similar approaches are already on place in other parts of the UK for benchmarking.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline all funding his Department and its arm's-length bodies, including Northern Ireland Screen, has provided to Kneecap since 2017.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to the use of community-based audiology providers to support patients with uncomplicated age-related hearing loss, in order to reduce audiology waiting lists.
answer_text: My Department is committed to the statutory provision of audiology services in the HSC.  It recognises, however, that there may be some opportunities to develop a mixed economy that involves the HSC and commercial providers in the community.  This type of model could contribute towards the reduction of waiting times in the HSC.  Extensive waiting times are due in part to a gap in the service where demand exceeds capacity and a backlog in referrals from the pandemic.  There are also noted difficulties in the recruitment and retention of staff. In this vein officials have had extensive consultation and engagement with commercial providers to understand their service models and those that are in place across the UK.   In the short term, should additional funding become available, it may be possible for non-HSC providers to help reduce HSC waiting times through the appropriate procurement process.  In the longer term, however, a mixed model of provision requires new additional recurrent money for both the service and its procurement/tendering exercise.   There is currently no additional funding for any service change and as you will be aware the HSC financial position is challenging.In considering this model of care, my Department is aware that similar approaches are already on place in other parts of the UK for benchmarking.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to estimate the amount of funding required to bring wastewater infrastructure in (i) Dervock; (ii) Mosside; (iii) Armoy; (iv) Stracoum; and (v) Ballycastle up to a standard which creates capacity to allow for new housing and supports economic development in the area.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:	Wastewater infrastructure includes wastewater treatment works (WwTW) and wastewater networks. The upgrade of the wastewater treatment works serving Dervock, Mosside, Armoy and Stranocum are not included within the current Price Control 21 Investment Programme (2021/22 - 2027/28) and, therefore, NI Water does not have detailed costs for any upgrade of these works at this time. Ballycastle WwTW currently has remaining treatment capacity and an upgrade of the works is not required.With regard to the wastewater networks serving Dervock, Mosside, Armoy and Stranocum, the drainage areas for these villages are not within a Drainage Area Plan study due to the population size being less than 2000 and, therefore, no upgrades or associated costs have been considered. NI Water has prepared a Drainage Area Plan for the Ballycastle catchment and this is currently with the NIEA to review and help determine any capital upgrades required. As a result, solutions and associated high level cost estimates are not currently available, however, NI Water envisages that this information should be available by the end of 2025. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has benchmarked Northern Ireland’s audiology service delivery model against models in other regions where community-based provision is more widely used.
answer_text: My Department is committed to the statutory provision of audiology services in the HSC.  It recognises, however, that there may be some opportunities to develop a mixed economy that involves the HSC and commercial providers in the community.  This type of model could contribute towards the reduction of waiting times in the HSC.  Extensive waiting times are due in part to a gap in the service where demand exceeds capacity and a backlog in referrals from the pandemic.  There are also noted difficulties in the recruitment and retention of staff. In this vein officials have had extensive consultation and engagement with commercial providers to understand their service models and those that are in place across the UK.   In the short term, should additional funding become available, it may be possible for non-HSC providers to help reduce HSC waiting times through the appropriate procurement process.  In the longer term, however, a mixed model of provision requires new additional recurrent money for both the service and its procurement/tendering exercise.   There is currently no additional funding for any service change and as you will be aware the HSC financial position is challenging.In considering this model of care, my Department is aware that similar approaches are already on place in other parts of the UK for benchmarking.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail whether Belfast City Council has any power to change Sunday trading laws; and (ii) , following the decision taken by Belfast City Council on 1 May 2025, to detail any action she is taking on what has been described as a temporary relaxation of enforcement of Sunday trading laws.
answer_text: Sunday trading is not within the remit of the Department for the Economy. This is a matter for the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail whether Belfast City Council has any power to change Sunday trading laws; and (ii) , following the decision taken by Belfast City Council on 1 May 2025, to detail any action he is taking on what has been described as a temporary relaxation of enforcement of Sunday trading laws.
answer_text: The hours during which shops may open on Sundays is set out in The Shops (Sunday Trading & c.) (Northern Ireland) Order 1997. Belfast City Council does not have the power to change this legislation.  My Department is aware that Belfast City Council is considering a pilot initiative which could involve a change to Sunday trading arrangements. My Officials have written to Belfast City Council to request more information on the proposals, and to highlight their responsibilities for enforcement of Sunday Trading under Article 8 of the 1997 Order.

answer_text: The cost of the Loughs Agency’s contracts with communications agencies, including VAT, in each of the last five accounting years is detailed below:	2020 – no contract in place 	2021 – £15,000 (inclusive of VAT at 20%)	2022 – £42,240 (inclusive of VAT at 20%)	2023 – £57,600 (inclusive of VAT at 20%)	2024 – £57,600 (inclusive of VAT at 20%)Loughs Agency has a business need for a number of communications services including marketing and communications support; media relations; design and brand development; media buying and planning; digital and website development; and event management.		Loughs Agency does not currently have the in-house expertise required to deliver the strategic communications necessary to support its work. Developing this capability internally would incur significant ongoing costs, which would outweigh the expense of securing external support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of advice by Police Scotland stating abortion buffer zones cannot be enforced when clinics are closed, whether she has sought clarification as to the situation in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The enforcement of the Abortion Services (Safe Access Zones) Act (Northern Ireland) 2023 is an operational matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issue raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the recent Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers, whether she will withdraw the PACE Code of Practice issued by her Department which states that officers or detainees should be treated as their birth sex unless they have a gender recognition certificate.
answer_text: The Equality Act 2010 does not apply in Northern Ireland; however, the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland has indicated that it will be working at pace to review guidance that it issues to public bodies in light of the Supreme Court judgment and make any amendments as necessary.Careful consideration will be given to the implications that may arise from the judgment, including the Department’s responsibilities under Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 which is the applicable equality legislation in this jurisdiction.The Codes of Practice published in support of the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE), which provide guidance and safeguards on the exercise of powers, such as strip search, will be considered in the context of the recent ruling.  Operation of the current codes remain an operational matter for the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a 30 mph speed limit will be introduced on Mowhan Road, Markethill, opposite the High School.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.When considering the appropriate speed limit for a road, my Department’s policy is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads, taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data. Taking into consideration the above factors together, with  a review of the latest injury collision data from the PSNI, officials have advised that the current speed limit on the Mowhan Road is considered to be appropriate.However, I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.  I have also asked that consideration be also given to the national speed limits that apply on rural roads and HGV speed limits.To ensure that other’s views are considered in the setting of speed limits here, I plan to hold a public consultation exercise later this year.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether a (i) public consultation; and (ii) consultation with political parties, on its Transgender Policy took place before it was adopted.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission’s Equality Scheme (2022-2026) states that “Consultation will seek the views of those directly affected by the matter/policy. This may include representative groups of Section 75 categories, our staff and their Trade Unions”. Consequently, before the Transgender Policy was approved for inclusion in the Assembly Commission’s Staff Handbook, internal consultation with Assembly Commission staff (including inward secondees, agency workers and contractors) took place from 4 to 29 October 2021. As there were no known transgender staff within the Assembly Commission staff body at that time, views on the draft policy were also sought from a number of relevant external groups. Further staff and Trade Union Side internal consultation took place from 14 November to 12 December 2022. As the Policy and Guidance involved changes to the Staff Handbook and were in keeping with the settled legislative and legal position at the time, they were approved by the Senior Management Team.  The Members of the Assembly Commission representing political parties were not consulted.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it sought legal advice before adopting its Transgender Policy.
answer_text: Legal advice was sought during the development of the Transgender Policy.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to list the outside bodies consulted about its Transgender Policy when it was being prepared.
answer_text: 11 organisations were consulted during the development of the Transgender Policy, in advance of its inclusion in the Assembly Commission’s Staff Handbook. Consultation responses were received from Assembly Commission staff, NIPSA, HERe NI, Focus - The Identity Trust and an external organisation which did not wish their details to be published.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will withdraw its Transgender Policy in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: As set out in AQW 26526/22-27, the Assembly Commission is currently seeking its own legal advice in order to review the relevant sections of the Assembly Commission’s Staff Handbook, in the context of the Supreme Court Ruling, including ‘Guidance on assisting and supporting transgender visitors and other users of Parliament Buildings’ which was approved by the Senior Management Team in September 2023.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the date on which its Transgender Policy was adopted.
answer_text: The Transgender Policy, which is a component of the Assembly Commission’s Staff Handbook, was approved by the Commission’s Senior Management Team on 28 February 2023. The Transgender policy applies to Assembly Commission staff, inward secondees, agency workers and contractors.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, further to the Rainbow Project’s Nothing About Us Without Us – Trans Voices In The Halls Of Power event, scheduled to be held in Parliament Buildings on 12 May 2025, whether the organiser’s Event Code of Conduct: Respectful Communication section is consistent with the ethos and values of the Assembly Commission, including freedom of speech.
answer_text: Sponsored events in Parliament Buildings take place under the terms of the Assembly Commission’s ‘Sponsored Events in Parliament Buildings Policy’ and must comply with the requirements of this policy, regardless of the individual policies of any event organiser.Sponsored event organisers and their employees, contractors, and guests must also comply with the Assembly Commission’s Policy ‘Conduct and Behaviour of Visitors in Parliament Buildings’.  The sponsors and organisers of an event are ultimately responsible for the conduct of their employees, contractors and guests during their event.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the duties of Assembly Usher Services staff during the Rainbow Project Nothing About Us Without Us – Trans Voices In The Halls Of Power event in Parliament Buildings on 12 May 2025; and (ii) whether this will differ from their duties in relation to other events.
answer_text: Usher Services staff perform a range of standard operational duties in Parliament Buildings in accordance with established procedures. These include, for example, managing access to the building, supporting the safety and security of all building users, assisting visitor needs and working with the Visitor Experience Team to facilitate the smooth logistical operation of sponsored events.The duties of Usher Services staff in relation to the Rainbow Project Nothing About Us Without Us – Trans Voices In The Halls Of Power event in Parliament Buildings on 12 May 2025 were consistent with those set out above for all events.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, further to the Rainbow Project’s Nothing About Us Without Us – Trans Voices In The Halls Of Power event, scheduled to be held in Parliament Buildings on 12 May 2025, whether the Assembly Commission’s Visitor Experience Team has discussed with the Rainbow Project how enforcement of the Rainbow Project’s Events Code of Conduct will be managed.
answer_text: Sponsored events in Parliament Buildings are required to be inclusive and comply with the Assembly Commission's equality and good relations obligations. Sponsored events take place under the terms of the Assembly Commission’s ‘Sponsored Events in Parliament Buildings Policy’ and must comply with the requirements of this policy, regardless of the individual policies of any event organiser.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the total cost of the First Minister's attendance at the funeral of the Pope, broken down by (a) travel; (b) accommodation; and (c) other expenses; and (ii) those who travelled with the First Minister.
answer_text: Cost of Flights£2942.24AccommodationTBCOther expenses£167.90TravelersFirst MinisterPrincipal Private Secretary

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, further to the Rainbow Project’s Nothing About Us Without Us – Trans Voices In The Halls Of Power event scheduled to be held in Parliament Buildings on 12 May 2025, whether the organiser’s Event Code of Conduct, which advises that if the event venue contains gendered facilities, such as toilets and/or changing facilities, everyone is entitled to use whichever facility they feel best aligns with their gender identity and/or expression, is consistent with (i) the Assembly Commission’s policies; and (ii) the law following the Supreme Court ruling on 16 April 2025.
answer_text: Sponsored events in Parliament Buildings must comply with the policies of the Assembly Commission, regardless of the individual policies of any event organiser.  The sponsoring Members and organisers of an event are ultimately responsible for the conduct of their employees, contractors and guests during the event.While the recent Supreme Court judgment in For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers principally concerned the Equality Act 2010, which does not apply in Northern Ireland, it is nonetheless recognised as a significant legal development requiring consideration.  As a consequence, the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland itself has indicated that it intends to issue written guidance on the implications of the judgment for the law in Northern Ireland in June 2025. In the interim, the Assembly Commission is seeking its own legal advice in order to review the relevant sections of the Assembly Commission’s Staff Handbook, in the context of the Supreme Court Ruling, including ‘Guidance on assisting and supporting transgender visitors and other users of Parliament Buildings’ which was approved by the Senior Management Team in September 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any impact the Supreme Court judgement on the legal definition of a woman will have on the policies of his Department and its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: In light of the recent UK Supreme Court judgment regarding biological sex, I will be setting out the department’s position in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any impact the Supreme Court judgement on the legal definition of a woman will have on the policies of his Department and its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Equality Commission is currently considering any potential implications here due to the Supreme Court ruling and will issue guidance in due course.This issue must be approached with compassion, understanding and dignity for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any impact the Supreme Court judgement on the legal definition of a woman will have on the policies of his Department and its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Department has written to the Equality Commission Northern Ireland (ECNI) and has received confirmation that ECNI is currently developing formal written guidance on this issue, anticipated to be published in June 2025. Once published, the guidance will be taken into consideration by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any impact the Supreme Court judgement on the legal definition of a woman will have on the policies of her Department and its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: The Equality Act 2010 does not apply in Northern Ireland but the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland has indicated that it will be working at pace to review guidance that it issues to public bodies in light of the Supreme Court judgment and make any amendments as necessary.  Careful consideration will be given to the implications of the judgment on the Department’s policies and that will be informed by any changes to guidance issued by the Equality Commission.   It is not possible to provide detail at this stage on what Departmental policies may need to be updated.  The Department’s arm’s-length bodies are individual public bodies for the purposes of responsibilities under Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 and so you may wish to contact them directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a response to the points raised by myself during the debate on the NI Water Funding and Governance motion in the Assembly on 29 April 2025.
answer_text: The points you raised in the motion debate are operational matters for NI Water; however I have sought the information as requested.NI Water has advised that, in respect of your points relating to the 10-house development on the Grange Road in Ballymena, the site is in a constrained catchment due to foul sewage capacity.  The developer applied for a Waste Water Impact Assessment and NI Water identified two storm water offsetting options, as you stated, at a cost of £160k and £175k respectively.      NI Water reports that, since the report was issued in July 2024, it has no record of any contact from the developer and no knowledge of the options that the developer has investigated.NI Water asserts that, if the developer is unable to find a suitable offset, they should contact NI Water directly to discuss alternatives. The use of onsite wastewater treatment as suggested should only be considered when the developer has agreed directly with NI Water that there are no other viable options.      NI Water has advised me that it would be happy to meet with the developer, and any other representatives, to discuss this site and the next steps. However it would be helpful if, in advance of any meeting, the developer provides details of the investigations undertaken and ruled out.  The meeting can be arranged by contacting NI Water initially via customerengagementteam@niwater.com.In respect of the site at Carncombe Road in Connor, the associated design deposit of £12,000 was paid by the developer on 13th June 2024 and it was stated within the conditional response letter that it can take approx. 12 months before the design process can begin.  NI Water expects to conclude the design and issue a final recommendation by July 2025, when the scheme will be priced by its framework contractors and a final offer letter will be issued.NI Water has 6 months under legislation to deliver the construction aspect following the financial obligation stated on the final letter being met by the developer. While NI Water has aligned with the communicated timeframes, it will seek to review construction phasing and resourcing in a bid to shorten the construction process as much as possible.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any impact the Supreme Court judgement on the legal definition of a woman will have on the policies of his Department and its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: I believe strongly in the need to protect the integrity of single sex spaces and I welcome the judgement from the Supreme Court, which provides clarity in law. I have tasked officials within my Department to analyse the judgement and consider the implications of the ruling on the policy responsibilities that fall to the Department and ALBs. I will be making a statement on this in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for patients with rheumatoid arthritis to have their regular six monthly appointment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: It is not possible to provide specific average review waiting times for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The appropriate review period is determined by individual clinical decisions, which consider several factors such as urgency, the stability of the patient’s condition, prescribed medication, and the capacity within the clinical team.Trusts are also unable to provide an average waiting time for these patients, as they are managed within general rheumatology services rather than as a distinct group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 24837/22-27, to detail who fulfils this function on other cross-border catchments such as the Erne and the Blackwater.
answer_text: The conservation and protection function on cross-border catchments within Northern Ireland is carried out by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, and the fisheries conservation function by my Inland Fisheries Inspectorate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 22967/22-27, for his assessment of the fall in the number of children born with Down syndrome post-2019.
answer_text: Data from the https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/services-and-teams/population-data-and-intelligence/statistics indicate that the birth prevalence of Down’s Syndrome fell by around 22% between 2019 and 2023, from 1.8 per 1,000 total births to 1.4 per 1,000 total births (source: Child Health System). However, similar prevalence rates for Down’s Syndrome have been recorded in earlier years. It is important to note that this reduction has occurred alongside a sustained decrease in overall birth numbers. Total registered births declined from 22,514 in 2019 to 20,029 in 2023, approximately 11%. NISRA has reported a further fall to 19,476 births in 2024, continuing this demographic trend. Lower overall birth numbers contribute directly to fewer cases of all congenital conditions, including Down’s Syndrome. Birth prevalence of Down’s Syndrome is also influenced by factors such as maternal age and the uptake of antenatal screening and diagnostic services. The Core Tables highlight a strong agerelated gradient, with Down’s Syndrome rates rising from 0.6 per 1,000 births (for births 2019-2023) in mothers aged 25–29, to 3.06 per 1,000 in those aged 35–39, and 6.79 per 1,000 for mothers aged 40–44. PHA will continue to monitor trends through official statistics and to support equitable access to antenatal screening, information, and diagnostic pathways for expectant parents across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any advice that has been offered to GP surgeries in relation to patients with rheumatoid arthritis who have been unable to have their regular six monthly appointment due to the backlog in the system to ensure they are issued with their required medication.
answer_text: There has been no specific advice offered to GP surgeries relating to patients with rheumatoid arthritis who are unable to have their six-monthly appointment to receive their required medications. Rheumatoid arthritis management is not specifically mentioned under the Northern Ireland Contract Assurance Framework. However, depending on the type of medication prescribed for rheumatoid arthritis, patients may come under the clinical care domain of additional monitoring of amber drugs.The ‘Red Amber List’ of specialist medicines is a guide for practitioners in both primary and secondary care. It provides professional guidance on where prescribing responsibility should lie for these specialist medicines thus ensuring clinicians make an informed choice with regard to their prescribing thereby facilitating access to these medicines by patients throughout Northern Ireland. This classification is decided by the Interface Pharmacist Network for Health and Social Care (HSC) and relates to a recommendation on prescribing responsibility for the medication and whether or not the supply of a medicine should be organised through the hospital pharmacy network.  ‘Amber Drugs’ are specialist medications which have significant pharmacological complexity and/or rarity of use to make the prescribing of the medicine relatively uncommon in the community.  For these reasons, ‘amber’ medications require a written shared care arrangement between a GP, the recommending specialist and the patient, covering respective roles and responsibilities for ongoing follow up. As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will now require that (i) details of babies born alive after an attempted abortion are collected in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the outcome of attempts to preserve the life of the child is recorded.
answer_text: I require Trusts to have detail of a baby born alive after an attempted abortion indicated on the health care record, however my Department does not require Trusts to collate this information. I refer you to my response to AQW 23073/22-27, (i) details of babies born alive after an attempted abortion are not collected in Northern Ireland.  I can confirm that should a baby be born alive after a termination of pregnancy procedure, the baby receives the support and care that is in the baby’s best interests and the baby’s condition is managed in line with professional guidance for neonatal practice.  I require that (ii) the outcomes of attempts to preserve the life of the child are recorded.  I can confirm that this information is recorded on the health care record within trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount Northern Ireland (i) has received; and (ii) will receive through the Barnett Formula 2025-26, further to increases in UK Government department budgets, for the purpose of providing funding to the public sector for support with additional costs associated with changes to Employer National Insurance contributions policy.
answer_text: ANSWERPresently, the Executive  has not received any funding associated with the increases to Whitehall department budgets for additional Employer National Insurance contributions.Treasury has confirmed that the Executive will receive £146 million of Barnett consequentials  as part of  the Westminster  Main Estimates process.  Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated.  As such, it is a matter for the Executive to agree how this funding will be allocated considering all competing priorities.Budget 2025-26, agreed by all Ministers on 3 April 2025, prioritises the finite amount of funding available providing indicative June Monitoring allocations to departments in line with Programme for Government priorities, including funding for cutting health waiting lists, Special Educational Needs and Lough Neagh.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the current status of Northern Ireland Co-operation Overseas, including its cost to date.
answer_text: NI-CO is currently in liquidation. A strategic review by the NI-CO Board in 2023 concluded that the company was no longer a going concern and should cease operations by 31st March 2024. This decision was largely predicated on the company’s inability to bid for EU contracts due to changes in the trading relationship with the EU. Having undertaken a full inquiry into the company’s affairs and formed the opinion that there were enough assets within the company to pay all debts, in August 2024 the NI-CO Board passed the relevant resolutions to recommend to Invest NI (as shareholder) that the company was voluntarily wound up by way of a Members’ Voluntary Liquidation. KPMG was appointed as Liquidators. The Liquidation process includes the continuing requirement for orderly engagement with EU auditors to meet the contractual obligations under previously received EU funding still within the control periods.  KPMG has provided interim receipts and payments to 31st March 2025 which show net costs of £333,956 (excluding VAT) since the company entered liquidation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the legal aid payments made in relation to cases involving incidents during the period 1969 - 1998 of alleged killing, injury or other misconduct and alleged criminal behaviour by (i) state forces; and (ii) terrorists in each of the last five years, broken down by legal firm.
answer_text: The Legal Services Agency records and administers legal aid cases on the basis of Natures and Matters.  In turn, and due to the resultant formatting of data within the legal aid case management system, it is not possible to identify or extract cases ‘involving incidents during the period 1969 - 1998’ without necessitating a manual case-by-case review of all paper (pre-2019) and electronic files, together with supporting documents, as such a generic categorisation does not exist in the database.Accordingly, it is not possible to attribute legal aid expenditure exclusively to these case types, nor to provide any further breakdown of such, including payments made to individual legal firms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) all occasions when (a) the Minister; and (b) officials from within her Department, travelled outside the British Isles at a cost to the public purse since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024; (ii) those from her Department, or in connection with her Department, that travelled in each case; and (iii) the overall cost to the public purse in each case, broken down by (a) travel; (b) accommodation; and (c) other expenses.
answer_text: Since restoration of the Executive in February 2024, there have been 10 occasions where officials in my Department undertook travel outside Britain and Ireland. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) all occasions when (a) the Minister; and (b) officials from within his Department, travelled outside the British Isles at a cost to the public purse since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024; (ii) those from his Department, or in connection with his Department, that travelled in each case; and (iii) the overall cost to the public purse in each case, broken down by (a) travel; (b) accommodation; and (c) other expenses.
answer_text: Since restoration of the Executive in February 2024, there have been 8 occasions where officials in my Department undertook travel outside the British Isles. The table below sets out the overall cost to the public purse and provides a breakdown by travel, accommodation, and other expenses.As Minister of Health, I flew economy class on 11 March 2025 for a six-day working trip to Washington DC. I was accompanied by the Special Adviser and my Private Secretary. This working engagement – to coincide with St Patrick’s Day celebrations – was focused on strengthening transatlantic partnerships and exploring innovative approaches to improving health and social care services in Northern Ireland.During the visit, I met senior officials from the Pan American Health Organisation (PAHO), the Global Health Institute at Georgetown University, the Lombardi Comprehensive Cancer Centre, the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA), which is an agency within the US Department of Health and Human Services, as well as leading healthcare providers, academics and research institutions. My final engagement was a tour of the George Washington University Hospital. Discussions centred on best practice in tackling waiting lists, enhancing mental health services and integrating cutting-edge technology into healthcare delivery.The visit was an invaluable opportunity to learn from international leaders in health and social care and to showcase Northern Ireland’s expertise on a global stage. I look forward to building on these discussions in the months ahead to deliver real improvements for patients. question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 25556/22-27, (i) whether the settlement covered the full costs of the roof repairs, including all expenses on experts; and (ii) whether the Assembly Commission’s outlays and legal costs were fully covered by the settlement.
answer_text: The proceedings issued by the Assembly Commission in respects of defects in work carried out to the roof of Parliament Buildings were settled on confidential terms following a judge-led mediation in January 2025.  An agreement on costs formed part of the settlement agreement. The settlement agreement has been filed with the High Court.  While the Assembly Commission is pleased to have concluded this matter, the terms of the settlement do not allow any further detail to be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the action short of strike undertaken by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service and the death of William Darragh on 21 December 2024, whether there has been a change of policy where crews will be asked to attend Category 2 calls in the last hour of their shift.
answer_text: Trade union partners have agreed to engage in negotiations regarding changes to shift patterns, including overlap and staggering while the underlying issue of prolonged handover delays are being worked on. Any changes to shift patterns must be progressed through established industrial relations processes and are dependent on operational stability. NIAS has completed a Demand and Capacity Review to provide an evidence base for future rota redesign, and a regional rota review remains a medium-term objective to be delivered in partnership with staff and trade unions.With regard to my engagement with trade unions in relation to handling Category 2 calls during the last hour of a crew’s shift, I met with trade union representatives on 23 June 2025 and reaffirmed my commitment to supporting measures that improve staff welfare and operational resilience. At that meeting, I confirmed my support for implementing the principles of the London model for hospital handover, which trade unions have referenced as best practice. NIAS has also maintained regular engagement with unions, including structured discussions in November 2025 with Trusts and SPPG, to progress solutions around ambulance handover.With regard to whether there has been a change of policy where crews will be asked to attend Category 2 calls in the last hour of their shift, I can confirm that the policy on attending Category 2 calls in the last hour of a crew’s shift has not changed. NIAS continues to allocate only Category 1 calls during this period. This approach prioritises the most critical patients and mitigates extended delays and compensatory rest requirements.The most significant operational constraint remains prolonged hospital handover delays, which routinely remove crews from the system and create unpredictable shift overruns. A regionally coordinated programme, mandated by HSC Trust Chief Executives and supported by my Department, is now in place to deliver improvement through three steps: elimination of handovers exceeding two hours; implementation of protocols to sustain improvement; and progression towards a 15-minute handover target. While early improvements have been observed, most notably in the Belfast and Western Trusts, the desired levels of performance are not yet being delivered consistently across all hospital sites. Clinical modelling confirms that reducing hospital turnaround time below one hour is critical to improving response performance and workforce stability.My Department remains committed to resolving action short of a strike (ASOS) through collaborative engagement with trade unions and system partners. Progress on rota reform and shift pattern changes will be supported by consistent delivery of hospital handover improvements, including the adoption of the London model principles. Further discussions with trade unions are planned, and NIAS has formally requested that ASOS arrangements be stood down.My priority is to ensure safe, effective and timely ambulance se

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 23073/22-27, how Health and Social Care Trusts ensure that, when a baby is born alive after an abortion, they comply with the requirements of the Births and Deaths Registration (Northern Ireland) Order 1976.
answer_text: When a baby is born alive after an abortion each of the 5 Health and Social Care Trusts act accordingly in line with requirements of the Births and Deaths Registration (Northern Ireland) Order 1976.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why staggered shifts have not been introduced for Northern Ireland Ambulance Service staff as a result of the current action short of strike which has been ongoing since November 2023.
answer_text: I recognise the challenges that underpin the ongoing Action Short of Strike (ASOS). I am keen to work with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) and the unions to address these and to that end, I intend to meet with both the Northern Ireland Public Service Alliance (NIPSA) and Unite soon to hear firsthand their concerns and to seek a way forward.NIAS has already implemented a range of actions to circumnavigate and mitigate the impact of ASOS with an aim to maximising ambulance capacity and crew availability. This includes working closely with Hospital Trusts in relation to releasing crews at shift handover times and working with NIAS staff and trade union colleagues to change operational shift rotas. The implementation of new staggered shift arrangements replacing legacy shift patterns that have been in place historically requires NIAS to undertake extensive collaborative working and engagement with staff / trade union and management. Significantly, new staggered shift patterns have been introduced in Omagh and Derriaghy, featuring varied shift timings. These rotas, which were initiated in May 2024, operate daily from 0600 to 0300 hours and are predominantly staffed by newly qualified recruits. These schedules are designed to complement the core rotas in both locations, with a minimum of 15 hours between shifts to minimise the need for compensatory rest.Additionally, in Carrickfergus, Rapid Response Vehicles (RRVs) now commence shifts at different times compared to Dual Crewed Ambulances (DCAs) and Independent Ambulance Service (IAS) crews. We encourage crews working overtime to participate in 'non-typical' shifts, such as the one in Armagh today from 1430 to 2330 hours. Moreover, a shift adjustment in Kilkeel has modified crew start and finish times from the previous 0800-2000 to the current 0700-1900 hours.A comprehensive review of the rota system across the entire South East Division has been conducted to assess the feasibility of introducing additional staggered shifts within NIAS. However, the implementation of a pilot to apply these new shift patterns has been temporarily paused to allow for further discussions with trade union representatives. A new start date will be determined following these consultations.A review of the current service model has been commissioned to ensure optimisation of clinical resources to respond. Proposals have been developed and NIAS is committed to progress this position.On consideration of all of the details of the multiple ASOS notices from recognised trade unions the resulting complexities of ambulance deployment for Northern Ireland across multiple teams and staff members who were either engaged in different forms of action, or not taking action resulted in NIAS taking pragmatic steps based on the following:The potential volume of late finishes due to protracted ambulance handover delays (even within the Category 2 cohort) which would undoubtedly result from allocating a Cat 2 call in the last hour
