question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he has taken to end the action short of strike in the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service.
answer_text: I have already appealed to the unions involved to call a halt to this action. I do recognise the challenges underpinning this action, including the severe unscheduled care pressures that lead to delayed ambulance handovers. I am keen to work with NIAS and the unions to address the issues facing the workforce. To that end, I intend to meet with both NIPSA and Unite soon to hear firsthand their concerns and to seek a way forward.I value my working relationship with the health unions and we worked hard this year to get a pay deal over the line that maintained parity with England. I do not believe this ongoing industrial action at NIAS serves the interests of patients or staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she first made contact with Translink in relation to installing Irish language signage at Belfast Grand Central Station.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of ambulance vehicles available in each Health and Social Care Trust at any one time.
answer_text: My Department is dedicated to providing availability and operational readiness of ambulance services across all Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland, including the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) ensures that a sufficient number of ambulance vehicles are available to cover all core shifts to allow the service to operate effectively 365 days a year (as outlined on Table 1 and 2 below). Table 1Core Shifts per Division (Day and Night) - A&E Ambulance  MondayTuesdayWednesdayThursdayFridaySaturdaySundayNI TotalDay65646464626060 Night47474747525251NorthDay20191918181819 Night12121212121212BelfastDay8888888 Night7777777EastDay13131313121111 Night9999111110SouthDay11111212111010 Night9999101010WestDay13131213131312 Night10101010121212Table 2Core Shifts per Division (Day and Night) -Rapid Response Vehicles (RRV)  MondayTuesdayWednesdayThursdayFridaySaturdaySundayNI TotalDay18171618181817 Late19182017181719NorthDay4224422 Late2442244BelfastDay4434444 Late5464444EastDay4444454 Late5444535SouthDay3443344 Late4334433WestDay3333333 Late3333333The core number of A&E ambulances that NIAS plans to have available across Northern Ireland for day shift in a week, ranges from 60-65; and for night shifts between 47-52. The core number of Rapid Response Vehicles (RRVs) that NIAS plans to have available for day shift in a week is 16-18; and for late shifts is 17-20.The number of vehicles operational at any one time varies depending on the day of the week and time of day, influenced by factors such as planned and unplanned maintenance. Specifically, within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, the core number of A&E ambulances available per day for day shifts in a week ranges from 18 to 20, and for night shifts, it is 12. The core number of RRVs available daily for day shifts during a typical week ranges from 2 to 4, and for late shifts is also 2 to 4.These numbers are carefully calculated based on capacity and demand, with considerations for geographical area and rurality to ensure that the distribution of resources meets the specific needs of each division within the Trusts. This strategic deployment aids in providing effective and responsive ambulance services across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the spend by (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies on Irish language in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Under Article 89 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, the Department of Education (DE) has a duty to encourage and facilitate the development of Irish-Medium education. This article states “Irish-medium education” means education provided in an Irish speaking school. This article also states that the Department may pay grants to anybody appearing to the Department to have as an objective the encouragement or promotion of Irish-medium education. This body is Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG). Funding provided to CnaG over the last 3 years is laid out in the table below under line a).CnaG also receive funding to take forward recommendations from A Fair Start report in relation to Irish-medium education. This funding is outlined in line b) of the table below.DE also has a small, earmarked pot of funding to support Irish-Medium Units attached to English-Medium Schools. This is laid out in line c) of the table below.Line d) shows the costs of any DE documents translated to the Irish language.All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme (CFS).  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements.  A school’s CFF allocation is determined by a combination of its overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  There are two specific factors within the CFF which seek to support Irish medium schools and units, reflecting the additional costs incurred by these schools in relation to the development of curriculum materials and administrative support for Irish-medium units. In all other aspects of the CFF, Irish medium schools are treated equitably and therefore only funding additional to that received by other schools has been included in this response. The total funding distributed to Irish Medium Schools and Units for Curricular and Administrative Support from 2022-23 to 2024-25, under the CFF is detailed in lines e) and f) in the table below.Within the one-year Irish Medium Primary Postgraduate Certificate in Education (PGCE) course at St. Mary’s University College, the Department provides funding to St Mary’s for the provision of a “Gaeltacht Experience” – this is a two-week immersive experience in the Gaeltacht district of Gweedore in County Donegal. The funding has been provided since 2009/10 following a review of Irish Medium Education in October 2008, which included the recommendation that “Irish-language skills should be developed during Irish-medium initial teacher education, with experience in the Gaeltacht strongly recommended as an integral part of this development.” This funding is laid out in line g) of the table below.My Department has also funded Altram to develop and pilot an Irish-Medium Pre-school Immersion Education course for Northern Ireland and to provide the Irish-Medium Pre-school Support Service. question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the projected total cost to the public purse of (i) maintenance work currently being carried out to the roof of Parliament Buildings to deal with the issue of water ingress and to rectify issues which arose due to work carried out by the previous contractor; and (ii) any other maintenance work currently being carried out on Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has a responsibility to maintain Parliament Buildings in accordance with its Grade A listed status.  This necessitates general annual expenditure to deal with ongoing repairs and maintenance, as well as additional expenditure when significant issues or projects arise. The costs of remedial work to address issues in the roof arising from work carried out by the previous contractor should be considered in the context that the Assembly Commission was pleased with the outcome of a full and final confidential financial settlement which was reached in January 2025.  Scaffolding was erected to make both the inner Courtyards and rear parapet safe and to carry out investigatory works to enable full scoping of the roof repairs.  The scaffolding will also be required for upcoming roof repairs which will take place in two phases.  Not all invoices have yet been received and therefore the costs to date for roof repairs have been estimated at £141,248 for scaffolding and £59,030 on professional fees.  The estimated total repair costs for the roof are £2.453m.  Routine maintenance tasks are taking place such as replacing boiler room pumps, fixing faulty taps, repairing broken radiator valves, and replacing damaged tiles.  This financial year, a total of £141,755 has been spent on repairs and maintenance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 25160/22-27, to detail where in the Partnership Agreement provides for the Minister to make an operational decision.

To ask the Minister of Justice, following the recent break-in at Dundonald House and in relation to finding an alternative location in which to store her Department's materials, to confirm whether the new storage facility is secure.
answer_text: I can confirm that the new storage facility is secure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following the recent break-in at Dundonald House, for her assessment of whether the building was a suitable location to store sensitive and personnel information owned by her Department.
answer_text: Whilst Dundonald House has been largely vacated since June 2023 as a result of health and safety concerns, some areas remained in use. Between June 2023 and March 2025, the Northern Ireland Prison Service used some areas of the building to hold personnel and other files.  These areas had appropriate and specific security measures in place to ensure that information was held safely and securely.The Prison Service also had a reasonable expectation that all government buildings, particularly those on the Stormont estate, are protected and secure from this type of activity. Whilst no files held in Dundonald House were compromised, following the recent incidents of unauthorised access to Dundonald House a further risk assessment was undertaken and the decision taken to relocate all Prison Service files to a storage location within the prison estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any information held in Dundonald House that was compromised as a result of the recent break-in.
answer_text: No information held by the Northern Ireland Prison Service in Dundonald House was compromised as a result of the recent incidences of unauthorised access.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of procurement credit cards held by staff in her Department; (ii) the amount expended by her Department through a procurement credit card since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024; and (iii) on what was the expenditure incurred.
answer_text: There are four procurement credit cards held by staff in my Department with expenditure totalling £57,678 incurred since February 2024. Credit cards are used primarily for online purchases from other public sector bodies, with almost £56,000 incurred on MOTs for Departmental vehicles and property certificate applications. Other minor expenditure includes IT and media related annual subscriptions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the recent break-in at Dundonald House, to detail (i) why the building was not manned or properly secured at the time; and (ii) any new security measures that have been taken since the break-in.
answer_text: Dundonald House was locked at the time of the recent unauthorised access. This occurred outside of normal opening hours for the building and was the result of a break-in which was reported to the PSNI. The building was staffed during normal business opening hours. Following the recent unauthorised access to the building, the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) undertook a review of risks and determined that its remaining files should be removed from Dundonald House.  This has now been completed and the Prison Service has fully vacated the building.  Following the incidents, additional arrangements were put in place by NIPS until all its files had been relocated, including physical security measures.The Department of Finance (DoF) has confirmed that, since the unauthorised access: all locks on the entry/exit doors to the building have been changed: chains and padlocks have been put in place where access is believed to have been gained; and access to the roof has been closed off.DoF is also arranging for wire mesh to be installed internally to cover the windows on the lower floors of the building to help prevent further unauthorised entry and has closed the pedestrian access point adjacent to Dundonald House. The need for on-going or further security measures at or around Dundonald House will be kept under review by DoF, given its responsibility for the building.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how making the operational decision on signage at Belfast Grand Central Station is compatible with the Partnership Agreement between the Department for Infrastructure and the Northern Ireland Transport Holding Company.
answer_text: The decision to introduce Irish language signage at Belfast Grand Central Station is compatible with the principles of leadership, engagement, and value as outlined in the Partnership Agreement.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of procurement credit cards held by staff in their Department; (ii) the amount expended by their Department through a procurement credit card since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024; and (iii) on what was the expenditure incurred.
answer_text: The Executive Office (TEO) currently holds 10 procurement cards. Total expenditure through these cards since restoration of the Executive in February in 2024 to 28th February 2025 was £106,784The analysis of the business areas where the procurement card expenditure was incurred is in table below:Card Holder Office£Brussels1,093 dFM Private Office6,576 FM Private Office6,451 Finance - Virtual Card17,879 Washington74,785 Total106,784 The procurement cards are used mainly for travel and accommodation expenses, event costs in international offices, and other costs would include items such as office stationery and equipment expenditure. The expenditure incurred by the Office of the NI Bureau in Brussels and the NI Bureau Washington includes promoting NI’s capabilities internationally and developing mutually beneficial relationships with targeted countries, regions and international organisations to enhance our credibility and realise tangible benefits for our community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the cost to the consumer of electricity in Northern Ireland as it compares to prices in (i) Great Britain; and (ii) the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The most recent report by the Retail Energy Market Forum (provided by the Utility Regulator) was published on 14 March 2025, detailing electricity prices from January to June 2024. Q4. 2024 QREMM report

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to fill the vacant Bee Inspector post.
answer_text: The Department does not employ dedicated bee inspectors, however, bee inspection duties are performed by appropriately trained staff from within the wider range of Agricultural Inspectorate duties. In recent months, due to a retirement and the unexpected death of an inspector, this has reduced the number of inspectors trained to perform bee inspections. The Department is in the process of recruiting staff to its overall inspector capacity. Several appointments have been made from recent competitions, with further competitions planned in the coming months, which will increase the inspector capacity from which bee inspection duties will be allocated.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the cost of installing (i) Irish language signage at Belfast Grand Central Station; and (ii) an Irish language option on all Translink ticket vending machines.
answer_text: The cost of the new physical signage at the Belfast Grand Central station is estimated to be £120k; and The cost for updating the ticket vending machines, is estimated to be £28k.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to publish the guest list for the Northern Ireland Bureau's St. Patrick's Day breakfast in Washington in 2025.
answer_text: The NI Bureau does not have permission from guests to release their names.  We can confirm that 320 people were invited to the breakfast drawn from the Bureau's diplomatic, economic, cultural, political, academic, media and think tank networks in the US and figures visiting from NI.  250 guests attended.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the percentage share of working-age people receiving disability benefits by main conditions of (i) mental and behavioural; (ii) musculo-skeletal; and (iii) other.
answer_text: The most recent Personal Independence Payment statistics were published in February 2025 and covered the period up to 30 November 2024. At this date, 217,660 claimants were in receipt of Personal Independence Payment. This represents approximately 15.12% of the working age population in Northern Ireland. Of all Personal Independence Payment claimants: 45% have a main disabling condition categorised as ‘Psychiatric disorders (Mental Illness)’; 31% have a main disabling condition recorded as ‘Musculoskeletal’ disease; and  	23% have other main disabling conditions recorded.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any tweets proposed by the deputy First Minister during her trip to the United States of America over the St Patrick’s Day period in March 2025 for which agreement was sought from the First Minister but not granted.
answer_text: There are operational practices in place within the Department to ensure there is joint agreement on external communications activity, in relation to the official departmental social media accounts.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the specific tweets posted by the deputy First Minister during her trip to the United States of America over the St Patrick’s Day period in March 2025 for which agreement was sought from the First Minister.
answer_text: There are operational practices in place within the Department to ensure there is joint agreement on external communications activity on posts on official department social media sites.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any information held by his Department and its arm's-length bodies on advice offered to schools when a pupil wishes to identify as an animal.
answer_text: The department nor any of its arm's-length bodies provide advice to schools on this issue.   In response to emergent misinformation on this topic, and to emphasise the importance of children and young people understanding the boundaries to healthy behaviours and relationships, a guide developed by INEQE Safeguarding Group, was previously made available, via the Safer Schools Northern Ireland App, for education professionals and parent/carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils in Northern Ireland that currently identify as an animal.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on pupils who identify as animals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils in Northern Ireland that identify differently to their birth gender.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on pupil’s gender identity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the responsibilities of the Loughs Agency when it comes to the environment, including monitoring and investigation of pollution, distinct from those exercised by his Department and other statutory agencies including NIEA and AFBI staff.
answer_text: Loughs Agency is responsible for the enforcement of the Foyle Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1952 and the Foyle Fisheries Act 1952 (Ireland), and a series of adaptable secondary regulations and provisions, stemming from EU Directives and legislation on water pollution and water quality. It also works with other agencies in both jurisdictions investigating pollution incidents, including fishkills, and monitoring water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of births in Northern Ireland in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency NISRA collates data on birth registrations as collected by the General Register Office (GRO). NISRA produces monthly totals for births registered and births occurring from 2006 to February 2025. Registration data is based on the date the birth was registered with GRO; this data is final for the years up to and including 2023 but is provisional for 2024 onwards. Occurrence data is based on date of birth, and is subject to change, as some births will have occurred but will not have been registered at the time of publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of staff employed in the Loughs Agency’s conservation and protectorate directorate; and (ii) the annual cost of this directorate.
answer_text: Loughs Agency currently employs 23 staff within its Conservation and Protectorate Directorate. In the 2024 calendar year the salary cost of the Directorate was £979k.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the responsibilities of the Loughs Agency’s conservation and protectorate directorate, distinct from those exercised by his Department and other statutory agencies including NIEA and AFBI staff.
answer_text: As set out in the North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, the Loughs Agency, and specifically the Agency’s Conservation and Protectorate Directorate, is responsible for the conservation and protection of the fisheries in the Foyle and Carlingford Areas.  This includes the enforcement of the Foyle Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1952, the Foyle Fisheries Act 1952 (Ireland), and a series of adaptable secondary regulations and provisions, stemming from EU Directives and legislation on pollution and water quality.The Loughs Agency operates with a cross-border remit within the internationally shared sea loughs and catchments of Lough Foyle and Carlingford Lough.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of nets seized by the Loughs Agency’s conservation and protectorate directorate in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of nets seized in each of the last 10 years by the Loughs Agency, using the legal powers enforced by the Conservation and Protection Directorate, are detailed below:YearNumber of Nets Seized201553201649201778201841201931202037202140202229202333202437
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the function of the Loughs Agency’s conservation and protectorate directorate, distinct from the responsibilities exercised by his Department, particularly given that commercial salmon fishing is suspended in the Foyle area.
answer_text: The main function of the Conservation and Protection Directorate within the Loughs Agency is to protect and conserve fish stocks and habitats throughout the Foyle and Carlingford Areas, as set out in the Foyle Fisheries Act 1952 (Northern Ireland) and the Foyle Fisheries Act (Ireland) 1952. The Loughs Agency operates with a cross-border remit within the internationally shared sea loughs and catchments of Lough Foyle and Carlingford Lough.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the specific responsibilities of the Loughs Agency’s science team, distinct from those exercised by his Department and other statutory agencies including NIEA and AFBI staff.
answer_text: The Loughs Agency’s Science Team provides scientific advice to inform and support the environmental management and policy development decisions made by the Agency in line with its statutory responsibilities under The North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of staff employed in the science team in the Loughs Agency; (ii) the annual cost of this science team; (iii) the number of science staff currently being recruited; (iv) any plans to recruit more science staff; and (v) the rationale for any expansion to the science team.
answer_text: Loughs Agency currently employs nine staff within its Science Team.  In 2024 the annual cost of the Science Team was £322k. The Agency is currently recruiting three staff into the Science Team.There are no plans to appoint any further science staff at this time.The current recruitment being undertaken will be used to fill two vacant permanent posts.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the legislative basis for the Loughs Agency in relation to aquaculture, distinct from the responsibilities exercised by his Department and other statutory agencies including the NIEA and AFBI.
answer_text: While the Foyle and Carlingford Fisheries (Northern Ireland) Order 2007 extended the functions of the Loughs Agency to include the development and licensing of aquaculture in the Foyle and Carlingford areas, in line with the North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999, the provisions of that Order have not all been commenced.  Therefore, it is my Department which is currently responsible for the development and licensing of aquaculture in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any specific responsibilities the Loughs Agency has to promote marine tourism, distinct from those exercised by the Department for Communities and local councils.
answer_text: The responsibilities of Loughs Agency in respect of marine tourism in the Foyle and Carlingford Areas are set out in the North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999. This includes the promotion of development of Lough Foyle and Carlingford Lough for commercial and recreational purposes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of boardrooms that the Loughs Agency maintains.
answer_text: Loughs Agency maintains three boardrooms.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department is supporting Mid and East Antrim Borough Council’s efforts to create a hydrogen hub in Ballymena, to support Northern Ireland manufacturers’ transition to hydrogen.

To ask the Minister for Communities, further to his recent visit to the United States of America, to outline any funding for communities to explore and celebrate the links between Northern Ireland and the United States of America ahead of the Semiquincentennial of the Declaration of Independence.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the total number of staff, including temporary staff, in the Loughs Agency; and (ii) whether any staff posts are treated as projects and not salary in the accounts.
answer_text: The total number of staff in Loughs Agency, including temporary staff, is 68. Two of these posts are externally funded and are treated as project costs in the accounts.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the (i) established; and (ii) approved staff complement in the Loughs Agency.
answer_text: Loughs Agency currently employs 68 people including 21 temporary staff and two project staff on fixed term contracts. The approved staff complement of the Agency is 53 (Full Time Equivalent).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures she has taken to cut back on the spend by (i) her Department; and (ii) its arm’s-length bodies on diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Given the extent of pressures facing my Department, guidance was issued to business areas within my Department stating that action should be taken to reduce spend where possible, including stopping discretionary spend. No specific guidance was issued in relation to spending on diversity, equality and inclusion training which, in my view, is important in ensuring that the Department meets its responsibilities under Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.Guidance was also issued to the Non-Departmental Public Bodies of my Department stating that consideration should be given to minimising discretionary spending. No specific guidance was issued in relation to diversity, equality and inclusion training as such matters would be a decision for the Accounting Officer of each organisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide a breakdown of organisations in receipt of payments from (i) her Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies for providing diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: My Department and its arm’s-length bodies (other than as noted below) made no payments to organisations in relation to the provision of diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.My Department does not hold information in relation to spend on diversity, equality and inclusion training by the following Non-Departmental Public Bodies of my Department, given their operational independence which is established in legislation: Police Service of Northern Ireland;Northern Ireland Policing Board; andOffice of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland.You may, therefore, wish to contact them directly to request the information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures she has taken to cut back on the spend by (i) her Department; and (ii) its arm’s-length bodies on diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Diversity, equality and inclusion training is provided centrally by the Department of Finance for all NICS departments and as such the Department would not make cuts to NICS this training.  None of the Department’s Arms Length Bodies have taken measures to cut back on their spend on diversity, equality and inclusion training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures he has taken to cut back on the spend by (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm’s-length bodies on diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The financial challenges facing the Health and Social Care sector in Northern Ireland, mean that the Department of Health and its Arm’s Length Bodies are required to continue to scrutinise spending and attempt to identify areas where savings can be made.In the case of the Department of Health and its Arm’s Length Bodies there was no spending on diversity, equality and inclusion training planned for the 2024-25 financial year and therefore no possibility that it could be reduced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a breakdown of organisations in receipt of payments from (i) her Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies for providing diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since February 2024 my Department has acquired Inclusion London to deliver Disability Equality Training (DET) to DfI Senior Civil Servants, in collaboration with the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC). With regards to the Department’s Arms Length Bodies, neither NI Water and Translink have paid for such external providers in the period.During the period, Waterways Ireland has engaged three external providers – Legal Island, the NOW Group and HR Business Suite.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a breakdown of organisations in receipt of payments from (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies for providing diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Department of Health has not made payment to any organisation to provide diversity, equality and inclusion training since February 2024. Regional Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts commissioned and paid for cultural competency training during 2024/25 delivered by Denis Long Consultancy. The Equality Unit in Business Services Organisation (BSO) delivers equality and diversity training to other HSC Arm’s Length Bodies as part of their service level agreements. Since the restoration of the Executive the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service has  received equality and diversity training from the National Fire Chiefs Council, Asian Fire Service Association, Women in Business NI and Women in Fire UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any measures he has taken to cut back on the spend by (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm’s-length bodies on diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Departmental spend in relation to diversity, equality and inclusion, specifically activity relating to LGBTQ+ issues and engagement with Stonewall, has been the subject of Assembly Questions (AQW 12684/22-27; AQW 12809/22-27; AQW 12955/22-27 : AQW 13187/22-27; AQW 17618/22-27; AQW 19695/22-27; AQW 19696/22-27;  AQW 19697/22-27; AQW 19698/22-27; AQW 22119/22-27; AQW 22583/22-27; AQW 24567/22-27 and AQO 1716/22-27), FoIs and media attention. All of which require staff resources to research and answer.		The Department of Finance is responsible for Civil Service people policies, and for actions developed and delivered on behalf of all Departments, through the annual Civil Service Diversity Action Plan.  Consequently, the majority of spend on equality, diversity and inclusion activity, including training, is incurred by the Department of Finance. 		The Department of Finance has one arm’s length body, the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB), which DoF undertakes the sponsorship role.  It is a North/South Body established under the Good Friday/Belfast Agreement. It has confirmed that training is delivered in line with business need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a breakdown of organisations in receipt of payments from (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies for providing diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: ANSWERA breakdown of the organisations receiving payments from (i) my Department and (ii) its arm’s length body, Special European Union Programmes Body (SEUPB), for providing diversity, equality and inclusion training since February 2024 is set out below. This includes training delivered by my Department for all Civil Service staff. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures she has taken to cut back on the spend by (i) her Department; and (ii) its arm’s-length bodies on diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Minister for the Economy (DfE) has not taken any measures to reduce spend on diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024. The Department of Finance provides most of the diversity, equality and inclusion training for DfE staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures he has taken to cut back on the spend by (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm’s-length bodies on diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: My Department and its ALB’s are operating in a constrained budgetary environment, and any spend on such training should demonstrate necessity and value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown of organisations in receipt of payments from (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies for providing diversity, equality and inclusion training since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Department, together with several of its arms-length bodies (ALBs), subscribe to online training provided by the Centre for Applied Learning (CAL), which provides general training to the Northern Ireland Civil Service.  This is provided at no cost to the Department. Education’s ALBs either access the training through a bulk licence held by the Education Authority or pay a small subscription fee based on number of staff.The subscription to CAL provides access to a wide range of training, including diversity training.  In addition to this, a number of ALBs have incurred expenditure on training provided by third party organisations since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024 as detailed below:Arm’s-Length BodyTraining providerThe Education AuthorityEmployers for Disability NINorthern Ireland Council For Integrated EducationVirginia Mendez – The Feminist ShopInstitute of Government and Public PolicyThe Department utilises online training provided by CAL.  This is provided at no cost to the Department, and as such there is no scope to cut back on spend.Similarly, Education’s ALBs utilise CAL training which provides access to a wide range of training.  As this is via a single licence / subscription there is similarly no scope to cut back spend specifically on diversity, equality and inclusion training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the recently announced revitalisation scheme for the Sandy Row area, to detail (i) the area covered by the scheme; (ii) the application deadline; (iii) the eligibility requirements; and (iv) when he anticipates successful applicants will receive funding.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is providing funding for the Sandy Row Revitalisation scheme, it will be taken forward by Belfast City Council. Therefore, the parameters for the scheme have been set by Belfast City Council.  The scheme launched on 18 March 2025 and details of the scheme are now available on Belfast City Council’s website.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to the commitment on page 22 of the Programme for Government 2024-2027 to invest £1.3 billion with the aim of stimulating regeneration and tourism across Northern Ireland, to detail the local councils that will be involved.
answer_text: The four City and Growth Deals in Northern Ireland cover every council area:Belfast Region City Deal (BRCD)Derry City & Strabane District Council City Deal (DCSDC)Causeway Coast & Glens Growth Deal (CCG)Mid South West Growth Deal (MSW)The Department of Finance website contains publicly available information on City and Growth Deals, including additional web links to each Deal’s website for further information and updates on progress. This information can be found at https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/topics/city-and-growth-deals.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to quantify the word few, as used in the Programme for Government 2024-2027 when stating that over the next few years £1.3 billion will be invested with the aim of stimulating regeneration and tourism across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The £1.3bn investment is in relation to some fifty projects, which range from cutting-edge innovation centres and research facilities to digital transformation projects, alongside developments in local infrastructure, exciting tourism offerings and regeneration of local cities and towns. Each project is at a different stage in the process, with some projects already in delivery while others are still in the business case process. Therefore, it is not possible to quantify exactly how many years the investment will take.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the recently announced revitalisation scheme for the Sandy Row area, to detail the role of Belfast City Council in determining business eligibility for the scheme.
answer_text: The Council will determine business eligibility for the scheme subject to the criteria set out in its application process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to the commitment on page 22 of the Programme for Government 2024-2027 to invest £1.3 billion with the aim of stimulating regeneration and tourism across Northern Ireland, to provide a list of the local partners referred to.
answer_text: City and Growth Deals are long-term capital investments designed as place-based initiatives and aimed at accelerating local economic growth in a specific region. The Deals are a partnership between the Executive, UKG and local Deal partners. There are some fifty projects across the four Deals, which range from cutting-edge innovation centres and research facilities to digital transformation projects, alongside developments in local infrastructure, exciting tourism offerings and regeneration of local cities and towns. At the heart of each Deal will be inclusivity, sustainability, and employability. Councils and universities, in the main, are delivering the projects, but some future projects may be delivered by the private sector.The four City and Growth Deals in Northern Ireland cover every council area:Belfast Region City Deal (BRCD)Partners:-Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough CouncilArds and North Down Borough CouncilMid and East Antrim Borough CouncilBelfast City CouncilLisburn & Castlereagh City CouncilNewry, Mourne and Down District CouncilQueen’s University BelfastUlster UniversityBelfast MetNorthern Regional CollegeSouth Eastern Regional College Southern Regional CollegeDerry City & Strabane District Council City Deal (DCSDC)      Partners:-Ulster UniversityWestern Health and Social Services TrustC-TRIC-LtdNorth West Regional CollegeCauseway Coast & Glens Growth Deal (CCG) Partners:-Causeway Coast & Glens Borough CouncilUlster UniversityNorth West Regional CollegeCauseway Enterprise AgencyGlenshane Community Development LtdGrow the GlensMid South West Growth Deal (MSW)                        Partners:- Armagh City ,Banbridge &Craigavon Borough CouncilMid Ulster District CouncilFermanagh & Omagh District CouncilThe NI City and Growth Deals initiative is a £1.3 billion package of capital investment by the NI Executive and the UK Government over the next 10-15 years.The Department of Finance website contains publicly available information on City and Growth Deals, including additional web links to each Deal’s website where you can find details for all the partners involved and further information and updates on progress including details of local partners involved. This information can be found at https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/topics/city-and-growth-deals.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of whether there is a correlation between the announcement of funding of up to £135 million a year to reduce waiting lists by treating an additional 70,000 patients, and a further £80 million a year to increase elective care capacity, as outlined in the Programme for Government 2024-2027, and what is available in the actual budget.
answer_text: The Executive agreed in April that £215m of DoH’s budget should be earmarked to address waiting times for red flag, cancer and time critical treatment; reduce waiting lists and build elective care capacity. This correlates to the Programme for Government (PfG) commitment, put forward by the Department of Health and agreed by the Executive, to invest up to £135m a year to reduce waiting lists by treating an additional 70,000 patients, along with an additional £80m a year to increase elective care capacity to remove the demand/ capacity gap that causes waiting lists to grow.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average cost of a hip replacement performed by the NHS in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The average cost of a hip replacement performed by Health & Social Care (HSC) Trusts in Northern Ireland is £8,045 per procedure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the statement in the Programme for Government 2024-2027 that the Executive is actively seeking to achieve a sufficient number of police officers in line with New Decade, New Approach commitments means that the PSNI will have 7,500 officers by the end of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: The workforce recovery business case, presently under review by the Department of Finance, articulates a clear plan to secure sufficient police officer and staff numbers over the next three years.  This initiative is broadly aligned with the commitments outlined in the New Decade, New Approach agreement, and aims to  achieve a target of approximately 7,000 officers and 2,500 staff by the end of this mandate.  The recovery plan, however, is a five-year programme which extends beyond the end of the mandate.  It is also constrained by a number of factors, including but not limited to the capacity of the Police Training College and the levels of attrition of officers.Affordability also remains a key concern, and so I have and will continue to raise the need for a better financial settlement for my Department, with the Finance Minister and my Executive colleagues, so that PSNI has adequate funding to stabilise and recover numbers back to a sustainable position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any discrepancy in relation to the Programme for Government 2024-2027 committing £25 million for childcare, whilst the Assembly has been told that the figure is £50 million.
answer_text: Pending the development of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, I secured £25m Executive funding in May 2024 to implement a package of measures this financial year.  This investment is clearly reflected within the Programme for Government as funding to support immediate actions in 2024/25. £50m has been earmarked for early learning and childcare within the draft Executive Budget for 2025/26. The funding secured in the draft Executive Budget will be finalised when we agree it as an Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) to detail the difference between the Barnett consequentials allocated in relation to childcare provision in England and Wales compared to what is being spent on childcare in Northern Ireland; and (ii) for a breakdown of where the other monies are being spent.
answer_text: The Executive provided £25 million in Budget 2024-25 and £50 million in the draft Budget 2025-26 for early learning and childcare measures here.  The Department of Education has policy responsibility for childcare and also spends around £1 million per year to fund the Brightstart School Age Childcare Grant.		The table below sets out the Barnett consequentials which were identified as relating to childcare. £millionMeasure2023-242024-25Address upfront childcare costs issue in UC     1.397      1.796 UC childcare maximum caps     0.143               -   9-36 month 30 hours working parents               -     57.151 Uplift existing entitlement for childcare              -        9.459 Wraparound Childcare     0.131      6.339 			Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, meaning it is for the Executive to allocate the funding in line with its own priorities. As consequentials form part of the overall funding allocated in either a Budget or In-year Monitoring exercise it is not possible to identify where individual consequentials have been spent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the £135 million a year, outlined in the Programme for Government 2024-2027, will be sufficient to treat an additional 70,000 patients.
answer_text: The revised Elective Care Framework (ECF), published last year, details the investment needed to reduce waiting lists here. In addition to the £80m for capacity building measures, my Department estimated that it would be possible to spend up to £135m per year, for up to 5 years, non-recurrently to clear waiting lists.  The table below provides a planning summary of possible spend of the £135m.Specialty/ProcedureApproximate patient numbersApproximate Cost Dermatology new outpatient assessment6,000 patients waiting over 4 years £2.8mCataract procedures2,800 patients waiting £5mPrimary hip/primary knee and other long waiting orthopaedic procedures3,500 patients waiting over 2 years +900 patients waiting over 4 years £42m (2 years waits)+£11m (4-year waits)Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy1,000 patients waiting over 4 years£4.5mTonsillectomies1,100 patients (many of whom are children) waiting over 4 years£3.5mRheumatologyoutpatient assessment 2,100 patients waiting over 3 years £1.4m Gynaecologyoutpatient assessment4,200 patients waiting over 3 years£2.5mHernia Treatment 800 patients waiting over 4 years  £4m Colonoscopy 1,905 patients waiting over 3 years £2.4mOGD1,025 patients waiting over 3 years £0.8m Other1,900 patients across a number of specialities£6mSchemeApproximate patient numbersApproximate CostReimbursement Scheme4,000 patients waiting over 1 year – largely orthopaedics£31mPrimary Care Elective Care17,000 patients across a number of specialities, including dermatology, gynaecology and MSK£3mMega Clinics20,000 patients across a number of specialities£10mOther including validation£5mTotal134.9mWhilst the Programme for Government includes a reference to investing up to £215m a year on the waiting list backlog, in line with the required investment levels detailed in the ECF, the draft 2025/26 NI budget does not contain any additional funding for waiting lists. The scale and pace at which additional activity can be delivered will depend on the early confirmation of additional resource investment for waiting list initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Lisbane landfill site has been used to dump toxic waste in the last five years.
answer_text: The Lisbane landfill was closed to any waste acceptance in June 2013.A variation of the landfill permit in 2018 was to allow the acceptance of inert wastes only. This was to bring the site to final approved restoration levels. As my officials are currently taking forward enforcement proceedings in relation to this site, I cannot provide any further comment at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of media reports stating that the General Medical Council erases the public disciplinary history of doctors who change their gender identities, to detail any efforts he has made to ensure there are no doctors working in Northern Ireland who have obtained a gender recognition certificate but have previously been subject to restrictions or suspensions, or have been struck off.
answer_text: I have been advised in writing by the Chief Executive and Registrar of the GMC that when a doctor transitions, their fitness to practise history, and all the information retained on them, remains the same, whether or not their gender identity changes. There is accordingly full transparency to HSC employers and the general public.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail why their Department has not responded to my Freedom of Information requests in relation to the meeting between the First Minister and the Chairperson of the Committee for the Executive Office on 23 October 2024.
answer_text: The Freedom of Information Requests referred to have both been answered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the measures in place to ensure that a convicted person's record transfers to their new name if they obtain a gender recognition certificate.
answer_text: When AccessNI carries out a criminal record check, multiple identifiers are used to link an individual to any available criminal records including an individual’s current, previous (and other) names used, date of birth, address and national insurance number (if known).

answer_text: ANSWERThe headcount of The Executive Office (TEO) Civil Servants working in the secretariat to the North South Ministerial Council as at 1 January 2025 was 10. Community background information (Special Category Data) is held on the 10 members of TEO Civil Servants working in the secretariat to the North South Ministerial Council. To protect the confidentiality of this data (due to small numbers) it is not possible to provide the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amounts awarded by the Arts Council to individual artists in the last three years; and (ii) how many of these were awarded without taking part in a competitive application process.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Arts Council of Northern Ireland.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Please see below table outlining the number and value of awards to individual artists in the last three years:Finance YearExchequerLotteryNoValue (£)No Value (£)“2022/23883,2733601,383,7542023/2416107,7763581,361,8502024/25 (as at 25 February 2025)119653,6732091,015,091TOTALS143844,7229273,760,695”“All awards were made as a result of open-calls for applications from eligible applicants who applied competitively for awards funded by limited programme budgets with the following exceptions:  8 awards to individuals made in 2022/23 through the Cathedral Building Fire Funding Programme.  That was a “closed-call” emergency measure programme funded by DfC which was restricted to arts organisations and artists whose businesses were lost as a result of the Cathedral Building Fire in October 2022. However, those applications were still subject to assessment against programme criteria. 1 Travel Award made in 2024/25 related to an NI dance artist travelling to a festival delivered by a partnership organisation in India; the festival is curated by the partnership organisation itself.  Again, the dancer’s application was subject to the normal assessment criteria for the TA scheme.Over the past 3 years, 9 artists have also been supported in the context of ACNI’s partnership working with the Office of the Northern Ireland Executive Brussels.  Artists are selected by ONIEB and paid via contract, not via grant awards: the 9 contracts totalled £5,405 and are not included in the table above.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Arts Council directly. Making direct contact with the Arts Council will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) delays; and (ii) cancellations across Translink (a) train; and (b) bus services in the last 30 days.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:NI Railways Service PerformanceNI Railways operated approximately 10,800 journeys over the past 30 days.  In accordance with Public Service Agreement parameters, 157 trains operated with a service delay (1.5 % of all scheduled services) and 44 trains were cancelled (0.4% of all scheduled services).   Metro and Glider Service Performance Total Scheduled Mileage: 984,470Journeys that Operated Late: 3,108 (3.0% of all scheduled journeys)Total Mileage not operated: 7,754 (0.8% of all scheduled miles) Ulsterbus Service Performance Total Scheduled Mileage: 4,282,406Journeys that Operated Late: we are unable to provide the journeys that operate late on Ulsterbus services due to the absence of a real time monitoring system. We are currently rolling out a real time passenger information system across Ulsterbus services which will provide this information in the future. Total Mileage not operated: 9,940 (0.2% of all scheduled miles)For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission how long the responses to the Committee for the Executive Office’s inquiry into Gaps in Equality Legislation, which are now the subject of a potential data breach, were available online.
answer_text: The submissions were published on 15 October 2024 and removed on 14 February 2025. A further four submissions were removed on 20 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission when it notified the Information Commissioner’s Office of a potential data breach in relation to the publication of responses to the Committee for the Executive Office’s inquiry into Gaps in Equality Legislation.
answer_text: The Information Commissioner’s Office was notified of this data breach on 17 February 2025. The Information Commissioner’s Office was further notified on 21 February 2025 that an additional four submissions had been removed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of babies that have been born alive after an abortion who have subsequently died since March 2020.
answer_text: This information is not collected by my Department. However, national guidance recognises that, although uncommon, livebirth may occur following a medical abortion. In any situation where this occurs HSC staff must always act in the best interests of the baby and appropriate care will be provided. A baby born alive or with signs of life after an abortion is entitled to protection under the law.Should a baby be born alive after a termination of pregnancy procedure, the baby receives the support and care that is in the baby’s best interests and the baby’s condition is managed in line with professional guidance for neonatal practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21847/22-27, whether any other Health and Social Care Trusts produce literature similar to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust patient information leaflet entitled sensitive disposal of pregnancy remains.
answer_text: Belfast, Northern, Southern and Western Health and Social Care Trusts produce similar literature to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust patient information leaflet entitled Sensitive Disposal Of Pregnancy Remains.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission when it became aware of a potential data breach in relation to the publication of responses to the Committee for the Executive Office’s inquiry into Gaps in Equality Legislation.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission became aware of this data breach on 13 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they were provided with a draft answer from their officials to AQW 16513/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 16513/22-27 was answered on 29 July 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21847/22-27, to detail (i) whether the process outlined in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust patient information leaflet entitled sensitive disposal of pregnancy remains is uniform across all Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) whether it applies to the remains of aborted babies.
answer_text: I can confirm that (i) the process outlined in the South Eastern Trust patient information leaflet entitled disposal of pregnancy remains is in the main uniform across all Health and Social Care Trusts, an element of variation will arise in terms of the burial versus cremation options provided by a Trust, for example a particular Trust may offer Trust arranged communal cremation, whereas another Trust may offer arranged communal burial.  All Trusts will offer choice so that individuals are able to make their own burial/cremation arrangements. (ii) this process applies to the remains of aborted babies as well the disposal of pregnancy remains resultant from early pregnancy loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process adopted in Northern Ireland when a baby which has been subject to abortion is born alive.
answer_text: Every baby born alive is entitled to the same protection under the law and Trusts and HSC staff must always act in the best interests of the baby.Should a baby be born alive after a termination of pregnancy procedure, the baby receives the support and care that is in the baby’s best interests and the baby’s condition is managed in line with professional guidance for neonatal practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of (a) business customers; and (b) residential customers that remain offline as at 19 February 2025 following Storm Éowyn; and (ii) the broadband providers concerned.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information, as communications providers have no obligation to provide it. The regulation of telecommunications, including broadband services, falls under the remit of Ofcom. Ofcom require communications providers to inform them of incidents that have a significant effect on the operation of a network or service. My officials have checked with Ofcom if they gather this information and Ofcom have indicated to the Department that they do not hold the information required to answer this question.
