answer_text: The amount (i) my Department; and (ii) its Non-Departmental Public Bodies (NDPBs) have spent on hospitality each month since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, is outlined in the table below:MonthCore Department and Agencies£Non-Departmental Public Bodies £Total Spend£February 20244,2801544,434March 20242,5513,6306,181April 20242,852962,948May 20245,2008546,054June 20244,9795,33310,312July 20241,1402,2723,412August 20242,20252,207September 20245,0632415,304October 20242,1191,2693,388November 20242,2041,0473,251December 20242,0401,5373,577January 20254,2992174,516(ii) The table above does not include hospitality expenditure by the following NDPBs of my Department, given their operational independence which is established in legislation: Police Service of Northern Ireland;Northern Ireland Policing Board; andOffice of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland.You may, therefore, wish to contact them directly to request the information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to fraud.
answer_text: My officials engage regularly with counterparts in the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to benefit fraud.Discussions centre on activity in relation to benefit fraud, digital capability and enhancements, and emerging risks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21848/22-27, to detail any data that is collected on the number of children born with Down syndrome in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of children born with Down syndrome in the last ten years, as recorded by Primary Care and extracted via General Practitioners Intelligence Platform (GPIP). Year 	- Individuals2024 - 342023 - 402022 - 462021 - 472020 - 462019 - 532018 - 582017 - 632016 - 672015 - 65This may not include all patients who are deceased, de-registered (moved away) or individuals who are yet to have a diagnosis code entered on their record.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the policy in (i) each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) private providers carrying out NHS work, in relation to asking patients, regardless of their sex, if they are pregnant before undergoing an MRI scan.
answer_text: HSC Trusts have confirmed they follow the policy detailed in MRI Local Rules Jan 2025 V4.0 which outlines that all individuals of child bearing potential complete a pre-screening pregnancy check included in the MRI Patient Safety Questionnaire.All HSC staff will work in line with their College's professional guidance.  That said, it its exceptionally difficult to implement as it requires significant staff time and training and there will be variation across the region with regard to progress, however the overarching requirement remains.Alliance Medical Diagnostic Imaging (AMDI) Northern Ireland operate a number of radiology services across Northern Ireland and, as the Employer, must decide on an age range of patients where pregnancy must be considered based on the local risk profile.  AMDI follows the same procedure of the HSCNI Trusts in Northern Ireland in applying the age range of 11-12 years of age, up to 55 years of age, which covers the age range as reported by the HSCNI Trusts in Northern Ireland of persons of childbearing capacity.People are all different and have personal health and care needs, but it is a legal responsibility of the Radiographer to establish whether there is a possibility of pregnancy before exposure to ionising radiation used in x-ray procedures, or non-ionising radiation as used in MRI, as a foetus is at a higher risk from the potential harmful effects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider installing a permanent pedestrian crossing at Markethill Primary School, given the access to the playpark in the area which is open outside of school hours.
answer_text: Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.I can advise that my officials will assess this location for the provision of a pedestrian crossing and, if successful, will be considered in a future works programme subject to available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any recourse available to the public to secure redress in instances where Fibrus is failing to provide a broadband service to its customers weeks after Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Customers of any Internet Service Provider, including Fibrus, can contact their provider regarding compensation for loss of service. For Fibrus customers this can be done online, once service has been restored at https://fibrus.com/compensation-claim/.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail any work that has been carried out by her Department to compare the prices of electric and fossil fuel heating; and (ii) for an assessment of whether customers are being penalised for switching to more environmentally friendly heating systems.
answer_text: The analysis of energy prices using publicly available sources of information is conducted by my Department in the development of policy. The reliance on imported fossil fuels to heat our homes contributes significantly to volatility in energy costs and harmful carbon emissions. The transition to delivering self-sufficiency in affordable, renewable energy, will provide the opportunity to decouple our energy costs from global commodity markets. It will also provide greater stability for consumers in energy and heating costs, drive necessary carbon reductions required in the transition to net zero and provide health benefits by helping to reduce air pollution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to publish a comparison of the cost of producing electricity before and after the Covid-19 pandemic.
answer_text: The cost of electricity generation depends on the fuel used.  For fossil fuel (mainly gas) based generators the cost of producing electricity is driven by the cost of gas.  This has been affected by the changes in the energy landscape due to external factors, such as the Ukraine war.  For renewable generators, the costs are largely driven by capital expenditure and interest rates.  Wind generation is the primary form of renewable generation in the North.	The available data refers to the years 2020 and 2022 in the Single Electricity Market.Values are in € per Megawatt-hour sold.		Cost of Generation by Fuel Type	2020	2022	Gas	58	247	Wind	27	35	Total	58	190

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with authorities on the Isle of Man in relation to the closure of three areas to trawlers towing bottom trawls for prawns and the resultant diminished value of an Isle of Man licence.
answer_text: The Isle of Man Department of Environment Food and Agriculture (DEFA) informed my department prior to its consultation on proposed fisheries spatial management measures, which ran from 6th February to 18th March 2024.My officials were in contact with DEFA during and after the consultation. The feedback from my department and the Northern Ireland fishing industry resulted in the potential impact of the proposed closures being reduced by approximately 18%. I wrote to the DEFA Minister, Honourable Clare Barber MHK, on 8 April 2024 requesting a meeting to discuss the management of the Western Irish Sea. We met on 17 June 2024 and I highlighted the impacts the closed areas would have on the Northern Ireland fishing industry and emphasised the need to work collaboratively.  We did not discuss the cost of an Isle of Man sea fishing licence. My officials are making arrangements for a follow up meeting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to reintroduce requirements to submit fit notes when it comes to welfare payments.
answer_text: The requirement to supply fit notes either on request or based on a pre-determined frequency remains in place for all working age income-based benefits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether capital tied up in a business is taken into account when it comes to benefit claims.
answer_text: For contribution-based benefits a person’s capital has no impact on the benefit claim.For means-tested benefits, any business assets, such as machinery, vehicles, stock, fixtures and cash held in the bank, used wholly or mainly for the purposes of a trade, profession or vocation of a business owned in whole or in part by the claimant for which they are engaged as a self-employed earner are disregarded.If a self-employed earner ceases to be so engaged the assets will be disregarded for such period as may be reasonable in the circumstances to allow for disposal of any such asset, or if the person ceased to be engaged in carrying on the trade, profession or vocation because of incapacity and can reasonably be expected to re-engage on recovery. This department maintains parity with the Department for Work and Pensions in respect of the policy governing the treatment of capital tied up in a business for benefit claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how much was received following the sale of the former Faughan Valley High School site.
answer_text: In January 2025 the Education Authority’s estate agent identified a potential purchaser for the former Faughan Valley site. The legal due diligence process is underway and it is hoped that EA will be in contract before the end of the financial year. As the sale has not yet completed no money has been received and it would not be appropriate to release commercially sensitive details about the sale pending the exchange of contracts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of reasons why electricity prices in Northern Ireland have not fallen to reflect the price of oil and gas.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator are responsible for regulating electricity and natural gas prices. Their system of regulation ensures prices reflect the underlying costs. Prices for the regulated electricity supplier, Power NI, have reduced in recent years to reflect changes in wholesale gas prices (which impact on wholesale electricity prices). It is worth noting the impact of the Energy Price Guarantee (EPG) Scheme, which was introduced in November 2022, and provided a discount on consumers bills. Importantly, it should be noted that supply companies operate hedging strategies when purchasing their power. This strategy means customers should experience lower volatility but the wholesale price may not be immediately reflected in the price paid. While wholesale electricity costs make up the majority of a consumer’s bill, there are other elements of an electricity bill which can impact the price paid by consumers. Oil prices do not generally impact electricity prices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the efforts being made to verify tenancy arrangements, beyond looking at tenancy agreements, when it comes to welfare payments.
answer_text: While social sector landlords verify their tenants housing costs directly with Universal Credit, claimants in the private rented sector are required to provide supporting evidence to verify their tenancy. Evidence of rent liability is usually included within a formal tenancy agreement. Universal Credit may accept alternative evidence of rent and occupancy, such as a letter from the landlord, a rent book or receipts, and recent utility bills confirming the claimant lives at the address. Universal Credit will support any claimant experiencing difficulties in verifying their housing costs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a breakdown of the community background of (i) applicants; and (ii) appointees to Ministerial appointments since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Statistics relating to public appointments are published in the Public Appointments Report for Northern Ireland - https://www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/topics/public-appointments-reports. Information relating to the specified time period is not currently available, but will be compiled and published in due course, subject to the appropriate data controls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a breakdown of the community background of (i) applicants; and (ii) appointees to Ministerial appointments since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Executive Office publishes Public Appointments Reports, in which community background, drawn from monitoring forms, is provided in statistical format. TEO is currently preparing reports covering the periods 2022/23 and 2023/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a breakdown of the community background of (i) applicants; and (ii) appointees to Ministerial appointments since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Public appointments are based on the merit principle; community background is not considered at any stage of the appointment process. To measure the effectiveness of commitments to diversity and equality of opportunity, applicants are provided with an Equal Opportunities Monitoring form, which they may complete voluntarily. This information is used by The Executive Office to publish an annual Public Appointments Report in statistical format which includes community background. The report for 2024/25 is not yet available but will be published at a later date at: Public appointments reports | The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a breakdown of the community background of (i) applicants; and (ii) appointees to Ministerial appointments since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: All of my Department’s public appointment competitions include an Equal Opportunities Monitoring form, which applicants can complete voluntarily. This form helps to measure the effectiveness of commitments to diversity and equality of opportunity, including community background. Importantly, the information collected is not shared with selection panels or anyone involved in the selection process, including me as the decision maker.This anonymised information is shared with The Executive Office (TEO) who publish annual statistical reports on its website (www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk) on behalf of all departments.  The report for 2024/25 is not yet available but will be published on the TEO website in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide a breakdown of the community background of (i) applicants; and (ii) appointees to Ministerial appointments since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Applicants for Ministerial appointments are asked to complete an Equal Opportunities Monitoring Form as part of the application process.   The completion of the form is voluntary and not all applicants provide community background information.  Any information provided is used for monitoring purposes only; it is not seen by the selection panel or by me when making appointments.  The community background of applicants and appointees for Ministerial appointments since February 2024 is as follows:   ProtestantCatholicNot declared as Protestant or Catholic or information not providedTotalNumber of Applicants26202773Number of Appointees45413Source: Submitted applicant Equality Monitoring forms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how he proposes to protect and enhance trout stocks on public estates in the long-term to ensure a healthy annual spawning cycle continues while Inland Fisheries promote commercial fishing.
answer_text: My department does not promote commercial fisheries but instead regulates them by the issuing of licences and monitoring fish stocks. Lough Erne is the only Public Angling Estate fishery where a commercial fishery operates, which is a pike fishery. Trout are not allowed to be harvested to protect the trout stocks present.The department has published a Fishery Management Plan for Lough Erne, which has recommendations to support the sustainability of fish stocks for both the commercial fishery and recreational angling.  DAERA Inland Fisheries enforcement staff also undertake regular patrols both on land and water to deter, disrupt and detect illegal fishing activity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the items (i) requested; and (ii) sent from Great Britain to deal with Storm Éowyn, both in the days before the storm and in its aftermath.
answer_text: The support received by NIE Networks came primarily via the NEWSAC agreement which is an enabling protocol for the transfer of resources between electricity network operators in the Britian, Ireland and the Isle of Man (including National Grid). Some support also came from EU Electricity Network partners and via COBR. NIE Networks requested lines staff and engineers, tree arborists, helicopters and generators from NEWSAC.  The following support was received from NEWSAC/EU Electricity Network Partners. Circa 300 linesmen and engineers; Circa 120 Tree arborists; Four helicopters; and 30 generators Additionally, six tree arborists were provided from the UK Environmental Agency via COBR. These resources were obtained in addition to NIE Networks’ own staff, existing local contracting partners and resources secured on the open market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 22119/22-27, for his assessment of the value for money of (i) the Northern Ireland Civil Service membership of the Stonewall Diversity Champions Programme; and (ii) the Northern Ireland Civil Service participation in Stonewall’s Workplace Equality Index.
answer_text: The Civil Service is an equal opportunities employer, and is committed to building an inclusive Civil Service that reflects the society it serves.  This work requires engagement with a wide range of internal stakeholders and external organisations including those from the disability, race and minority ethnic sectors as well as LGBTQ+ groups.  Membership of the Stonewall Diversity Champions Programme provides the Civil Service with support to make its workplaces more LGBTQ+ inclusive via the provision of advice, support and resources.  		 I would refer the member to AQW 18261/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how he proposes to protect and enhance trout stocks on public estates in the short-term to ensure a healthy annual spawning cycle continues while Inland Fisheries promote commercial fishing.
answer_text: My department does not promote commercial fisheries but instead regulates them by the issuing of licences and the monitoring of fish stocks. Lough Erne is the only Public Angling Estate fishery where a commercial fishery operates, which is a pike fishery. Trout are not allowed to be harvested to protect the trout stocks present.DAERA Inland Fisheries enforcement staff undertake regular patrols both on land and water to deter, disrupt and detect illegal fishing activity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 22027/22-27, to detail what year the Northern Ireland Prison Service first housed a biological male in female prison accommodation.
answer_text: Given the low number of transgender prisoners committed to the care of NI Prison Service, provision of this information would create a risk that the individual concerned could be identified.  To disclose this information would contravene that individuals’ rights under data protection principles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there is any guidance or requirement for the Northern Ireland Prison Service to house men and women in separate accommodation.
answer_text: QUESTION NO 14 - AQO 1613/22-27 ANSWER There is extensive guidance on the housing of men and women in prison including Prison Rules which state that women prisoners shall be held in separate accommodation. There are currently no plans to make any legislative changes or amendments to the rules. This practice is monitored by the Northern Ireland Prison Service, through its internal audit and governance processes, independently scrutinised though Criminal Justice Inspectorate inspections, Prisoner Ombudsman’s investigations, the Independent Monitoring Board and other statutory bodies to ensure that the spirit of Mandela Rules are met within custodial environments. Hydebank Wood Secure College & Women’s Prison is already a mixed gender site, where the challenges of holding males and females in separate accommodation are managed effectively on a daily basis. Hydebank was recently the subject of a full inspection by His Majesty’s Chief Inspector of Prisons, the Criminal Justice Inspectorate for Northern Ireland (CJINI), the Education Training Inspectorate, and Regulation & Quality Improvement Authority. They collectively assessed outcomes at this establishment as good in each of the four healthy prisons tests. The complexity of managing the mixed site was noted alongside the performance of Hydebank being judged as amongst the highest level within the UK.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the spend on the Afghan Citizens Resettlement Scheme and Afghan Relocation and Assistance Policy, as referred to on page xi of the Northern Ireland Spring Supplementary Estimates 2024-25, broken down by (i) reason for spend; and (ii) local council area.
answer_text: The Afghan Citizens Resettlement Scheme and Afghan Relocation and Assistance Policy Scheme (Afghan schemes) are run by the Home Office. The Executive Office (TEO) leads the scheme’s delivery locally, primarily through funding the NI Refugee Resettlement Consortium to welcome and support Afghans arriving via the Schemes.  Home Office provided funding of £1,219k in the financial year 2024/2025 for spend across the Afghan resettlement schemes. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given that Justice of the Peace are no longer appointed, whether he will review the need for Applications for Cremation to contain a statutory declaration signed by a Justice of the Peace or Commissioner for Oaths.
answer_text: My Department conducted a consultation from June to October 2024, on proposals to update the current cremation regulations.This consultation included a proposal to replace the Statutory Declaration and the need for countersigning by a Justice of the Peace or a Commissioner of Oaths, with a Statement of Truth to be signed by the person applying for a cremation.The replies to the consultation covered a range of issues and some complex and sensitive matters require further analysis. I will consider these in due course to inform my view on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the objective of the Homes for Ukraine Scheme and Strategic Migration Partnership Ukraine, as referred to on page xx of the Northern Ireland Spring Supplementary Estimates 2024-25; and (ii) a breakdown of the spend.
answer_text: The Homes for Ukraine scheme (the ‘scheme’) is jointly run by the Home Office and the Ministry for Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) with The Executive Office leading the scheme’s delivery locally. This includes the engagement and communication functions delivered by Strategic Migration Partnerships in other jurisdictions. Funding is provided by MHCLG. The scheme enables people to act as sponsors for Ukrainian nationals and their families seeking refuge from the war. Over 2,500 Ukrainians have been welcomed here under the scheme since March 2022, all of whom have full access to public services, including health, education, and benefits.The breakdown of the £2,509k figure in the Spring Supplementary Estimates 2024/25 is (i) administration costs: £138k, (ii) service delivery: £624k, and (iii) host thank you payments: £1,747k.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans, including any grants available for community groups, to celebrate the 80th anniversary of VE Day.
answer_text: The Department provides funding on an annual basis to all local councils for community festivals through the Community Festivals Fund. Community groups wishing to mark the occasion may be eligible to apply for support through their local council.The Public Record Office of Northern Ireland (PRONI) in partnership with the Northern Ireland War Memorial are organising a series of four public lunchtime talks at PRONI to mark the 80th anniversary of VE Day. They will run from March to May 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they raised the issue of China’s human rights record, and specifically its treatment of Christians, during their recent meeting with the Consul General of China.
answer_text: During our recent meeting with the new Consul General for China, whilst seeking to identify potential areas for mutually beneficial collaboration, we also raised human rights concerns, including the treatment of Christians.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21694/22-27, to explain the discrepancy in funding between different sectors.
answer_text: The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  Therefore, schools with higher numbers of newcomer pupils or pupils with free school meal entitlement attract additional funding regardless of sector.  Similarly, smaller schools receive Small Schools Support funding which increases the per pupil funding of these schools, irrespective of sector.    School budgets, calculated through the CFF, are reflective of the individual circumstances of each school and all schools are treated in a fair and equitable basis, regardless of sector. In addition, earmarked funds are allocated to schools for specific initiatives such as Extended Schools, Entitlement Framework, Nurture Provision etc.As previously outlined, analysis of resource allocations and expenditure for individual schools is available on the Education Authority’s website.  Details of the Common Funding Scheme are available on the Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of a report in the Belfast Telegraph on 8 February 2025, to detail any action he is taking in relation to alleged fraud in Northern Ireland’s benefit system.
answer_text: I am taking a zero tolerance approach and ensuring that my Department uses every tool at its disposal to prevent and detect benefit fraud. In my statement to the Assembly on 11 February on Welfare System: Tackling Fraud and Error, I announced that I would establish a Task and Finish Group to look at every intervention my department takes in relation to fraud and drill down on the high-risk areas, including exploring digital developments to support counter fraud activity. This is a priority for me and the work of the Task and Finish Group has already commenced. I am determined to use every tool to tackle this issue including the recovery of money from those who claim it under false pretences. I have repeatedly bid for additional resources to support greater investment in benefit security activities, and I will continue to make the case to Executive colleagues for this funding. My department will also reintroduce the practice of publicising sentencing outcomes for those convicted of benefit fraud with effect from 1 April 2025. My department receives approximately 10,000 fraud allegations per year and all allegations are investigated. I take fraud in the benefit system seriously and I will continue to focus resources on this important issue.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how they ensure that a range of views on modern gender theory are involved in the development of departmental policy.

As stated in the Department’s Equality Scheme, we are committed to seeking the views of individuals directly affected by the decisions, the Equality Commission, representative groups of Section 75 categories, other public authorities, voluntary and community groups, our staff and their trades unions and other groups which have a legitimate interest in the matter.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) to detail why there was no public consultation in relation to the Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill; and (ii) to publish the responses of the political parties represented on the Assembly Commission to the consultation referenced in paragraph 10 of the Explanatory and Financial Memorandum of the Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill.
answer_text: (i)   The Assembly Commission has very specific responsibilities which relate to providing the staff, support and services required by the Assembly. Bills are brought forward by the Assembly Commission very rarely, and only where some element of the particular responsibilities of the Assembly Commission is to be modified. Such Bills do not confer rights or impose obligations on any member of the public. On 18 March 2020, the Assembly Commission agreed that determining Members’ salaries and pensions should continue to be the role of an independent body, but that determination of allowances should return to the Assembly Commission.  The Assembly supported this position by resolution on 30 June 2020. There has been no substantive change in policy since that date.  In accordance with the statutory duty on public authorities under section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, the Assembly Commission considered whether public consultation on the Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill (the Bill) was required.  The Bill has very limited purposes ie it renames the Independent Financial Review Panel (IFRP), removes one element of its functions, and makes a number of incidental changes to the manner in which is to discharge those functions.  The Bill will have no impact on equality of opportunity among the relevant section 75 groups. Given the limited and technical purposes of the Bill, and the fact that it reflects a position which has now been the settled approach of the Assembly Commission, and the Assembly, for almost five years, the Assembly Commission did not consider that public consultation was required.	Guidance which requires other types of Bills, such as Executive Bills and Members Bills, to be subject to public consultation, does not apply to Assembly Commission Bills.As the Bill progresses through the legislative process, it will be scrutinised by an Ad Hoc Committee which will consider what level of consultation it wishes to carry out. If the Bill is passed and the Remuneration Board is established, the legislation will require the Board to publish a draft determination and consult on that determination. This was not an obligation imposed on the IFRP and the Assembly Commission intends that it will enhance transparency in the mechanism for determining Members’ salaries. The Bill specifies a range of consultees, and the Remuneration Board may consult with such other persons as it thinks appropriate.(ii)  As part of the discussions to reach an agreed approach with the Assembly Commission, consultation with the five political parties represented on the Assembly Commission took place in 2019, when a consultation document was issued to Assembly Commission members on 21 November 2019 to establish the views of these parties and identify possible areas of agreement in relation to reform of the IFRP. Two responses were received to this consultation and were published on the Assembly website in 2020 following a Freedom of Information request.  The re

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the price differential between motorcycle tests in Northern Ireland and Great Britain; and (ii) any steps she is taking to make these fees equal.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) is responsible for carrying out motorcycle driving tests in the North. The fees charged for motorcycle driving tests here and in Great Britain are outlined in the table below:Practical test typeWeekday(NI)Weekday(GB)Evening/Weekend (NI)Evening/Weekend (GB)Motorcycle test (on-road)£80£75£110£88.50Motorcycle test (off-road)£45£15.50£60£15.50Extended test for disqualified riders£160£150N/A£177Motorcycle theory test£23£23N/AN/A           The  DVA operates as a Trading Fund, which means the income it receives from fees should cover the cost of delivering the services it provides.  Following public consultation, driving test fees here were increased from 1 October 2023 to ensure that the DVA has sufficient income to cover the cost of providing its services, whilst meeting its statutory obligations.The Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA) is responsible for the delivery of motorcycle driving tests in Britain.  Whilst I am unable to comment on how the DVSA sets its fees, I understand they have not increased their driving test fees for over 10 years, which may account for the differential.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the publication of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls and their statutory obligation to represent the interests of victims, for their assessment of whether violence against women and girls in the period between 1969 and 1998 was wrong.
answer_text: All violence against women and girls is wrong. All women and girls deserve to live in a place that is free from gender-based violence, abuse and harm. The Strategic Framework seeks to achieve this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the delay statistics for trains (i) in; and (ii) out of Belfast Grand Central Station at (a) peak times; and (b) off-peak times.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink doesn't differentiate times of the day in their punctuality statistics or have a definition of “peak times”. Translink has Public Service Agreement (PSA) punctuality percentage figures for individual routes or the entire network per financial period, but they are not related to Belfast Grand Central Station as an individual entity.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of diesel generators held by NIE Networks to assist people who have lost power; and (ii) how this compares with the situation in other parts of the UK.
answer_text: NIE Networks had a fleet of nearly 300 generators in circulation during Storm Éowyn operated by more than 85 NIE Networks staff. This was made up of around 175 generators, of varying capacity and fuel type, that NIE Networks own and a further 120 generators of varying capacity and fuel type, secured on a temporary basis.It is important to note that generators are not a long-term solution for customers without power as they have a limited run hour capacity and safe installation requires a two-person team. The focus of NIE Networks during storms is the prompt reinstatement of customers, including the safety and care of those with special medical needs.NIE Networks does not hold information on how its generator stock compares with the other Distribution Network Operators across Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to amend the publication of abortion data to include the reasons for abortion, as is the case in England and Wales.
answer_text: The most recent publication on abortion data was released on 30 January 2025 and can be accessed at the following link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-abortion-statistics-202324.The information presented is derived from the notification forms submitted to the Chief Medical Officer for Northern Ireland following each abortion procedure as required under regulation 10 of the 2020 Regulations.As noted in the publication, there are limitations on the published data because of small numbers and/or limited available data meaning that further breakdowns cannot be provided in line with the publication provided in England and Wales.  For example:•	It is not possible to include the socioeconomic background of women in Northern Ireland obtaining abortions, as the data collected on the notification forms is postal district only (and not full postcode) and therefore it is not possible to assign patients to a Local Government District for this analysis. •	There was limited data available on the number of previous abortions to provide meaningful reporting on this.In line with England and Wales, the statutory grounds for abortion in Northern Ireland are published.  Where the numbers for a particular ground are small, these have been combined with other grounds to prevent patient disclosure.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, in light of the Assembly agreeing a motion on 28 January 2025 on Commitment to the Proper Use of Public Monies, for its assessment of whether there is a clear distinction between party political work and constituency work.
answer_text: The Review of the use of certain allowances under the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016 was undertaken under the authority of the Clerk/Chief Executive as the Assembly Commission’s Accounting Officer.  Assembly Commission Members had no role in the review and the Assembly Commission has not taken a view on the report.The debate on 28 January 2025 allowed Members to express different and legitimately-held views and opinions on the approach to the Review, its contents and its conclusions.  The majority of Members who spoke in the debate gave their support to the recommendations of the review, including measures around the nature of the work undertaken by the employees of Members.  The recommendations included:Keeping records of, and completing regular declarations in relation to, any hours worked on party political activity by Members’ employees. Including a provision prohibiting any significant party-political activity during hours of employment in contracts of employment for Members’ employees.Providing details of Members’ employees who hold other employment remunerated from public money.Revising job descriptions for Members’ employees.The Clerk/Chief Executive has previously outlined to Members that she was considering the recommendations and that she intended to, as appropriate,either introduce a number of new administrative measures to implement them, or recommend to the Assembly Commission where changes are required to be made to the Determination.  The Assembly Commission will consider any recommendations the Clerk/Chief Executive makes to it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the annual spend per pupil in (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools in the (a) Controlled sector; (b) Maintained sector; (c) Integrated sector; and (d) Irish-medium sector.
answer_text: Details of the 2023-24 total delegated expenditure per pupil in primary schools and post-primary schools in the sectors requested are included in the table below:2023-24 Spend Per Pupil  Primary£Post Primary£Controlled Integrated 3,8095,062Controlled Irish Medium 5,235-Controlled 3,7545,239Maintained Irish Medium 4,4046,370Other Maintained3,593-Maintained3,8105,318Grant Maintained Integrated 3,7585,751The Department is unable to disaggregate all expenditure attributed to schools from the Education Authority’s block grant on an individual school basis.On an annual basis, the Education Authority publishes an analysis of resource allocations and expenditure for individual schools on its website. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the Assembly agreeing a motion on 28 January 2025 on Commitment to the Proper Use of Public Monies, which included noting that there is an onus on the First Minister to correct the Assembly record of 7 October 2024, to detail whether the First Minister will be correcting the Assembly record.

To ask the Assembly Commission, in light of the Assembly agreeing a motion on 28 January 2025, including regretting that the Review of the use of certain allowances under the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016, by the Assembly’s Head of Legal Services, was a purely paper-based exercise, to detail any action it is taking to investigate the issues further.
answer_text: The Review of the use of certain allowances under the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016 was undertaken under the authority of the Clerk/Chief Executive as the Assembly Commission’s Accounting Officer.  Assembly Commission Members had no role in the review and the Assembly Commission has not taken a view on the report.The debate on 28 January 2025 allowed Members to express different and legitimately-held views and opinions on the approach to the Review, its contents and its conclusions.  The non-binding motion did not call on the Assembly Commission to undertake a further review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what progress has been made on a new school build for Cullybackey College.
answer_text: The Integrated Consultant Team are progressing the spatial co-ordination and developed design, with the end stage report (RIBA Stage 3) anticipated to be lodged with the Department for consideration in the second quarter of 2025. It is anticipated that the Planning application will be lodged in summer 2025.The current programme anticipates construction to commence in the last quarter of 2026, however this will be dependent on a number of factors, including completion of site acquisitions, planning approval being granted and budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, in light of the Assembly agreeing a motion on 28 January 2025 on Commitment to the Proper Use of Public Monies, to detail any action it will take to review the audit process in relation to the spending of public money by MLAs.
answer_text: The Review of the use of certain allowances under the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016 was undertaken under the authority of the Clerk/Chief Executive as the Assembly Commission’s Accounting Officer.  Assembly Commission Members had no role in the review and the Assembly Commission has not taken a view on the report.The debate on 28 January 2025 allowed Members to express different and legitimately-held views and opinions on the approach to the Review, its contents and its conclusions.  In particular, it was acknowledged during that debate that the motion did not recognise the extensive audit arrangements, conducted by two distinct independent external auditors, which are already in place for both payments to Members, under the provisions of the Determination, and payments to Political Parties under the Northern Ireland Assembly Financial Assistance for Political Parties Scheme.  Reports of these annual audits are considered by the Assembly Commission Audit and Risk Committee, which also includes independent membership, and by the Northern Ireland Audit Office. The Northern Ireland Audit Office also received a copy of the review report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the District Electoral Area changes, as outlined in the recommendations of the Local Government Boundaries Commissioner published in 2022.
answer_text: Further to my response to AQW16649/22-27, I am considering the draft Local Government Boundaries (NI) Order, regarding the wards into which each local government district is divided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Gambling Commission having powers to regulate the industry in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Gambling Commission has powers to regulate the National Lottery including where it operates in Northern Ireland.  The Commission also has powers to regulate remote (online) gambling operating in Northern Ireland where the gambling equipment is situated in Great Britain, and remote gambling operating in Great Britain where the gambling equipment is situated in Northern Ireland.  Any remote operator in the UK must obtain a remote gambling licence from the Gambling Commission before they may lawfully advertise remote gambling in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he will commission research into problem gambling; and (ii) how problem gambling in Northern Ireland compares with the situation in Great Britain.
answer_text: The Department is currently awaiting the results from a Northern Ireland gambling prevalence survey which will give an indication of problem gambling. In Northern Ireland, 2016 figures suggest 2.3% of the population were considered to be experiencing problem gambling. Updated results are expected in the spring. Based on 2018 data, the figure in Great Britian is 0.5%. Following a change in methodology, and therefore not directly comparable with previous surveys, recent findings from the Gambling Commission’s Gambling Survey for Great Britian suggest 4.2% of the population are experiencing problem gambling. The Gambling Commission believe that the new approach improves the quality of the statistics.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will introduce a gambling levy in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 21179/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any lessons that have been learned from Storm Éowyn in relation to his Department's (i) preparations; and (ii) response.
answer_text: Emergency Planning is given a high priority within my Department and officials routinely carry out multi-agency planning events in preparation for emergency incidents, the last of which took place in November 2024.My Department formally activated its Major Emergency Response Plan (MERP) in response to the ‘Red’ weather warning issued by the Met Office at 09:42 a.m. on Thursday 23 January in relation to Storm Éowyn. Any activation of the MERP necessitates a review into the response in order to identify learning that could be helpful in future civil contingency events. That review will be completed in the coming months. I want to place on record my thanks to staff in the department, both on the ground and those managing the response, for their commitment and efforts during the response to Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures he is taking to end the Education Authority’s use of the North when referring to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: EA’s communications guidelines refer to Northern Ireland consequently, ‘the North’ would not be used by the EA. However, I am aware that in a recent social media post, an extract from a third-party media statement referred to Northern Ireland as ‘the North.’ This quote is not attributable to the EA, therefore, I am satisfied that no additional measures are required at this time.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given their responsibility to promote equality of opportunity for people within the Section 75 equality categories, whether their Department has a position in relation to the number of genders it recognises.
answer_text: Gender recognition is governed by the Gender Recognition Act 2004, an Act of the Westminster Parliament.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the lessons that have been learned from how to deal with people on the critical care list as a result of the fallout from Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Electricity Networks (NIE Networks) operate a Critical Care Register where they keep details of consumers reliant on electrical equipment for their day-to-day care. In the case of electricity customers this includes medical equipment such as oxygen concentrators, patient vital signs monitoring systems and home dialysis. There is a set process already in place to assist critical care customers as detailed below:Customers on the Critical Care Register are prioritised by NIE Networks when supply is disrupted.In the event of a scheduled interruption, NIE Networks routinely contacts Critical Care Register customers at least three days in advance to notify them of the expected duration of the interruption and in the event of a power cut or loss of supply they are kept informed. In the event of an unplanned power cut, Critical Care Register customers are given priority. NIE Networks maintains regular contact with the customer during the power cut with up-to-date information until power is restored, proving alternative support when necessary.NIE continues to work tirelessly to repair the unprecedented level of damage from Storm Éowyn. There will of course be time to reflect on lessons learned when all households have been restored with electricity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of case specific conferences under paragraph 7 of the Guidance on the care and management of transgender prisoners that have been convened since the introduction of the guidance; (ii) the number of these cases relating to (a) trans men; and (b) trans women; and (iii) the number of occasions that conferences resulted in prisoners being sent to prison accommodation which did not correspond to their biological sex.
answer_text: The number of times the Northern Ireland Prison Service has held a case conference as per paragraph 7 of the Guidance on the Care and Management of Transgender Prisoners since the guidance was introduced in August 2022 is so low that disclosure could lead to the identification of individuals.  To disclose this information would contravene individuals’ rights under data protection principles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the cost of case specific conferences under paragraph 7 of the guidance on the care and management of transgender prisoners, since the introduction of the guidance.
answer_text: The costs of convening case conferences under the Guidance on the Care and Management of Transgender Prisoners since the introduction of the Guidance in August 2022 are not identifiable. The time taken to prepare for and convene these conferences are not distinguishable from the duties routinely undertaken daily by Governors in the overall management of the Prison establishments. It is therefore not possible to disaggregate these costs and apportion a defined spend on such work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 19861/22-27, (i) to clarify her responsibility in relation to the bodies listed; and (ii) to provide the information requested, similar to the information provided by other Executive Ministers.
answer_text: The bodies listed in the response to AQW 19861/22-27 are Non-Departmental Public Bodies (NDPBs) of the Department.   As Minister, I am responsible inter alia for the legislation underpinning those NDPBs, any related Ministerial appointments and agreement of annual budgets to each NDPB.  Each NDPB also has an Accounting Officer who is responsible for safeguarding public funds in their charge and ensuring those funds are applied only to the purposes for which they were voted and more generally for efficient and economical administration.   On that basis, and as indicated in my previous response, the Member may wish to contact each of the Department’s ALBs for the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 19863/22-27, for his assessment of whether the spend by NI Water represents good value for money for the taxpayer.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water however I have sought the information requested. NI Water has advised the Department that the majority of the expenditure referred to, is required as part of funding for staff training events at the company’s Antrim Learning & Development Centre. The training programmes have facilitated approximately 4,000 training days across a workforce of approximately 1,500 staff. NI Water provides hospitality at these training sessions as a cost-effective alternative to subsistence payments which staff would be entitled to claim. The expenditure is value for money in terms of negating the cost and additional administration of processing subsistence claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 19540/22-27, to detail the Department or statutory body that provides permission for discharge points into waterways which ultimately end up in a lough.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates discharges to the water environment under the Water Order (as amended) 1999. A robust regulatory regime is in place, through the issue of Water Order Discharge Consents. These Consents require dischargers to treat industrial effluent and sewage to a level that can be sustained by the receiving environment and to maintain their treatments to avoid pollution.Discharge consents are issued for domestic discharges (e.g. septic tanks) at domestic dwellings, private sewage infrastructure serving residential / commercial properties, and to NI Water for its wastewater infrastructure.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the estimated annual cost of alcohol abuse to the health service in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Approximately £30m was spent on substance use related services in 2022/23. This figure represents an estimate of the combined expenditure across a range of services provided by the Public Health Agency, Strategic Performance & Planning Group and the Health & Social Care Trusts. However, treatment costs extend beyond specific commissioned services. A recent NI Audit Office report estimated that 200 hospital beds per day are occupied by patients with substance use listed as a contributing factor, and estimates have shown that alcohol alone costs the health service in the region of £250m per annum. More generally, while the financial cost can never bring home the full impact that substance use related harm has on individuals, families and communities across Northern Ireland, the harms related to the use of alcohol and other drugs cost Northern Ireland’s economy hundreds of millions of pounds every year. The cost of alcohol misuse alone was estimated at up to £900 million in 2008/09, and if we were to add in the costs of the harms related to other drugs this would almost certainly take this figure to approximately £1.5 billion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the actions taken by the Education Authority to maximise support for Balnamore Primary School once a decline in pupil numbers was evident.
answer_text: The Department requires the statutory planning authorities (Education Authority (EA) and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS)), school managing authorities and the sectoral bodies to identify current and future education needs and develop solutions to meet these on an area basis.The EA along with the Board of Governors explored and considered many options looking at sustainability and the Department’s Sustainable Schools Policy for the future of Balnamore Primary School (PS). The options considered (Do Nothing, Shared Education, Transformation to Integrated Status, Federation, Amalgamation and the final option Discontinuance of the School). However, in considering the options, the EA determined that with the number of alternative controlled primary schools in the area with available places these options did not provide sustainable provision for the school. This resulted in the published proposal DP 729 which proposed that ‘Balnamore Primary School will discontinue with effect from 31 August 2024, or as soon as possible thereafter’.Under Article 14 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 the EA undertook pre-publication consultation with governors, staff and parents/guardians outlining the EA’s Case for Change detailing the rationale for the proposal.  During the pre-publication consultations, respondents raised a range of issues that they felt influenced the downturn in enrolment numbers. In response, the EA stated “the EA worked with the Board of Governors of Balnamore PS through the Chairperson and Principals.  The EA provided coordinated support to address a range of issues in the interests of the pupils, staff and school community”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the longest ambulance downtime in the last 30 days, broken down by hospital; and (ii) the number of occasions that downtime exceeded two hours.
answer_text: Table 1 shows the longest time that an ambulance waited to handover a patient at an emergency department, by hospital, during the last 30 days.  The target time for ambulance handover is 15 minutes, however due to pressures on the unscheduled care system, there has been an increase in ambulance handover delays.  Table 1 also shows the number of ambulances that waited over 2 hours to handover patients at emergency departments during the same time period.  Whilst ambulance handover delays overall are unacceptable, approximately 75% of handovers are completed within 2 hours.Table 1 – longest ambulance waiting time by hospital and number of waits over 2 hours (25/12/2024 – 23/01/2025)*HospitalLongest ambulance waiting timeNumber ambulances waits over 2 hoursAltnagelvin11 hours 32 mins123Antrim17 hours 10 mins393Causeway12 hours 29 mins161Craigavon11 hours 51 mins330Daisyhill7 hours 39 mins64Mater16 hours 9 mins76RBHSC1 hour 40 mins0Royal Victoria14 hours 48 mins514South West Acute 5 hours 33 mins49Ulster19 hours 22 mins377*This data is taken from an internal management information report used for operational monitoring purposes.  Reducing ambulance handover delays outside emergency departments has been a key priority due to the impact this has on NIAS’s capacity to respond to calls in the community.  A 3-hour ambulance handover backstop was introduced in December 2022 and amended to a 2-hour backstop in February 2023 to reduce long waits outside emergency departments and release ambulances more quickly.My Department has provided funding to implement an Integrated Clinical Hub for NIAS.  Clinicians within the Hub are able to assess calls and direct callers to the most appropriate health care using ‘Hear & Treat’ and ‘See & Treat’ pathways, where these provide safe pathways for patients.  NIAS has been set clear targets to increase the use of these pathways.Regional guidance was issued to Trusts setting out escalation processes to release waiting ambulances in the event of NIAS having an outstanding Cat 1 call or a Cat 2 response time that exceeds 45 minutes as delays impact on people in the community who are waiting for an ambulance.Ambulance handover delays are monitored on a daily basis, including average delays and longest delays at each hospital.  This information is circulated widely within the HSC system including to the Trust Chief Executives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to specify how many genders the Department and its Arm's Length Bodies recognise when it comes to the practical operation of policy papers, such as the Northern Ireland Prison Service Guidance on the care and management of transgender prisoners.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 8 - AQO 1473/22-27			ANSWERMy Department is not in the business of putting people into boxes nor is it a responsibility of the Department to define the number of gender categories that exist.   Our responsibility is to support everyone who comes into contact with the justice system, regardless of their gender, ethnicity, socio-economic status, health, disability, age, sexual orientation and many other considerations.  [Many of those who come into contact with the justice system are among the most vulnerable within our society and have a range of complex needs, including addictions, poor mental and physical health.  Indeed, many have suffered trauma or have been victims themselves.  It is for this very reason that all those who come into contact with the justice system are treated with respect, as individuals with their own needs and complexities, irrespective of how they identify.] As I have already advised this Chamber, when a person is committed to a prison establishment as directed by the Court, the Prison Service will consider their specific needs on a case-by-case basis in line with best practice.  Where information is available to suggest a prisoner may be transgender, Guidance on the Care and Management of Transgender Prisoners is invoked.  The case specific conference will consider the unique circumstances of that individual prisoner and will determine how their time in custody will be managed.  In doing so, a person-centred and risk-based approach on decisions in respect of accommodation and care, informed by advice from colleagues in the South Eastern Health & Social Care Trust, is taken.Transgender prisoners are offered the same regime as every other prisoner. The Prison Service is at all times committed to ensuring that all people in prison are held safely and securely whilst being treated with dignity and respect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration that was given to the ongoing new development taking place in Balnamore when measuring the potential for growth and recovery of Balnamore Primary School.
answer_text: The assessment of any Development Proposal (DP) is a complex process. Each DP is assessed on its own merits and follows a comprehensive process that includes significant research, engagement with key stakeholders, consultation on a Case for Change, objections received within a two-month statutory objection period; other views including meetings with locally elected politicians, advice from policy teams within the Department; the professional advice of the Education and Training Inspectorate, and any other information considered pertinent to the proposal. Careful consideration has been given to all pertinent and relevant evidence which has been included in the report provided to me on which I based my decision and can be accessed on the Department’s website.https://www.education-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19093/22-27, for an assurance that these issues will be considered if the Rainbow Project applies for funding from his Department, or its arm's-length bodies, in the future.
answer_text: If, in the future, the Rainbow Project applies for funding from my Department or an Arm’s Length Body sponsored by my Department, its funding application will be considered in line with any criteria set for the funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 20028/22-27, to detail the challenges referenced in this answer.
answer_text: My officials advise there is no detail available about the nature of the challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to use the regulation making powers in Section 15 of the Betting, Gaming, Lotteries and Amusements (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My officials are currently engaging on a cross Departmental and cross jurisdictional basis to gather data in relation this issue.  Analysis of this and the results of a Gambling Prevalence Survey, expected in March, will inform my decision on the use of these powers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale as to why more time was not given for the recovery of Balnamore Primary School.
answer_text: My rationale in relation to DP 729 is detailed on the Department’s website and can be accessed here.The purpose of the Sustainable Schools Policy is to ensure that children and young people have access to high quality education that is delivered in schools that are educationally and financially sustainable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the average time taken by her Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The average time taken to respond to FOI/EIR requests from February 2024 to date is 10 working days.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average time taken by his Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since February 2024 the average length of time taken by my Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request is 16.3 working days.  This does not include historical Freedom of Information requests that are dealt with by PRONI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the average time taken by his Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The average time taken by the Department of Justice to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024 to date, has been 15 working days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average time taken by his Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, the Department’s average response time to requests for information under Freedom of Information legalisation, is 15.75 days. The time taken to prepare a response to each FoI depends on a variety of factors including, time to obtain any required clarification, locating relevant information, consideration of any required redaction and potential consultation with 3rd parties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19023/22-27, for a breakdown of the proposals for which the funding was awarded in each case, both by the Department and the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“All of the respective funding relating to AQW 19023 related to projects supported by the Homelessness Prevention Fund with details on each of the financial years previously outlined, noted below.2020/21Funding was awarded to The Rainbow Project under the Homelessness Prevention Fund to deliver a new LGBTQ+ Housing Support Project. The purpose of the project was to support LGBTQ+ people experiencing or at risk of experiencing homelessness in accessing support and services through provision of a dedicated Housing Support Officer. This was in line with Objective One of the Homelessness Strategy 2017-22 to prioritise homelessness prevention. 2021/22Funding was awarded to The Rainbow Project under the Homeless Prevention Fund to continue delivery of the LGBTQIA+ Housing Support Project as outlined above. 2022/23Funding was awarded to The Rainbow Project under the Homeless Prevention Fund to continue delivery of the LGBTQIA+ Housing Support Project, in line with the priorities of the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 and the Homelessness Reset Plan in response to Covid-19.2023/24Funding was awarded to The Rainbow Project under the Homeless Prevention Fund to continue delivery of the LGBTQIA+ Housing Support Project as outlined above.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the average time taken by his Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since the formation of the Executive in February 2024, my Department has responded to 170 requests for information under the Freedom of information Act 2000 to date and the average response time of those requests is 13.4 working days.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average time taken by his Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The average time taken by my Department to provide a response to a Freedom of Information request, including requests made under the Environmental Information Regulations 2004, since the restoration of the NI Executive is 17 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the average time taken by his Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: My department recognises the need to respond to requests for information under the Freedom of Information Act or Environmental Information Regulations 	promptly, and no later than 20 working days following the date of receipt of the request. 		The average time taken by the department to respond to FOI/EIR requests since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024 is 14 working days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the average time taken by his Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since February 2024, my Department has received 198 requests under the Freedom of Information Act 2000 of which 90% of the responses have been issued on time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the average time taken by their Department to respond to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: We can confirm that the average time taken for officials to respond to Freedom of Information requests since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024 is 17 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Information Commissioner's Office decision notices that have been issued against his Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Department of Education has received no decision notices from the Information Commissioner’s Office since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of Information Commissioner's Office decision notices that have been issued against his Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Information Commissioner’s Office has not issued any decision notices against my Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Information Commissioner's Office decision notices that have been issued against his Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, the Department has received two decision notices from the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO). One Departmental decision was upheld, although my Department was requested to offer further advice and guidance to the requestor.The second found against the Department’s decision to exempt information pursuant to Section 43 (commercial interests). The Department issued the withheld information. All decision notices issued by the ICO can be found at https://icosearch.ico.org.uk/s/search.html?collection=ico-meta&profile=decisions&query

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of Information Commissioner's Office decision notices that have been issued against her Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Department has received three Information Commissioner’s Office decision notices since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024, all of which have been positive and upheld DoJ’s original FOI or DPA responses and internal appeals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Information Commissioner's Office decision notices that have been issued against her Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since February 2024 there has been one Information Commissioner’s Office decision notice issued against the Department of Finance. The decision notice partially upheld the complaint.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of times (i) she; and (ii) her Special Adviser has been involved in clearing a response to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: (i) Neither the Minister nor her Special Advisor have been involved in clearing responses to Freedom of Information (FOI) requests since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.        (ii) On receipt, each FOI request is allocated to an “owner” in the relevant business area. The owner will normally be at Grade 5 or Chief Executive level. It is the responsibility of the owner to clear the FOI response for issue.(iii) This process is set out in the Departmental Response Management Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of Information Commissioner's Office decision notices that have been issued against his Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: This information is publicly available. The Information Commissioner’s Office publishes decision notices on its website and the webpage contains a search (by public authority) facility: https://icosearch.ico.org.uk/s/search.html?collection=ico-meta&profile=decisions&query

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of Information Commissioner's Office decision notices that have been issued against his Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure has had no decision notices issued against it by the Information Commissioner’s Office since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.The Information Commissioner’s Office has issued the Department for Infrastructure with one decision notice in that time.  The ICO rejected the appeal, accepting that my Department’s handling of the request for information had been correct.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Information Commissioner's Office decision notices that have been issued against his Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: Since February 2024, the Information Commissioner’s Office has issued one Decision Notice against my Department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of Information Commissioner's Office decision notices that have been issued against their Department since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: The Information Commissioner has issued two Decision Notices relating to how the Department processed Freedom of Information requests since February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of times (i) she; and (ii) her Special Adviser has been involved in clearing a response to a Freedom of Information request since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: None.
