question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the cost of producing the Civil Service Inclusive Language Guide, including a breakdown of all payments made to outside bodies.
answer_text: There was no cost to the production of the Civil Service Inclusive Language guide which was developed by staff in-house in consultation with internal and external stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will withdraw the document entitled Guidance for Schools, EOTAS Centres and Youth Service on Supporting Transgender Young People, published by the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) published its non-statutory guidance. Future work in relation to their Transgender Young People guidance remains a matter for the EA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the status of the Civil Service Inclusive Language Guide within the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the response provided for AQW 17025/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement with those critical of modern gender theory when producing the Civil Service Inclusive Language Guide.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the response provided for AQW 17025/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether departmental documents will now avoid the use of the words mother, father, wife and husband.
answer_text: My Department aims to use inclusive language in departmental documents so that everyone can recognise themselves and feel included. The Civil Service Inclusive Language Guide provides guiding principles and suggestions to encourage civil servants to be more conscious of the language they use and to better understand how language can impact others and make them feel included or excluded.There is no Civil Service policy preventing the use of the words ‘mother’, ‘father, ‘wife’ and ‘husband’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on Belfast Transport Hub - Discharge of Condition 17 for LA04/2017/1388/F, including whether the planning condition has been met; and (ii) whether he will give an assurance that community liaison will include the Blackstaff Residents’ Association.
answer_text: My Department is currently processing a number of applications to discharge planning conditions which were attached to planning permission Ref: LA04/2017/1388/F for the new transport hub at Belfast Grand Central Station. An application to remove condition 5 on the planning permission which controls the amount of Enterprise services between Belfast and Dublin is also currently with my Department for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Belfast Transport Hub - Discharge of Condition 15 for LA04/2017/1388/F, including whether the planning condition has been met.
answer_text: My Department is currently processing a number of applications to discharge planning conditions which were attached to planning permission Ref: LA04/2017/1388/F for the new transport hub at Belfast Grand Central Station. An application to remove condition 5 on the planning permission which controls the amount of Enterprise services between Belfast and Dublin is also currently with my Department for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Belfast Transport Hub - Discharge of Condition 08 for LA04/2017/1388/F, including whether the planning condition has been met.
answer_text: My Department is currently processing a number of applications to discharge planning conditions which were attached to planning permission Ref: LA04/2017/1388/F for the new transport hub at Belfast Grand Central Station. An application to remove condition 5 on the planning permission which controls the amount of Enterprise services between Belfast and Dublin is also currently with my Department for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the outside bodies her Department consulted with in relation to the production of the Civil Service Inclusive Language Guide.
answer_text: The Civil Service Inclusive Language Guide was developed as guidance to promote understanding of the important role language plays in creating an inclusive workplace. The guidance aims to encourage civil servants to be more conscious of the language they use and to better understand how language can impact others and make them feel included or excluded.In developing the Guide, there was engagement with a range of internal and external stakeholders.  Internal stakeholders consulted include the Civil Service Diversity Champions Network and Civil Service staff networks, i.e. Disability; LGBTQ+, Race and Ethnicity; and Women’s Networks. External stakeholders consulted on the development of the Inclusive Language Guide were the Employers for Disability NI and the Equality Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether there is a current application by Translink to remove Condition 5, in relation to restricting the number of daily Belfast to Dublin train services, on planning permission LA04/2017/1388/F for the Belfast Transport Hub; and (ii) to detail the number of conditions or amendments that have been made to the planning conditions.
answer_text: My Department is currently processing a number of applications to discharge planning conditions which were attached to planning permission Ref: LA04/2017/1388/F for the new transport hub at Belfast Grand Central Station. An application to remove condition 5 on the planning permission which controls the amount of Enterprise services between Belfast and Dublin is also currently with my Department for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 16723/22-27, (i) to detail the expected time frame for the examination; and (ii) whether the Assembly Commission will commit to publishing the findings of the examination in full.
answer_text: This matter is being examined under the authority of the Clerk/ Chief Executive as the Assembly Commission’s Accounting Officer.  Assembly Commission Members will have no role in it.  Paragraph 43 of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016, as amended (‘the Determination’) sets out the circumstances in which payments made by the Assembly Commission to an MLA may be recouped. It is therefore important that any consideration of compliance with the Determination is dealt with in a robust way which gives primary focus to following due process and examining the evidence.  The Clerk/ Chief Executive will review the initial evidence gathered by 15 November 2024 to determine the nature of any further work or action required.  However, a running commentary will not be provided while the process is ongoing.The Clerk/ Chief Executive will notify the Assembly Commission of the outcome of the review once the examination of issues and the implementation of any required actions is complete.  The form in which the outcome is then made public will be considered at that point by the Clerk/ Chief Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the funding provided by his Department and its arm's-length bodies to the Rainbow Project in each of the last five years; and (ii) whether he will review this funding in light of evidence provided at the meeting of the Committee for Education on 16 October 2024, in which it was noted that the Rainbow Project signposted to GenderGP advice on accessing puberty blockers despite their ban.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) has commissioned the Rainbow Project to provide a range of services across NI Northern Ireland for LGBT+ clients over the last 5 years to the total value of: £936,379.2024-25£103,6572023-24£307,8672022-23£218,1962021-22£217,0982020-21£89,561£936,379There are no plans to review funding for this organisation as their work addresses the needs of an identified Section 75 group who often experience marginalisation and discrimination in society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the funding provided by her Department and its arm's-length bodies to the Rainbow Project in each of the last five years; and (ii) whether she will review this funding in light of evidence provided at the meeting of the Committee for Education on 16 October 2024, in which it was noted that the Rainbow Project signposted to GenderGP advice on accessing puberty blockers despite their ban.
answer_text: My Department made payments of £150 and £500 to the Rainbow Project in 2020/21 and 2022/23 respectively.  That does not include any funding that may have been provided to the Rainbow Project by the Department’s Non-Departmental Public Bodies.  You may wish to contact the organisations directly to request the information: Police Service of NI; Northern Ireland Policing Board; Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust; Office of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland; RUC George Cross Foundation; Probation Board for Northern Ireland; Northern Ireland Police Fund and, Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland.I am aware of the evidence session at the Committee for Education on 16 October and the reference to the GenderGP website. I have no plans to review the funding to the Rainbow Project.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) to detail the funding provided by their Department and its arm's-length bodies to the Rainbow Project in each of the last five years; and (ii) whether they will review this funding in light of evidence provided at the meeting of the Committee for Education on 16 October 2024, in which it was noted that the Rainbow Project signposted to GenderGP advice on accessing puberty blockers despite their ban.
answer_text: The Executive Office has not provided any funding to the Rainbow Project in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the funding provided by his Department and its arm's-length bodies to the Rainbow Project in each of the last five years; and (ii) whether he will review this funding in light of evidence provided at the meeting of the Committee for Education on 16 October 2024, in which it was noted that the Rainbow Project signposted to GenderGP advice on accessing puberty blockers despite their ban.
answer_text: Neither the Department nor any of its arms-length bodies have provided funding to the Rainbow Project in the last five years.I am aware of the evidence provided by the Rainbow Project to the Committee for Education on 16 October 2024.  However, given my response to i) above, no review is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the percentage of pollutants, resulting in the growth of blue-green algae at Lough Neagh, for which departments and their agencies are responsible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the role and responsibilities of the recently advertised Head of Regulatory Divergence Co-ordination post.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to list any declarations of interest declared by the First Minister relating to the work of the Executive Office on the  Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime Programme
answer_text: Ministerial declarations of interest are published at the link below:https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/special-advisers-documents

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department is taking to remove Palestinian flags obscuring traffic lights on Garvaghy Road, Portadown.
answer_text: My Department does not permit the attachment of flags to its property.  Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts. One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised flags or attachments pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard. Where there is no such danger, my Department may liaise with other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution. However, given the Department’s ongoing resource limitations, situations where there is no clear road safety risk are considered a low priority.Officials have assessed the location and deemed the road safety risk to be low and therefore no further action will be taken.Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively and find a long term solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of whether there is a conflict of interest in a former Minister, who had policy remit for higher education, being employed by a university without a formal recruitment process.
answer_text: As autonomous institutions, universities are responsible for their own policies and procedures, including recruitment and retention.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of people employed as lecturers in (i) Queen’s University Belfast; and (ii) the Ulster University as a result of coming from an exceptional talent pool which resulted in no formal recruitment process, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: As autonomous institutions, both Queen’s and Ulster University are responsible for their own policies and procedures, including recruitment and retention. My Department does not hold the information requested.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to list any individuals who travelled with the First Minister in her ministerial car, on non-ministerial business, including the number of occasions.
answer_text: We do not have Departmental Ministerial cars for the First Minister and deputy First Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of babies born in ambulances parked in hospital grounds in each of the last three years, broken down by hospital; and (ii) to detail the (a) average time; and (ii) longest time the mother spent in the ambulance in each case, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s (NIAS) corporate team have confirmed that the Trust does not capture this information and are therefore unable to answer this Assembly question. The NIAS information system currently captures the number of calls made by the public of a maternity related nature only, not the number of live births that take place in the back of an ambulance at hospital.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to recent media reports, to confirm whether there is an NSMC Brexit Senior Group.
answer_text: The Post UK Withdrawal from the EU Senior Officials Group (formerly the UK Withdrawal from the EU Senior Officials Group) was established through the NSMC and provides a forum for discussion on post UK withdrawal related issues of common interest to both jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the eight new surgical beds in Antrim Area Hospital will be sufficient to meet demand if emergency surgery no longer takes place at the Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the eight new surgical beds in Antrim Area Hospital will be sufficient to meet demand if emergency surgery no longer takes place at the Causeway Hospital. ANSWER The Trust is considering a range of measures to accommodate the additional demands on the Antrim Area Hospital site should the proposal to centralise emergency surgery be approved. These include:

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the installation date for the MRI scanner at the Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: The current time line for installation of the Causeway Hospital MRI scanner is the end of May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the response to the current consultation on emergency surgery at the Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: The Northern Trust Board has approved a 14-week public consultation process, which will run, from Friday 23 August until Friday 29 November, 2024, as it continues to develop plans for the reconfiguration of general surgical services across its hospital sites to enable a sustainable surgical configuration in the context of the Review of General Surgery. My Department and PHA will continue to work with the Trust throughout the consultation process. Any change to services will be taken in line the Department's Guidance on Change and Withdrawal of services and will require my approval.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether the Sinn Féin member on the Assembly Commission has declared an interest arising from its examination into matters relating to Michael McMonagle.
answer_text: The Assembly appoints Members to the Assembly Commission in the knowledge that they are representatives of their political parties.  However, this matter will not be considered at a meeting of the Assembly Commission and Assembly Commission Members will have no role in making decisions on it.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether Sinn Féin has a veto on the (i) terms of reference; and (ii) findings of the Assembly Commission’s examination into matters relating to Michael McMonagle.
answer_text: No Assembly Commission Member, or political party, is, or will be, involved in decisions around how the examination of these matters will be conducted, or any decisions which may have to be taken as a result.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any role that the Sinn Féin member of the Assembly Commission has in relation to its examination into matters relating to Michael McMonagle.
answer_text: No Assembly Commission Member has any role in relation to the examination into matters relating to Michael McMonagle.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) greatest; and (ii) average bed occupancy in Antrim Area Hospital in the last 12 months.
answer_text: From 1 October 2023 to 30 September 2024, bed occupancy in Antrim Hospital excluding paediatrics, maternity and palliative care was: Greatest: 118%Average: 108%This information is taken from Trust PAS system and it includes patients in beds in inpatient wards and those waiting for a bed in the Emergency Department.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, in light of Sinn Féin’s membership of the Assembly Commission, to detail any measures it will take to ensure there is public confidence in its examination into matters relating to Michael McMonagle.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission takes matters relating to the administration of public funds extremely seriously.  When agreeing the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) (Amendment) Determination (Northern Ireland) in 2020, the Assembly Commission reiterated that there should continue to be rigorous consideration by officials to ensure that every MLA complied with the requirements of the Determination.  At that time, the Assembly Commission reaffirmed its longstanding position that no elected MLA (including Members of the Assembly Commission) should be involved in decisions around the administration, or review, of individual claims made by MLAs under the Determination. The examination of issues arising in relation to Michael McMonagle and Sinn Féin is being undertaken to ensure that there has been compliance with the Assembly Members’ (Salaries and Expenses) Determination.  This process is being conducted by a senior official under the authority of the Clerk/ Chief Executive as the Assembly Commission’s Accounting Officer.  Assembly Commission Members will have no role in it.It is important that this matter is dealt with in a robust way which follows due process and examines the evidence.  Therefore, a running commentary will not be provided while it is continuing.  Section 43 of the Determination sets out the circumstances in which payments made by the Assembly Commission to an MLA may be recouped.  Should any issues of non-compliance be confirmed, these will be dealt with in accordance with the provisions of the Determination for the recovery of funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether an assessment has been made as to the Barnett consequentials of the 5.5 per cent pay increase for NHS workers recommended by the pay review body should it be approved; and (ii) whether he will commit to using all such monies to increase the wages of NHS staff in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Executive does not have certainty at this stage around the level of additional funding that will be made available by the British Government in Barnett consequentials as a result of increased spending in England. Allocations received through Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated meaning that the Executive can determine allocations for specific priorities and programmes regardless of the nature of the comparable spending that gave rise to the Barnett allocation. It will be for the Executive to determine its priorities and make allocations accordingly. It is anticipated that there will be additional allocations made to departments as part of the upcoming October monitoring round, but it will therefore be for the Executive to decide what allocations are made to the Department of Health. Any determination around pay for Health workers will ultimately be a matter for the Health Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the changes recommended by the Local Government Boundaries Commissioner for Northern Ireland in 2022.
answer_text: My officials are currently progressing work on a draft Order to give effect to the 2022 final recommendations of the Local Government Boundaries Commissioner.  The Final Recommendations Report of the Local Government Boundaries Commissioner, together with the draft Order and, if necessary, a statement of modifications will be laid in the Assembly as soon as possible following my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the potential loss of emergency surgery at Causeway Hospital is consistent with his comments in the Assembly Chamber on 1 October 2024, when he stated that Causeway Hospital will remain a key element of the hospital network in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Northern Trust’s Strategic Vision for Causeway Hospital (March 2024) includes a clear commitment to maintaining a 24/7 Emergency Department and acute inpatient services in Causeway Hospital and sets out an ambitious vision for the development and enhancement of services in Causeway. The Trust’s review of general surgical services is currently subject to public consultation, and the Trust has been clear that its preferred option does not impact on Causeway’s status as an acute hospital. This is consistent with the Minister’s position that Causeway Hospital will remain a key element of the hospital network in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of surgeries performed in the Causeway Hospital in each of the last three years, broken down by type of surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total spend on the helipad at the Royal Victoria Hospital to ensure a safe run off of any potential petroleum spill.
answer_text: There is no provision for fuel storage or helicopter refuelling on the helipad at the Royal Victoria Hospital therefore no risk of a petroleum spill from these operations that would require a safe run off. In line with Civil Aviation Authority standards a fire suppression system has been installed to mitigate against the risk of a helicopter crash and subsequent fire.  The construction works for the helipad were procured using the Belfast Health and Social Cares Trust’s appointed Measured Term Contractor. Total costs for the helipad project including construction works, fees and certification were in the region of £1.3 million.The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service have advised that to March 24 the helipad was required 962 times. For this financial year current records indicate that the helipad has been required 104 times.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the number of times, including dates, that Michael McMonagle entered Parliament Buildings since being charged with sexual offences against children; and (ii) any groups, including schools and youth groups, that visited Parliament Buildings on the dates Michael McMonagle was in Parliament Buildings during this period.
answer_text: Data relative to the use of Assembly ID passes is retained on the Assembly’s Visitor Management System for a period of 90 days after use.  The pass issued to Michael McMonagle was cancelled on 1 October 2024, and there is no record of it being used in the preceding 90 days which extend back until 4 July 2024.  It has been widely reported that Mr McMonagle attended a publicity event in the Great Hall on 14 February 2023. When Mr McMonagle attended the Great Hall on 14 February 2023, he used a visitor pass. These passes are valid for one day and are issued to any visitor to Parliament Buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail her Department's long-term plans for County Hall, Ballymena.
answer_text: The Department is currently assessing its accommodation requirements in the Ballymena area. Options regarding plans for County Hall, Ballymena over the longer-term will be considered as part of this assessment and will be concluded in consultation with the relevant departments who occupy the accommodation. Information on Office Estate accommodation plans is published on the DoF website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the tendering process for the work on the helipad at the Royal Victoria Hospital to ensure a safe run off of any potential petroleum spill.
answer_text: There is no provision for fuel storage or helicopter refuelling on the helipad at the Royal Victoria Hospital therefore no risk of a petroleum spill from these operations that would require a safe run off. In line with Civil Aviation Authority standards a fire suppression system has been installed to mitigate against the risk of a helicopter crash and subsequent fire.  The construction works for the helipad were procured using the Belfast Health and Social Cares Trust’s appointed Measured Term Contractor. Total costs for the helipad project including construction works, fees and certification were in the region of £1.3 million.The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service have advised that to March 24 the helipad was required 962 times. For this financial year current records indicate that the helipad has been required 104 times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the total amount spent on the helipad at the Royal Victoria Hospital to ensure a safe run off of any potential petroleum spill represented value for money.
answer_text: There is no provision for fuel storage or helicopter refuelling on the helipad at the Royal Victoria Hospital therefore no risk of a petroleum spill from these operations that would require a safe run off. In line with Civil Aviation Authority standards a fire suppression system has been installed to mitigate against the risk of a helicopter crash and subsequent fire.  The construction works for the helipad were procured using the Belfast Health and Social Cares Trust’s appointed Measured Term Contractor. Total costs for the helipad project including construction works, fees and certification were in the region of £1.3 million.The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service have advised that to March 24 the helipad was required 962 times. For this financial year current records indicate that the helipad has been required 104 times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the importance of correct public messaging on issues related to the employment of child sex offenders, in light of her responsibility in relation to laws relating to sexual offences against, and the protection of, children.
answer_text: As this question is about “public messaging on issues related to the employment of child sex offenders”, I am unable to provide a substantive response as this does not fall within my area of responsibility.While my Department is responsible for maintaining the legal framework in respect of sexual offences in this jurisdiction, ‘public messaging’ in this context does not relate to the law on sexual offences.  AccessNI, which comes under DoJ, is solely responsible for the disclosure of criminal history information, rather than public messaging on matters of safeguarding.  In an attempt to provide a little detail that may be relevant to this subject matter, AccessNI Enhanced disclosures can include information in regarding:- Convictions and other non-court disposals (for example cautions, informed warnings);Relevant Barred List information; and Information held by police that police consider relevant to the specific role the person is seeking to undertake – this could include details about unsuccessful prosecutions or on-going investigations. AccessNI disclosures are provided with the aim of assisting employers to make more informed recruitment decisions.  It is the responsibility of individual organisations to decide the level of AccessNI check they require – they should do this in reference to the eligibility criterion set out in legislation and the guidance published by the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will introduce mandatory child safeguarding training for MLAs in light of issues around Michael McMonagle.
answer_text: Safeguarding is a matter of individual responsibility for each Member and the Assembly Commission does not have powers to require Members to undertake training.The Assembly Commission’s Parliamentary Excellence Programme delivers knowledge and development sessions for Members and their staff. Assembly Commission officials consult party whips, the smaller parties and independent Member on topics for inclusion in the programme.  Topics suggested by Members are welcome and safeguarding awareness training for Members can be organised upon request by emailing the Clerking and Member Support office (CAMSOfficeDL@niassembly.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to recent media reports, to detail (i) their assessment of whether it was a cost-effective use of public money to spend over £500 to send a civil servant to Portugal to brief the First Minister in advance of the Assembly recall on 8 August 2024; (ii) the rationale for not briefing the First Minister via a written briefing or online video meeting; (iii) the date and time of the official's flight (a) to Portugal; and (b) from Portugal; and (iv) whether the official was a Special Adviser to the First Minister.
answer_text: Given the seriousness of the situation, the First Minister returned from her family holiday to deal with the ongoing threat and intimidation which was having a devastating impact on the victims and communities.The department put in place travel arrangements to ensure the First Minister was not travelling alone on departmental business. The accompanying Civil Servant assisted with the preparations for the First Minister to jointly chair an urgent meeting of the Executive.  At this emergency meeting of the Executive, Ministers were briefed by the Chief Constable on public disorder and ongoing racist attacks. The First Minister and deputy First Minister updated MLAs during an urgent recall of the Assembly

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any plans it has to come to the Assembly Chamber to answer questions on the issues around Michael McMonagle.
answer_text: The Business Committee has next scheduled the Assembly Commission to answer Oral Questions from Members on Tuesday 3 December 2024.  Standing Order 18 provides only for Ministers to make oral or written statements to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) who cancelled Michael McMonagle's Assembly pass; and (ii) the date this took place.
answer_text: Mr McMonagle’s Member-sponsored pass to Parliament Buildings was revoked by the Director of Parliamentary Services on 2 October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans there are to extend the Employment (Allocation of Tips) Act 2023 to Northern Ireland, which makes it mandatory for all tips to be passed on to workers without deductions.
answer_text: Employment Law is a devolved matter.  My ‘Good Jobs’ Employment Rights Bill consultation included proposals to introduce legislation to ensure tips left to workers are passed on to them in full.  The consultation closed on 30 September and my Department is analysing responses to inform next steps.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether Part 4 of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination permits political parties to fund the employment of a press officer.
answer_text: Part 4 of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016, as amended (‘the Determination’), does not prescribe the job roles a Member may require, and this is a matter for the Member themselves as the employer.  However, the Determination sets out a number of general tests in paragraph 31 which must be met in order for the recovery of staff costs to be permitted.  These include that the staff costs must be incurred in connection with the Member's exercise of functions as a Member, that political parties cannot benefit from staff costs incurred by Members, and that resources made available to Members cannot be used for party political activities.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether Michael McMonagle was paid through the Financial Assistance for Political Parties Scheme or through Part 4 of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination.
answer_text: Mr McMonagle’s employment was with individual Members, therefore, the costs associated with these periods of employment have been claimed under Part 4 of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016, as amended.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the MLAs who employed Michael McMonagle through Part 4 of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination; and (ii) the relevant dates in each case.
answer_text: Mr Michael McMonagle was employed from 2 March 2020 to 31 May 2020 by Ms Michelle O’Neill MLA, and then from 1 June 2020 to 8 July 2022 by Miss Jemma Dolan MLA.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any money paid by the Northern Ireland Assembly to Michael McMonagle through (i) the Financial Assistance for Political Parties Scheme; and (ii) Part 4 of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission paid the following amounts to Michael McMonagle through:Financial Assistance for Political Parties Scheme £0.00; Part 4 the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016, as amended £89,099.18.  This is the amount that was paid to Michael McMonagle over the course of his employment.  The gross cost, including employers pension and employer’s national insurance, was £109,296.83.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it is taking any measures to recover funds used to employ Michael McMonagle as a Sinn Féin press officer.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission takes matters relating to the administration of public funds seriously.  The Assembly Commission has responsibility for the administration of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016, as amended (‘the Determination’), and as the Speaker informed the Assembly on 7 October 2024, officials are actively looking at issues that have arisen in relation to Mr Michael McMonagle.  A detailed examination is underway by officials to ensure that there has been compliance with the Determination.  Should any issues of non-compliance be identified, these will be dealt with in accordance with the provisions of the Determination for the recovery of funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of his Department continuing to offer a distinct service for transgender individuals in relation to Personal Independence Payments; and (ii) whether he has any plans to discontinue this service.
answer_text: My department is no longer offering a distinct service solely to transgender individuals. Having reviewed this service, I have asked officials to ensure this accessible friendly contact channel is available for everyone who wishes to use it, regardless of their identity.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will review its policy of not requiring MLAs to notify the Assembly Commission about employment matters unless those matters are relevant to payroll services, in light of issues around Michael McMonagle.
answer_text: In October 2021, the Assembly Commission established a service which provides direct HR support for Members. The service is provided by the Assembly Commission’s in-house HR professionals and extends to Members in their capacity as employers. The service aligns with the provisions of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016.   The service provided covers the development of policies and procedures and template forms for Members to use in their role as an employer and also the provision of direct advice and guidance to Members on a range of HR matters such as recruitment, dispute resolution, redundancy, etc.However, this service is provided in the context that Members retain all employer responsibilities. While the HR service can provide advice and support, it will not undertake any aspect of the employment relationship between a Member and her or his staff. Members are therefore not required to notify the Assembly Commission about employment matters, but can access the advice and guidance provided by the HR Services to Members if they have a specific employment query.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people who have contacted the Personal Independence Payment Centre by way of the email address provided for transgender customers.
answer_text: Between August 2023 and December 2024, 31 queries sent to this email address were subsequently forwarded to the Personal Independence Payment Centre. Emails older than 14 months are not retained.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 15259/22-27, to detail the sites her Department is responsible for.
answer_text: The list of sites for which my Department has responsibility, as owner or leaseholder, is available on the Department of Finance website and is updated regularly.The list, referred to as the ‘Schedule of DoF Properties Division Holdings’ can be accessed at the following link  https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/office-estate-review-further-information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether specific staff are assigned to deal with transgender individuals in relation to Personal Independence Payments.
answer_text: There are no specific staff assigned to deal with transgender individuals in relation to Personal Independence Payment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the public money paid to victims of Historic Institutional Abuse has come from the block grant.
answer_text: Payments made to victims and survivors of Historical Institutional Abuse are paid out of the block grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department began offering a distinct service for transgender individuals in relation to Personal Independence Payments.
answer_text: There is no distinct service offered for transgender individuals in relation to Personal Independence Payment. My Department did, however, provide a dedicated email address for transgender people who need support to access the Department’s benefit services. This was set up on 7 October 2018 and covers all operational benefit branches across the Department, including Personal Independence Payment. Having reviewed this service, I have asked officials to ensure this accessible friendly contact channel is available for everyone who wishes to use it, regardless of their identity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rationale for his Department offering a distinct service for transgender individuals in relation to Personal Independence Payments.
answer_text: There is no distinct service offered for transgender individuals in relation to Personal Independence Payment. My Department does, however, provide a dedicated email address for transgender people who need support to access benefit services. This was set up on 7 October 2018 and covers all operational benefit branches across the Department, including Personal Independence Payment. Having reviewed this service, I have asked Officials to ensure that this accessible friendly contact channel is available for everyone who wishes to use it, regardless of their identity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the statutory authority for the Police Ombudsman to delegate powers to officials.
answer_text: The power for the Police Ombudsman to delegate their powers is contained within Schedule 3, section 9 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1998 which states that "….any functions of the Ombudsman under this Act may be performed by any officer of the Ombudsman authorised for the purpose by the Ombudsman."

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any additional funding made available to the Victims and Survivors Service to cover the work arising from the Historical Institutional Abuse Redress Board.
answer_text: The Victims and Survivors Service (VSS) have provided a range of support services to victims and survivors of historical institutional abuse (HIA) since December 2020.The total expenditure by VSS on HIA services for the past 4 years is:2020/212021/222022/232023/24£’000£’000£’000£’0001459207901,054This expenditure includes supporting potential applicants to the HIA Redress Board with the preparation of their Statement of Experience.We continue to keep under review the funding provided to VSS.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the engagement they have had with (i) institutions of the Roman Catholic Church; and (ii) charitable organisations, in relation to seeking contributions towards payments made by the Historical Institutional Abuse Redress Board.
answer_text: Paul Sweeney was appointed in July 2021 as an independent facilitator to take forward negotiations with the six voluntary institutions - Barnardo’s, De La Salle Brothers, Good Shepherd Sisters, Irish Church Missions, Sisters of Nazareth, and Sisters of Saint Louis.On 14 October 2021, Ministers at the time hosted a Roundtable meeting between officials, representatives of the six voluntary institutions, and the independent facilitator to discuss contributions from institutions.  Regular bilateral engagements have been taking place with officials, the independent facilitator, and the institutions’ representatives since.To date, interim payments have been received from three institutions - Barnardo’s, the De La Salle Order, and the Good Shepherd Sisters. Negotiations are ongoing, and at different stages with each institution. In addition, officials and the independent facilitator have also engaged with the Catholic and Church of Ireland Archbishops of Armagh to keep them updated on discussions with institutions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they are aware of correspondence from the Sisters of Nazareth to the Committee for the Executive Office that states they have not received a formal request from your Department for a contribution towards the Historical Institutional Abuse Redress Scheme.
answer_text: Our officials have received a copy of this correspondence and have addressed a number of points with the Sisters of Nazareth.  Discussions are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether Assembly officials were informed of Michael McMonagle’s arrest in 2021 for sexual offences against children.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission was not made aware of Michael McMonagle’s arrest as an employee of an MLA, nor the reason for it.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will review its child protection policy in light of Michael McMonagle’s access to Parliament Buildings after his arrest in 2021 for sexual offences against children.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has currently within its Staff Handbook, a policy entitled ‘Protection of Children, Young People and Vulnerable Adults’. In 2024, the Assembly Commission’s auditors completed an audit of the Youth Assembly. The audit report included recommendations in relation to safeguarding and child protection, including that the Enhanced Check for those working with children and vulnerable adults should be reviewed every three years. The Policy (which has been renamed as the Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy) has updated to address these recommendations and will be considered by the Assembly Commission’s Senior Management Team at its meeting on 25 October 2024.The Policy covers all Assembly Commission staff, agency workers and inward secondees. The principles of the Policy also apply to contractors working in Parliament Buildings or events sponsored by the Assembly Commission in other venues on behalf of the Assembly Commission. The Policy does not cover Member’s staff.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether a pass to Parliament Buildings has ever been granted to Michael McMonagle (i) in his role within Sinn Féin; and (ii) while working in any another capacity.
answer_text: Assembly passes are allocated to political parties using the d’Hondt method based on party strength.  In addition, each MLA is allocated three passes for distribution to their staff.  Passes are issued as either MLA staff or party staff passes.Michael McMonagle was previously issued with an Assembly ID pass at the request of Mr Philip McGuigan MLA.  He was not issued with an ID pass whilst working in any other capacity.  When Mr McMonagle attended the Great Hall on 14 February 2023, he used a visitor pass. These passes are valid for one day and are issued to any visitor to Parliament Buildings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail why their office was not represented at the launch of the Research Report 2024, When you know what they are capable of: Paramilitary-related Gendered Coercive Control.
answer_text: An official from the EVAWG Programme team attended the launch of the ‘When you know what they are capable of’: Paramilitary-related Gendered Coercive Control research, which took place on the same day as the launch of the Strategic Framework in the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to compensation for Historical Institutional Abuse, to detail the total amount of payments made by (i) the state; (ii) the Roman Catholic Church, broken down religious order; and (iii) charitable organisations, broken down by charity.
answer_text: The Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry Report recommended that any voluntary institution found by the Inquiry to have been guilty of systemic failings should be asked to make an appropriate financial contribution to the overall cost of the HIA Redress Board and any specialist support services. Paul Sweeney was appointed in 2021 as an independent facilitator to take forward negotiations with the six voluntary institutions:  Barnardo’s; the De La Salle Order; Good Shepherd Sisters; Irish Church Missions; Sisters of Nazareth; and Sisters of Saint Louis.  To date interim payments have been received from Barnardo’s, the De La Salle Order, and the Good Shepherd Sisters. The ongoing negotiations are sensitive, and at different stages with each institution. It is therefore not appropriate to disclose the amount that we have received from each institution at this point. It is important that we give space to allow the negotiations to progress in a constructive manner.  Final payments will not be able to be confirmed until all applications made to the HIA Redress Board, by the closing date of 2 April 2025, have been processed.We have committed to releasing the details of contributions once the process has been completed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of their statutory obligation to represent the interests of victims, for their assessment of the victims’ accounts of the role of the IRA in the abuse of women and girls, as highlighted in the Research Report 2024, When you know what they are capable of: Paramilitary-related Gendered Coercive Control.
answer_text: We remain committed to ensuring that the needs of victims and survivors continue to be met, and the new Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls is intended to address the full spectrum of violence, abuse and harm against women and girls. All women and girls deserve to live in a place that is free from gender-based violence, abuse and harm. A key part of the Framework is providing support to victims and survivors of violence.Officials from the EVAWG Programme team continue to work closely with their counterparts in the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism & Organised Crime (EPPOC). EVAWG is represented on the EPPOC Programme Board.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of their strategy on violence against women and girls, to detail the action their Department is taking following the Research Report 2024, When you know what they are capable of: Paramilitary-related Gendered Coercive Control.
answer_text: The Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) seeks to address the range of violence, abuse and harm faced by women and girls here, and recognises the need to work collaboratively across both government and society in tackling all forms of violence including paramilitary violence. Our officials in the EVAWG Programme team continue to work closely with their counterparts in the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism & Organised Crime (EPPOC).Senior officials from each Programme attend and participate in the governance arrangements of other Programme, ensuring full visibility and alignment of all actions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the total amount of payments made by the Historical Institutional Abuse Redress Board attributable to facilities run by (i) the state; (ii) the Roman Catholic Church, broken down religious order; and (iii) charitable organisations, broken down by charity.
answer_text: At the 30 September 2024, the HIA Redress Board has received 4,711 applications for redress and made determinations totalling £95,190,750. A total of £92,288,425 has been paid directly into the applicant or applicant’s solicitor’s account. Applications for redress have been made in respect of more than 100 institutions which the Redress Board considers fall within the definition of an institution, as set out in section 2(3) & (4) of the Historical Institutional Abuse (NI) Act 2019. A list of these institutions is available on the HIA Redress Board’s website.All applications for redress are considered by independent panels made up of one judicial and two non-judicial panel members. The level of compensation awarded is determined by the respective panel based on the evidence and severity of abuse detailed in each application. In cases where an application references multiple institutions, the Redress Board will determine whether abuse occurred in each of the named institutions but does not apportion an award across the specified institutions, in line with section 12(6) of the Act. As determinations are not disaggregated between each relevant institution, it is not possible to detail the total amount of payments attributable to each institution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to media reports on 25 September 2024, for a breakdown of the number of reports of illegal drug dealing on hospital premises in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the number of reports of illegal drug dealing on hospital premises is not routinely kept by HSC Trusts.  Three Trusts have, however, been able to give an indication as to the prevalence of this activity on HSC premises.South Eastern HSC Trust, Western HSC Trust and Northern HSC Trust information is shown in the table below:TrustYearNumber of incidents of intent to supply a controlled drug on hospital premisesSouth Eastern Trust2019/20Two separate alleged incidents of intent to supply a controlled drug to a patient. PSNI informed2020/21Zero incidents.   2021/22Two separate incidents of intent to supply a controlled drug to a patient. PSNI informed2022/23Zero incidents.    2023/24One incident of alleged criminality on hospital premises related to the supply of controlled drugs. Under investigation by PSNINorthern Trust2019/20Two reports2020/21One report2021/22Three reports2022/23Three reports2023/24Four reports (to date)Western Trust2019/20None2020/21None2021/22None2022/23None2023/24NoneBelfast HSC Trust and Southern HSC Trust both confirmed they do not hold this type of information.  Any potential criminal activity on Trust premises is reported to the PSNI who should be approached for further information on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether current number of animal welfare officers is sufficient to meet the sector's needs.
answer_text: Local councils are responsible for enforcing the Animal Welfare Act 2011 for non-farmed animals.  They have full autonomy as to how their statutory obligations are delivered under the legislation, including the employment of Animal Welfare Officers.  Therefore, this is a matter for councils to consider.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to legislate on the issue of puppy and kitten breeding.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals (Dog Breeding Establishments and Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 empowers local councils to license and inspect dog breeding establishments.  I have no immediate plans to legislate to bring forth regulations for the cat breeding sector.  However, I do intend to deliver Lucy’s Law to regulate the third-party sale of both kittens and dogs.  It is anticipated that a consultation will be published on this matter early in the new year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister why the draft Programme for Government does not include a commitment to address age discrimination.
answer_text: The draft Programme for Government (PfG) sets out our proposed collective priorities of The Executive. The PfG does not include everything that every department is working to deliver.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the current number of animal welfare officers; (ii) the number of animal welfare officers currently on sick leave; and (iii) the lowest number of animal welfare officers that were in post and carrying out their duties in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Local councils are responsible for enforcing the Animal Welfare Act 2011 for non-farmed animals.  They have full autonomy as to how their statutory obligations are delivered under the legislation, including the employment of Animal Welfare Officers.  Therefore, this is a matter for councils to consider.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a breakdown of the funding provided by their department for the extension and refurbishment of the Gasyard Centre, Londonderry.
answer_text: TEO provided funding of £2,236,942 for the construction of the extension and refurbishment of the Gasyard Heritage and Exhibition Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (i) has raised money for the Orange Order Grand Master’s appeal; and (ii) promoted this appeal on social media.
answer_text: NIFRS has not raised money for this appeal and has not promoted this appeal on social media.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will seek assurances from senior management at the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service that firefighters at Armagh Fire Station will no longer pose with, or publish photographs of, the GAA All-Ireland Senior Football Championship trophy.
answer_text: On 16 September 2024 a member of the Armagh All Ireland winning football team attended Armagh Fire Station along with family members as an expression of gratitude and recognition of the personnel from that Station for their response to a road traffic collision on 4 November 2023 in which 3 people tragically lost their life. Two of the victims of this tragic road traffic collision were relatives of the Armagh player who attended the Station. He is also a road safety ambassador for ‘The Road Ahead Support Group’ charity to which the Armagh Firefighters made a personal donation during the Station visit. Another victim of the tragedy was the wife of a former Armagh All Ireland Football winner and along with his family he also attended the event.The families were keen to thank Armagh Firefighters for their commendable operational response to the incident and the dedication and compassion that the Firefighters showed during that time. This tragedy had a significant impact on the families of the deceased, the local community and the Firefighters who responded, many of whom knew the victims. The sympathies of both the Department and NIFRS continue with the families at this time.NIFRS believes that this approach was consistent with NIFRS responsibilities as a fair and equitable employer and that it was the right thing to do in supporting not only the families impacted by this incident but also the Firefighters who responded to this incident.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether people in Northern Ireland can apply for National Institute for Health and Care Research funding for cancer research; and (ii) whether his Department would encourage people to do so.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health Research and Care Research funding is available for all devolved administrations. Within these programmes there are many opportunities for NI cancer researchers to apply for funding and I would encourage everyone eligible to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will set a target for clinical trials in relation to brain cancer in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Officials in my Department are working with key stakeholders to develop a new Cancer Research Strategy for Northern Ireland.  This will ensure that clinical trials are one of the priority areas in our response to the treatment of all types of cancer in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14535/22-27, for a time frame on answering the three issues highlighted in the closing paragraph of his answer.
answer_text: Work that will help inform the answers to the three issues highlighted in the closing paragraph of AQW 14535/22-27 commenced with the Cervical Cytology Review in the Southern Trust, in October 2023.  This Review is due to conclude shortly, after which factual reports will be produced.  This will be followed by a detailed analysis of findings which will be supported by appropriate independent experts. I expect these reports to be completed before the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14535/22-27, when work on the three issues highlighted in the closing paragraph of his answer commenced.
answer_text: Work that will help inform the answers to the three issues highlighted in the closing paragraph of AQW 14535/22-27 commenced with the Cervical Cytology Review in the Southern Trust, in October 2023.  This Review is due to conclude shortly, after which factual reports will be produced.  This will be followed by a detailed analysis of findings which will be supported by appropriate independent experts. I expect these reports to be completed before the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to match funding the money raised by Brainwaves NI.
answer_text: I commend those involved in raising money to support Brainwaves . However, the Department is dealing with a very challenging financial  position and difficult decisions are being made across the service in order to deliver a balanced budget in 24/25. Unfortunately this means that I cannot commit to match fund the money raised by Brainwaves, as this would exacerbate the scale of the breakeven challenge my Department is having to manage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of additional staff in InterTradeIreland since the introduction of the Protocol.
answer_text: There are three additional staff in the timeframe referenced.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what engagement they have had with victims of republican violence regarding the future use of prison buildings at the Maze Long Kesh site.
To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Post Office Horizon IT scandal, to detail why Fujitsu have been placed on a government contract worth up to £576 million.
answer_text: On behalf of Health and Social Care (HSC) NI, Business Services Organisation tendered for the Technology Framework Agreement 2 (TFA2).This tender was publicly advertised, under the Public Contracts Regulations 2015, via Find a Tender Service (FTS) on 25 August 2023, and closed on 6 October 2023.   Following these tender submissions, an evaluation process took place, and bidders were awarded a place on the framework which commenced on 30 August 2024. Fujitsu bid for and obtained a place on Lot 1 of the Framework, which is for Service Delivery, Transformation and Operational Services.  They were not awarded a place on Lot 2.   As this is a framework, there is no commitment to spend, and as per the rules for the TFA2, for any services that HSC require under Lot 1 a secondary competition will be held, between those bidders who were awarded a place on Lot 1 of the framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any increase in the budget of InterTradeIreland since the introduction of the Protocol.
answer_text: Since the introduction of the protocol there has been an increase in the budget for IntertradeIreland of £1,841,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14683/22-27, to detail the specific number of individual theatrical productions and costings in the last three years.
answer_text: I refer you to my previous answer to AQW 14683/22-27. As previously advised, the Arts Council has informed the Department that a manual exercise would be required to count the specific number of individual productions and costings, which is estimated to be in the hundreds, and would require significant time and resource. The Arts Council has estimated that to provide a full answer to this question would take 38.75 hrs, costing approximately £968, and therefore would not be possible without incurring disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there are any plans to close (i) Limavady PSNI Station; and (ii) Ballycastle PSNI Station.
answer_text: The management of the PSNI estate is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. It is therefore suggested that you should direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the cost of his recent visit to the United States of America.
answer_text: I recently led an Invest NI trade mission to both Chicago and Toronto with representatives from 12 local businesses. In addition to supporting the 12 businesses I was also able to raise the profile of the benefits afforded by the north’s dual market access. The total cost of my visit to the US and Canada was £16,954. This relates to the total cost for five people including myself. Breakdown:Flights £9053Accommodation £4408Transport £2447Miscellaneous £1046

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost of interpreters to the NHS in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Face-to-face interpreting services for the HSC in Northern Ireland are provided by independent self-employed interpreters who are registered with the Business Services Organisation (BSO).  BSO manages the administration of the HSC service as commissioned by the Department of Health. Telephone interpreting for the HSC is delivered by companies such as The Big Word and the contract is held by the Department for Communities.Last year during 2023/24 the Department spent £4.4m on face-to-face interpreting across each of the Trust areas as shown in the table below.  The highest expenditure was in the Southern Trust area at £1.577 million followed by the Belfast Trust which was £1.55 million with the least expenditure in the South Eastern Trust which was £310, 020. Demand for interpreting service has now returned to pre-pandemic levels of approximately 130,000 requests per annum and is expected to increase over the coming years per annum as it did before 2020/21.  In 2020/21, during the pandemic, there was considerably less patient contact with HSC services in general when the total cost of face-to-face interpreting was £2,111,062. In the Southern Trust it was £831,472, Belfast Trust it was £630,453 and South Eastern Trust area it was £173,764.The pro rata projected expenditure for this year based on demand to August 2024, is £4,176,973. This, however, includes the summer months when demand is usually lower. Expenditure on telephone interpreting and translating has increased from £162,034 in 2020/21 to £202,680 last year. It is projected to increase again to £264,138 this year.HSC Interpreting Costs 2020/21 – 2024/25Trust/Year2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25Projection (on April-August)BHSCT630,4531,062,8431,433,9321,551,2491,384,034SEHSCT173,764275,160285,380310,020319,354NHSCT280,312489,743582,522618,913592,286SHSCT831,4721,264,6581,493,0311,577,0961,539,229WHSCT195,061322,609352,274368,329342,070Sub Total (face- to-face)2,111,0623,415,0134,147,1394,425,6084,176,973HSC Telephone interpreting and translation162,034171,836155,739202,630264,138 Total All      2,273,097          3,586,849          4,302,878          4,628,238          4,441,111 Thank you for your interest in this vital service that ensures improved access to health and social care services for Patients or Clients who do not speak English proficiently.
