question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement he has had with local residents in the vicinity of Belfast Grand Central Station on the potential introduction of dual language signage.
answer_text: I am committed to the visibility and promotion of Irish Language across public services and in our society.  I am fully supportive of the work that Translink has been doing with the local community throughout the development of the Belfast Grand Central Station and I am confident that the issue of dual language signage at the station will be resolved.I have not formally met with local residents on the issue of bilingual signage at the station but did meet with a number of local elected representatives earlier this year in March regarding the matter and to hear updates on their ongoing communication with key stakeholders and community groups on the promotion of the Irish Language on Public Transport and across the city.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they are committed to the opening of the prison buildings at the Maze site.
answer_text: No commitments have been made in relation to any future use of the prison buildings at the site.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the publication of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, for their assessment of whether violence against women and girls in the period between 1969 and 1998 was wrong.
answer_text: All violence against women and girls is wrong. All women and girls deserve to live in a place that is free from gender-based violence, abuse and harm. The Strategic Framework seeks to achieve this.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the agreed departmental understanding of the word woman as employed in the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: The Strategic Framework is intended to address the full spectrum of gender-based violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to publish a palliative care strategy.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of palliative and end of life care in supporting people with palliative care needs to live well and achieve the best quality of life as their illness progresses.My Department has indicated that it would be supportive of a new palliative care strategy for adults.   It is important to recognise however, the wide-ranging aspects of palliative and end of life care that are not restricted to Health and consideration of additional resources that would be required to implement a new strategy.   My Department has commenced a scoping exercise to consider the current provision of adult palliative care services and identify projected need. The outcome of the scoping exercise will provide a richer and more complete source of data that will help inform the future planning and commissioning of adult palliative care services across the region and will also underpin our future strategic direction.I also welcome the recent publication of the report of the Committee for Health on access to palliative care services.  I believe the report can provide a road map that will inform a strategic direction now and into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14687/22-27, how his Department can monitor progress in this area when this basic data is not collected.
answer_text: The Department through existing reporting mechanisms collects and monitors global information in relation to children and young people waiting on an ASD diagnostic pathway from all HSC Trusts. This information is recorded by length of wait but as it is a global figure cannot identify or report the age or length of time any child or young person has been on that waiting list.Waiting lists are managed in line with Integrated Elective Access Protocols (IEAP) guidelines. Each HSC Trust maintains individual information on all referrals accepted onto their waiting lists. Information on individual children referred for an autism assessment will be available from their local HSC Trust. HSC Trusts report on and are held accountable for the overall waiting times for children and length of wait. The Provision of autism assessment is delivered as a routine service and referrals are managed in chronological order of acceptance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether an assessment has been made of the potential negative impact of dual language signage in Belfast Grand Central Station on community relations.
answer_text: I am committed to the visibility and promotion of Irish Language across public services and in our society.  I am fully supportive of the work that Translink has been doing with the local community throughout the development of the Belfast Grand Central Station and I am confident that the issue of dual language signage at the station will be resolved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will give consideration to the founding of a Harry Ferguson museum of innovation in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Museums Council is the lead body supporting local museums on behalf of my Department. Any support available in relation to the founding of a Harry Ferguson museum of innovation would be for the Northern Ireland Museums Council to consider, which I have asked them to explore.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the financial management of InterTradeIreland.
answer_text: InterTradeIreland finance and governance policies and procedures are derived from the requirements of the legislatively required financial memorandum which has been drawn up and agreed by the partner departments and the respective finance departments in both jurisdictions. These are subject to regular review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14687/22-27, to detail the action he is taking to address this gap of knowledge in his Department.
answer_text: The Department through existing reporting mechanisms collects and monitors global information in relation to children and young people waiting on an ASD diagnostic pathway from all HSC Trusts. This information is recorded by length of wait but as it is a global figure cannot identify or report the age or length of time any child or young person has been on that waiting list.Waiting lists are managed in line with Integrated Elective Access Protocols (IEAP) guidelines. Each HSC Trust maintains individual information on all referrals accepted onto their waiting lists. Information on individual children referred for an autism assessment will be available from their local HSC Trust. HSC Trusts report on and are held accountable for the overall waiting times for children and length of wait. The Provision of autism assessment is delivered as a routine service and referrals are managed in chronological order of acceptance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of Tourism Ireland's promotion of Northern Ireland on the world stage.
answer_text: Tourism Ireland’s promotion of Ireland as a tourism destination is a key, and successful, driver for the economic growth of the North’s tourism sector.Tourism Ireland presents the North as a key part of the island of Ireland campaigns globally. In 2023, Tourism Ireland publicity relating to the North reached over half a billion people. Recent analysis on the return on Tourism Ireland’s marketing investment highlight a return of 25:1 in relation to visitor spend for the four largest markets: US, Britain, France and Germany.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to his reported comments in press articles in relation to mandatory vaccinations, to detail his rationale for consulting on imposing special restrictions or requirements, including requiring a person to be vaccinated, as part of the draft proposals for a public health bill.
answer_text: Current public health legislation is over 55 years old and focuses on infectious diseases. The threat to human health in the 21st century is unfortunately much broader and includes biological, chemical and radiological threats as well as infectious diseases. To protect us from these threats we need to better and more quickly identify these threats, quickly find where they came from, prevent them from spreading further or causing more harm and effectively treat those affected. Given the breadth and scale of this challenge, it is appropriate that a broad range of potential policy options are provided to the public for consideration to help shape this important legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the financial management of SafeFood.
answer_text: Departmental responsibility of the Food Safety Promotion Board (FSPB, aka Safefood) rests jointly with the Departments of Health in Ireland and the Department of Health, Northern Ireland, and it is funded on the basis of a 70/30% ratio respectively. The Body prepares an annual Business Plan and 3 yearly Corporate Plans, which must be agreed along with their budget by the Health and Finance Departments in each jurisdiction, before formal agreement at a North South Ministerial Council (NSMC).The Body  complies with all corporate governance guidance provided by both jurisdictions and within the provisions of an agreed Financial Memorandum. Safefoods accounts are audited on an annual basis and annual statements of accounts  are submitted to the NSMC as well as to the Comptroller and Auditor General for Northern Ireland and the Comptroller and Auditor General for Ireland who cooperate in examining and certifying the accounts. Safefood’s annual report and accounts are laid before the Northern Ireland Assembly and both Houses of the Oireachtas. The Body also has an advisory board with an audit and risk committee.I have not been alerted to any cause for concern by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to his comments in the Assembly on 10 September 2024, to detail the proportion of Tourism Ireland’s budget that is spent promoting Northern Ireland specifically as a tourism destination.
answer_text: Tourism Ireland is responsible for marketing Ireland overseas with core funding provided for this purpose rather than to support specific regions. The North features prominently in Tourism Ireland’s extensive programme of promotional activity in 14 key markets across Britain, North America, Mainland Europe and Australia.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the financial management of the North South bodies funded by his Department.
answer_text: I have Ministerial responsibility for the North/South Language Body. The legislation establishing the Language Body states that “The Body will comply with the provisions of a Financial Memorandum drawn up for the Body by the Northern Ireland and Irish Departments paying grants to the Body and the Finance Departments.” The Financial Memorandum prescribes the detailed financial arrangements to be complied with by the Body.I am assured that the Language Body is satisfying the governance and accountability conditions and requirements that are set out in the Financial Memorandum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any courthouses that have had the Royal Coat of Arms removed in the last ten years, including the reason for removal.
answer_text: The display of Royal Arms at courthouses and in courtrooms is legislated for under Section 66 of the Justice (Northern Ireland) Act 2002.No courthouses have had the Royal Coat of Arms removed in the last ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of whether there is evidence to support the claim made during an evidence session of the Committee for the Executive Office on 18 September 2024 that the Housing Executive is institutionally racist.
answer_text: The Housing Executive is not institutionally racist. Anyone who makes claims to the contrary should back it up with evidence which has not been provided to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any contact he had with the IFA in relation to the possible use of Windsor Park for 2028 UEFA European Football Championship matches prior to the UK Government announcement on the Casement Park project on 13 September 2024.
answer_text: I met with the Irish FA in March 2024, and they advised that redeveloping the National Football Stadium at Windsor Park for hosting 2028 UEFA European Championship Football matches was not under consideration. I will be speaking with the Irish FA and other partners to seek other ways in which Northern Ireland can still play a role in Euro 2028 and create a legacy for many years to come.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he remains committed to the Casement Park project.
answer_text: Casement Park is part of the Regional Stadia Programme, which is an Executive commitment of more than a decade, which remains in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the courthouses which still display the Royal Coat of Arms in the courtroom.
answer_text: The display of Royal Arms in courtrooms is prohibited by Section 66 of the Justice (Northern Ireland) Act 2002. This legislation does not prevent the display of Royal Arms anywhere in - the courtrooms in the Royal Courts of Justice in Belfast,the courtrooms in the Courthouse in Armagh,the courtroom in the Courthouse in Banbridge,Court No. 1 in the Courthouse in Downpatrick,the courtrooms in the Courthouse in Magherafelt, orthe courtrooms in the Courthouse in Omaghwhere they were displayed immediately before the coming into force of the Act, and are still displayed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action he is taking to reduce waiting times for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  A Paediatric Surgeon is currently going through an urgent validation of Peg Tube waiting lists.  This process will identity which children are suitable for potential transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  BHSCT are carrying out discussions with Alder Hey to ascertain the feasibility of this arrangement.  If agreed clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be in place to determine what children will be transferred.Belfast Trust is planning to provide a waiting list initiative in October on a Friday, with patients being nursed in RBHSC over the weekend. This initiative will be dependent on bed and theatre resources and unscheduled bed pressures.  Gastrostomy surgery is also planned in the Ulster Hospital.  Belfast Gastrostomy Nurses will provide training to staff in SET to help facilitate this. This initiative will be dependent on bed and theatre resources and unscheduled bed pressures.  There is also currently a review of surgical work performed in RBHSC and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery.The Trust are also examining the potential of partnership working with Private Sector.  This would mean using RBHSC capacity over the weekend to deliver Waiting List Initiatives in the Children’s Hospital. This initiative will be dependent on bed and theatre resources and unscheduled bed pressures.  In the meantime, paediatricians and paediatric surgeons along with other clinical teams at the RBHSC will continue to support these children and their families.My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions to examine their progress and success.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current average; and (ii) the longest waiting time for a child awaiting percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy insertion surgery.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  There are 93 children currently on the waiting list for this elective procedure with the longest wait being from June 2021.  All of these children are on Belfast Health and Social Services Trust’s (BHSCT) list as the procedure is only carried out at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC). Due to the introduction of the new ICT system, Encompass at BHSCT it is not possible to examine the data further at this stage.  It is hoped that when the Encompass system is fully operational across all Trusts in NI that the extraction and publication of data will be more streamlined and accessible.The Trust is currently carrying out an urgent validation of Peg Tube waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will announce a public inquiry in relation to the issues around screening for cancer in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The current Cervical Cytology Review in the Southern Trust is due to conclude shortly, after which a factual report will be produced before a detailed analysis of all findings will be undertaken. It is important to await the outcome of these processes as they will help determine the next steps.As Health Minister I remain committed to finding the most appropriate method to identify what happened, why it happened (and who was at fault) as well as ensuring it never happens again.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the projected total cost if magnetic pouches for mobile phones were rolled out across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It is anticipated that the delivery of the small-scale pilot will cost a maximum of £250,000.  I do not wish to pre-empt the findings of the pilot as it will be important to assess the impact of the approach and the views of the parents, pupils and teachers involved.  The findings of the pilot will be used to inform future policy decisions in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether he intends to continue his No Mow May policy in 2025; and (ii) whether any local section offices raised safety concerns in relation to the late commencement of grass cutting.
answer_text: My Department is committed to protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity when managing roadside verges. Our approach to verge management aims to maintain the right balance between road safety and the control of grass and weeds, while achieving a greater focus on environmental protection. By reducing the areas of verge cut on many roads to a single swath or, where possible without compromising road safety, reducing to a single cut later in the season, it is possible to increase the focus on environmental benefit. The month of May is especially important as bees and butterflies are actively foraging for food, sourced from dandelions and other flowering plants, found in grassy areas. By refraining from unnecessary grass-cutting during May, where possible, we are also helping to tackle pollution as well as locking away the very significant carbon below our feet. This approach will deliver benefits in promoting biodiversity. The level and timing of grass cutting that can be carried out in future financial years will, however, depend on the 2025-26 final budget determination for my Department.Road safety is always our priority and we continue to cut sight lines at junctions to ensure visibility for road-users. The No Mow May approach is not prioritised over road safety. Due to the extent of roadside verges to be maintained, grass cutting season commences in April on a scheduled basis. The schedule includes sightlines across the network, and only those areas that are not considered to compromise road safety are left unmown.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether there is a legislative or practical impact on their Department and its responsibilities as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: The Command Paper committed to establishing a new working group to consider all related issues, including the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why Elranatamab is not available in Northern Ireland to treat myeloma.
answer_text: The Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland. Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions.  In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.In circumstances where NICE has not yet fully appraised, or does not recommend a particular drug, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) which is responsible for commissioning does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland.However, in advance of, or in the absence of, a published NICE determination SPPG will seek and potentially apply decisions made by other well-established UK bodies as part of its Managed Entry process. In this case, in line with advice from the Scottish Medicines Consortium (SMC), SPPG will make elranatamab (Elrexfio®) available to patients in Northern Ireland on an interim commissioning (cost per case) basis, pending any recommendation from NICE to inform longer-term commissioning decisions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) each EU law referenced in Annex 2 of the Northern Ireland Protocol which impacts the work of their Department; (ii) each such EU law adopted since the publication of Annex 2 which impacts the work of their Department; and (iii) each EU law which has been removed from application in respect of their Department and its responsibilities.
answer_text: There are no EU laws referenced in Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework, past or present, that directly impact on the work of The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to introduce a screening programme for prostrate cancer.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee which advises the four nations, does not currently recommend screening for prostate cancer.  This is because the Prostate Specific Antigen (PSA) test is not accurate enough to detect prostate cancer that needs treatment. It is currently unclear if other tests such as a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan, with or without PSA, are accurate enough. Research is looking at whether a method for predicting prostate cancer risk using a combination of a blood test and other information about a man could be more accurate. However, more studies are necessary to confirm the early results. My Department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes. Anyone with symptoms which they believe may be related to the prostate gland should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) those from his Department, or in connection with his Department, that travelled to the United States of America at a cost to the public purse for St Patrick's Day events in March 2024; and (ii) the overall cost to the public purse, broken down by (a) travel; (b) accommodation; and (c) other expenses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the basic annual payment for foster parents was last increased; and (ii) what plans there are to review and increase the payment.
answer_text: The allowances paid to foster carers in Northern Ireland (NI) are for costs associated with day to day care; and are outlined within the annual published Fostering Model Scheme. The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis in respect of the decision for an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. The current published allowance rates are 2024-25 rates, and these include the annual uplift increase of 3% which was applied to foster care allowance rates for the 2024-25 financial year. The current allowances rates are published on the Department of Health website and are available to view at Foster care allowances | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk) . A decision on the allowance rates payable for 2025-26, will be undertaken as soon as the budget for 2025-26 is known and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplifts will be backdated to April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the most recent Project Assessment Review of the Casement Park project; (ii) whether there has been a Project Assessment Review carried out since July 2019, when a review produced a delivery confidence level of Amber and Red; and (iii) when the next Project Assessment Review is scheduled to take place.
answer_text: Since the Project Assessment Review of July 2019, which produced a delivery confidence level of Amber/Red, a review of the Casement Park Project, as a host stadium for the EURO 2028 Tournament, was carried out in August 2024. This produced a delivery confidence level of red for the first time. A further Project Assessment Review has not been scheduled at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that it is five years since the Reshaping Stroke Care Consultation, (i) to detail the (a) scanning; (b) thrombolysis or clot busting drugs; (c) occupational therapy; (d) physiotherapy; (e) speech and language therapy; and (f) discharge processes grade for each stroke service provider as assessed by the Sentinel Stroke National Audit Programme; and (ii) for his assessment of the grading of each.
answer_text: Stroke Sentinel National Audit Programme (SSNAP) has been used for several years to monitor process of Stroke Care in Northern Ireland (NI).  All 5 of the HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland (NI) have participated in the SSNAP since 2016.  SSNAP provides key performance data to benchmark, standardise and manage stroke acute and community performance.  Key SSNAP acute performance data, is grouped within 10 domains scanning, access to stroke unit, thrombolysis, specialist assessment, occupational therapy, physiotherapy, speech and language therapy, multi-disciplinary team (MDT) working, standards by discharge and discharge process.  A summary listing the 10 SSNAP domains and the key indicators contained within each can be publicly access via their website SSNAP - Home (strokeaudit.org). (ii) I have reviewed the latest SSNAP team centred data (Apr-Jun 24).  It should be noted BHSCT SSNAP reporting has likely been adversely impacted by the commencement of Encompass in June 24.  The majority of Trusts are performing strongly in respect of scanning and discharge process.  However, there is some regional variability in thrombolysis and an action plan has been developed to drive improvement.  Workforce challenges are impacting performance regarding occupational therapy, physiotherapy and speech and language therapy.   SSNAP performance data  is used within a number of  Stroke Network sub-groups to reduce regional variation and drive improvement in line with the Stroke Action Plan including;  the introduction of Artificial Intelligence in stroke imaging across 7 hospital sites;the completion of a thrombolysis audit identifying the primary reasons why individuals did not receive thrombolysis and recommendations for improvement;It is also important to recognise that, alongside this, there is ongoing Quality Improvement work across acute and community services in each Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of hospital beds that have been available in each financial year since 2006-07, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: The numbers of available beds in each financial year from 2006/07 to 2017/18, broken down by hospital, are included in the ‘Hospital Statistics: Inpatient and Day Case Activity Downloadable Data 2017/18’ CSV file for the ‘Hospital statistics: inpatient and day case activity 2017/18’ publication. This file is available for download at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/hospital-statistics-inpatient-and-day-case-activity-201718The numbers of available beds in each financial year from 2018/19 to 2023/24, broken down by hospital, are included in the ‘Hospital Statistics: Inpatient and Day Case Activity Downloadable Data 2023/24’ Excel file for the ‘Hospital statistics: inpatient and day case activity 2023/24’ publication. Please note, on 9th November 2023, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) launched ‘encompass’ - a new electronic patient record system. Its rollout across the other Trusts will follow on a phased basis throughout 2024/25.Inpatient and day case activity data for consultant-led episodes completed on or after 9th November 2023 in South Eastern HSCT are still being developed following the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records. Therefore, South Eastern HSCT data has not been included in the data table for 2023/24. This file is available for download at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/hospital-statistics-inpatient-and-day-case-activity-202324

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail why an official record was not kept of material relevant to the Covid-19 Inquiry from devices used by officials and Ministers; and whether this practice continues.
answer_text: Over 700 strands of informal messages, some of which are duplicates have been disclosed by TEO to the UK Covid Inquiry under Module 2C.  These records include messages between Senior Civil Servants, Ministers and SpAds. NICS introduced guidance entitled Official Information Held on Non-Corporate Channels in June 2022.  The guidance requires that information relating to departmental business should be recorded on Content Manager.  The guidance states that official information should not normally be processed using anything other than official devices and official channels.  Where this is not possible, the official information must be saved onto corporate systems as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) those from their Department, or in connection with their Department, that travelled to the United States of America at a cost to the public purse for St Patrick's Day events in March 2024; and (ii) the overall cost to the public purse, broken down by (a) travel; (b) accommodation; and (c) other expenses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the policy that NHS follow-up care is not available to patients who go private for operations.
answer_text: There are no plans to review the current policy. Clarification of extant policy on the treatment of individuals availing of private healthcare and the interface with secondary care and primary care services was issued in late 2024 to Health and Social Care Trusts and to GP practices.     https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/hsc-circular-clarification-guidance-roles-and-responsibilities-accessing-private-healthcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, as a consequence of the designation of Irish as an official language in the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022, for his assessment that Irish no longer meets the qualifying definition under the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages.
answer_text: The Identity and Language (NI) Act 2022 gives official recognition of the status of the Irish language in Northern Ireland in the context of national and cultural identity.  The Act does not make Irish an official language of Northern Ireland and it makes no comment on its status viz a viz the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) her Department’s policy on the retention and disposal of records; (ii) when the policy was initiated; (iii) when the policy was last revised; and (iv) any breaches of the policy that have occurred.
answer_text: The Department’s Retention and Disposal Schedule details the retention and disposal of records. This Schedule was implemented in 2012 and there have been two further revisions, the current one being 2021. There have been no reported breaches of the policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of civil servants that have been disciplined for not retaining records required by the Covid-19 Inquiry.
answer_text: None.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) his Department’s policy on the retention and disposal of records; (ii) when the policy was initiated; (iii) when the policy was last revised; and (iv) any breaches of the policy that have occurred.
answer_text: It is my department’s policy to manage the retention and disposal of records in line with relevant legislative, regulatory, fiscal and business obligations. The DfE Retention and Disposal Schedule became active on 14 February 2024 and will be revised at least every three years.	No breaches of the policy have occurred.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any engagement that has taken place between Northern Ireland Civil Service officials and (a) the Enhanced Coordination Mechanism on VAT and excise; and (b) the Specialised Committee, meeting as the Special Body on Goods; and (ii) whether any substantial changes been made as a result of these engagements.
answer_text: TEO officials have not attended any meetings of the Enhanced Coordination Mechanism on VAT and excise, or any meetings of the Special Body on Goods.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any estimate that has been made of the financial cost of agency, bank, and other temporary staffing costs in 2021-22, broken down by hospital.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trust expenditure on agency staff is incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained.  The use of agency staff in HSC, therefore, contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. A Regional Agency Reduction Implementation and Planning Group has been established to co-ordinate the improvement of staff utilisation, with the aim of reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend. The main function of the Group is to develop and implement a plan, with local and regional commitment and coordination, to safely facilitate, and manage the service impact of, reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend.Work is currently progressing regionally with the aim of reducing agency and locum expenditure across HSC. Significant progress has already been made since the implementation of the new agency frameworks with expenditure of ‘off-framework’ agency for nurses & midwives reducing by £120m against the base year 2022/23. A new framework aimed at reducing medical and dental locum costs is due to come into effect by the end of December 2025.Alongside this work our strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training has lead to significant progress being made in stabilising and growing the workforce in recent years,  The most recent data for end of September 2025 reports that since publication of the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy in 2018 there has been a 18.7% increase in whole time equivalent staff directly employed across the HSC.  That amounts to 10,630 additional whole time equivalent staff, including 1,331 (+31.7%) more wte medical and dental staff, 2,875 (+19.0%) more wte registered nurses and midwives, 1,017 (+23.9%) more wte allied health professionals and 971 (+25.8%) more wte social workers, directly employed by the HSC.The Department monitors Health and Social Care Trusts’ expenditure on agency and bank staff on a quarterly basis for specific staffing categories, across financial years broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.  Expenditure on Agency and Bank staff by Health and Social Care Trust, for the financial year 2021/22, is set out below. answer_text: The NICS Retention and Disposal Policy relates to all records, regardless of format, held by departments in the NICS. It does not extend to the handling and disposal of IT devices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Northern Ireland Civil Service has an electronic document and records management system which enables the control, retention and transfer of records in electronic format.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10421/22-27, why figures for red flag and urgent cases were not provided for the (i) Southern Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (iii) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.

The information provided in response to AQW 10421/22-27 was, therefore, provided by the HSC Trusts. As requested, each HSC Trust provided information regarding the longest waiting time. Each Trust also provided contextual information regarding average waiting time and the South Eastern and Western HSC Trusts chose to present this additional information by priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build for Cullybackey College, including (i) the preferred site; and (ii) the current estimated timescales associated with (a) commencement on site; and (b) completion of the project.
answer_text: (i) Business Case approval for the new school build project was granted on 4 July 2024.  The preferred site option identified in the Business Case was Dreen Road, Cullybackey.(ii) The current programme estimates commencement on site in December 2026, with completion in February 2029, however these projected timelines may be impacted by several factors including planning approval, site acquisition as well as budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the 2024 Basic Payment Scheme payments will be made to farmers.
answer_text: Payment of the 2024 Basic Payment Scheme (BPS) commenced on 2 September 2024, with £258.4 million issued to 23,254 farmers, representing 98% of eligible applicants, on the first day. Farmers in Northern Ireland receive their full BPS payments earlier than any other region of the United Kingdom and earlier than in the European Union.Payments will continue to be released following successful validation checks for the small number of remaining applications.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action he has taken since coming to office to advance the recommendations from the 2013 report into sectarianism in boxing, specifically the recommendation that boxers from Northern Ireland should have the option of representing Great Britain at the Olympic Games and other global tournaments.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“This was addressed in 2018 by Irish Athletic Boxing Association (IABA) and Sport NI are not aware of any amendments to this.  See link to IABA website https://iaba.ie/new-iaba-governance-advisory-group/.  “With respect to the Good Friday Agreement, IABA has outlined clear pathways for all boxers within Northern Ireland to enable them to box for either Team Ireland or Team GB in international competitions.  IABA has agreements established with all the UK boxing NGB’s (England Boxing, Boxing Scotland, Welsh Boxing and the British Amateur Boxing Association) to outline the potential routes to box for Team GB through each of the home nations.  It produced a leaflet detailing this which is published on the IABA website, see - http://iaba.ie/new-iaba-governance-advisory-group/iaba-boxing-leaflet-a4-dl-july-2018/ “Evidence of the ability to box for Team GB was demonstrated at the Paris 2024 Olympics, where Amy Broadhurst (who had previously represented Team Ireland) represented Team GB.  See link “Amy Broadhurst: GB fighter defends switch from Ireland after Olympics agony - BBC Sport” https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/articles/cn00kwvl5pzo”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with Sport NI will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Royal College of Pathologists Consulting Report for the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, dated 18 May 2023, for his assessment of whether patients who have had a negative test can have confidence in screening.
answer_text: The Royal College of Pathologists’ Report, regarding the cytology screening service in the Southern Trust, recommended, as a priority, that primary HPV testing in Northern Ireland should be implemented.Primary HPV testing was introduced into the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme from 11 December 2023.  Nearly all cervical cancers (99.7%) are caused by persistent infection with high-risk human papilloma virus ( HPV)  and primary HPV testing has been shown to be more effective than cytology (as the first test) at identifying those most likely to have cervical cell changes which could develop into cancer.In a small number of cases, cervical screening will give an incorrect result. This means it may say someone does not have HPV or cell changes when they do (a false negative). Going for cervical screening when invited can help reduce this risk, as it is likely that any HPV or cell changes that were missed on a previous test would be picked up by the next test. While no screening programme is 100% effective, cervical screening saves lives and it is essential that women keep coming forward for their cervical smear test when invited to do so as part of our well-established screening programme in Northern Ireland.  Women can be assured that our screening programme is now using a test which is more effective at detecting those at higher risk of developing cervical cancer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Royal College of Pathologists Consulting Report for the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, dated 18 May 2023, to detail any action being taken to address the lack of screeners.
answer_text: As you may be aware, the Southern Trust stopped providing a cervical screening laboratory service in October 2023.  The primary human papilloma virus (HPV) testing pathway, using an automated testing process, was introduced into the Northern Ireland cervical screening programme in December 2023. This was recommended by the UK National Screening Committee because it is a more accurate test, and will save more lives, than using cervical cytology (a manual test undertaken by screeners). Only samples which test positive for the presence of HPV are now required to have a cytology report. This change means that the volume of cytology testing has reduced by around 80% across Northern Ireland, requiring just one laboratory provider going forward. A service reconfiguration project is underway to transfer this service to a single site laboratory in Belfast Trust. The staffing requirements of the future service have been considered as part of the business case, alongside ensuring future sustainability of the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the Barnett consequentials for 2024-25 arising from enhanced childcare provision in England.

To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Royal College of Pathologists Consulting Report for the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, dated 18 May 2023, why Northern Ireland has agreed deviations from quality assurance standards used in England.
answer_text: Northern Ireland does not have agreed deviations from the standards used by the NHS Cervical Screening Programme in England, as published at https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/cervical-screening-programme-standards.These standards have been endorsed for use in Northern Ireland by the Northern Ireland Screening Committee, chaired by the Chief Medical Officer. The Department issued correspondence on standards for the cervical screening programme in June 2019, which can be viewed at - https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/hss-md-13-2019.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what discussions he had with his Executive colleagues to identify savings to retain Winter Fuel Payments for all pensioners.

To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Royal College of Pathologists Consulting Report for the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT), dated 18 May 2023, why it took eight months from when concerns were raised about the performance of the screeners until a paper was brought to the SHSCT senior management team.
answer_text: In October 2021, senior staff within the SHSCT cervical cytology laboratory identified a concern in relation to cervical cytology screener performance within the laboratory. I have been advised that immediate steps were taken to address any ongoing risk, pending the outcome of a more detailed review of all screener performance data by the senior team within the laboratory. Following the more detailed review, the senior laboratory team escalated the issue to the Trust’s Senior Management Team. The Trust then established a working group to look at the issue and make a recommendation on the way forward. The Trust however recognises that these concerns should have been progressed and raised more quickly with their Senior Management Team. The SHSCT has apologised for this delay.I am clear the issue should have been identified and dealt with earlier.I am focused on identifying (i) what happened, (ii) why it happened (and who was at fault) and (iii) what can be done to try to ensure it never happens again. Work across all three questions is progressing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Royal College of Pathologists Consulting Report for the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, dated 18 May 2023, to detail why evidence of persistent under-achievement by two screeners between 2010 and 2022 was not acted upon.
answer_text: Cytology is a qualitative test and it is well recognised in published studies that there will be differing opinions between individuals who report on cytology slides. Laboratories use internal processes and monitoring to control and minimise these differences. It is also recognised that within a cervical cytology service, the performance of any screener can vary from time to time for a range of reasons.During the period 2008 to 2021, some screeners’ performance data, in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT), did fall below the recognised standard. I am advised that this underperformance was identified by the SHSCT, and steps were taken by the Trust to address this, including update training, peer support and repeat audit. Whilst screener performance was recorded during these years it was not assessed from year to year, and the performance of some screeners did not reach the required standard despite having had the required update training and support. The Southern Trust recognise that the lack of assessment of performance comparing from year to year, was not part of the standard operating procedures and represented a system failure.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of staff employed in their Department, broken down by the section or area in which they work.
answer_text: The number of permanent staff employed in The Executive Office (TEO) is detailed in the table below. This reflects the staff in post as at 31 July 2024. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any occasions in the last three years of LGBT+ groups offering tutoring to employees of the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) to aid with promotions within the NICS; and (ii) whether this was promoted within the NICS email or intranet system.
answer_text: There have been no occasions in the last three years of LGBT+ groups offering tutoring to employees of the Civil Service to aid with promotions within the NICS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the gauge of the tracks at Belfast Grand Central Station had to be modified to comply with EU requirements.
answer_text: As this exact question has previously been asked by Jim Allister MLA I would refer you to my earlier answer for AQW 13411/22-27. The new railway lines being installed at Belfast Grand Central Station have been built in line with the 1600mm Irish track gauge, which has been used across the Island of Ireland since it was specified in the Railway Regulation (Gauge) Act of 1846.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when and how the UK government informed his Department that they would remove Northern Ireland from their IT system and that there would be no winter fuel payments made if his Department did not maintain parity with the rest of the UK; and (ii) whether there was a precedent for this when it came to welfare reform.
answer_text: On 28 August 2024 a senior official from the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) advised my Department, verbally, that a decision on maintaining/breaking parity on Winter Fuel Payments was required by Friday 30 August to allow for the necessary IT system changes to be applied.  DWP advised that any delay in the decision beyond this date, given the complexity of applying the relevant IT changes across all devolved administrations, would mean that it would not be possible to issue any Winter Fuel Payments to pensioners in Northern Ireland before the end of 2024.My Department currently makes use of DWP IT systems to deliver a wide range of social security benefits in Northern Ireland.  This includes those benefits introduced as part of the package of Welfare Reforms. While DWP will consider making appropriate changes to their IT systems to accommodate any DfC differences, such changes can take considerable time to implement and any additional associated costs are normally paid for by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what involvement his Department had (a) in making representations for; or (b) in the process of, naming Newry as a potential beneficiary of £20 million from the Levelling Up Fund; and (ii) whether there was a competitive process within which other towns could compete.
answer_text: The Levelling Up Fund is a capital fund developed by the former British Government and delivered centrally under its’ Levelling Up Agenda.NI Executive departments have no role in the process of identifying potential beneficiaries of Levelling Up Funding, or any input to the process by which towns might avail of this funding stream.All decisions in relation to the allocation and distribution of Levelling Up Funding are made by the British Government. Any questions regarding the allocation and distribution of these funds are a matter for the Ministry of Housing, Communities & Local Government.
